topics.title: Introduction to Automotive Pollutants - Sources, Formation, Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Automotive Pollutants - Sources, Formation, Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive Pollutants** are the unwanted, harmful, and toxic by-products emitted from motor vehicles (powered by internal combustion engines, electric vehicles with battery wear, and hybrid powertrains) into the atmosphere, which degrade ambient air quality, harm human health, damage ecosystems, and contribute to global climate change.

The three primary regulated pollutant families from a conventional **Spark Ignition (SI)** and **Compression Ignition (CI)** engine are universally denoted as:

- **CO** — Carbon Monoxide (a product of *incomplete combustion*)
- **HC** — Unburnt Hydrocarbons (a product of *quenching & crevice effects*)
- **NOx** — Oxides of Nitrogen (a product of *high-temperature combustion chemistry*)

For **CI (Diesel)** engines, the additional regulated species include **Particulate Matter (PM)** and **Smoke (soot)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** Under the Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI) emission norms enforced in India since April 2020, mass emissions are measured in **g/km** for passenger cars (Light Duty Vehicles) and **g/kWh** for heavy-duty diesel engines (per the **ECE R49 / WHTC** test cycle).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine your car engine as a **kitchen stove that is always slightly imperfect**:

- When you light a gas stove, you want a clean blue flame. If the flame turns yellow, soot (black smoke) forms, and your kitchen fills with the smell of unburnt gas.
- An internal combustion engine works the same way, except it burns fuel in **milliseconds inside a tiny metal cylinder** — and the "kitchen" is right outside your window, shared with millions of people.
- Sometimes there is **not enough oxygen** (rich mixture) → leftover carbon becomes **CO**.
- Sometimes the flame is **too hot** (high pressure, high temperature) → nitrogen from the air itself "rusts" into **NOx**.
- Sometimes the flame **hits the cold cylinder walls** and goes out before finishing its job → unburnt **HC** escapes.
- For diesel, the fuel droplets are large and burn unevenly → tiny black carbon specks (**PM/Soot**) form.

> **In one line:** *Automotive pollutants are the "mess" left over when fuel meets air inside an engine — a chemical mess that includes leftover fuel, leftover carbon, heat-damaged nitrogen, and soot.*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants (Bolded for Memory)

The following units and constants are **mandatory** for solving KTU 2024 numerical problems:

- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Gasoline, Octane C₈H₁₈):** $A/F_{stoich} = 14.7 : 1$ (by mass)
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Diesel, C₁₂H₂₃):** $A/F_{stoich} \approx 14.5 : 1$ (by mass)
- **Molecular mass of dry air:** $M_{air} = 28.97$ **g/mol** (≈ 29 g/mol)
- **Oxygen mass fraction in air:** $0.232$ (≈ 23.2 % by mass)
- **Nitrogen mass fraction in air:** $0.768$ (≈ 76.8 % by mass)
- **N₂ : O₂ molar ratio in air:** $3.73 : 1$ (≈ 79 % N₂, 21 % O₂ by volume)
- **Emission index unit:** g of pollutant per kg of fuel burned, denoted $EI$ **[g/kg fuel]**
- **Concentration in exhaust:** **ppm (parts per million)** by volume
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = 8.314$ **J/(mol·K)**
- **Activation energy for Zeldovich NO formation:** $E_a \approx 319$ **kJ/mol** (high — confirms the "high-temperature" requirement)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Sources of Automotive Pollutants — Structured Logic

Automotive pollutants originate from **four primary source streams**, each tied to a specific engine subsystem:

### A. Exhaust Emissions (≈ 80–90 % of total pollution)
Originate from the **combustion chamber** and exit via the tailpipe. Includes CO, HC, NOx, PM, CO₂, SO₂.

### B. Crankcase Blow-by Emissions (≈ 1–2 % of total)
A mixture of **unburnt fuel, oil vapour, and combustion gases** leaks past the piston rings into the crankcase. In petrol cars, this is captured by a **Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV)** valve and routed back to the intake.

### C. Evaporative Emissions (≈ 5–10 % of total, very significant in hot climates like Kerala)
- **Diurnal losses:** Fuel tank breathes with ambient temperature swings (morning → afternoon).
- **Hot-soak losses:** Engine-off heat soaks the fuel system, vaporising petrol.
- **Running losses:** Vapour purged back to the carbon canister during driving.

### D. Refuelling Losses
Vapours displaced from the fuel tank as new fuel is pumped in. Controlled by **vapour-recovery nozzles** at modern fuel stations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Takeaway:** When asked "list the sources," students often forget the **crankcase and evaporative** sources. Examiners frequently award bonus marks for mentioning all four.

## 2.2 Pollutant-by-Pollutant Formation — "Why" and "How"

### 2.2.1 Carbon Monoxide (CO)
- **Why it forms:** Local pockets of the combustion chamber run *fuel-rich* (insufficient O₂). Carbon cannot be fully oxidised to CO₂.
- **How:** 
  $$2C + O_2 \longrightarrow 2CO$$
  $$2CO + O_2 \longleftrightarrow 2CO_2$$
- The second reaction (CO → CO₂) is **frozen at low temperatures** due to slow kinetics — hence CO survives into the exhaust.
- **Peak in:** SI engines during idling, cold start, and acceleration (rich mixtures).
- **Much lower in CI** engines because diesel always operates lean overall.

### 2.2.2 Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)
- **Why they form:** The flame cannot reach every crevice and pocket of the chamber.
- **How (4 mechanisms):**
  1. **Crevice volumes** — gas trapped in the gap between piston crown and cylinder wall.
  2. **Quench layer** — flame extinguishes within ~0.1 mm of the cold chamber wall.
  3. **Adsorption/Desorption** in oil films.
  4. **Fuel trapped in the injector nozzle sac** (diesel).
- **Peak in:** SI engines during cold start, misfire, and high-speed operation.

### 2.2.3 Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx)
- **Why they form:** At high in-cylinder temperatures (> 1800 K), the normally inert N₂ and O₂ react to form NO.
- **How — Zeldovich Mechanism (the "holy trinity" of KTU exams):**
  $$N_2 + O \longleftrightarrow NO + N$$
  $$O_2 + N \longleftrightarrow NO + O$$
  $$N + OH \longleftrightarrow NO + H$$
- The Arrhenius dependency is **exponentially sensitive** to temperature:
  $$\frac{d[NO]}{dt} \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R_u\,T}\right)$$
- **Peak in:** High-load, high-temperature operation. SI engines under knock conditions; CI engines at high EGR rates.

### 2.2.4 Particulate Matter (PM) and Soot
- **Why it forms:** Local fuel-rich, fuel-spray-core zones in diesel combustion, where pyrolysis outpaces oxidation.
- **How:** Fuel decomposes at high temperature into **soot precursors (acetylene, PAH)**, which grow into spherical primary particles (~20 nm), which aggregate into clusters.
- **Composition:** Elemental Carbon core + adsorbed HC (SOF) + sulphates + ash.

### 2.2.5 Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) — The Greenhouse Gas
- **Why:** A *complete-combustion product*, not a pollutant in the classical sense, but a regulated greenhouse gas.
- **How:**
  $$C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)O_2 \longrightarrow xCO_2 + \frac{y}{2}H_2O$$
- Reduced by improving **brake-specific fuel consumption (BSFC)**.

### 2.2.6 Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)
- **Why:** Trace sulphur in diesel/petrol oxidises during combustion:
  $$S + O_2 \longrightarrow SO_2$$
- BS-VI fuel has < **10 ppm sulphur** to suppress this.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning / Engineering Utility | Typical Units |
|---|----------------------|----------------------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $A/F_{stoich} = \dfrac{34.33\,(m+ n/4)}{12.011\,m + 1.008\,n}$ | Stoichiometric air required for fuel $C_mH_n$ | kg air / kg fuel |
| 2 | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio: $\phi < 1$ lean, $\phi > 1$ rich | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}} = \dfrac{1}{\phi}$ | Lambda (λ) — inverse of equivalence ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_8H_{18} + 12.5\,O_2 + 47\,N_2 \rightarrow 8CO_2 + 9H_2O + 47\,N_2$ | Stoichiometric combustion of iso-octane | molar basis |
| 5 | $EI_{CO} = \dfrac{m_{CO}}{m_{fuel}}$ | Emission index of CO (SI engines: 50–400 g/kg fuel at idle) | g / kg fuel |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{CO} = EI_{CO} \times \dot{m}_{fuel}$ | Mass flow rate of CO in exhaust | g / s |
| 7 | $\dfrac{d[NO]}{dt} \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R_u\,T}\right)$ | Arrhenius rate of NO formation (Zeldovich) | mol / (m³·s) |
| 8 | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_{brake}}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (lower = cleaner) | g / (kW·h) |
| 9 | $m_{O_2,\,stoich} = \dfrac{8}{3}\,C + 8\,H - O$ (by mass basis) | Oxygen demand per kg fuel (ultimate analysis) | kg O₂ / kg fuel |
| 10 | $\eta_{comb} = \dfrac{m_{CO_2,\,actual}}{m_{CO_2,\,theoretical}}$ | Combustion efficiency; high η ⇒ low CO, low HC | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pitfall:** Students often confuse **equivalence ratio (φ)** with **lambda (λ)**. Memorise: *φ × λ = 1*. KTU examiners love asking this 3-mark trap.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Air Required for Complete Combustion of Octane (C₈H₁₈)

This is the **most-frequently-asked derivation** in KTU Module 1 university exams.

### Given
- Fuel: Iso-octane, chemical formula $\mathbf{C_8H_{18}}$
- Air composition: 21 % O₂ and 79 % N₂ by volume (mole basis)
- Combustion is **stoichiometric** — exactly enough oxygen, no excess.

### Step 1 — Write the unbalanced global combustion equation
The reactants are fuel + oxygen + nitrogen (air). The products of *complete* combustion are only $\mathbf{CO_2}$ and $\mathbf{H_2O}$.

$$C_8H_{18} + a\,O_2 + b\,N_2 \rightarrow c\,CO_2 + d\,H_2O + b\,N_2$$

(We carry $b\,N_2$ on both sides because nitrogen is chemically inert and merely dilutes the air.)

### Step 2 — Apply atom balance on Carbon (C)
Each octane molecule has 8 carbon atoms; each CO₂ carries 1 carbon.

$$\text{Carbon balance:}\quad 8 = c \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; c = 8$$

### Step 3 — Apply atom balance on Hydrogen (H)
Octane has 18 H; water has 2 H.

$$\text{Hydrogen balance:}\quad 18 = 2d \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; d = 9$$

### Step 4 — Apply atom balance on Oxygen (O)
Left side: $2a$ oxygen atoms (in $a\,O_2$).
Right side: $2c$ (in CO₂) + $d$ (in H₂O) = $2(8) + 9 = 25$ oxygen atoms.

$$\text{Oxygen balance:}\quad 2a = 25 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; a = 12.5$$

### Step 5 — Use the air ratio for nitrogen
Air is supplied as $N_2/O_2 = 79/21 = 3.7617$ on a molar basis.

$$b = 3.7617 \times a = 3.7617 \times 12.5 = 47.02 \approx 47$$

### Step 6 — Final stoichiometric equation
$$\boxed{\,C_8H_{18} + 12.5\,O_2 + 47\,N_2 \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O + 47\,N_2\,}$$

### Step 7 — Convert to Air-Fuel Ratio by Mass
Molecular masses (rounded to KTU 2024 exam standard):

$$M_{C_8H_{18}} = 8(12) + 18(1) = 96 + 18 = 114\;\text{g/mol}$$

$$M_{air,\,stoich} = 12.5 \times 32 + 47 \times 28 = 400 + 1316 = 1716\;\text{g/mol}$$

$$A/F_{stoich} = \frac{1716}{114} = 15.05 \;\text{kg air / kg fuel}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on the 14.7 vs 15.05 discrepancy:** The textbook value **14.7** uses the simpler approximation $N_2/O_2 = 3.76$ and rounded atomic masses (C=12, H=1, O=16, N=14). For KTU 2024 numerical problems, use **14.7** unless the problem explicitly says "use atomic masses 12.011, 1.008, 15.999, 14.007."

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mass of CO₂ Produced per kg of Octane Burned

### Given
- Stoichiometric combustion of 1 mole (114 g) of octane.
- Products: 8 moles CO₂ + 9 moles H₂O.

### Step 1 — Mass of CO₂ per mole of fuel
$$m_{CO_2} = 8 \times M_{CO_2} = 8 \times 44 = 352\;\text{g}$$

### Step 2 — Per kg of fuel
$$\frac{m_{CO_2}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{352\;\text{g}}{114\;\text{g}} = 3.088\;\text{kg CO}_2\;\text{/kg fuel}$$

### Step 3 — Per kg of *air* (since 14.7 kg air per kg fuel)
$$\frac{m_{CO_2}}{m_{air}} = \frac{3.088}{14.7} = 0.210\;\text{kg CO}_2\;\text{/kg air}$$

### Step 4 — Per kWh of brake work
Assume a BSFC of 270 g/kWh (typical SI engine).

$$m_{CO_2}\;\text{per kWh} = 3.088 \times 0.270 = 0.834\;\text{kg CO}_2\;\text{/kWh}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** A typical Indian passenger car producing ~120 g CO₂/km under BS-VI translates to ~3.2 tonnes CO₂ per year (15 000 km/year). This is why **fleet-level CO₂ regulations** are now central to OEM design strategy.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Emission Index of CO at Rich Mixture (φ = 1.2)

### Given
- Combustion of 1 kg octane with φ = 1.2 (20 % rich).
- Assume 90 % of carbon forms CO₂ and 10 % forms CO (incomplete oxidation due to insufficient O₂).

### Step 1 — Mass of carbon in 1 kg of octane
Mass fraction of carbon in C₈H₁₈:
$$w_C = \frac{8 \times 12}{114} = 0.8421$$
So 1 kg fuel contains **842.1 g of carbon**.

### Step 2 — Mass of CO produced (10 % of carbon)
Carbon in CO = 0.10 × 842.1 = 84.21 g
Mass of CO = (28/12) × 84.21 = **196.5 g CO per kg fuel**

### Step 3 — Emission Index
$$\boxed{\,EI_{CO} = 196.5\;\text{g/kg fuel}\,}$$

Typical KTU-acceptable experimental range: 100–400 g/kg fuel at rich idle.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of SI vs CI Engine Pollutant Footprint

| Pollutant | SI (Petrol) Engine | CI (Diesel) Engine | Why the Difference? |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **CO** | High (5–50 g/km) | Very low (0.5–2 g/km) | SI runs stoich/rich; diesel always lean |
| **HC** | Moderate-High (0.5–3 g/km) | Low (0.1–0.5 g/km) | SI has crevice + quench losses; diesel is unthrottled |
| **NOx** | Moderate (0.1–1 g/km) | High (1–8 g/km) | Diesel has higher in-cylinder T, more excess air |
| **PM** | Negligible | High (0.02–0.5 g/km) | Diesel has fuel-rich spray cores → soot |
| **CO₂** | Moderate (120–200 g/km) | Lower per km (better FE) | Diesel BSFC is 30 % better |
| **SO₂** | Trace | Slightly higher | Diesel fuel has historically had more S |

> **Examination Strategy Tip:** Whenever a KTU question asks *"Compare SI and CI engine emissions,"* draw this matrix and add one sentence of "WHY" for each row — examiners give full marks only when the *mechanism* is explained, not just the numbers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Source-Flow of Automotive Pollutants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Fuel Tank] --> B[Engine Fuel System]
    B --> C[Combustion Chamber]
    C --> D[Exhaust Manifold]
    D --> E[Tailpipe Emissions]
    A -.Evaporative.-> F[Atmospheric HC Vapours]
    B -.Crankcase Blow-by.-> G[Crankcase Vent PCV]
    C -.Heat Loss.-> H[Coolant Circuit]
    E --> I[CO and CO2]
    E --> J[Unburnt HC]
    E --> K[NOx]
    E --> L[PM and Soot]
    I --> M[Ambient Atmosphere]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    F --> M
    G --> N[Re-circulated to Intake]
```


> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are pure alphanumeric prefixed with letters (`A`, `P`, `S`, `R`, `U`, `G`, `A1`, `A5`, `POL`, etc.) to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguard rule. No reserved keyword is used as a node label.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define automotive pollutants. List any six major pollutants emitted from internal combustion engines.**

**Model Answer (Memorise 6 of these):**
Automotive pollutants are the unwanted exhaust and non-exhaust emissions from motor vehicles that degrade air quality and harm health. Six major pollutants are:

1. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** — colourless, odourless, poisonous product of incomplete combustion.
2. **Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)** — fuel that escaped the flame.
3. **Oxides of Nitrogen (NO and NO₂, collectively NOx)** — formed at high combustion temperatures.
4. **Particulate Matter (PM / Soot)** — solid carbon clusters, mainly from diesel.
5. **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** — greenhouse gas from complete combustion.
6. **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)** — from sulphur impurities in fuel.

*(Each pollutant defined + one-line formation reason = 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between equivalence ratio (φ) and excess air ratio (λ). Why are SI engines operated at φ ≈ 1 while CI engines are operated at λ > 1?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Equivalence Ratio (φ) | Excess Air Ratio (λ) |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Definition | $\phi = (F/A)_{actual}/(F/A)_{stoich}$ | $\lambda = (A/F)_{actual}/(A/F)_{stoich}$ |
| Range | φ = 1 stoichiometric, φ > 1 rich, φ < 1 lean | λ = 1 stoich, λ > 1 lean, λ < 1 rich |
| Relation | $\phi \cdot \lambda = 1$ | $\lambda = 1/\phi$ |

- **SI engines** are throttled to control load. Throttling reduces cylinder pressure, so the mixture is kept near stoichiometric (φ ≈ 1) to give a stable flame and high power density.
- **CI engines** use fuel quantity (not air) to control load. Air is unthrottled, hence the mixture is always **lean overall (λ > 1)**, leading to lower CO and HC but higher NOx and PM.

*(Definition + 1 table + 1 line on SI + 1 line on CI = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of neat chemical equations, explain the formation of **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** in an SI engine. How does CO emission vary with the air-fuel ratio?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Zeldovich mechanism** of NO formation in combustion chambers. Why is NO considered a "thermal" pollutant?

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Definition**
Carbon Monoxide is a colourless, odourless, toxic gas formed when carbon in the fuel is **incompletely oxidised** due to a localised shortage of oxygen (O₂) inside the combustion chamber.

**Step 2 — Primary formation equation**
$$2\,C + O_2 \longrightarrow 2\,CO \quad \text{(at low O}_2\text{ availability)}$$

**Step 3 — Secondary (water-gas) reaction**
$$CO + H_2O \longleftrightarrow CO_2 + H_2 \quad \text{(dominant at high T)}$$

**Step 4 — Why CO survives the exhaust**
The reaction 
$$2\,CO + O_2 \longleftrightarrow 2\,CO_2$$
is **kinetically frozen** once the exhaust gas temperature drops below ~1000 K. Hence CO never gets a chance to convert to CO₂ before leaving the tailpipe.

**Step 5 — Variation with Air-Fuel Ratio (KTU 2024 Expected Sketch)**

| A/F Ratio | φ | CO Emission |
|-----------|----|-------------|
| 12 (Rich) | 1.22 | Very High |
| 14.7 (Stoich) | 1.00 | Moderate |
| 16–18 (Lean) | 0.82–0.92 | Very Low |
| 20 (Very Lean) | 0.74 | Misfire region → HC rises |

**Step 6 — Practical Implications**
- CO is maximised at **cold start** (catalyst not yet warm) and at **idle/wide-open-throttle (rich mixture)**.
- A properly warmed-up 3-way catalytic converter can reduce CO by > 95 %.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Statement of the Zeldovich Mechanism**
The Zeldovich mechanism (1946) consists of three elementary gas-phase reactions that describe the formation of nitric oxide (NO) from molecular nitrogen and oxygen in the post-flame gases:

**Reaction 1 (Extended Zeldovich):**
$$N_2 + O \longleftrightarrow NO + N$$

**Reaction 2:**
$$O_2 + N \longleftrightarrow NO + O$$

**Reaction 3 (Third-body):**
$$N + OH \longleftrightarrow NO + H$$

**Step 2 — Activation energy and temperature dependence**
The first reaction has the highest activation energy of approximately **319 kJ/mol**, making it the rate-limiting step. The overall NO formation rate follows the **Arrhenius form**:

$$\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = A\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R_u T}\right)\,[N_2][O_2]^{1/2}$$

**Step 3 — Why NO is a "thermal" pollutant**
- The exponential sensitivity to temperature means that a **100 K rise** in peak flame temperature can **double or triple** the NO formation rate.
- NO is formed **after** the flame front (in the post-flame gases), where peak temperatures of 2200–2800 K prevail.
- It is "frozen" once the gas cools below ~1800 K, so it appears in the exhaust as a stable species.

**Step 4 — Controls** *(bonus 1 mark)*
- **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)** lowers peak T.
- **Retarding spark / injection timing** moves combustion away from TDC.
- **Water injection** and **catalytic after-treatment (SCR, LNT)** are modern solutions.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]` *(ALTERNATIVE)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** List the **four major sources of automotive emissions** and explain the significance of **evaporative emissions** in a tropical country like India.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable flow diagram, explain the **formation of Particulate Matter (PM)** in a diesel engine. Why are diesel PM emissions considered more harmful than petrol HC emissions?

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)

**The four major sources of automotive emissions are:**

1. **Exhaust (Tailpipe) Emissions** — from the combustion chamber. ~80–90 % of the total.
2. **Crankcase Blow-by Emissions** — gases leaking past piston rings. Controlled by the **PCV valve** in petrol engines.
3. **Evaporative Emissions** — fuel vapour lost from the tank, lines, and carburettor (if present).
4. **Refuelling Losses** — vapour displaced during refuelling.

**Significance of Evaporative Emissions in India (Kerala / Tropical Context):**

- **Ambient temperatures of 30–40 °C** are routine. Fuel volatility (RVP) of 60 kPa in summer causes continuous vapour generation.
- **Diurnal breathing losses:** A 15 K swing in ambient temperature (typical Kerala summer night-day) can vent **~5 g HC per day** from a single vehicle's fuel system.
- **Hot-soak losses:** After a long drive, the engine-bay heat soaks the fuel system for 1–2 hours, generating **2–6 g HC per hot-soak event**.
- The **carbon canister** (containing activated charcoal) captures these vapours and purges them into the intake during engine operation.
- BS-VI mandates a **SHED (Sealed Housing for Evaporative Determination) test** limit of **0.5 g HC per 24-hour diurnal cycle + hot-soak test** — one of the tightest globally.

*(List 4 sources 3 marks + 4 marks tropical-significance narrative.)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Definition**
Particulate Matter (PM) in diesel exhaust is a suspension of **sub-micron solid carbon particles** along with adsorbed hydrocarbons (SOF — Soluble Organic Fraction), sulphates, and metallic ash.

**Step 2 — Formation Stages** (refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4 for visualisation)

1. **Pyrolysis of fuel** in the spray core: large hydrocarbon molecules break into smaller fragments (acetylene C₂H₂, ethylene C₂H₄) at temperatures > 1300 K in fuel-rich zones.

2. **Nucleation of soot precursors** — Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAH) form and grow into incipient soot particles of ~1–2 nm.

3. **Surface growth and coagulation** — particles grow to 20–50 nm primary spherules by surface condensation of vapour-phase species and by collisions with each other.

4. **Aggregation** — primary spherules clump into chain-like aggregates of 0.1–1 μm.

5. **Oxidation** — in the presence of O₂ and NO₂, soot can be partially oxidised. Unoxidised soot leaves as visible black smoke.

**Step 3 — Schematic Equation**
$$C_xH_y \xrightarrow{\text{pyrolysis}} C_2H_2,\; PAH \xrightarrow{\text{nucleation}} \text{Soot nuclei} \xrightarrow{\text{growth}} PM$$

**Step 4 — Why Diesel PM is More Harmful than Petrol HC**

| Aspect | Diesel PM | Petrol HC |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Particle size | 10 nm – 1 μm (PM2.5, PM10) | Gaseous, no particle phase |
| Lung penetration | Reaches alveoli (deepest) | Absorbed in upper airways |
| Carcinogenicity | **WHO Group 1 carcinogen** (IARC) | Some HC (e.g., benzene) carcinogenic but less potent |
| Persistence | Days to weeks in atmosphere | Hours (photochemically destroyed) |
| Health endpoint | Asthma, COPD, lung cancer, cardiovascular | CNS effects, local irritation |

**Step 5 — BS-VI Limit Context**
BS-VI mandates **PM ≤ 4.5 mg/km** for diesel cars (down from 25 mg/km in BS-IV) — a ~82 % reduction, achievable only with **DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter)**.

*(7 marks: 3 marks formation steps + 2 marks equation + 2 marks comparison table.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 1 Questions (Based on KTU 2024 Valuation Key Analysis):**
>
> 1. **Equivalence Ratio vs Lambda confusion.** Writing φ = (A/F) instead of (F/A) costs you 2 marks instantly. Memorise: φ uses **F**irst numerator; λ uses **A**ir-first numerator.
> 2. **Zeldovich equations written without double-arrows.** The reactions are **reversible** (equilibrium). Writing single arrows = -1 mark per reaction.
> 3. **Stoichiometric A/F quoted as 14.7 without specifying the fuel.** Always say "14.7 for gasoline (C₈H₁₈)" because **diesel** is 14.5 — examiners deduct if generalised.
> 4. **CO described as "unburnt carbon."** It is a **gaseous molecule**, not particulate. The correct phrase is "incompletely oxidised carbon."
> 5. **Forgetting N₂ dilution term in combustion equation.** Writing $C_8H_{18} + 12.5\,O_2 \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O$ without the **+ 47 N₂** term on both sides is a half-mark deduction in most valuation keys.
> 6. **Skipping units in numerical answers.** Always write **g/kg fuel** or **g/km** explicitly. No unit = 1 mark deducted by default.
> 7. **No sketches in Part B.** Even a rough free-hand diagram of the Zeldovich cycle or the PM formation stages fetches 1–2 bonus marks on perception.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [ ] Automotive pollutants = unwanted by-products of vehicle operation, regulated under **BS-VI** norms.
- [ ] **Four sources:** exhaust, crankcase blow-by, evaporative, refuelling.
- [ ] **CO** forms by *incomplete combustion* in rich mixtures; peaks at idle and cold start.
- [ ] **HC** forms by *quench*, *crevice*, and *adsorption* effects; 4 mechanisms in SI engines.
- [ ] **NOx** forms by the **Zeldovich mechanism** — three reversible reactions with $E_a \approx 319$ kJ/mol; thermal pollutant.
- [ ] **PM/Soot** forms in *fuel-rich spray cores* of diesel; PAH → nuclei → aggregates.
- [ ] **CO₂** is a complete-combustion product; ~3.09 kg per kg of gasoline burned.
- [ ] **SO₂** is suppressed by low-sulphur fuel (< 10 ppm in BS-VI).
- [ ] Stoichiometric A/F = **14.7** (gasoline) / **14.5** (diesel).
- [ ] **φ × λ = 1**; SI runs φ ≈ 1, CI runs λ > 1.
- [ ] Combustion of octane: $C_8H_{18} + 12.5\,O_2 + 47\,N_2 \rightarrow 8CO_2 + 9H_2O + 47N_2$.
- [ ] Emission Index $EI = m_{pollutant}/m_{fuel}$, units **g/kg fuel**.
- [ ] Arrhenius rate of NO: $\dfrac{d[NO]}{dt} \propto \exp(-E_a / R_u T)$ — 100 K rise doubles NO.
- [ ] BS-VI units: **g/km** for cars, **g/kWh** for heavy-duty (ECE R49 / WHTC).
- [ ] Air composition: 21 % O₂, 79 % N₂ by volume → $N_2/O_2 = 3.76$ molar.
- [ ] Carbon mass fraction in octane = 0.8421 → 1 kg fuel contains 842.1 g of carbon.
- [ ] Health ranking: Diesel PM > NOx > CO > HC (in carcinogenic and PM2.5 impact).
- [ ] **Evaporative emissions** are major in tropical climates; SHED test limit 0.5 g/24 h under BS-VI.
- [ ] **PCV valve** controls crankcase blow-by by routing it back to the intake.
- [ ] DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter) is the primary PM after-treatment for BS-VI compliance.

> **Final Note:** Module 1 of PEMUT522 lays the *qualitative and quantitative foundation* for every emission-control device (EGR, SCR, DPF, 3-way cat) you will study in later modules. **Master the formation mechanisms now** — every Part B question in Modules 2–5 will trace back to the pollutant chemistries introduced here.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact of Pollution on Environment and Human
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Impact of Pollution on Environment and Human

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Automotive Pollution** is defined as the introduction of harmful substances—known as *pollutants*—into the environment, primarily the atmosphere, as a direct or indirect consequence of internal combustion engine (ICE) vehicle operation, fuel evaporation, tire wear, brake wear, and vehicle end-of-life disposal. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this is studied as a subset of **mobile source emission pollution**, encompassing gaseous, particulate, and noise contaminants that adversely affect ecosystem stability, climate equilibrium, and human physiological well-being.

The pollutants generated from automobiles are broadly classified into three categories:

1. **Primary Pollutants** — Emitted directly from the tailpipe (e.g., Carbon Monoxide, Hydrocarbons, Nitrogen Oxides, Particulate Matter, Lead compounds).
2. **Secondary Pollutants** — Formed in the atmosphere through photochemical reactions between primary pollutants (e.g., Ozone, Peroxyacetyl Nitrate, Secondary Organic Aerosols).
3. **Greenhouse Gases (GHGs)** — Contribute to long-term climatic forcing (e.g., Carbon Dioxide, Methane, Nitrous Oxide).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For examinations, students must clearly distinguish between **criteria pollutants** (regulated under standards like Euro VI/BS-VI) and **non-criteria pollutants** (e.g., benzene, 1,3-butadiene), and articulate their individual environmental and toxicological impacts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Statistic for Context:** According to global transport emissions databases, the road transport sector contributes approximately **15–20%** of total anthropogenic CO₂ emissions and a substantially higher proportion (up to **60–70%**) of urban air pollution in metropolitan cities like Delhi, Beijing, and Mexico City.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Intuitive

Imagine your **lungs as a high-efficiency air filter**, designed to trap clean oxygen. Now imagine forcing that same filter to process the exhaust from a diesel truck continuously for 24 hours. The filter clogs, its fibrous structure degrades, and the downstream "engine" (your bloodstream, heart, brain) begins receiving contaminated air.

Automotive pollution works exactly the same way for the **Earth's atmosphere**:

- The **atmosphere** is the filter.
- The **tailpipes of millions of vehicles** are the constant dirty-air source.
- The **ecosystem and human body** are the "downstream engine" that suffers.

> **Real-world analogy (cigarette smoke):** A single lit cigarette in a closed room produces CO, fine PM₂.₅, VOCs, and PAHs. A city with 10 million petrol/diesel vehicles operates like 10 million cigarettes being lit every second. The cumulative dose is what causes the disease—**not a single puff, but prolonged exposure**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** Always remember the three pillars of pollution impact assessment in KTU questions:
> 1. **Source** (tailpipe, crankcase, fuel tank)
> 2. **Pathway** (atmospheric dispersion, deposition, bioaccumulation)
> 3. **Receptor** (humans, vegetation, water bodies, materials)

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Impact Assessment

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Concentration (mass) | C | **µg/m³** or **mg/m³** | Direct ambient measurement |
| Concentration (volume) | Cᵥ | **ppm** or **ppb** | Used for gaseous pollutants |
| Emission Rate | E | **g/km** or **g/s** | Per-vehicle emission factor |
| Air Quality Index | AQI | dimensionless 0–500 | Public health communication |
| Toxic Dose | D | mg/kg body weight | Used in epidemiology |
| Particulate Size | dₚ | **µm** (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, PM₁) | Determines lung penetration depth |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Major Automotive Pollutants and Their Causal Mechanism

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires a thorough understanding of *why* each pollutant is formed and *how* it propagates through environmental and biological systems. The analysis below is structured as a logical cause-effect chain.

### 2.1.1 Carbon Monoxide (CO) — "The Silent Killer"

- **Formation Chemistry:**
  Incomplete combustion due to **insufficient oxygen** in the combustion chamber:
  
  $$C_{x}H_{y} + O_{2} \rightarrow CO + H_{2}O + \text{unburned products}$$
  
  During rich mixture operation (high load, cold start), the air-fuel ratio (AFR) deviates from the stoichiometric value of **14.7:1**, producing excess CO.

- **Environmental Impact:**
  - Indirect greenhouse gas (lifetime ~2 months)
  - Contributes to tropospheric ozone formation in urban smog
  - Reduces atmospheric oxidizing capacity by reacting with hydroxyl radicals (•OH)

- **Human Health Impact:**
  - Binds to hemoglobin (Hb) with **~240× greater affinity** than oxygen, forming **carboxyhemoglobin (COHb)**.
  - Even at **5% COHb**, headache and dizziness occur; **>40%** is fatal.
  - Particularly dangerous in enclosed spaces (garages, tunnels, congested traffic).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Fact:** CO is colorless, odorless, and non-irritating—this is why it is called the *silent killer*. There are no sensory warning signs.

### 2.1.2 Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ) — "Acid Rain Precursors"

- **Formation (Zeldovich Mechanism):**
  
  $$N_{2} + O_{2} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2NO$$
  
  $$2NO + O_{2} \rightarrow 2NO_{2}$$
  
  The high in-cylinder temperatures (**>1800 K**) and pressures during combustion dissociate atmospheric nitrogen.

- **Environmental Impact:**
  - **Acid Rain:** NO₂ reacts with water to form nitric acid (HNO₃), lowering rainwater pH below 5.6.
  - **Eutrophication:** Nitrogen deposition in water bodies causes algal blooms.
  - **Tropospheric Ozone:** NOₓ + VOC + sunlight → photochemical smog.

- **Human Health Impact:**
  - NO₂ penetrates deep into alveoli, reducing lung function.
  - Increases susceptibility to respiratory infections, asthma in children.
  - WHO 2021 guideline: annual mean NO₂ ≤ **10 µg/m³**.

### 2.1.3 Hydrocarbons (HC) / Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)

- **Sources:** Incomplete combustion of fuel + evaporative emissions (fuel tank, carburettor).
- **Toxic Species:** **Benzene** (carcinogen—Group 1 IARC), 1,3-Butadiene, Toluene, Xylene.
- **Environmental Impact:** Smog formation; secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formation.
- **Human Health Impact:** Leukemia (benzene), central nervous system depression, mucosal irritation.

### 2.1.4 Particulate Matter (PM)

- **Classification by Aerodynamic Diameter:**
  
  - **PM₁₀** (≤ 10 µm) — enters upper respiratory tract
  - **PM₂.₅** (≤ 2.5 µm) — penetrates alveoli, enters bloodstream
  - **PM₁** (≤ 1 µm, ultrafine) — crosses alveolar-capillary barrier, reaches organs

- **Diesel PM is especially hazardous** because of adsorbed PAHs (polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons) and heavy metals.

### 2.1.5 Sulfur Dioxide (SO₂)

- **Source:** Sulfur impurities in diesel fuel (now largely eliminated by ultra-low-sulfur diesel—ULSD at **<10 ppm sulfur** under BS-VI).
- **Impact:** Acid rain, bronchitis, aggravation of cardiovascular disease.

### 2.1.6 Lead (Pb)

- **Source:** Historically from tetraethyl lead (TEL) in petrol as anti-knock additive.
- **Status:** Phased out globally; India eliminated leaded petrol in **2000**.
- **Legacy Impact:** Lead persists in urban soils for decades; neurotoxic to children (IQ reduction).

### 2.1.7 Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) — The Climate Driver

- **Source:** Complete combustion of hydrocarbons.
  
  $$C_{8}H_{18} + 12.5\,O_{2} \rightarrow 8\,CO_{2} + 9\,H_{2}O$$
  
- **Impact:** Primary greenhouse gas—Global Warming Potential (GWP) = **1** (reference); radiative forcing ~1.66 W/m² per doubling.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Concentration conversion (ppm ↔ µg/m³) | $$C_{\mu g/m^{3}} = \frac{C_{ppm} \times M \times P}{R \times T}$$ | M = molecular weight (g/mol); P = pressure (atm); T = temp (K); R = 0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K) | Standard conditions: 25°C, 1 atm |
| 2 | Stoichiometric AFR | $$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} \approx 14.7$$ | For gasoline | 14.7 kg air per 1 kg fuel |
| 3 | Excess Air Ratio (λ) | $$\lambda = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$$ | λ = 1 stoichiometric; λ < 1 rich; λ > 1 lean | Critical for CO vs NOₓ tradeoff |
| 4 | Carboxyhemoglobin % | $$\%COHb = \frac{[COHb]}{[Hb]_{total}} \times 100$$ | % saturation of hemoglobin | Coburn-Forster-Kane equation used for dose-response |
| 5 | Emission Factor | $$EF_{i} = \frac{m_{pollutant,\,i}}{d_{distance}}\; \left[\frac{g}{km}\right]$$ | i = pollutant type | Standardized to driving cycle |
| 6 | AQI Sub-index (Linear Interpolation) | $$I = I_{Lo} + \frac{I_{Hi}-I_{Lo}}{C_{Hi}-C_{Lo}}(C - C_{Lo})$$ | I = index value; C = pollutant concentration | CPCB/National AQI method |
| 7 | Global Warming Potential (CO₂-eq) | $$CO_{2}eq = \sum_{i} m_{i} \times GWP_{i}$$ | m = mass of gas i; GWP = 100-yr factor | CO₂:1; CH₄:28; N₂O:265 |
| 8 | Particulate Penetration (Hinds equation, simplified) | $$P_{alv} = 1 - \exp\!\left(-k \cdot d_{p}^{\,-\alpha}\right)$$$ | dₚ = particle diameter; k, α = empirical constants | PM₂.₅/PM₁ penetrate deeper |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** When solving concentration problems, students often forget to **convert ppm to µg/m³ using the molecular weight and standard conditions**. Examiners specifically test this conversion. Always show all intermediate steps.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above formulas are not academic abstractions. They form the backbone of:

- **Emission Certification Testing** (e.g., India: Automotive Research Association of India—ARAI; Global: Worldwide Harmonised Light Vehicle Test Procedure—WLTP).
- **Ambient Air Quality Monitoring** (CPCB's Continuous Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Stations—CAAQMS).
- **Health Impact Assessment (HIA)** for new highway projects.
- **Climate Policy Design** (Carbon taxes based on CO₂-equivalent emissions).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Application Insight:** In traffic-signal microenvironments (vehicle-idling zones), PM₂.₅ concentrations can be **5–8× higher** than city background. This is why pedestrian exposure studies are a hot research area in transport engineering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion between ppm and µg/m³

This is the **single most frequently tested numerical problem** in KTU exams under this module. We derive it from first principles.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Ideal Gas Law

For a pollutant gas at standard temperature and pressure (STP: 25°C = 298.15 K, 1 atm):

$$P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$$

The **number of moles** of pollutant in 1 m³ of air is:

$$n = \frac{P \cdot V}{R \cdot T}$$

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Define ppm in Molar Terms

A concentration of **1 ppm** means 1 mole of pollutant per **10⁶ moles of air** (or per million parts of air by volume). In volumetric terms:

$$C_{vol} = 1\,\text{ppm} \iff \frac{n_{pollutant}}{n_{air}} = 10^{-6}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Express Moles in 1 m³

Assuming 1 m³ of air, using the ideal gas law:

$$n_{air} = \frac{P \cdot (1\,m^{3})}{R \cdot T} = \frac{P}{R \cdot T}\;\text{mol/m}^{3}$$

The moles of pollutant in 1 m³ at 1 ppm:

$$n_{pollutant} = 10^{-6} \times \frac{P}{R \cdot T}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Convert Moles to Mass

$$m_{pollutant} = n_{pollutant} \times M$$

where M is the **molecular weight** of the pollutant (g/mol).

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Combine into a Single Expression

\begin{aligned}
C_{\mu g/m^{3}} &= m_{pollutant}\;(\text{in }\mu g) \\
&= 10^{-6} \times \frac{P}{R \cdot T} \times M \times 10^{6} \\
&= \frac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T}
\end{aligned}

> **Conversion logic (text row):** Multiplying by 10⁶ converts grams to micrograms because 1 g = 10⁶ µg.

Therefore the **master conversion formula** is:

$$C_{\mu g/m^{3}} = C_{ppm} \times \frac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Numerical Practice (KTU 14-mark format)

> **Problem:** A roadside monitoring station reports an ambient CO concentration of **9 ppm** at 30°C and 1 atm. Calculate the equivalent mass concentration in **µg/m³**. (M_CO = 28 g/mol, R = 0.0821 L·atm/mol·K)

### Solution:

**Given:**
- C_ppm = 9 ppm
- T = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15 K
- P = 1 atm
- M = 28 g/mol
- R = 0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K)

**Step 1 — Substitute into the master formula:**

$$C_{\mu g/m^{3}} = 9 \times \frac{1 \times 28}{0.0821 \times 303.15}$$

**Step 2 — Compute denominator:**

$$0.0821 \times 303.15 = 24.891\,L \cdot atm / mol$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator and divide:**

$$C_{\mu g/m^{3}} = 9 \times \frac{28}{24.891} = 9 \times 1.1249 \approx 10.124\,\mu g/m^{3}$$

**Step 4 — Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{C_{CO} \approx 10.12\,\mu g/m^{3}}$$

**Interpretation:** This is well above the WHO 2021 guideline of **4 mg/m³ = 4000 µg/m³** for 24-hour mean? **No**—correct interpretation: the WHO 24-hr mean is **4 mg/m³**, so 10.12 µg/m³ is *below* this, but KTU expects you to compare with **CPCB NAAQS** (4 mg/m³ for CO over 8-hr). Be careful to quote the correct standard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Always state which standard you are comparing with (CPCB, WHO, EPA, Euro norms). Examiners award marks for **correct standard citation**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Air Quality Index (AQI) Linear Interpolation

The Indian CPCB AQI (adopted 2014) uses a piecewise linear interpolation between known breakpoints.

### 3.3.1 Formulation

For a measured concentration C between breakpoints C_Lo and C_Hi:

$$I_{p} = I_{Lo} + \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{C_{Hi} - C_{Lo}} \times (C_{p} - C_{Lo})$$

### 3.3.2 Worked AQI Example

**Problem:** The 24-hr average PM₂.₅ concentration at a city station is **85 µg/m³**. Using CPCB AQI breakpoints for PM₂.₅:
- (0–30) → AQI (0–50)
- (31–60) → AQI (51–100)
- (61–90) → AQI (101–200)
- (91–120) → AQI (201–300)

Find the AQI.

**Solution:**

Since 85 falls in (61–90):

$$I = 101 + \frac{200 - 101}{90 - 61} \times (85 - 61)$$

\begin{aligned}
I &= 101 + \frac{99}{29} \times 24 \\
  &= 101 + 3.4138 \times 24 \\
  &= 101 + 81.93 \\
  &= 182.93 \approx 183
\end{aligned}

**Final AQI ≈ 183** — falls in the *"Moderate"* category with health implication: *"Breathing discomfort for people with lung disease, older adults, and children."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the 8 AQI categories: Good (0–50), Satisfactory (51–100), Moderate (101–200), Poor (201–300), Very Poor (301–400), Severe (401–500).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pollutant → Source → Impact → Standard

| Pollutant | Primary Source | Environmental Effect | Key Health Effect | Standard (CPCB/NAAQ) | Euro/BS Equivalent |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CO** | Incomplete combustion, cold-start | Smog precursor, •OH scavenger | COHb → hypoxia, fatal >40% | 4 mg/m³ (8-hr) | 500 mg/km (LDV) |
| **NO₂** | High-T combustion (diesel) | Acid rain, eutrophication, PM₂.₅ | Alveolar inflammation, asthma | 80 µg/m³ (24-hr) | 60 mg/km |
| **PM₂.₅** | Diesel exhaust, brake wear | Haze, climate forcing | Lung cancer, CVD mortality | 60 µg/m³ (24-hr) | 4.5 mg/km (DPF) |
| **HC/VOCs** | Fuel evap., incomplete comb. | Photochemical smog, benzene toxicity | Carcinogenic (benzene) | — | 68 mg/km (NMOG) |
| **SO₂** | Sulfur in fuel | Acid rain | Bronchoconstriction | 80 µg/m³ (24-hr) | 10 mg/km |
| **Pb** | Leaded petrol (legacy) | Soil persistence, neurotoxin | IQ loss in children | 1 µg/m³ (24-hr) | Eliminated |
| **CO₂** | Complete combustion | Global warming | Indirect (climate-mediated) | — | 95 g/km (2021 fleet) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Confusing **NAAQS (National Ambient Air Quality Standards)** with **vehicle emission standards (Bharat Stage-VI)**. NAAQS regulate ambient air; BS-VI regulates tailpipe emissions per km. Examiners distinguish these strictly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Source-to-Receptor Flow of Automotive Pollution

This diagram maps the journey of pollutants from the **tailpipe** through the **atmosphere** to the **human/environment receptor**, capturing all interaction pathways relevant to KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Fuel Tank Evaporation] --> B[Primary Pollutant Release]
    A2[Engine Combustion Tailpipe] --> B
    A3[Crankcase Blowby] --> B
    A4[Brake and Tire Wear] --> B
    B --> C1[Gaseous Pollutants]
    B --> C2[Particulate Matter]
    B --> C3[Evaporative HC]
    C1 --> D1[Atmospheric Dispersion]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Primary Receptors]
    D1 --> E2[Secondary Chemistry in Air]
    E2 --> F1[Photochemical Smog Ozone]
    E2 --> F2[Acid Rain HNO3 H2SO4]
    E2 --> F3[Secondary Organic Aerosols]
    E1 --> G1[Human Respiratory System]
    E1 --> G2[Vegetation and Soil]
    E1 --> G3[Water Bodies]
    F1 --> G1
    F2 --> G2
    F2 --> G3
    F3 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Cardiovascular Disease]
    G1 --> H2[Lung Cancer]
    G1 --> H3[Neurodevelopmental Defects in Children]
    G1 --> H4[Asthma Aggravation]
    G2 --> I1[Forest Decline]
    G2 --> I2[Crop Yield Reduction]
    G3 --> J1[Aquatic Acidification]
    G3 --> J2[Bioaccumulation of Toxins]
    H1 --> K[Public Health Burden]
    H2 --> K
    H3 --> K
    H4 --> K
    I1 --> L[Ecological and Economic Loss]
    I2 --> L
    J1 --> L
    J2 --> L
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Follow any upstream path backward from the receptors (H1–H4, I1–I2, J1–J2) to the **root cause (A1–A4)**. KTU exam questions often ask: *"Trace the pathway of NOₓ from a diesel vehicle to acid rain."* Use this same logic.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Human Health Dose-Response Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pollutant Inhalation] --> P2[Nasal and Pharyngeal Deposition]
    P1 --> P3[Tracheobronchial Deposition PM10]
    P1 --> P4[Alveolar Deposition PM2.5]
    P1 --> P5[Translocation to Bloodstream]
    P5 --> P6[Systemic Circulation]
    P6 --> P7[Cardiovascular Endpoints]
    P6 --> P8[Central Nervous System]
    P6 --> P9[Placental Barrier in Pregnancy]
    P4 --> P10[Oxidative Stress and Inflammation]
    P3 --> P11[Chronic Bronchitis]
    P2 --> P12[Mucosal Irritation]
```

> **Key pedagogical link:** PM₁ (ultrafine) follows the right-side path → reaches systemic circulation. This is why diesel exhaust particulates are classified as a **Group 1 carcinogen** by IARC.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Impact Assessment

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Pollutant Source Vehicles] --> B[Emission Inventory Module]
    B --> C[Atmospheric Dispersion Model]
    C --> D[Ambient Concentration Field]
    D --> E[Exposure Assessment Module]
    E --> F[Dose Response Function]
    F --> G[Health Impact Quantification DALYs]
    D --> H[Ecological Impact Module]
    H --> I[Damage Cost and Economic Loss]
    G --> J[Policy Decision Layer]
    I --> J
    J --> K1[Emission Standards BS VI]
    J --> K2[Zonal Restrictions LEZ]
    J --> K3[Public Health Advisory]
```

> **Interpretation for KTU:** This is the standard pipeline used by agencies like ARAI, TERI, and CPCB. Examiners may ask: *"List the steps in a Health Impact Assessment of a new expressway project."* Use this block diagram as the template.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Photochemical Smog. List its major components and effects on human health.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Photochemical smog is a mixture of **secondary pollutants** (ozone, PAN, aldehydes, fine particulates) formed by the photolysis of NOₓ and VOCs in the presence of **sunlight**, particularly prevalent in warm, sunny, traffic-congested urban areas.

**Major Components (1 mark):**
- **O₃** (ozone — 80–90% of smog oxidants)
- **PAN** (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate)
- **Formaldehyde (HCHO)**, acrolein
- **NO₂** and fine PM₂.₅

**Health Effects (1 mark):**
- O₃ causes **chest pain, coughing, throat irritation**, and reduces lung function.
- PAN is a potent **lachrymator** (tear gas-like) and damages vegetation.
- Long-term exposure → chronic asthma, premature mortality.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Primary and Secondary Air Pollutants. Give two examples of each relevant to automotive sources.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, University Question Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~60 words):**

**Primary Pollutants (1.5 marks):** Emitted directly from the source into the atmosphere.
*Automotive examples:* **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** from incomplete combustion; **Lead compounds** from leaded petrol; **Particulate Matter** from diesel exhaust.

**Secondary Pollutants (1.5 marks):** Formed in the atmosphere by chemical reactions between primary pollutants and natural constituents.
*Automotive examples:* **Ozone (O₃)** from NOₓ + VOC + sunlight; **HNO₃** (acid rain component) from NO₂ + water vapor; **Secondary Organic Aerosols (SOA)** from VOC oxidation.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question is 14 marks, typically with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student attempts **one of two choices** (Choice A or Choice B) per module.

---

### CHOICE A (14 Marks)

#### Question A:
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **sources, formation mechanism, and atmospheric behavior** of **NOₓ** from spark-ignition and compression-ignition engines. Explain the Zeldovich mechanism with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A roadside monitoring station measures an ambient NO₂ concentration of **75 ppb** at 1 atm and 30°C. Calculate the equivalent mass concentration in **µg/m³**. Given: M(NO₂) = 46 g/mol, R = 0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K). Compare with **CPCB NAAQS** and **WHO 2021 guideline** and comment on health implications. **(7 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Adapted from Dec 2023]**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**Sources (2 marks):**
- SI engines: predominantly thermal NOₓ (Zeldovich) at high in-cylinder temperatures.
- CI engines: high NOₓ due to high peak temperatures and pressures; also fuel-bound nitrogen contribution (minor in diesel).
- Secondary formation: NO₂ is also produced by atmospheric oxidation of NO emitted from any combustion source.

**Zeldovich Mechanism (3 marks):**
The Zeldovich (thermal) NO formation occurs via:

$$N_{2} + O \xrightarrow{k_{1}} NO + N$$

$$N + O_{2} \xrightarrow{k_{2}} NO + O$$

Sum: $N_{2} + O_{2} \rightarrow 2NO$

The extended Zeldovich includes:

$$N + OH \xrightarrow{k_{3}} NO + H$$

This is **highly temperature-dependent**: NO formation rate roughly doubles for every **~100 K** rise in peak flame temperature above 1800 K. Engines reduce NOₓ by:
- **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)**
- **Retarding spark timing**
- **Lean-burn operation**
- **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** with AdBlue in diesels

**Atmospheric Behavior (2 marks):**
- NO → NO₂ via reaction with O₃ or O₂.
- NO₂ + hν → NO + O → O₃ (in presence of VOCs).
- NO₂ + H₂O → HNO₃ (acid rain).
- NO₂ contributes to **secondary inorganic aerosol** (nitrate PM₂.₅).
- Lifetime: a few days in troposphere.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Given:**
- C_ppb = 75 ppb = 75 × 10⁻³ ppm = 0.075 ppm
- T = 303.15 K, P = 1 atm
- M = 46 g/mol

**Step 1 — Apply master formula:**

$$C_{\mu g/m^{3}} = C_{ppm} \times \frac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$C_{\mu g/m^{3}} = 0.075 \times \frac{1 \times 46}{0.0821 \times 303.15}$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator:**

$$0.0821 \times 303.15 = 24.891\,L \cdot atm/mol$$

**Step 4 — Compute ratio:**

$$C_{\mu g/m^{3}} = 0.075 \times \frac{46}{24.891} = 0.075 \times 1.848 = 0.1386\,g/m^{3}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to µg/m³:**

$$\boxed{C_{NO_{2}} = 138.6\,\mu g/m^{3}}$$

**Step 6 — Comparison with standards:**

| Standard | Limit (24-hr) | Status of 138.6 µg/m³ |
|---|---|---|
| CPCB NAAQS | 80 µg/m³ | ❌ **Exceeds by 73%** |
| WHO 2021 | 25 µg/m³ (24-hr) | ❌ **Exceeds by 5.5×** |
| WHO 2021 Annual | 10 µg/m³ | ❌ **Exceeds by 13.8×** |

**Health Commentary (1 mark):**
At this concentration, vulnerable groups (asthmatics, children, elderly) face **acute respiratory distress**. Long-term exposure correlates with **impaired lung development in children** and **premature mortality** in adults. Immediate mitigation: **low-emission zone (LEZ) policy**, **BS-VI standards enforcement**, **traffic diversion**.

---

### CHOICE B (14 Marks)

#### Question B:
> **(a)** With a neat flow diagram, explain the **sources and effects of Particulate Matter (PM)** from automotive exhaust. Discuss the difference between **PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, and PM₁** with respect to their **deposition regions in the human respiratory system**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An expressway is proposed near a residential zone. A traffic survey estimates **20,000 vehicles/day** with an average fleet PM₂.₅ emission factor of **0.025 g/km**. The average trip length is **15 km**. Estimate the **total daily PM₂.₅ emission load in kg/day** and discuss the likely impact on the local community. **(7 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**Sources (2 marks):**
- **Tailpipe PM:** Especially diesel—composed of elemental carbon (soot), adsorbed hydrocarbons, sulfates, and metals.
- **Non-tailpipe PM:** Brake dust (Fe, Cu, Ba), tire wear (rubber, Zn), road dust resuspension.
- **Secondary PM:** Sulfates and nitrates formed in atmosphere.

**Particle Size vs Deposition (3 marks):**

| Size | Aerodynamic Diameter | Deposition Site | Health Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PM₁₀** (Thoracic) | ≤ 10 µm | **Upper airways** (nose, throat, trachea) | Cough, bronchitis |
| **PM₂.₅** (Respirable) | ≤ 2.5 µm | **Bronchioles and alveoli** | Alveolar inflammation, CVD |
| **PM₁** (Ultrafine) | ≤ 1 µm | **Crosses alveolar-capillary barrier** → systemic circulation | Organ damage, brain translocation |

**Effects on Humans (1 mark):**
Chronic exposure to PM₂.₅ reduces **life expectancy by ~0.98 years per 10 µg/m³** increase (Pope et al., Harvard Six Cities Study). Linked to **lung cancer** (IARC Group 1), **stroke**, **heart attack**, and **low birth weight**.

**Effects on Environment (1 mark):**
- **Haze** and reduced visibility
- **Acid rain precursor** (sulfate/nitrate)
- **Climate forcing** (direct via absorption/scattering; indirect via cloud condensation nuclei)
- **Soiling of buildings** and materials

*(Flow diagram for this answer: refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid block for HRR dose-response.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**Given:**
- Vehicles per day, N = 20,000
- Emission factor, EF = 0.025 g/km
- Average trip length, d = 15 km

**Step 1 — Total vehicle-km per day:**

$$VKT_{daily} = N \times d = 20{,}000 \times 15 = 300{,}000\,\text{vehicle-km/day}$$

**Step 2 — Daily PM₂.₅ emission load:**

$$E_{daily} = VKT_{daily} \times EF = 300{,}000 \times 0.025 = 7{,}500\,g/day$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kg/day:**

$$\boxed{E_{daily} = 7.5\,kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Impact Discussion (3 marks):**
- A daily release of **7.5 kg PM₂.₅** localized near a residential zone is **significant** when compared with CPCB airshed emission ceilings.
- **Dispersion Modeling Required:** Gaussian Plume (ISCST3 / AERMOD) prediction is needed for the downwind concentration field.
- **Likely impacts:**
  - Exceedance of **CPCB PM₂.₅ 24-hr NAAQS (60 µg/m³)** in the immediate **300–500 m buffer** of the expressway.
  - Increased **childhood asthma** incidence in nearby schools (within 200 m).
  - **Crop yield reduction** in adjacent agricultural patches due to particulate deposition on leaves.
- **Mitigation:**
  - Mandatory **BS-VI/Euro-VI equivalent fleet** on this corridor.
  - **Green belt** of 200 m width with species like *Ficus benghalensis* and *Morus alba*.
  - **Sound barriers** (also trap particulates).
  - **Land-use planning**—maintain ≥ 500 m setback for sensitive receptors (hospitals, schools).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up units:** Writing g/km as µg/km in final answers. Always double-check.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply N × d × EF** in the correct sequence for emission load problems.
> 3. **Citing the wrong standard:** Using WHO instead of CPCB (or vice versa) without clarification. Examiners in Kerala often prefer **CPCB NAAQS** for the Indian context.
> 4. **Skipping the interpretation step:** A numerical answer without a comparison and impact statement gets only **50–60%** marks for that sub-part.
> 5. **Confusing PM₁₀ with PM₂.₅ in respiratory deposition questions**—the depth of penetration is *fundamentally different* and examiners note this.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1**

- **Three pollutant classes:** Primary, Secondary, Greenhouse — know one example for each from automotive origin.
- **The "Big Four" tailpipe pollutants:** CO, HC, NOₓ, PM — the cornerstone of every KTU question.
- **Zeldovich mechanism:** Thermal NO formation, **temperature-dominant above 1800 K**, reduces with EGR/quench.
- **CO binds Hb at 240× the affinity of O₂** — the single most test-worthy toxicology fact.
- **PM₂.₅ penetration depth:** alveoli; **PM₁** translocates to bloodstream.
- **Master conversion formula:** $C_{\mu g/m^{3}} = C_{ppm} \times \frac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T}$ — **practice this calculation 5+ times**.
- **Standards trio (always cite the right one):** CPCB NAAQS, WHO 2021, Euro/BS-VI. Do not interchange.
- **AQI categories:** 0–50 Good → 401+ Severe. Practice linear interpolation.
- **Photochemical smog requires sunlight** — it disappears at night. The term *"London smog"* (sulfurous) ≠ *"Los Angeles smog"* (photochemical).
- **Lead phasing:** India eliminated leaded petrol in **2000**; WHO/UNEP export ban dates matter for one-mark questions.
- **Greenhouse gases from vehicles:** Primarily **CO₂** (complete combustion); **CH₄** and **N₂O** minor slip emissions.
- **CO₂ equivalence (GWP):** CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265 (100-yr horizon, IPCC AR5).
- **Vulnerable population matrix:** Children, elderly, pregnant women, asthmatics, outdoor workers — always mention in health-impact answers.
- **Common pathway diagram in answers:** Source → Atmosphere → Receptor → Health/Environmental Impact. Structure every 7-marker this way.
- **Final numerical-safety rule:** Always check units, always state the standard, always state one health implication. This alone can secure **2–3 extra marks** on a 7-mark sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emission Standards and Regulations - Overview of Global and Regional Emission Standards - Euro, Bharat, US, European Union Standards, Indian Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Emission Standards and Regulations - Overview of Global and Regional Emission Standards - Euro, Bharat, US, European Union Standards, Indian Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Automotive Emission Standard** is a legally enforceable set of quantitative limits, defined by a regulatory authority, that specifies the maximum permissible mass of regulated gaseous pollutants (CO, HC, NOx, PM) and the test procedures under which these limits are measured from a vehicle's exhaust and evaporative systems during standardized driving cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Emission standards form the regulatory backbone of automotive pollution control. They translate atmospheric science and health-based research into binding engineering design constraints for the automobile industry.

**Emission Regulation** is the broader legislative and administrative framework that encompasses:
- Pollutant identification (CO, HC, NOx, PM, CO₂, NH₃, PN)
- Test cycles (NEDC, WLTP, FTP-75, MVEG)
- Type-approval procedures (whole vehicle, family, engine)
- Conformity of production (CoP) and in-service conformity (ISC)
- On-Board Diagnostics (OBD) and durability requirements

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy: "The Speed Limit on a Highway"**
> Think of emission standards like speed limits on a road. The government decides how fast (how much pollutant) is safe for the city's air (the atmosphere). Cars (vehicles) are tested on a standard track (test cycle) to ensure they don't exceed that limit. Just as speed limits tighten over time (50 → 40 → 30 km/h) as cities get more crowded, emission standards get progressively stricter (Euro 1 → Euro 6 → Euro 7) as evidence of health impact grows. The "enforcement camera" is the **Type-Approval certificate** — without it, a vehicle cannot be legally sold.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Every Emission Regulation

| Pillar | Description |
|---|---|
| **Pollutant Limits** | Numerical cap on mass of pollutant per km (g/km) or per bhp-hr (g/bhp-hr) |
| **Test Cycle** | Standardized driving schedule (urban, extra-urban, highway) |
| **Type Approval** | Government certification that a vehicle model complies with the limits |

## 1.4 Why Standards Exist — The Driver

The three primary drivers for emission regulation are:
1. **Public Health** — WHO links ambient PM₂.₅ and NO₂ to ~7 million premature deaths annually
2. **Climate Change** — CO₂ is a greenhouse gas (GHG); transport contributes ~24% of global CO₂ emissions
3. **Environmental Protection** — Acid rain (SO₂, NOx), tropospheric ozone (HC + NOx photolysis), and smog

> [!TIP]
> **Key Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - **g/km** — used for light-duty vehicles (LDV), measured over a test cycle
> - **g/kWh** or **g/bhp-hr** — used for heavy-duty engines (EURO VI uses WHTC cycle)
> - **mg/km** — used for particulate number (PN) and PM2.5 in newer norms
> - **ppm** or **% vol** — used in crankcase and evaporative emission limits
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Standard: The Five-Layer Structure

Every emission standard (Euro, Bharat, US-EPA, China-6) has the same structural anatomy:

1. **Vehicle/Engine Class Definition** — LDV (M1, N1), HDD (M3, N3), 2W, 3W
2. **Test Fuel Specification** — Sulfur content, octane/cetane, density
3. **Driving/Engine Cycle** — NEDC, WLTP, FTP-75, WHTC, ETC
4. **Pollutant Limit Values** — CO, NOx, HC, NMOG, PM, PN, NH₃
5. **Implementation Date** — Year from which new type-approvals must comply

## 2.2 Pollutants Regulated Worldwide

| Pollutant | Symbol | Health/Environmental Impact | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon Monoxide | CO | Binds Hb; impairs O₂ transport | Incomplete combustion |
| Hydrocarbons | HC, NMOG, THC, NMHC | Ozone precursor (smog) | Unburnt fuel, fuel evaporation |
| Nitrogen Oxides | NOx (NO + NO₂) | Acid rain, PM₂.₅, ozone | High-T combustion |
| Particulate Matter | PM / PM₁₀ / PM₂.₅ | Lung disease, cancer | Diesel soot, brake dust |
| Particulate Number | PN | Ultra-fine lung penetration | Solid nanoparticles |
| Carbon Dioxide | CO₂ | Greenhouse gas (climate) | Complete combustion |
| Ammonia | NH₃ | PM₂.₅ precursor (secondary) | SCR (DEF) |
| Formaldehyde | HCHO | Toxic air pollutant | Cold-start HC oxidation |
| Methane | CH₄ | GHG (~25× CO₂) | CNG/LPG vehicles |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 8 equations — they are tested directly in KTU ESE Part A & Part B questions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{mass} = \dfrac{C \times \dot{V} \times t \times \rho}{d}$ | Mass emission from volumetric concentration over distance $d$ | g/km |
| 2 | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_{brake}}$ | Brake-Specific Fuel Consumption (relates CO₂ to fuel economy) | g/kWh |
| 3 | $CO_2 = m_{fuel} \times 3.169$ (petrol) | Stoichiometric carbon balance for gasoline | g CO₂/g fuel |
| 4 | $CO_2 = m_{fuel} \times 3.169$ (diesel ≈ 3.18) | Stoichiometric carbon balance for diesel | g CO₂/g fuel |
| 5 | $C_xH_y + \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \rightarrow x CO_2 + \dfrac{y}{2} H_2O$ | Complete combustion of hydrocarbon (stoichiometry) | — |
| 6 | $\eta_{cat} = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Catalytic converter conversion efficiency | % |
| 7 | $PN = \dfrac{N_{particles} \times 10^6}{V_{dilute}}$ | Particulate number concentration | #/km or #/kWh |
| 8 | $\dfrac{CO}{CO_2} = \dfrac{2(1 - \lambda)}{1 + \lambda}$ for HC fuel | Air-Fuel Ratio ($\lambda$) from CO emission ratio | — |

### 2.3.1 Explanation of the CO/CO₂/λ Relationship (Schmid's Formula)

This is the **most-tested equation** in the module. It relates the excess-air factor (λ) to the measured CO/CO₂ ratio in the exhaust of a gasoline engine.

$$\dfrac{[CO]}{[CO_2]} = \dfrac{2(1-\lambda)}{\lambda + \dfrac{m+1}{m}} \quad \text{(general)}$$

For a representative gasoline surrogate **C₈H₁₅** (m = 8/15 carbon atoms per H):

$$\dfrac{CO}{CO_2} = \dfrac{2(1-\lambda)}{\lambda + 1.067}$$

> **Why this matters for KTU:** If an examiner gives a measured CO and CO₂ volume %, you can solve for λ → equivalence ratio φ → check if combustion is rich/lean.

## 2.4 The Evolution Logic — Why Standards Keep Tightening

| Reason | Effect on Standard |
|---|---|
| New pollutant discovered (e.g., NH₃ from SCR, PN from GDI) | New limit added |
| Health studies lower the safe threshold | Existing limit reduced |
| Test cycle found to under-estimate real emissions (WLTP replaced NEDC) | New cycle adopted |
| New technology matures (GPF, SCR, LNT) | Standard tightens to force adoption |
| Geographic expansion (e.g., EU+India both adopt Euro 6/BS-VI) | Harmonization |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEM Design Driver:** Determines engine calibration, after-treatment sizing, OBD thresholds
- **Fuel Specification Driver:** Bharat-VI forced India to move from 350 ppm S → 10 ppm S diesel
- **Legal Compliance:** Without type-approval, vehicle cannot be sold — a regulatory "license to operate"
- **Trade Policy:** Different standards = non-tariff trade barriers (e.g., India delayed BS-VI by 2 years)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio from CO/CO₂ Measurement

This is a classic KTU 14-mark question. We derive the relation between the **air-fuel equivalence ratio** (φ) and the exhaust **CO/CO₂** volume ratio.

### Step 1: Define the Combustion Reaction

For a generic fuel $C_xH_yO_z$ burning in air ($\text{O}_2 + 3.76 \text{ N}_2$), with excess air factor $\lambda$:

$$C_xH_yO_z + \lambda \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{z}{2}\right) \left(O_2 + 3.76 N_2\right) \rightarrow a\,CO_2 + b\,CO + c\,H_2O + d\,O_2 + \lambda \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{z}{2}\right) 3.76 N_2$$

### Step 2: Apply Atom Balance

- **C balance:** $a + b = x$
- **H balance:** $2c = y \Rightarrow c = y/2$
- **O balance:** $2a + b + c + 2d = z + 2\lambda\left(x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{z}{2}\right)$

### Step 3: Solve the System

Combining C and O balances, and assuming no free H₂ in the products:

$$a = \dfrac{x\lambda - x + 2\lambda z - 2z + \lambda y - y/2 + z}{...}$$

After algebraic simplification (the "heavy" part — examiners give full credit if the setup is correct):

$$\dfrac{[CO]}{[CO_2]} = \dfrac{b}{a} = \dfrac{2x(1-\lambda) + (\lambda - 1)(z - y/2) + \lambda z - z}{\lambda(x + y/4 - z/2) + (z/2)}$$

For a clean hydrocarbon fuel ($z = 0$), this reduces to:

$$\dfrac{[CO]}{[CO_2]} = \dfrac{2x(1-\lambda)}{x\lambda + y/4}$$

### Step 4: KTU-Ready Final Form (D'Alleva's simplification)

$$\boxed{\dfrac{CO}{CO_2} = \dfrac{2(1-\lambda)}{\lambda + \dfrac{y}{4x}}}$$

For a **C₈H₁₅** surrogate (representative gasoline), $y/(4x) = 15/32 = 0.469$:

$$\dfrac{CO}{CO_2} = \dfrac{2(1-\lambda)}{\lambda + 0.469}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Board Style

> **[KTU Sample - 14 marks pattern]**
> A 4-cylinder gasoline engine operates at a part-load condition. The dry exhaust gas analysis gives:
> - CO = 0.50 % vol
> - CO₂ = 14.5 % vol
> - O₂ = 2.10 % vol
> - HC, NO, etc. negligible
>
> **Calculate the relative air-fuel ratio (λ) and the air-fuel ratio (A/F) by mass.**
> Take fuel: C₈H₁₈ (octane), $\rho_{air} = 1.293$ kg/m³, $\rho_{fuel} = 745$ kg/m³. Stoichiometric A/F for C₈H₁₈ = 15.13:1.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Apply the derived formula

$$\dfrac{CO}{CO_2} = \dfrac{0.50}{14.5} = 0.0345$$

$$0.0345 = \dfrac{2(1-\lambda)}{\lambda + 0.469}$$

**Step 2:** Cross-multiply

$$0.0345(\lambda + 0.469) = 2(1-\lambda)$$

$$0.0345\lambda + 0.01618 = 2 - 2\lambda$$

$$2.0345\lambda = 1.98382$$

$$\boxed{\lambda = 0.975}$$

**Step 3:** Since $\lambda < 1$, the mixture is **rich** (slightly fuel-rich — consistent with measurable CO).

**Step 4:** Calculate actual A/F

$$\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{actual} = \lambda \times \left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = 0.975 \times 15.13 = 14.75:1$$

**Step 5:** Interpret — at $\lambda = 0.975$, the engine is operating with **2.5% deficiency of air**. The three-way catalyst (TWC) can still handle this; below λ ≈ 0.95, CO emissions spike and conversion efficiency drops.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Global Standards

| Standard Region | Light-Duty Cars | Heavy-Duty Engines | Test Cycle (LDV) | Lead-Free Phase-out | Stringency vs Euro 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **European Union (Euro 1→7)** | Euro 1 (1992) to Euro 7 (2025) | Euro I (1992) to Euro VI (2013) | NEDC → WLTP (2017) | 2000 (complete) | Baseline (1.0×) |
| **India (Bharat Stage)** | BS-I (2000) to BS-VI (2020) | BS-I to BS-VI (2019 for HDV) | Modified NEDC → WLTP equivalent | 2000 | Euro 4 (BS-IV) → Euro 6 (BS-VI) |
| **United States (EPA + CARB)** | Tier 1 (1994) → Tier 2 (2004) → Tier 3 (2017) | EPA 1998 → 2007 → 2010 GHG Phase 1/2 | FTP-75, HWFET, US06, SC03 | 1996 | ≈ Euro 6 (often stricter) |
| **China** | China-I (2000) → China-VI a/b (2020) | China I → China VI | NEDC → WLTP | 2000 | Euro 6 equivalent |
| **Japan** | 1973 (early), JP 2009 | — | JC08 (replaced by WLTP) | — | Comparable to Euro 4/5 |
| **Brazil (PROCONVE)** | L1 (1988) → L7 (2022) | P7 (2012) | NEDC-derived | — | Euro 5/6 equivalent |

## 3.4 Mathematical Formulation — Emission Factor Approach

For a fleet of $N$ vehicles, the total emission is:

$$E_{fleet} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} E_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (EF_i \times VKT_i \times P_i \times 10^{-6})$$

where:
- $EF_i$ = emission factor of vehicle class $i$ (g/km)
- $VKT_i$ = vehicle-km travelled per year
- $P_i$ = number of vehicles in class $i$

This is the **bottom-up emission inventory equation** used by India's ARAI-IVP lab to demonstrate compliance to CPCB.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Global Emission Standards Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT["GLOBAL AUTOMOTIVE EMISSION STANDARDS"]:::root
    EU["EUROPEAN UNION"]:::region
    US["UNITED STATES"]:::region
    IN["INDIA BHARAT STAGE"]:::region
    CN["CHINA"]:::region
    JP["JAPAN"]:::region
    BR["BRAZIL PROCONVE"]:::region
    RUS["RUSSIA"]:::region

    EUX["EURO 1 to EURO 7"]:::eu
    EUHD["EURO I to EURO VI HDV"]:::eu
    EUT["TEST CYCLES: NEDC, WLTP, WHTC, ETC"]:::eu

    EPAT["EPA TIER 1 to TIER 3"]:::us
    CARB["CARB LEV I to LEV III"]:::us
```
