$$\delta_q \approx \frac{\lambda_g}{C_{p,g} \cdot \rho_u \cdot S_L}$$

Where:
- $\lambda_g$ = thermal conductivity of unburned gas (W/m·K)
- $C_{p,g}$ = specific heat at constant pressure (J/kg·K)
- $\rho_u$ = density of unburned gas (kg/m³)
- $S_L$ = laminar burning velocity (m/s)

### 2.1.3 Source #3 — Oil Layer Absorption–Desorption (~10–15%)

Liquid fuel **dissolves into the oil film** on the cylinder wall during compression, then **desorbs back into the cylinder** after the flame passes, when bulk gas temperatures are higher and partial-pressure conditions favor re-volatilization.

### 2.1.4 Source #4 — Fuel Puddles and Deposits (~15–25%)

Liquid fuel impinges on the **intake port walls, intake valve backs, and piston crown**, forming a puddle. This puddle releases HC mass into the charge with a phase lag.

### 2.1.5 Secondary Sources

- **Late burning / after-flame** in the exhaust port.
- **Valve leakage** past worn valve guides.
- **Exhaust system oxidation** of HC (negative source — *destroys* HC, especially in CI engines with hot EGR).

## 2.2 Unburned Hydrocarbons (HC) in CI Engines

In CI engines, HC arises primarily from **mixture preparation inhomogeneity**. The spray mixing process produces three distinct zones:

| Zone | Description | Effect on HC |
|---|---|---|
| **Over-mixed (lean)** region | Too much air, fuel below lean flammability limit | Fuel evaporates but **fails to ignite** → raw HC |
| **Under-mixed (rich)** region | Too much fuel, insufficient air | Forms **soot + unburned HC** |
| **Wall-impingement region** | Spray strikes cold liner/piston | Quench layer of unburned fuel on wall |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** CI engines have *lower* HC emissions than SI engines because of the **high HC oxidation rate in the hot, fuel-rich products** and the **post-flame oxidation in the expansion stroke** (700–900 K after-flame). SI engines cannot match this because flame temperature is higher but **residence time is shorter**.

## 2.3 Carbon Monoxide (CO) — Formation Mechanism

CO is the product of **incomplete oxidation** of carbon in the fuel. Its formation is governed by two competing processes:

1. **Generation** during rich combustion via the partial oxidation: $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + \tfrac{n}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow n\text{CO} + (n+1)\text{H}_2$$
2. **Oxidation to CO₂** in the post-flame zone (reverse water-gas shift, RWGS): $$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$$

The CO oxidation is **kinetically frozen** (quenched) when the local temperature falls below ~**1000 K** in the expansion stroke. Hence:

- **Equilibrium CO** (calculated from equilibrium constant at flame T) > **Actual CO** (because reaction does not have time to complete).
- CO emissions **rise sharply with equivalence ratio φ > 1** (rich).
- For **φ < 1** (lean), CO is very low because excess O₂ drives CO → CO₂.
- For **SI engines running lean (φ ≈ 0.95–1.0)** at high load, CO is small (~**0.1–0.5%** by volume).
- For **CI engines** (globally very lean, φ ≈ 0.3–0.7), CO is generally very low (~**100–500 ppm**) except during **cold-start and transient** operation.

## 2.4 NOx — The Three Formation Routes

NOx is overwhelmingly **NO (≈ 90–95%)**, with the remainder being NO₂. There are three routes:

### 2.4.1 Thermal NOx (Zeldovich Mechanism) — **DOMINANT**

Air contains **78% N₂**. At high temperatures, the triple bond of N₂ (bond energy ≈ **941 kJ/mol**) breaks via collisions with O, OH, and H radicals.

The **extended Zeldovich mechanism** consists of three reactions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{N}_2 + \text{O} &\rightleftharpoons \text{NO} + \text{N} \quad &(k_1) \\
\text{N} + \text{O}_2 &\rightleftharpoons \text{NO} + \text{O} \quad &(k_2) \\
\text{N} + \text{OH} &\rightleftharpoons \text{NO} + \text{H} \quad &(k_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Reaction (1) is the **rate-limiting step** because of the strong N≡N triple bond. Reaction (3) is the *extended* Zeldovich path; it becomes important at higher pressures and in fuel-rich conditions.

The overall rate exhibits a very strong **Arrhenius temperature dependence**:

$$r_{\text{NO}} \approx k_1 \cdot [\text{N}_2] \cdot [\text{O}] \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T}\right)$$

With an apparent activation energy $E_a \approx 320$ kJ/mol. This is why **a 100 K rise in peak temperature roughly DOUBLES NOx**.

### 2.4.2 Prompt NOx (Fenimore Mechanism)

In the flame front, hydrocarbon radicals (especially **CH**) react with N₂ to form HCN/NH, which are subsequently oxidized to NO. Dominant in **fuel-rich flames** but only ~**5% of total NOx** in engines.

$$\text{CH} + \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{HCN} + \text{N}$$
$$\text{HCN} \rightarrow \text{NH} \rightarrow \text{N} \rightarrow \text{NO}$$

### 2.4.3 Fuel NOx

Nitrogen bound in the fuel molecule (amines in coal, heavy fuel oils) oxidizes to NO. For petroleum fuels, nitrogen content is < **0.1%**, so fuel NOx is **negligible in road-vehicle engines**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Quench distance | $\delta_q = \dfrac{\lambda_g}{C_{p,g} \rho_u S_L}$ | m | Wall-quench thickness |
| 2 | Crevice HC mass | $m_{HC,cr} = \rho_f \cdot V_{cr} \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_{end}}{P_{ref}}\right)^{1/n}$ | kg | Piston crevice source |
| 3 | NOx (Zeldovich) rate | $r_{NO} = A \exp(-E_a/RT) [\text{N}_2][\text{O}]$ | mol/m³·s | $E_a \approx 320$ kJ/mol |
| 4 | Water–gas shift | $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$ | — | Kinetically frozen < 1000 K |
| 5 | Equivalence ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | — | φ > 1 rich; φ < 1 lean |
| 6 | Air–fuel ratio | $A/F = \dfrac{A/F_{stoich}}{\phi}$ | kg/kg | For gasoline, A/F_stoich ≈ 14.7 |
| 7 | Equilibrium constant | $K_p = \dfrac{P_{CO_2} P_{H_2}}{P_{CO} P_{H_2O}} = \exp(-\Delta G/RT)$ | — | Favors CO₂ at low T, CO at high T |
| 8 | N₂ dissociation onset | $T > 1600$ K (significant) | K | Threshold for thermal NOx |

> [!WARNING]
> **No vertical bars (`|`) in tables!** Use `\vert` or `\mid` when writing inline math in tables, as per the protocol.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation)** lowers the flame temperature by diluting the charge — it attacks **thermal NOx** (the dominant route) by reducing both peak $T$ and $[\text{O}_2]$.
- **Lean-NOx traps (LNT)** and **SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction)** are *after-treatment*; they do not change *formation* but change *emission*.
- **Wall-guided / Spray-guided GDI** and **tumble / swirl control** reduce HC by suppressing wall quenching and crevice growth.
- Understanding CO formation is critical to designing **cold-start catalyst light-off strategies** — when the catalyst is below 250 °C, almost all engine-out CO passes through.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — The Zeldovich (Thermal) NOx Rate Equation

We derive the simplified form commonly used in engine textbooks (Heywood, Turns).

**Starting point:** the rate-limiting step

$$\text{N}_2 + \text{O} \xrightarrow{k_1} \text{NO} + \text{N}, \quad k_1 = 7.6 \times 10^{13} \exp\!\left(-\frac{38370}{T}\right) \;\text{cm}^3/(\text{mol·s})$$

The rate of formation of N atoms is:

$$r_N = k_1 [\text{N}_2][\text{O}] - k_{-1}[\text{NO}][\text{N}]$$

Assuming **steady state for N atoms** (since N is highly reactive and short-lived):

$$\frac{d[\text{N}]}{dt} = 0 = k_1[\text{N}_2][\text{O}] - k_2[\text{N}][\text{O}_2] - k_3[\text{N}][\text{OH}]$$

Solving for [N]:

$$[\text{N}]_{ss} = \frac{k_1[\text{N}_2][\text{O}]}{k_2[\text{O}_2] + k_3[\text{OH}]}$$

The total rate of NO formation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{NO} &= k_1[\text{N}_2][\text{O}] - k_{-1}[\text{NO}][\text{N}] + k_2[\text{N}][\text{O}_2] + k_3[\text{N}][\text{OH}] \\
r_{NO} &= 2 k_1[\text{N}_2][\text{O}] \quad \text{(when NO is small, the back-reaction terms are negligible)}
\end{aligned}
$$

A more accurate *engineering* form (Lavoie, Heywood 1970) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} &= \frac{6 \times 10^{13}}{T^{0.5}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{69090}{T}\right) \cdot [\text{N}_2][\text{O}_2]^{0.5} \\
&\quad + 6.4 \times 10^{9} \, T^{0.5} \exp\!\left(-\frac{31600}{T}\right) \cdot [\text{O}_2][\text{NO}] 
\end{aligned}
$$

The first term is **forward** (NO formation); the second is **reverse** (NO decomposition). At high T, the forward term dominates; in the expansion stroke, the reverse can dominate.

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — CO Equilibrium in Combustion Products

The water–gas shift reaction at the end of combustion is:

$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$$

Equilibrium constant (a function of T only, ideal gas):

$$K_p(T) = \frac{P_{CO_2} P_{H_2}}{P_{CO} P_{H_2O}} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta G^0(T)}{RT}\right)$$

For a stoichiometric CH₄-air flame, assume:
- $\xi$ = moles of CO at equilibrium
- Initial moles (per mole of CH₄ burned at φ = 1): CO = 0, H₂O = 2, CO₂ = 1, H₂ = 0

Mass balance gives:

$$[\text{CO}] = \xi, \quad [\text{H}_2\text{O}] = 2-\xi, \quad [\text{CO}_2] = 1+\xi, \quad [\text{H}_2] = \xi$$

Substituting (with total moles $n_t = 3$):

$$K_p = \frac{(1+\xi)(\xi)}{(2-\xi)(\xi)} = \frac{1+\xi}{2-\xi}$$

Solving for $\xi$:

$$\xi_{CO} = \frac{2K_p - 1}{1 + K_p}$$

At $T = 2000$ K, $K_p \approx 0.232$ → $\xi \approx -0.42$ — but $\xi$ cannot be negative, indicating equilibrium predicts *no CO* at this temperature and stoichiometric conditions.

At $T = 1500$ K (typical expansion stroke), $K_p \approx 3.6$ → $\xi \approx 0.86$, i.e., **~86% of carbon is still CO at equilibrium**! This shows why **kinetic freezing** at ~1000 K is what allows significant CO to remain in the exhaust.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** This derivation explains why CO emissions drop drastically when engines are run lean (excess O₂ shifts the equilibrium to the right by Le Chatelier) and why thermal oxidation in the exhaust manifold is essential before the catalyst.

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — HC Mass from Crevice Volume (Simplified)

Consider the piston top-land crevice. At the start of compression, the crevice is filled with the unburned charge at intake conditions. By the time the flame has passed, the in-cylinder pressure has risen to $P_3$ (just after combustion). The crevice gas is at the *peak* pressure because it was compressed during the combustion event.

Mass of HC in the crevice:

$$m_{HC,cr} = \rho_u \cdot V_{cr} \cdot \left(\frac{P_3}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma} \cdot (1 - X_{b,cr})$$

Where $X_{b,cr}$ is the burned fraction in the crevice (≈ 0 because of wall quenching).

Simplified form for an isothermal-ish process in the crevice (n = 1.35, polytropic):

$$m_{HC,cr} \approx \rho_u \cdot V_{cr} \cdot \left(\frac{P_3}{P_1}\right)^{0.74}$$

**Worked Example (typical SI engine):**
- $V_{cr} = 1.2\%$ of clearance volume = $0.012 \times 50\;\text{cm}^3 = 0.6\;\text{cm}^3$
- $P_1 = 1\;\text{bar}$, $P_3 = 30\;\text{bar}$
- $\rho_u$ at intake = **1.2 kg/m³** (stoich gasoline-air)

$$m_{HC,cr} = 1.2 \times 0.6 \times 10^{-6} \times (30)^{0.74} = 1.1 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{kg/cycle}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Variables

> [!TIP]
> **This matrix is a KTU 2024 "must-memorize"** for 14-mark questions. Examiners frequently ask: *"Discuss the effect of air-fuel ratio, load, speed, and spark timing on HC, CO, and NOx."*

| Variable | HC | CO | NOx |
|---|---|---|---|
| **φ ↑ (richer)** | ↑ (wall-quench + crevice) | ↑↑ (rich quench) | ↓ (less O₂, lower T) |
| **φ ↓ (leaner)** | ↓ slightly (less fuel in crevice) | ↓↓ (excess O₂) | ↑ then ↓ (T max at φ ≈ 0.9) |
| **Load ↑ (high BMEP)** | ≈ or slightly ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ (T ∝ load) |
| **Speed ↑** | ↓ (less time for re-emission) | ↓ | ≈ (T similar) |
| **Spark advance ↑** | ↓ (more time for oxidation) | ↓ | ↑ (longer high-T residence) |
| **EGR ↑** | ↑ (quench, slower burn) | ↑ (lower T) | ↓↓ (intended) |
| **Compression ratio ↑** | ↑ slightly (more crevice) | ↓ (higher T) | ↑ (higher T) |
| **Intake T ↑** | ↑ (more crevice growth) | ↑ | ↑ |

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Typical Engine-Out Emissions

For a 2.0 L, stoichiometric, PFI, gasoline SI engine at WOT, 2000 rpm:

| Pollutant | Engine-out (g/km equivalent) | After TWC (g/km) | Euro 6 Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| CO | 15–25 | 0.5–1.0 | 1.0 |
| HC | 1.5–2.5 | 0.05–0.1 | 0.1 |
| NOx | 1.0–2.0 | 0.05–0.1 | 0.06 |

This shows the **Three-Way Catalyst (TWC)** reduces all three by ~95% when at light-off, but cold-start HC and CO bypass the cold catalyst.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HC Formation Sources in SI Engine — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SI Engine Unburned HC Sources] --> B[Crevice Volumes - ~40 percent]
    A --> C[Wall Quenching - ~15 percent]
    A --> D[Oil Layer Absorption Desorption - ~12 percent]
    A --> E[Fuel Puddles and Deposits - ~20 percent]
    A --> F[Valve Leakage and Late Burn - ~10 percent]
    A --> G[Exhaust System Re-emission - ~3 percent]

    B --> B1[Piston top land crevice]
    B --> B2[Head gasket crevice]
    B --> B3[Valve head crevice]

    C --> C1[Quench distance delta q]
    C --> C2[Flame kernel cools at wall]

    D --> D1[Fuel dissolves during compression]
    D --> D2[Re-volatilizes after flame]

    E --> E1[Intake port wall impingement]
    E --> E2[Intake valve back deposits]
    E --> E3[Piston crown accumulation]

    F --> F1[After flame in exhaust port]
    F --> F2[Worn valve guide leakage]

    G --> G1[Negative source in CI - hot oxidation]
```

## 4.2 NOx Formation Pathways — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[In-cylinder conditions] --> B[High peak temperature greater than 1800 K]
    A --> C[High local O2 from lean region]
    A --> D[Long residence time at high T]

    B --> E[Thermal NOx Zeldovich - DOMINANT]
    C --> E
    D --> E

    A --> F[Flame front rich CH radicals]
    F --> G[Prompt NOx Fenimore - minor]

    A --> H[Fuel bound nitrogen]
    H --> I[Fuel NOx - negligible in gasoline]

    E --> J[Engine out NOx]
    G --> J
    I --> J

    J --> K[EGR - reduces T]
    J --> L[SCR and LNT - after treatment]
    J --> M[Retarded timing - reduces T]
```

## 4.3 CO Formation and Oxidation — Quench Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hydrocarbon fuel] --> B[Combustion in cylinder]
    B --> C{Are local conditions rich phi greater than 1?}
    C -- YES --> D[Partial oxidation to CO]
    C -- NO --> E[Complete oxidation to CO2]
    D --> F[Post flame zone - high T and O2]
    F --> G{CO plus H2O gives CO2 plus H2}
    G --> H{Temperature above 1000 K?}
    H -- YES --> I[Further oxidation of CO to CO2]
    H -- NO --> J[Kinetic freeze - CO persists]
    I --> K[Engine out CO]
    J --> K
    E --> K
```

## 4.4 SI vs CI Engine Pollutant Dominance — Comparative Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Engine type] --> B[SI Engine - kinetically controlled]
    A --> C[CI Engine - mixing controlled]

    B --> B1[Homogeneous premixed flame]
    B --> B2[Residence time short]
    B --> B3[Wall quench severe]
    B --> B4[Equivalence ratio near 1]

    C --> C1[Diffusion flame with spray]
    C --> B2
    C --> C2[Locally rich and lean zones]
    C --> C3[Long high T post flame]

    B1 --> D1[HC dominant]
    B2 --> D1
    B3 --> D1
    B4 --> D2[CO moderate - rich operation]

    C1 --> E1[NOx dominant - high peak T]
    C2 --> E1
    C3 --> E2[Particulates and soot]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**"What is crevice volume and how does it contribute to unburned HC emissions in an SI engine?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A crevice volume is any small, narrow cavity adjacent to the combustion chamber — such as the piston top-land crevice above the top compression ring, the head-gasket crevice, or the valve-head crevice — with a very high surface-to-volume ratio. During compression and combustion, the unburned charge trapped in this crevice is **wall-quenched and fails to burn**. As in-cylinder pressure falls after combustion, this crevice gas **expands back into the cylinder** carrying unburned hydrocarbons into the exhaust. The crevice effect accounts for approximately **38–50% of total HC emissions** in port-fuel-injected SI engines.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**"Why are NOx emissions higher in CI engines than in SI engines, despite CI engines operating globally lean?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Although a CI engine runs globally very lean (φ ≈ 0.3–0.7), the **locally rich, fuel-rich zones inside the spray** release more chemical energy per unit mass of gas, producing **very high local peak temperatures (>2200 K)**. Thermal NOx follows an Arrhenius dependence $\exp(-E_a/RT)$ with $E_a \approx 320$ kJ/mol, so the small fraction of gas that reaches such extreme temperatures generates **a disproportionately large amount of NOx**. In contrast, SI engines with a homogeneous, near-stoichi mixture have a lower peak temperature (~2300 K but uniform) and a much shorter high-temperature residence time, yielding **less thermal NOx** (but more HC and CO).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **mechanism of formation of unburned hydrocarbons (HC) in a 4-stroke SI engine**. Discuss the relative contribution of each source and the effect of air–fuel ratio, spark timing, and EGR on HC emissions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **simplified Zeldovich mechanism for thermal NOx formation**. Discuss how peak cylinder temperature, equivalence ratio, and EGR affect the NOx formation rate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**HC Sources in SI Engine (sketch described, 7 marks):**

The four primary HC sources in a PFI SI engine are:

1. **Crevice Volumes (~40%):** The piston top-land crevice above the top compression ring, head-gasket, and valve-head crevices. During combustion, peak in-cylinder pressure forces unburned charge into the crevice, where it is wall-quenched. As pressure falls, this gas is **expelled back into the bulk cylinder** as raw HC. Contribution ≈ 38–50%.

2. **Wall Quenching (~15%):** When a flame front approaches a cold wall, heat loss to the wall exceeds heat release from reaction, and the flame quenches within a critical **quench distance** $\delta_q = \lambda_g / (C_{p,g} \rho_u S_L)$. The unburned thin layer remains as HC.

3. **Oil Layer Absorption–Desorption (~12%):** Liquid fuel dissolves in the oil film on the cylinder wall during compression and desorbs back into the charge after the flame passes.

4. **Fuel Puddles and Deposits (~20%):** Liquid fuel impinging on the intake port walls, intake valve backs, and piston crown forms puddles that slowly release HC into the gas phase.

**Effect of Operating Variables:**

| Variable | Effect on HC |
|---|---|
| **AFR (φ ↑, richer)** | HC ↑ (more crevice growth, more fuel-wall interaction) |
| **Spark advance ↑** | HC ↓ (more time for post-flame HC oxidation in the expansion stroke) |
| **EGR ↑** | HC ↑ (slower flame, lower T, more quench) |
| **Coolant T ↓** | HC ↑ (more wall quenching) |
| **Engine speed ↑** | HC ↓ (less time for crevice expulsion and re-emission) |

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Derivation of Zeldovich Mechanism (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Identify the three reactions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{N}_2 + \text{O} &\rightleftharpoons \text{NO} + \text{N} \quad &k_1 = 1.8 \times 10^{8} \, T \exp(-38370/T) \\
\text{N} + \text{O}_2 &\rightleftharpoons \text{NO} + \text{O} \quad &k_2 = 1.8 \times 10^{4} \, T \exp(-4680/T) \\
\text{N} + \text{OH} &\rightleftharpoons \text{NO} + \text{H} \quad &k_3 = 7.1 \times 10^{7} \, T \exp(-450/T)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply steady-state for the N atom (intermediate).**

$$0 = \frac{d[\text{N}]}{dt} = k_1[\text{N}_2][\text{O}] - k_2[\text{N}][\text{O}_2] - k_3[\text{N}][\text{OH}]$$

$$\Rightarrow [\text{N}]_{ss} = \frac{k_1[\text{N}_2][\text{O}]}{k_2[\text{O}_2] + k_3[\text{OH}]}$$

**Step 3: Total rate of NO formation.**

$$r_{NO} = k_1[\text{N}_2][\text{O}] - k_{-1}[\text{NO}][\text{N}] + k_2[\text{N}][\text{O}_2] + k_3[\text{N}][\text{OH}]$$

Substituting [N]ss and simplifying (with NO in its infancy, the back reaction is negligible):

$$r_{NO} \approx 2 k_1[\text{N}_2][\text{O}] = 2 k_1 [\text{N}_2] \cdot \left(\frac{k_{O_2,eq}[\text{O}_2]}{k_{O,eq}}\right)^{0.5}$$

**Step 4: Final Arrhenius form.**

$$r_{NO} \propto \sqrt{T} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) \quad \text{with} \quad E_a \approx 320 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

**Effect of variables:**
- **Peak T ↑**: $r_{NO}$ increases **doubled per 100 K rise** (extreme sensitivity).
- **φ ↑ (richer)**: Less O₂ and lower peak T → **NOx ↓** (but CO and HC ↑).
- **EGR ↑**: Dilutes the charge, lowers peak T → **NOx ↓** (the principal reason for EGR in SI).

---

### Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of CO formation in SI and CI engines**. Using a water-gas shift equilibrium analysis, show that CO emissions depend strongly on the local equivalence ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **formation of unburned HC in SI and CI engines**. Why is HC significantly lower in CI engines despite their mixing-controlled combustion? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Mechanism of CO Formation (7 marks):**

CO is formed as the **intermediate product of incomplete carbon oxidation**. The governing chemistry is the **water-gas shift reaction**:

$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad K_p(T) = \frac{P_{CO_2} P_{H_2}}{P_{CO} P_{H_2O}}$$

**Step 1: Initial combustion of a hydrocarbon produces a CO-rich product in the reaction zone.**

For CH₄ at φ = 1: $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (full), but in practice the **dissociation equilibrium** holds:

$$K_{CO,eq} = \frac{P_{CO} P_{H_2O}}{P_{CO_2} P_{H_2}}$$

The fractional dissociation to CO is:

$$x_{CO} = \frac{1 - K_p}{1 + K_p} \quad \text{(at stoichiometric fuel-air)}$$

**Step 2: At high temperature (T > 2000 K), $K_p$ is large** → equilibrium favors **CO formation** (Le Chatelier — dissociation of CO₂ is endothermic).

**Step 3: At low temperature (T < 1000 K)**, the CO → CO₂ reaction **kinetically freezes**. The **expansion stroke cools the gas below 1000 K in a few milliseconds**, insufficient time for the slow CO oxidation.

**Step 4: Local equivalence ratio effect.**
- **φ > 1 (rich)**: Insufficient O₂ to convert all CO to CO₂ → CO ↑↑.
- **φ < 1 (lean)**: Excess O₂ shifts equilibrium right → CO ↓.
- **SI engine at part load, φ ≈ 0.95–1.0**: CO ≈ 0.1–0.5 vol%.
- **CI engine at φ ≈ 0.4 (very lean)**: CO < 200 ppm except during cold-start.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Comparison of HC in SI vs CI (7 marks):**

| Aspect | SI Engine | CI Engine |
|---|---|---|
| Charge prep | Pre-mixed | Mixing-controlled (diffusion flame) |
| HC sources | Crevices, wall-quench, oil layer, fuel puddles | Over-mixed, under-mixed, wall-impingement zones |
| Dominant source | Crevices + wall-quench (~55–65%) | Spray zones (~80%) |
| Post-flame HC oxidation | Limited (short residence, low T in expansion) | Strong (high T, long residence, fuel-rich products) |
| Engine-out HC (typical) | 1.5–2.5 g/km | 0.1–0.4 g/km |

**Why CI engines have lower HC:**

1. **Long high-T residence time:** CI combustion is slow and expansion-stroke gases remain at **700–1100 K for several milliseconds**, allowing even fuel that did not burn in the flame to **oxidize in the post-flame**.
2. **Hot fuel-rich products:** Unlike the lean exhaust of SI engines, CI products contain abundant O atoms and OH radicals from incomplete combustion, accelerating HC oxidation.
3. **Hot walls:** The walls of a CI engine are hotter due to high load, further accelerating HC oxidation.
4. **Lack of crevice gas expulsion:** The crevice is filled with *air* in CI (no fuel in the crevice) — the main crevice HC source is *absent*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Confusing "globally lean" with "low NOx"** in CI engines — local rich zones are the actual NOx source.
> - **Forgetting the rate-limiting step** in the Zeldovich derivation — reaction (1), not all three, is rate-limiting; the back-reaction term is not zero in the expansion stroke.
> - **Mixing up Zeldovich (thermal) NOx with Fenimore (prompt) NOx** — they are mechanistically distinct, and KTU specifically asks about Zeldovich in Module 2.
> - **Writing HC sources without ranking them** — always rank crevices first, wall-quench second.
> - **Forgetting the kinetic-freeze at ~1000 K** when explaining why CO remains in SI exhaust.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 / Pollutant Formation**

- **HC in SI = 4 sources:** Crevices (40%), Wall-quench (15%), Oil-layer (12%), Fuel puddles (20%). Rank them in this order.
- **Quench distance formula:** $\delta_q = \lambda_g / (C_{p,g} \rho_u S_L)$.
- **HC in CI:** Over-mixed, under-mixed, spray-impingement; **oxidation in the hot expansion stroke** is what keeps CI HC low.
- **CO formation:** Incomplete oxidation; governed by **water-gas shift** $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$; **kinetically frozen at T < 1000 K**.
- **CO dependence on φ:** Sharp rise for φ > 1; very low for φ < 0.9.
- **NOx — three routes:** Thermal (Zeldovich, dominant), Prompt (Fenimore, 5%), Fuel (negligible in gasoline).
- **Zeldovich reactions (memorize all 3):** $\text{N}_2 + \text{O} \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N}$; $\text{N} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}$; $\text{N} + \text{OH} \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{H}$.
- **Zeldovich rate-limiting step:** $\text{N}_2 + \text{O}$ (strongest bond, highest $E_a$).
- **NOx Arrhenius:** $E_a \approx 320$ kJ/mol → **doubled per 100 K**.
- **Effect of EGR:** NOx ↓↓ (intended), HC ↑, CO ↑, BSFC ↑ slightly.
- **Effect of load:** NOx ↑ (T rises), CO ↑ (slight), HC ≈.
- **Effect of spark advance:** NOx ↑ (longer high-T residence), HC ↓ (more oxidation time).
- **Equivalence ratio extremes:** φ ≈ 0.9 gives **maximum NOx**; φ > 1 gives **maximum CO**.
- **CI engine peculiarities:** Global lean but local rich → high NOx; less crevice HC source; high-T post-flame oxidizes HC.
- **SI engine peculiarities:** Homogeneous mixture → less NOx; many HC sources → high HC; stoichiometric → moderate CO.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oxidation of Soot, Role of Soot Inhibitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Oxidation of Soot, Role of Soot Inhibitors ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition **Soot Oxidation** is the heterogeneous, thermochemical process by which solid carbonaceous particulate matter (soot), generated during the incomplete combustion of hydrocarbon fuels in compression-ignition (diesel) engines, is chemically converted into gaseous products (primarily carbon dioxide and carbon monoxide) through reaction with oxidizing species such as molecular oxygen (O₂), nitrogen dioxide (NO₂), and water vapor (H₂O). In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT522 – Automotive Pollution and Control)**, soot oxidation is treated as the central kinetic and thermodynamic phenomenon governing the regeneration of the **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)**, a mandatory after-treatment device under **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** and **Euro VI** emission norms. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMUT522 - Module 2):** > Soot oxidation chemistry forms the theoretical backbone for understanding **DPF regeneration strategies** (active, passive, and forced). Every after-treatment calculation in this module is anchored to the Arrhenius dependence of soot burn-off rate on catalyst loading, oxygen partial pressure, and peak in-cylinder/exhaust temperature. ### Soot – A Quick Recap Soot is a **black, porous, fractal aggregate of nano-spherules** (~20–50 nm primary particle diameter), composed of ~85–95% elemental carbon, with surface-adsorbed **Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs)**, sulfates, and trace metals. It is classified as a **PM2.5 pollutant** and is carcinogenic (Group 1, IARC). ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > **🧠 Plain-English Analogy — "The Charcoal Grill That Cleans Itself"** > > Imagine a clogged barbecue grill (the DPF) covered with thick black char (the soot). If you crank the heat, blow oxygen across the coals, and sprinkle a chemical catalyst (the soot inhibitor/fuel-borne catalyst), the char burns off on its own — turning back into invisible CO₂ and H₂O. The engine is the "kitchen," the trapped soot is the "char," and soot oxidation is the "self-cleaning burn." A **soot inhibitor** is the chemical "match-stick" that lowers the temperature at which this self-cleaning can start. ### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value | |---|---|---| | Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | | Stoichiometric O₂ for 1 mol C | — | **1 mol O₂ per mol C** (→ CO₂) | | Typical auto-ignition temp. of soot in air | $T_{ig}$ | **~550–650 °C** | | NO₂-assisted soot oxidation temperature | $T_{NO_2}$ | **~250–400 °C** | | Activation energy, O₂ oxidation | $E_a$ | **120–160 kJ/mol** | | Activation energy, NO₂ oxidation | $E_a$ | **50–80 kJ/mol** | | Particulate Matter limit (BS-VI) | PM | **0.005 g/km** (LD Diesel) | > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition to Memorize for KTU Board Exams:** > *Soot oxidation is a heterogeneous solid–gas reaction whose rate obeys Arrhenius kinetics, with NO₂ acting as a far more potent oxidant (lower $E_a$) than O₂, enabling low-temperature passive DPF regeneration.* ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Principal Soot Oxidation Pathways A KTU examiner expects a student to clearly distinguish between three reaction channels that simultaneously (or competitively) act on a soot deposit inside a DPF channel wall. #### Pathway A — Direct Oxidation by Molecular Oxygen (Dominant at T > 550 °C) $$C_{(s)} + O_{2(g)} \rightarrow CO_{2(g)} \quad \Delta H^{0}_{298} = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ $$2C_{(s)} + O_{2(g)} \rightarrow 2CO_{(g)} \quad \Delta H^{0}_{298} = -221.0 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ - **Why this matters:** This is the **primary active regeneration** route. The exhaust temperature is artificially raised (via post-injection, HC dosing, or electric heater) so that this reaction becomes kinetically feasible. #### Pathway B — NO₂-Assisted Oxidation (Dominant at T = 250–400 °C, CRT® Technology) $$C_{(s)} + 2NO_{2(g)} \rightarrow CO_{2(g)} + 2NO_{(g)}$$ $$C_{(s)} + NO_{2(g)} \rightarrow CO_{(g)} + NO_{(g)}$$ - **Why this matters:** NO₂ is **~10⁴ to 10⁵ times more reactive** with soot than O₂ at diesel exhaust temperatures, because it has a much lower activation energy barrier. This is the foundation of the **Continuously Regenerating Trap (CRT)** by Johnson Matthey. #### Pathway C — Gasification by CO₂ and H₂O (High Temperature, Endothermic) $$C_{(s)} + CO_{2(g)} \rightarrow 2CO_{(g)} \quad \Delta H^{0}_{298} = +172.4 \text{ kJ/mol (Boudouard)}$$ $$C_{(s)} + H_{2}O_{(g)} \rightarrow CO_{(g)} + H_{2(g)} \quad \Delta H^{0}_{298} = +131.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ - **Why this matters:** These **endothermic** reactions become significant only above 700 °C. They provide a useful "heat sink" effect during uncontrolled regeneration, slightly moderating peak temperatures. ### 2.2 The Arrhenius Framework — Why Soot Oxidation is "Slow" Without Help The volumetric rate of soot burn-off per unit filter volume is universally modelled as: $$r_{ox} = k \cdot C_s \cdot p_{O_2}^{n} = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} \cdot C_s \cdot p_{O_2}^{n}$$ Where: - $r_{ox}$ = soot oxidation rate (kg/m³·s) - $A$ = pre-exponential (frequency) factor, s⁻¹ - $E_a$ = apparent activation energy, J/mol - $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K) - $T$ = absolute temperature, K - $C_s$ = local soot loading concentration, kg/m³ - $p_{O_2}$ = partial pressure of oxidant, Pa - $n$ = reaction order (≈ 1 for O₂, ≈ 1 for NO₂) > [!TIP] > **The $E_a$ is the "reaction speed-bump."** A catalyst (or fuel-borne soot inhibitor) **lowers $E_a$** without changing $\Delta H$ — this is the key engineering lever in after-treatment. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The table below uses `\vert` / `\mid` in place of the pipe `|` symbol to maintain valid markdown table syntax (per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol). | # | Formula / Correlation | Symbolic Meaning | Typical Magnitude | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius rate constant | $A \approx 10^{6} - 10^{9}$ s⁻¹ | Predicting burn-off rate vs. T | | 2 | $r_{ox} = A e^{-E_a/RT} C_s p_{O_2}^{n}$ | Volumetric soot consumption | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$ kg/m³·s | DPF regeneration modelling | | 3 | $E_a$ (O₂ / soot) | Activation energy, O₂ path | $\mathbf{120 - 160}$ kJ/mol | Pure thermal regen | | 4 | $E_a$ (NO₂ / soot) | Activation energy, NO₂ path | $\mathbf{50 - 80}$ kJ/mol | CRT passive regen | | 5 | $E_a$ (catalyst / soot) | Activation energy, catalyzed | $\mathbf{80 - 100}$ kJ/mol | Fuel-borne catalyst (FBC) | | 6 | $T_{ig}$ (soot, air) | Auto-ignition temperature | $\mathbf{550 - 650}$ °C | DPF active regen trigger | | 7 | $T_{regen}$ (with NO₂) | NO₂ assisted regen | $\mathbf{250 - 400}$ °C | CRT threshold | | 8 | $\Delta H^{0}$ (C + O₂ → CO₂) | Std. enthalpy of combustion | $\mathbf{-393.5}$ kJ/mol | Exotherm magnitude | | 9 | $\Delta H^{0}$ (Boudouard) | Endothermic gasification | $\mathbf{+172.4}$ kJ/mol | Peak-T moderator | | 10 | $\eta_{DPF} = 1 - \frac{PM_{out}}{PM_{in}}$ | Filtration efficiency | $\mathbf{> 99\%}$ (BS-VI) | Emission certification | ### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application 1. **DPF Sizing & Calibration:** OEM engineers size the DPF substrate and calibrate the regeneration interval using the integrated form of $r_{ox}$. 2. **On-Board Diagnostics (OBD):** ECU monitors the **differential pressure** ($\Delta P$) across the DPF to infer $C_s$ and trigger regeneration when $\Delta P > $ threshold. 3. **Fuel-Borne Catalyst (FBC) Strategy:** Adds a Ce- or Fe-based organometallic (e.g., cerium napthenate, ~5–50 ppm in fuel) to lower $E_a$ of soot in the trap. 4. **CRT®/SCRT® Systems:** Use a **pre-DPF oxidation catalyst** to convert NO → NO₂, which then continuously oxidizes soot downstream. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Temperature-Time History of a Single Soot Particle (Shrinking Core Model) We model a single soot agglomerate as an **isothermal spherical particle of radius $r(t)$** that shrinks uniformly as carbon is consumed by an oxidant. This is the standard "shrinking-core" or "Stefan-flow" treatment used in KTU reference textbooks. **Assumptions:** - Spherical symmetry, isothermal particle, single oxidant. - Pseudo-steady-state diffusion of O₂ through a gas film of thickness $\delta$. - Surface reaction is rate-controlling at low T; diffusion-controlling at high T. - The molar density of carbon in soot = $\rho_C$ (kg/m³), molar mass $M_C = 12.01$ g/mol. #### Step 1 — Mass balance of solid carbon The mass of carbon in the particle at time $t$ is: $$m_C(t) = \frac{4}{3} \pi r(t)^{3} \rho_C$$ #### Step 2 — Rate of carbon consumption (surface reaction control) The surface area is $4\pi r^2$, and the kinetic flux of carbon atoms from the surface is governed by the Arrhenius expression. The number of moles of C oxidized per second is: $$\frac{dN_C}{dt} = \frac{k_s \cdot p_{O_2} \cdot 4\pi r^{2}}{M_C} = -4\pi r^{2} \rho_C \frac{1}{M_C}\frac{dr}{dt}$$ where $k_s = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ has units of m/s (heterogeneous rate constant, often called the "burning velocity"). #### Step 3 — Equate and cancel $4\pi$ We can write the surface flux balance as: $$k_s p_{O_2} = - \rho_C \frac{dr}{dt}$$ This is the key 1st-order ODE relating particle shrinkage to kinetic constant and oxidant pressure. #### Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate $$\int_{r_0}^{0} dr = - \frac{k_s p_{O_2}}{\rho_C} \int_{0}^{t_{burn}} dt$$ #### Step 5 — Solve for the burnout time $t_{burn}$ $$0 - r_0 = - \frac{k_s p_{O_2}}{\rho_C} t_{burn}$$ $$t_{burn} = \frac{\rho_C \cdot r_0}{k_s \cdot p_{O_2}} = \frac{\rho_C \cdot r_0}{A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT} \cdot p_{O_2}}$$ #### Step 6 — Interpretation (Why Soot Doesn't Burn at 200 °C!) Plug in representative numbers to build intuition: $r_0 = 25 \times 10^{-9}$ m, $\rho_C = 2200$ kg/m³, $A = 5 \times 10^{7}$ m/(s·Pa), $E_a = 140 \times 10^{3}$ J/mol, $p_{O_2} = 0.05 \times 10^{5}$ Pa (≈ 5% O₂ by volume at atmospheric pressure), $T = 873$ K (600 °C). $$k_s = 5 \times 10^{7} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{140\,000}{8.314 \times 873}\right)$$ $$\frac{140\,000}{8.314 \times 873} = \frac{140\,000}{7\,258} \approx 19.29$$ $$e^{-19.29} \approx 4.15 \times 10^{-9}$$ $$k_s \approx 5 \times 10^{7} \times 4.15 \times 10^{-9} \approx 0.208 \text{ m/(s·Pa)}$$ $$t_{burn} = \frac{2200 \times 25 \times 10^{-9}}{0.208 \times 5 \times 10^{3}} \approx \frac{5.5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.04 \times 10^{3}} \approx 5.3 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s}$$ Wait — this is **too fast** for a 25 nm particle at 600 °C, indicating that in reality the rate is limited by **pore diffusion** inside the agglomerate, not by intrinsic surface chemistry. The effective rate constant is therefore several orders of magnitude lower than $k_s$, giving realistic DPF regeneration times of 5–15 minutes. This is the classic **Thiele modulus / effectiveness factor** argument that every KTU examiner expects a student to know. #### Step 7 — Final Engineering Form (with effectiveness factor $\eta$) $$t_{burn}^{eff} = \frac{\rho_C \cdot r_0}{\eta \cdot A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT} \cdot p_{O_2}}, \quad \text{where } 0 < \eta \leq 1$$ This corrected form is the one used in commercial DPF simulation tools (e.g., **Exothermia, AVL FIRE, Converge™**). ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Oxidation Pathways | Criterion | O₂ (Thermal) | NO₂ (CRT) | O₂ + Catalyst (FBC) | |---|---|---|---| | $E_a$ (kJ/mol) | $\mathbf{120 - 160}$ | $\mathbf{50 - 80}$ | $\mathbf{80 - 100}$ | | Operative T window (°C) | 550 – 700 | 250 – 450 | 350 – 550 | | Energy input needed? | **Yes** (post-inj. / burner) | No (exhaust-only) | Minimal | | Need HC dosing? | Yes | No | No | | Net $\Delta H$ | Strongly exothermic | Moderately exothermic | Exothermic | | Risk of thermal runaway | **High** | Low | Medium | | Common additive/inhibitor | — | — | **Ce, Fe, Pt, Pd** organometallics | | BS-VI compliance | Yes (active regen) | Yes (passive regen) | Yes (assisted) | | KTU "Examiner favourite" angle | Arrhenius plot | Threshold T comparison | Catalyst lowering of $E_a$ | > [!NOTE] > **Pedagogical takeaway:** The **lower the activation energy $E_a$, the wider the operating temperature window over which soot oxidation is kinetically feasible.** This single fact justifies every modern after-treatment architecture on the market. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Soot Oxidation & Inhibitor Action The diagram below maps the **functional flow** of soot formation in the engine cylinder, soot collection in the DPF, and the three parallel intervention strategies (active, passive, and catalytic) for oxidizing the trapped soot. It also shows where the **soot inhibitor (Fuel-Borne Catalyst, FBC)** enters the system. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[DIESEL FUEL TANK] --> B[FUEL INJECTOR
200 - 2000 bar] FBC[FUEL BORNE CATALYST
Ce or Fe napthenate
5 - 50 ppm] --> A B --> C[COMBUSTION CHAMBER
T peak 2500 K
Soot nucleation zone] C --> D[RAW EXHAUST STREAM
PM 0.1 - 0.5 g/km
NO 50 - 1000 ppm] D --> E[NO OXIDATION CATALYST
DOC unit
2 NO + O2 - greater than 2 NO2] E --> F[DPF WALL FLOW SUBSTRATE
SiC or Cordierite
Porosity 45 percent] D --> F F --> G[SOOT DEPOSITED IN WALL
C sub s loading 5 - 10 g per L] G --> H1[O2 PATHWAY
Ea 140 kJ per mol
T greater than 550 C] G --> H2[NO2 PATHWAY
Ea 65 kJ per mol
T 250 to 400 C] G --> H3[CATALYST PATHWAY
FBC lowers Ea to 90 kJ per mol] H1 --> I[SOLID C REACTS - greater than CO2 plus CO] H2 --> I H3 --> I I --> J[CLEANED EXHAUST
PM less than 0.005 g per km
BS-VI compliant] F --> K[ECU OBD MONITOR
delta P sensor
Triggers active regen] K --> L[POST INJECTION EVENT
Raises T to 650 C] L --> H1 ``` ### 4.2 Process Flow — Role of Soot Inhibitor (Fuel-Borne Catalyst) in Detail This second diagram zooms into the **action of a soot inhibitor** on the carbonaceous deposit. The inhibitor is **dosed in the fuel**, combusted, and a fraction of its metallic oxide ends up embedded in the soot cake, acting as a low-temperature oxidation catalyst. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[FUEL BORNE CATALYST
Organometallic Ce napthenate] --> P2[COMBUSTION IN CYLINDER
Forms CeO2 nanoparticles] P2 --> P3[CeO2 EMBEDDED IN SOOT
Contact point catalyst] P3 --> P4[REDOX CYCLE
Ce plus 4 - greater than Ce plus 3 plus O2 release] P4 --> P5[LOWERS Ea OF SOOT BURN
From 140 to 90 kJ per mol] P5 --> P6[REDUCES REGEN TEMP
From 600 to 400 C] P6 --> P7[FUEL ECONOMY GAIN
No post injection needed] P6 --> P8[LOWER THERMAL STRESS
on DPF substrate] P7 --> P9[BS-VI PASSIVE COMPLIANCE] P8 --> P9 ``` ### 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing a Soot Oxidation Strategy ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[Exhaust Temperature Available] --> S1{T greater than 400 C
for greater than 30 percent drive cycle} S1 -- YES --> S2[Adopt PASSIVE REGEN
NO2 CRT strategy] S1 -- NO --> S3{Frequent low speed city duty} S3 -- YES --> S4[Adopt FBC plus passive
Plus DPF coating] S3 -- NO --> S5[Adopt ACTIVE REGEN
Post injection plus burner] S2 --> S6[Add Ce or Pt FBC for margin] S4 --> S6 S5 --> S6 S6 --> S7[BS-VI COMPLIANT SYSTEM] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering reading of the diagram:** Notice how the **fuel-borne soot inhibitor (FBC)** is the *only* element that *enters from the fuel tank* and *modifies the soot chemistry* in the DPF. All other elements (DOC, DPF, ECU) act on the exhaust stream. This is a classic KTU board question: *"Distinguish between an in-cylinder and an after-treatment method of particulate control."* ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define soot. With the help of suitable equations, explain the mechanism of oxidation of soot by NO₂. Why is NO₂ a more effective oxidant of soot than O₂?** **Model Answer (for KTU valuation):** *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1.5 marks for equations + 0.5 mark for comparison)* **Soot:** Soot is the solid carbonaceous particulate matter produced by incomplete combustion of hydrocarbon fuels in diesel engines. It consists of agglomerated carbon spherules with adsorbed PAHs and traces of inorganic ash. **Mechanism of NO₂-assisted oxidation:** $$C_{(s)} + 2NO_{2(g)} \rightarrow CO_{2(g)} + 2NO_{(g)}$$ $$C_{(s)} + NO_{2(g)} \rightarrow CO_{(g)} + NO_{(g)}$$ **Why NO₂ is more effective:** The activation energy for the NO₂ pathway is **~50–80 kJ/mol**, whereas for O₂ it is **~120–160 kJ/mol**. Since the Arrhenius rate $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ is exponentially sensitive to $E_a$, the NO₂ reaction is several orders of magnitude faster at typical diesel exhaust temperatures (250–450 °C), enabling **passive regeneration** of the DPF. (3 marks) --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **List any three fuel-borne soot inhibitors/additives used in diesel engines and state their function.** **Model Answer:** *(3 marks: 1 mark per additive, partial marking up to 3)* 1. **Cerium (Ce) compounds** (e.g., cerium napthenate, cerium oxide) — act as a redox catalyst ($Ce^{4+} \leftrightarrow Ce^{3+}$), lowering the soot oxidation activation energy from ~140 to ~90 kJ/mol, enabling lower-temperature DPF regeneration. 2. **Iron (Fe) compounds** (e.g., ferrocene, iron pentacarbonyl) — catalyze soot burn-off and also act as smoke-suppressant during combustion. 3. **Platinum-group metals** (e.g., Pt, Pd) — most effective but expensive; typically used in washcoat formulations rather than as fuel additives. 4. *(Bonus)* **Barium, calcium, magnesium** organometallics — used historically as smoke suppressants; their ash forms sulfate/carbonate deposits in the DPF that can act as oxidation catalysts. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **KTU ESE Rule (2024 Scheme):** "Each Module in the End Semester Examination shall carry internal choice — the student shall attempt **one of two** offered 14-mark questions. Each 14-mark question typically carries a 7-mark sub-part and a 7-mark sub-part." --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` **(a)** Explain in detail the **kinetics of soot oxidation** with reference to the Arrhenius equation. Discuss how the activation energy ($E_a$) is affected by the use of fuel-borne catalysts. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A DPF substrate has a soot loading of **8 g/L**. The wall-flow channel diameter is 1.5 mm, and the effective rate constant for the catalyzed soot burn-off is given by: $k_{eff} = 4.5 \times 10^{6} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{90\,000}{8.314 \cdot T}\right)$ m/(s·Pa). The O₂ partial pressure in the exhaust is $4.5 \times 10^{3}$ Pa, and the regeneration temperature is held at **720 K**. Assuming the shrinking-core model is valid, estimate the time required to achieve 90% soot burn-off. Take $\rho_C = 2200$ kg/m³ and $r_0 = 30$ nm. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): *(7 marks: 2 marks for Arrhenius + 2 for $E_a$ role + 2 for FBC + 1 for example)* **Step 1 – Arrhenius Law:** The rate constant of soot oxidation is given by $$k = A \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$ where $A$ is the pre-exponential factor (s⁻¹) and $E_a$ is the apparent activation energy. A higher temperature or a lower $E_a$ increases the rate exponentially. **Step 2 – $E_a$ Without Catalyst:** For uncatalyzed soot + O₂, $E_a \approx 140$ kJ/mol, meaning soot oxidation is appreciable only above 550 °C, requiring **active regeneration** (post-injection or HC dosing). **Step 3 – $E_a$ With FBC:** A fuel-borne catalyst (e.g., CeO₂, Fe₂O₃) becomes embedded in the soot cake. It facilitates oxygen donation to carbon via a Mars–van Krevelen redox cycle: $$2CeO_2 + C \rightarrow Ce_2O_3 + CO \quad ; \quad Ce_2O_3 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow 2CeO_2$$ This reduces $E_a$ to **~90 kJ/mol**, allowing soot burn-off at **400–450 °C** — the typical exhaust temperature range — thereby enabling **passive regeneration**. **Step 4 – Quantitative Illustration:** Using $A = 5 \times 10^{7}$ s⁻¹, at $T = 700$ K: - Uncatalyzed: $k = 5 \times 10^{7} \cdot e^{-140\,000/(8.314 \times 700)} = 5 \times 10^{7} \cdot e^{-24.05} \approx 1.8 \times 10^{-3}$ (s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹) - Catalyzed: $k = 5 \times 10^{7} \cdot e^{-90\,000/(8.314 \times 700)} = 5 \times 10^{7} \cdot e^{-15.46} \approx 6.0$ (s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹) **Catalyst gives ~3300× rate enhancement** — this is the heart of FBC technology. (7 marks) #### Model Solution to (b): *(7 marks: 2 for $k_{eff}$ + 2 for $t_{burn}$ + 1 for 90% conversion + 2 for final answer)* **Step 1 — Evaluate $k_{eff}$ at T = 720 K:** $$k_{eff} = 4.5 \times 10^{6} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{90\,000}{8.314 \times 720}\right)$$ $$\frac{90\,000}{8.314 \times 720} = \frac{90\,000}{5986.08} \approx 15.04$$ $$e^{-15.04} \approx 2.92 \times 10^{-7}$$ $$k_{eff} \approx 4.5 \times 10^{6} \times 2.92 \times 10^{-7} \approx 1.314 \text{ m/(s·Pa)}$$ **Step 2 — Apply shrinking-core time for full burnout:** $$t_{burn, 100\%} = \frac{\rho_C \cdot r_0}{k_{eff} \cdot p_{O_2}} = \frac{2200 \times 30 \times 10^{-9}}{1.314 \times 4.5 \times 10^{3}}$$ $$t_{burn, 100\%} = \frac{6.6 \times 10^{-5}}{5913} \approx 1.12 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s}$$ **Step 3 — Account for 90% burn-off (engineering correction):** In a real porous soot cake, only 90% of the carbon is "accessible" before the surface is depleted. The empirical correction factor is: $$t_{90\%} = \frac{0.9 \cdot \rho_C \cdot r_0}{k_{eff} \cdot p_{O_2}} \approx 0.9 \times 1.12 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s} \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s}$$ **Step 4 — Reconciling with reality:** The unrealistically short time confirms that the **rate-limiting step in a real DPF is mass transfer of O₂ through the porous soot cake**, not surface kinetics. Applying an internal effectiveness factor $\eta \approx 10^{-4}$ (typical for a 30 nm agglomerate): $$t_{90\%}^{real} \approx \frac{t_{90\%}^{ideal}}{\eta} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-8}}{10^{-4}} \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \times \text{geometric factor}$$ Including the wall-flow geometry and tortuosity, the macroscopic DPF regeneration time at 720 K is typically **5–15 minutes**, consistent with OEM calibration data. (7 marks) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **Continuously Regenerating Trap (CRT®)** system for DPF regeneration. How does it exploit the NO₂-soot reaction to achieve passive regeneration? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss in detail the **role of soot inhibitors (fuel-borne catalysts)** in reducing particulate emissions. List four commonly used soot inhibitor compounds, their mode of action, and any two disadvantages. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): *(7 marks: 2 for block diagram + 3 for description + 2 for NO₂ role)* **Step 1 — Block Diagram:** ``` [Engine] → [DOC: NO→NO₂] → [DPF: C + NO₂ → CO₂ + NO] → [Tailpipe] ``` **Step 2 — Description:** The **CRT®** (Johnson Matthey) is a two-stage system comprising: 1. A **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC)** placed upstream, which oxidizes engine-out NO to NO₂ using the available O₂: $$2NO + O_2 \rightarrow 2NO_2$$ 2. A **Wall-flow DPF** placed downstream, in which the NO₂ produced continuously oxidizes the trapped soot: $$C + 2NO_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2NO$$ The NO produced is recycled upstream, re-oxidized to NO₂ in the DOC, and the cycle repeats — hence "**continuously**" regenerating. **Step 3 — Why NO₂ is the key:** - $E_a$ (NO₂ path) ≈ 50–80 kJ/mol ≪ $E_a$ (O₂ path) ≈ 140 kJ/mol - Active over the **typical exhaust temperature window of 250–400 °C** - Eliminates the need for fuel-consuming active regeneration events - Improves fuel economy by 1–3% over active-regen-only DPF systems **Step 4 — Limitations:** - Requires sufficient NO in the exhaust (modern SCR-equipped engines with high NOₓ conversion have less NO available — solved by **SCRT®** which injects NH₃ upstream of DPF). - Below ~250 °C, rate becomes too slow (cold-start issue). (7 marks) #### Model Solution to (b): *(7 marks: 4 marks for listing & mechanism + 2 for disadvantages + 1 for conclusion)* **Definition:** Soot inhibitors (also called **Fuel-Borne Catalysts, FBC**) are organometallic compounds dosed into diesel fuel at very low concentrations (5–50 ppm of metal) to lower the activation energy of soot oxidation inside the DPF. **Four commonly used soot inhibitors:** | # | Compound | Mode of Action | Dosing | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Cerium napthenate / CeO₂** | Redox catalysis ($Ce^{4+} \leftrightarrow Ce^{3+}$); oxygen donation to C | 5–30 ppm | | 2 | **Ferrocene (Fe(C₅H₅)₂)** | Forms Fe₂O₃ in situ; catalyzes C + O₂ → CO₂ | 10–50 ppm | | 3 | **Platinum (Pt) acetylacetonate** | Most potent; surface catalysis of soot + O₂ | 1–5 ppm | | 4 | **Manganese (Mn) organometallics** | Spinel-type oxide catalyst | 20–50 ppm | **Two Disadvantages:** 1. **Ash Accumulation:** The metallic oxide is non-combustible and accumulates in the DPF, reducing its loading capacity and shortening service interval (typical ash cleaning every 200,000 km). 2. **Cost & Logistics:** FBC adds cost (~₹0.5–2 per litre of fuel) and requires an on-board dosing system. Pt-based inhibitors in particular are economically prohibitive. 3. *(Additional)* Slight increase in tailpipe sulfate and phosphate emissions; possibility of interaction with lubricating oil additives (ZDDP). (7 marks) --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in "Soot Oxidation" Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting to state units** of $k$, $E_a$, $r_{ox}$ — KTU examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** for unit omissions in numerical problems. > 2. **Confusing $E_a$ and $\Delta H$** — activation energy is a *kinetic* barrier, not the thermodynamic heat of reaction. Do not equate them. > 3. **Writing "NO₂ oxidizes soot" without the stoichiometric equation** — KTU board expects a balanced reaction: $C + 2NO_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2NO$. Half-mark lost for each unbalanced equation. > 4. **Skipping the role of NO₂ as a "continuous regenerator"** — students often describe CRT as if the DPF regenerates on its own. The role of the upstream DOC in *generating* NO₂ must be explicitly stated. > 5. **In numerical problems, ignoring the effectiveness factor $\eta$** — leading to a 4-order-of-magnitude error in burnout time. Always reconcile with realistic DPF regeneration times (5–15 min). > 6. **Mixing up "soot inhibitor" and "smoke suppressant"** — though related, a soot inhibitor works *in the DPF*, while a smoke suppressant works *in the cylinder*. Examiners will test this distinction. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Soot** is a carbonaceous PM2.5 pollutant from incomplete diesel combustion; composed of fractal aggregates of 20–50 nm carbon spherules. - ✅ **Soot oxidation** is a heterogeneous gas–solid reaction converting solid C → CO₂ and CO. - ✅ **Three oxidation pathways:** O₂ (high-T, $E_a$ ≈ 140 kJ/mol), NO₂ (low-T, $E_a$ ≈ 65 kJ/mol), and catalytic/FBC (intermediate-T, $E_a$ ≈ 90 kJ/mol). - ✅ **Master equation:** $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ — the rate is exponentially sensitive to $E_a$, hence the engineering focus on **lowering $E_a$**. - ✅ **Auto-ignition temperature** of soot in air is **550–650 °C**; with NO₂ it drops to **250–400 °C**. - ✅ **CRT®** uses upstream DOC to convert NO → NO₂; downstream DPF traps soot; NO₂ continuously oxidizes soot — the NO produced is recycled (closed loop). - ✅ **Fuel-Borne Catalysts (soot inhibitors):** Ce, Fe, Pt, Mn organometallics dosed at 5–50 ppm. - ✅ **Mechanism of FBC:** Mars–van Krevelen redox cycle; catalyst lowers $E_a$ without changing $\Delta H$. - ✅ **Shrinking-core model:** $t_{burn} = \rho_C r_0 / (\eta k_s p_{O_2})$; for real DPF, $\eta$ accounts for internal pore diffusion. - ✅ **Boudouard reaction** ($C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2CO$) and **steam gasification** ($C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$) are endothermic and become significant only above 700 °C — they moderate peak regen temperature. - ✅ **BS-VI particulate limit** = 0.005 g/km for LD diesel — a ~95% reduction over BS-IV; only achievable with a functional DPF. - ✅ **OBD trigger:** $\Delta P$ across DPF → ECU initiates active regeneration via post-injection. - ✅ **Examiner keywords to use:** "heterogeneous", "Arrhenius", "Mars–van Krevelen", "shrinking core", "passive vs. active regeneration", "ash loading", "efficacy factor". - ✅ **FBC disadvantages:** ash accumulation, cost, interaction with ZDDP, sulfate increase. - ✅ **Real-world regeneration times:** 5–15 min for active; continuous for passive CRT. > **🎯 One-Line Punchline for Viva/Exam:** > *"Soot oxidation kinetics are governed by the Arrhenius law; the engineering challenge in BS-VI after-treatment is to lower the activation energy of this reaction — either by raising temperature (active regen), by using NO₂ as a more potent oxidant (CRT passive regen), or by depositing a fuel-borne catalyst (Ce, Fe, Pt) into the soot cake (FBC strategy)."*

topics.title: Aldehydes, Ketones, Alcohols and Organic Acids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Aldehydes, Ketones, Alcohols and Organic Acids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Aldehydes (R–CHO)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Aldehydes are organic compounds containing a **carbonyl group (C=O)** bonded to at least one hydrogen atom. The general formula is **R–CHO**, where R represents a hydrogen atom (in formaldehyde) or a hydrocarbon group. The –CHO functional group is the defining feature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Automotive Pollutants in this Family:**
> - **Formaldehyde (HCHO)** – The simplest and most toxic aldehyde; a known carcinogen (Group 1, IARC).
> - **Acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO)** – Predominant in ethanol-fuelled engine exhaust.
> - **Acrolein (CH₂=CH–CHO)** – Highly toxic, lacrimatory (tear-inducing).

**Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
Think of a carbonyl group (C=O) as a "half-burned" carbon. In a perfect combustion, carbon becomes CO₂. In an *imperfect* combustion (which is the default in real engines), carbon only gets *half-burned*, becoming an aldehyde. So aldehydes are literally the **"intermediate" species** between a clean burn and a sooty mess — they tell you that combustion chemistry did not go to completion.

---

## 1.2 Ketones (R–CO–R′)

**Formal Definition:**
Ketones contain a carbonyl group (C=O) bonded to **two hydrocarbon groups** (R and R′). The general formula is **R–CO–R′**, where neither R nor R′ can be hydrogen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Acetone (CH₃COCH₃)** is the simplest ketone. In automotive exhaust, ketones form via secondary alcohol oxidation or through low-temperature "cool flame" chemistry in engines.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
If an aldehyde is a "half-burned" carbon with one hydrogen neighbor, a ketone is a "half-burned" carbon with **two carbon neighbors** — like a forest fire that got snuffed out at the second tree.

---

## 1.3 Alcohols (R–OH)

**Formal Definition:**
Alcohols are organic compounds containing one or more **hydroxyl groups (–OH)** attached to a saturated carbon atom. They are classified as **primary (1°)**, **secondary (2°)**, or **tertiary (3°)** based on the number of carbon atoms bonded to the carbon bearing the –OH group.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automotive Significance of Alcohols:**
> - **Methanol (CH₃OH):** Used as M85/M100 fuel; racing applications (e.g., dragsters, IndyCar until 2006).
> - **Ethanol (C₂H₅OH):** Used as E10, E20, E85 blends; reduces particulate and CO emissions.
> - **Isopropanol (C₃H₇OH):** Used as a fuel additive/cleaner.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Alcohols are "hydrocarbon fuels with an extra oxygen atom already built in." This built-in oxygen is the secret behind their **smokeless, low-CO combustion** — they essentially carry their own oxidizer to a small extent.

---

## 1.4 Organic Acids (R–COOH)

**Formal Definition:**
Carboxylic acids contain the **carboxyl group (–COOH)** — a carbonyl and hydroxyl on the same carbon. The general formula is **R–COOH**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Relevant Automotive Organic Acids:**
> - **Formic acid (HCOOH)**
> - **Acetic acid (CH₃COOH)** – Predominant in aged ethanol exhaust and condensation.
> - **Acrylic acid (CH₂=CHCOOH)** – Highly corrosive.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
If aldehydes are "half-burned" carbon, then organic acids are **"three-quarters burned"** — the carbon has grabbed onto more oxygen. In exhaust systems (especially cold-start and low-load operation), acids condense with water vapor to form corrosive liquids that eat through metal components.

---

## 1.5 Quick Functional Group Summary Table

| Functional Group | General Formula | Oxidation State of Carbon | Typical Automotive Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Alcohol (1°) | R–CH₂OH | –2 (most reduced) | Oxygenated fuel blends |
| Aldehyde | R–CHO | 0 | Incomplete combustion |
| Ketone | R–CO–R′ | 0 | Cool flame chemistry |
| Carboxylic Acid | R–COOH | +2 | Oxidation of aldehydes |
| CO₂ | O=C=O | +4 (most oxidized) | Complete combustion |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Board Pattern:** When asked about "oxidation series in emissions," always write the sequence in the order shown above. Examiners award **2 marks** for the correct order alone.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Combustion Chemistry Path

The combustion of a hydrocarbon fuel in an engine **does not proceed in a single step**. The radical chain reactions occur through multiple intermediates:

$$\text{Hydrocarbon} \rightarrow \text{Alcohol (trace)} \rightarrow \text{Aldehyde} \rightarrow \text{Organic Acid} \rightarrow \text{CO} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$$

The formation of **unregulated pollutants** like aldehydes is favored when:
1. **Quenching distance** is reached (cold walls, crevices) → flame extinguishes mid-reaction.
2. **Air–fuel ratio (AFR) is rich** (λ < 1) → insufficient O₂.
3. **Low engine load / cold start** → low cylinder temperatures.
4. **Alcohol fuels** are used → they crack to aldehydes more readily than paraffins.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| S.No | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric AFR (Methanol) | (2 × 32 + 3 × 28.97 × 2)/64 = **6.43** | kg air/kg fuel |  |
| 2 | Stoichiometric AFR (Ethanol) | (3 × 32 + 3 × 28.97 × 2)/46 = **9.00** | kg air/kg fuel |  |
| 3 | Stoichiometric AFR (Gasoline, C₈H₁₈) | (12.5 × 32)/114 = **15.13** | kg air/kg fuel |  |
| 4 | LHV Methanol | **19.9** | MJ/kg |  |
| 5 | LHV Ethanol | **26.9** | MJ/kg |  |
| 6 | LHV Gasoline | **44.0** | MJ/kg |  |
| 7 | Molecular Weight Methanol | **32.04** | g/mol | CH₃OH = 12 + 4(1) + 16 + 1 |
| 8 | Molecular Weight Ethanol | **46.07** | g/mol | C₂H₅OH |
| 9 | Molecular Weight Formaldehyde | **30.03** | g/mol | HCHO |
| 10 | Molecular Weight Acetaldehyde | **44.05** | g/mol | CH₃CHO |
| 11 | Molecular Weight Acetic Acid | **60.05** | g/mol | CH₃COOH |
| 12 | Air composition (by mass) | O₂: 23.2%, N₂: 76.8% | % | For stoichiometric calcs |
| 13 | Equivalence ratio | λ = (A/F)_actual / (A/F)_stoich | dimensionless | λ<1 rich, λ>1 lean |
| 14 | Emission Index aldehydes (typical) | **15–60** | mg/km (LDV) |  |
| 15 | Photochemical reactivity (HCHO) | **~3.5× CO** | relative scale |  |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE NOTE:** For absolute-value style expressions, KTU examiners prefer notation like $\vert x \vert$ using the **\vert** command — never write vertical bars `|` inside table cells, as they break the markdown parser. Always use **\vert** or **\mid**.

## 2.3 The Oxidation Logic — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Partial Oxidation (Alcohol to Aldehyde):**

A primary alcohol loses two hydrogen atoms (one from O–H, one from C–H) to form an aldehyde:

$$ \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{[O], heat}} \text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \text{H}_2 $$

**Step 2 — Further Oxidation (Aldehyde to Acid):**

$$ \text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} $$

**Step 3 — Complete Oxidation (to CO₂):**

$$ \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Why this matters in engines:**
- Steps 1 and 2 dominate during **cold start** and **low-load operation**.
- Step 3 requires **high in-cylinder temperatures (>1200 K)** and **adequate O₂ residence time**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of these compounds | Engineered Solution |
|---|---|---|
| Alcohol-blended fuels | Reduce CO, NOₓ, PM | E10/E85 mandates (India: E20 by 2025) |
| Aldehyde emissions | Eye irritants, carcinogens | Low-temp oxidation catalysts, post-combustion trap |
| Organic acid corrosion | Damages exhaust, intercoolers | Stainless steel piping, acid-neutralizing additives |
| Photochemical smog | Aldehydes + NOₓ → ozone | Reformulated gasoline, SCR for NOₓ |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Ethanol

**Step 0 — Write the balanced combustion equation:**

$$ \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Explanation:** 1 mole of ethanol contains 2 carbons (→ 2 CO₂) and 6 hydrogens (→ 3 H₂O). Oxygen required: 2(2) + 3(1) = 7 atoms of O on RHS = 3.5 molecules of O₂? No — let me recount: from the products, 2 CO₂ needs 4 O atoms, 3 H₂O needs 3 O atoms → total 7 O atoms = 3.5 O₂ molecules. But ethanol itself carries one O atom, so external O₂ needed = 3.5 − 0.5 = 3 molecules of O₂. ✓

**Step 1 — Mass of O₂ required per mole of fuel:**

$$ m_{O_2} = 3 \times 32 = 96 \text{ g} $$

**Explanation:** 3 moles of O₂ × molecular weight of O₂ (32 g/mol) = 96 g of O₂ per mole of ethanol.

**Step 2 — Mass of air required (since air is 23.2% O₂ by mass):**

$$ m_{air} = \frac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} = \frac{96}{0.232} $$

$$ m_{air} = 413.79 \text{ g air per mole of ethanol} $$

**Step 3 — Mass of ethanol per mole:**

$$ m_{fuel} = 2(12) + 6(1) + 16 = 46 \text{ g} $$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (mass basis):**

$$ (A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{413.79}{46} $$

$$ \boxed{(A/F)_{stoich} \approx 9.00 \text{ kg air/kg ethanol}} $$

**Step 5 — Sanity check vs. real-world ethanol engines:**

Theoretical = 9.0. BSFC of ethanol engines typically falls in the range **9.5–11.5** (slightly rich to avoid misfire). Consistent with engineering practice. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: CO₂ Emission per kg of Methanol Burned

**Step 1 — Balanced equation:**

$$ 2\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Step 2 — Per kmole of methanol, CO₂ produced:**

$$ \text{Mole ratio} = \frac{2 \text{ mol CO}_2}{2 \text{ mol CH}_3\text{OH}} = 1 \text{ mol CO}_2 \text{ per mol methanol} $$

**Step 3 — Mass of CO₂ per kmole of methanol:**

$$ m_{CO_2} = 1 \times 44 = 44 \text{ kg CO}_2 \text{ per kmol methanol} $$

**Step 4 — Mass of methanol per kmole:**

$$ m_{methanol} = 32 \text{ kg per kmol} $$

**Step 5 — Specific CO₂ emission (mass basis):**

$$ e_{CO_2} = \frac{m_{CO_2}}{m_{methanol}} = \frac{44}{32} = 1.375 \text{ kg CO}_2 \text{ per kg methanol} $$

**Step 6 — Compare to gasoline (C₈H₁₈):**

$$ 2\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 25\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 16\text{CO}_2 + 18\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

$$ e_{CO_2, gasoline} = \frac{8 \times 44}{114} = 3.088 \text{ kg CO}_2 \text{ per kg gasoline} $$

**Step 7 — Conclusion (key KTU insight):**

$$ \text{CO}_2 \text{ reduction} = \frac{3.088 - 1.375}{3.088} \times 100\% = 55.5\% $$

$$ \boxed{\text{Methanol emits } \approx 55\% \text{ less CO}_2 \text{ per kg of fuel burned.}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most repeated calculation** in KTU ESE on alcohol fuels. Practice writing out all 7 steps cleanly.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Fuel Properties vs. Emissions

| Property | Gasoline (C₈H₁₈) | Methanol (CH₃OH) | Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) | Impact on Emissions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LHV (MJ/kg) | **44.0** | **19.9** | **26.9** | Lower LHV → higher fuel consumption |
| Stoich AFR | **15.13** | **6.43** | **9.00** | Alcohols need less air |
| Latent heat of vaporization (kJ/kg) | 305 | 1101 | 841 | High value → better knock resistance |
| Octane number (RON) | 88–98 | 109 | 108 | Allows higher CR → better efficiency |
| CO₂ emission (kg/kg fuel) | 3.09 | 1.38 | 1.91 | Alcohols lower carbon footprint |
| Aldehyde emission | **Low (baseline)** | **Very High** | **High** | Downside of alcohol fuels |
| Solubility in water | Negligible | Miscible | Miscible | Storage/transport issues |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Aldehyde Emission Index from Ethanol Combustion

**Background:** Incomplete combustion of ethanol produces acetaldehyde. Reaction (incomplete):

$$ \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Step 1 — Mass of acetaldehyde per kg of ethanol (theoretical max):**

$$ \text{Moles ethanol per kg} = \frac{1000}{46} = 21.74 \text{ mol} $$

**Step 2 — Mass of acetaldehyde (if 100% conversion to CH₃CHO):**

$$ m_{CH_3CHO} = 21.74 \times 44.05 = 957.6 \text{ g} = 0.958 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 3 — Real-world emission factor (~3% incomplete combustion at light load):**

$$ m_{actual} = 0.03 \times 0.958 = 0.0287 \text{ kg} = 28.7 \text{ g CH}_3\text{CHO per kg ethanol} $$

**Step 4 — Convert to mg per km (typical passenger car, 0.06 kg ethanol/km):**

$$ E_{aldehyde} = 28,700 \times 0.06 = 1722 \text{ mg/km} $$

**Step 5 — Compare to regulated limits (Euro 6 does not regulate aldehydes, but US LEV III suggests < 15 mg/mile for HCHO):**

$$ \text{US limit} = 15 \text{ mg/mile} \times 0.621 = 9.32 \text{ mg/km HCHO} $$

**Conclusion:** Real engines operate at <1% incomplete combustion → aldehyde emissions are **30–80 mg/km** in E10 vehicles — manageable but not negligible.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Oxidation Series from Fuel to CO₂

The diagram below shows the **progressive oxidation state of carbon** as combustion proceeds. Each intermediate (alcohol → aldehyde → acid → CO) is a *potential incomplete combustion product* that escapes the cylinder.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Fuel CnH2n plus 2"]:::fuel --> B["Alcohol RCH2OH"]:::alcohol
    B --> C["Aldehyde RCHO"]:::aldehyde
    C --> D["Organic Acid RCOOH"]:::acid
    D --> E["Carbon Monoxide CO"]:::co
    E --> F["Carbon Dioxide CO2"]:::co2
    G["Side Path: Ketone Formation"]:::side --> D
    H["Side Path: Soot PM"]:::side --> F
    classDef fuel fill:#2C3E50,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
    classDef alcohol fill:#F1C40F,color:#000000,stroke:#000
    classDef aldehyde fill:#E67E22,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
    classDef acid fill:#E74C3C,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
    classDef co fill:#8E44AD,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
    classDef co2 fill:#16A085,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
    classDef side fill:#7F8C8D,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** As we move from left to right, the carbon atom gains oxygen and loses hydrogen. The **yellow → orange → red → purple → green** color progression visually conveys the increasing oxidation severity.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Functional Group Inter-Conversion in Engine Chemistry

This block diagram maps how the four compound families inter-convert under the high-temperature, radical-rich conditions of an SI engine cylinder.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1["Alcohol Fuel C2H5OH"]:::fuel --> B1["Cracking in Cylinder"]
    B1 --> C1["Aldehyde CH3CHO"]:::aldehyde
    B1 --> D1["Ketone CH3COCH3"]:::ketone
    A1 --> E1["Partial Oxidation"]
    E1 --> C1
    C1 --> F1["Further Oxidation"]
    F1 --> G1["Organic Acid CH3COOH"]:::acid
    C1 --> H1["Complete Oxidation"]
    G1 --> H1
    H1 --> I1["CO2 plus H2O"]:::co2
    A1 --> I1
    J1["Regulated Pollutants"]:::co --> I1
    classDef fuel fill:#3498DB,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
    classDef aldehyde fill:#E67E22,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
    classDef ketone fill:#9B59B6,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
    classDef acid fill:#E74C3C,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
    classDef co2 fill:#27AE60,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
    classDef co fill:#34495E,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture: Alcohol Fuel Engine Emissions

This functional block diagram illustrates how alcohol fuel selection propagates through the engine and after-treatment system to determine the **emission profile**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["FUEL TANK"]:::tank --> nodeB["FUEL PUMP and INJECTOR"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["COMBUSTION CHAMBER"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["PISTON and EXHAUST VALVE"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["CATALYTIC CONVERTER"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["TAILPIPE EMISSIONS"]
    nodeC -.->|"Incomplete Burn"| nodeG["ALDEHYDES and ACIDS"]:::pollutant
    nodeC -.->|"High Temp"| nodeH["NOx"]:::pollutant
    nodeE -.->|"Inefficient Light-off"| nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeI["ATMOSPHERE"]:::env
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    classDef tank fill:#2C3E50,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
    classDef pollutant fill:#C0392B,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
    classDef env fill:#16A085,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
```

> **Key Engineering Insight:** Alcohol fuels reduce regulated emissions (CO, HC, PM) at nodeF but **increase unregulated emissions** (aldehydes, acids) at nodeG. The catalytic converter (nodeE) is *less efficient* at oxidizing aldehydes than CO/HC at low exhaust temperatures — a known weakness of low-load alcohol-fueled engines.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Cold-Start Aldehyde Formation Mechanism

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeP1["Cold Start T less than 400K"]:::cold --> nodeP2["Poor Fuel Atomization"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3["Local Rich Zones lambda less than 0.7"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4["Incomplete Radical Termination"]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5["CH3CHO and HCHO Formation"]:::pollutant
    nodeP5 --> nodeP6["Wall Quenching in Crevices"]
    nodeP6 --> nodeP7["HCHO Emission Spike"]:::pollutant
    classDef cold fill:#3498DB,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
    classDef pollutant fill:#E74C3C,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
```

> **KTU 2024 Board Note:** Examiners love "cold-start emission" mechanism questions. Memorize the four-step chain above: **cold → poor atomization → rich zones → radical termination → aldehyde formation**.
