# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between aldehydes and ketones with one example each, in the context of automotive exhaust emissions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Aldehyde (R–CHO) | Ketone (R–CO–R′) |
|---|---|---|
| Functional group | Carbonyl + at least one H atom | Carbonyl + two R groups |
| General position | Terminal carbon | Internal carbon |
| Automotive example | Formaldehyde (HCHO), Acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO) | Acetone (CH₃COCH₃) |
| Photochemical reactivity | **Higher** | Lower |
| Health impact | Carcinogenic, irritant | Narcotic at high conc. |
| Source in engine | Incomplete combustion of HC, alcohol fuels | Cool flame chemistry, partial oxidation of secondary alcohols |

> **Valuation tip:** A clean table is worth **2 marks**; the example from automotive context is worth **1 mark**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Why are alcohol-blended fuels considered "cleaner" than gasoline, and what is the major emission trade-off?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Reasons for cleaner combustion (2 marks):**
1. Alcohols contain **chemically bound oxygen** → more complete combustion → **lower CO** emissions.
2. No C–C bonds in methanol (only C–H, C–O, O–H) → **lower tendency to form soot/PM**.
3. Higher latent heat of vaporization → **lower peak cylinder temperature** → **reduced thermal NOₓ**.
4. Higher octane rating → **higher compression ratio possible** → improved thermal efficiency.

**Major trade-off (1 mark):**
Alcohol combustion produces **significantly higher aldehyde emissions** (especially formaldehyde and acetaldehyde). Aldehydes are unregulated toxic air pollutants contributing to photochemical smog and direct health hazards (cancer, eye irritation).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Q: (a)** With neat chemical equations, discuss the formation of aldehydes and ketones in SI engines. Explain how their emissions are controlled. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An SI engine operates on a stoichiometric mixture of ethanol. Calculate:
  - (i) The stoichiometric air-fuel ratio by mass. **(3 marks)**
  - (ii) The mass of CO₂ produced per kg of fuel. **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) The percentage reduction in CO₂ emission compared to gasoline (C₈H₁₈). **(2 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Aldehydes and Ketones in SI Engines (7 marks):**

**(i) Aldehyde formation mechanism (3 marks):**
In SI engines, aldehydes form primarily via **partial oxidation of unburnt hydrocarbons** during the expansion stroke and **quenching at cold cylinder walls**. The simplified reaction:

$$ \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{incomplete}} \text{R–CHO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO} $$

For ethanol specifically:

$$ \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

Peak aldehyde emissions occur during **cold start** (low T_cat) and **low load** (low cylinder T).

**(ii) Ketone formation (2 marks):**
Ketones (mainly acetone) form via:
- Oxidation of secondary alcohols formed as intermediates.
- Low-temperature "cool flame" reactions: $T = 600\text{–}800$ K.

$$ 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CH}_3\text{COCH}_3 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**(iii) Control strategies (2 marks):**
- **Three-way catalytic converter** with close-coupled design → faster light-off.
- **Post-combustion oxidation catalyst** downstream of TWC.
- **Optimized cold-start injection strategies** → reduce rich cold operation.
- **Alcohol-resistant fuel system materials** to prevent acid corrosion.

---

**Part (b) — Calculations (7 marks):**

**(i) Stoichiometric AFR for ethanol (3 marks):**

Balanced equation:

$$ \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

Moles: 1 mol ethanol reacts with 3 mol O₂.

$$ m_{O_2} = 3 \times 32 = 96 \text{ g} $$

$$ m_{air} = \frac{96}{0.232} = 413.79 \text{ g} $$

$$ m_{fuel} = 46 \text{ g} $$

$$ \boxed{(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{413.79}{46} = 9.00 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}} $$

**(ii) CO₂ per kg of ethanol (2 marks):**

$$ m_{CO_2} \text{ per mol} = 2 \times 44 = 88 \text{ g} $$

$$ e_{CO_2} = \frac{88}{46} = 1.913 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kg ethanol} $$

**(iii) % reduction vs gasoline (2 marks):**

For gasoline (C₈H₁₈):

$$ \text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 12.5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 8\text{CO}_2 + 9\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

$$ e_{CO_2,gasoline} = \frac{8 \times 44}{114} = 3.088 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kg gasoline} $$

$$ \%\text{ reduction} = \frac{3.088 - 1.913}{3.088} \times 100 = 38.05\% $$

$$ \boxed{\approx 38\% \text{ reduction in CO}_2 \text{ per kg of fuel}} $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: (a)** Discuss the role of organic acids in automotive pollution. Explain their impact on engine components, exhaust systems, and the environment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Methanol (CH₃OH) is being considered as an alternative fuel. With suitable chemical equations, calculate:
  - (i) The mass of air required for stoichiometric combustion of 1 kg of methanol. **(3 marks)**
  - (ii) The lower heating value (LHV) given that methanol's enthalpy of combustion is −726 kJ/mol. **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) The mass of aldehyde (HCHO) that would form if 2% of the methanol undergoes incomplete combustion to formaldehyde. **(2 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Organic Acids in Automotive Pollution (7 marks):**

**Definition and source (2 marks):**
Organic acids (R–COOH), mainly **formic acid (HCOOH)** and **acetic acid (CH₃COOH)**, form as secondary oxidation products of aldehydes in exhaust. They condense with water vapor in cooler parts of the exhaust system, especially during cold start and low-load operation.

**Impact on engine components (2 marks):**
- **Corrosion** of mild steel exhaust manifolds, mufflers, and tailpipes.
- **Intercooler corrosion** in turbocharged engines (acid + condensate).
- **Catalyst degradation** — phosphate/sulfate acid deposition on washcoat.
- **Damage to rubber/elastomer seals** and O-rings in fuel and exhaust systems.

**Environmental impact (2 marks):**
- Lower atmosphere acid rain contributor.
- Precursor to peroxyacyl nitrates (PAN) in photochemical smog.
- Toxic to aquatic life in urban runoff.

**Control strategies (1 mark):**
- Use of stainless steel (SS304/SS409) exhaust components.
- Optimized exhaust gas temperatures to prevent condensation.
- Acid-resistant catalyst substrates.

---

**Part (b) — Methanol Calculations (7 marks):**

**(i) Stoichiometric air for 1 kg methanol (3 marks):**

Balanced equation:

$$ 2\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

Mole ratio: 2 mol methanol : 3 mol O₂ → 1 : 1.5

$$ n_{methanol} = \frac{1000}{32} = 31.25 \text{ mol} $$

$$ n_{O_2} = 1.5 \times 31.25 = 46.875 \text{ mol} $$

$$ m_{O_2} = 46.875 \times 32 = 1500 \text{ g} = 1.5 \text{ kg} $$

$$ m_{air} = \frac{1.5}{0.232} = 6.466 \text{ kg} $$

$$ \boxed{m_{air} = 6.47 \text{ kg per kg methanol}} $$

**(ii) LHV of methanol (2 marks):**

$$ \Delta H_c = -726 \text{ kJ/mol} $$

$$ \text{LHV} = \frac{726 \text{ kJ/mol}}{0.032 \text{ kg/mol}} = 22,687.5 \text{ kJ/kg} = 22.69 \text{ MJ/kg} $$

$$ \boxed{\text{LHV}_{\text{methanol}} \approx 22.7 \text{ MJ/kg}} $$

> **Note:** The standard reference value is 19.9 MJ/kg. The difference is because the given ΔH_c may include HHV in some textbooks. KTU typically accepts 19.9–22.7 depending on whether latent heat of water vaporization is included. **Write both values and indicate the assumption.**

**(iii) HCHO from 2% incomplete combustion (2 marks):**

Incomplete reaction:

$$ \text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{HCHO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

Mass of methanol undergoing incomplete combustion:

$$ m_{incomplete} = 0.02 \times 1 = 0.02 \text{ kg} = 20 \text{ g} $$

Moles of methanol:

$$ n = \frac{20}{32} = 0.625 \text{ mol} $$

From stoichiometry: 1 mol methanol → 1 mol HCHO

$$ m_{HCHO} = 0.625 \times 30 = 18.75 \text{ g} $$

$$ \boxed{m_{HCHO} = 18.75 \text{ g per kg of methanol burned}} $$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in This Module:**
> 1. **Forgetting the atomic mass of oxygen (16) vs. molecular O₂ (32).** Use 32 for O₂ in stoichiometric calculations — this single error loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Writing CO₂ mass as 44 instead of using mole ratios first.** Always go *moles → moles → mass*. Skipping the mole step is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing LHV and HHV.** KTU 2024 expects LHV for engine calculations. Stating HHV without clarification = −1 mark.
> 4. **Writing "aldehydes" when the question asks for a specific aldehyde.** HCHO and CH₃CHO have very different toxicities. Examiners award 1 extra mark for naming the exact compound.
> 5. **Ignoring λ in the AFR equation.** If the question says "operating at λ = 0.9," you MUST multiply the stoichiometric AFR by 0.9. This is the most common step-skip in KTU scripts.
> 6. **Not balancing equations before computing.** A typical 7-mark calculation question has 2 marks reserved for the balanced equation. Always write it first, even if it seems "obvious."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Module 2:**

- ✅ **Aldehydes (R–CHO):** Terminal carbonyl, e.g., HCHO, CH₃CHO. Formed in **incomplete combustion**, **cold start**, and **alcohol-fueled engines**. Carcinogenic; contribute to photochemical smog.
- ✅ **Ketones (R–CO–R′):** Internal carbonyl, e.g., acetone. Form via **cool flame chemistry** at 600–800 K. Less toxic than aldehydes but still regulated in workplace exposure.
- ✅ **Alcohols (R–OH):** Methanol and ethanol are the only commercially relevant automotive alcohols. **Methanol** (CH₃OH) has 1 carbon, 4 H, 1 O. **Ethanol** (C₂H₅OH) has 2 C, 6 H, 1 O. Used as **oxygenated fuels** to reduce CO and PM.
- ✅ **Organic Acids (R–COOH):** Acetic acid is the predominant species. Forms via **further oxidation of aldehydes**. Highly **corrosive** to exhaust components. Cold-start condensation is the main damage mechanism.
- ✅ **Key Molecular Weights (memorize):** CH₃OH = 32, C₂H₅OH = 46, HCHO = 30, CH₃CHO = 44, CH₃COOH = 60, CO₂ = 44, O₂ = 32, N₂ = 28.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric AFRs:** Methanol = 6.43, Ethanol = 9.0, Gasoline = 15.13.
- ✅ **LHVs (memorize):** Methanol = 19.9 MJ/kg, Ethanol = 26.9 MJ/kg, Gasoline = 44 MJ/kg.
- ✅ **CO₂ reduction (well-known result):** Methanol → ~55% less CO₂/kg; Ethanol → ~38% less CO₂/kg compared to gasoline.
- ✅ **Oxidation sequence (board-favorite):** Alcohol → Aldehyde → Organic Acid → CO → CO₂. Write this left to right; it is a guaranteed 2–3 mark question.
- ✅ **Cold-start aldehyde spike:** The four-step chain **cold → poor atomization → rich zones → radical termination → aldehyde formation** is a common 5-mark question.
- ✅ **Control technologies:** **Three-way catalyst (TWC)** for CO/HC/NOₓ; **close-coupled catalyst** for faster light-off; **acid-resistant exhaust materials** (SS304/SS409); **optimized cold-start injection**.
- ✅ **Photochemical reactivity order (descending):** HCHO > C₂H₄ > CO > CH₄. Aldehydes are **more reactive than CO** in smog formation — important for KTU essay questions.
- ✅ **Engine trade-off formula:** Alcohols give **lower regulated emissions but higher unregulated emissions**. State this trade-off explicitly in any 14-mark answer.

---

*End of Module 2 Notes — Aldehydes, Ketones, Alcohols and Organic Acids | PEMUT522 — Automotive Pollution and Control | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Influencing Formation of Pollutants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Influencing Formation of Pollutants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In an internal combustion (IC) engine, the **formation of pollutants is governed by the thermodynamic state, chemical kinetics, and fluid-mechanical conditions prevailing inside the combustion chamber during the finite duration of combustion.** The principal regulated pollutants — **Carbon Monoxide (CO), Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC), Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx), and Particulate Matter (PM)** — are generated as direct or secondary consequences of the non-ideal combustion of hydrocarbon fuels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> The formation of pollutants is **not a single phenomenon** but the cumulative result of multiple engine design and operating parameters such as air–fuel ratio, ignition/spark timing, combustion chamber geometry, engine speed, engine load, fuel properties, peak cycle temperature, residual gases, and the prevailing in-cylinder turbulence.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Campfire & The Wind"

Imagine trying to cook food on a **wood-fired chulha (campfire)**:

| Scenario | What Happens | Engine Equivalent |
|----------|--------------|-------------------|
| **Too much wood, no air** | Smoky, sooty flame, CO-rich yellow flame | **Rich mixture** (λ < 1) → ↑ CO, ↑ HC, ↑ Smoke |
| **Just the right wood and air** | Clean, blue, hot flame | **Stoichiometric** (λ = 1) → ↑ NOx, ↓ CO |
| **Too much air, very lean** | Flame lifts off, incomplete burn near edges | **Lean mixture** (λ > 1) → ↑ HC (misfire), ↓ CO |
| **Burning too fast / hot** | Pan gets black from soot, but flame is hot | **High load / high EGR** → ↑ NOx, ↑ PM |
| **Slow smouldering** | Thick white smoke | **Cold start / poor atomization** → ↑ HC, ↑ CO |

> [!NOTE]
> The "campfire" analogy clearly conveys the central theme: **engine-out emissions are a function of the local fuel-air chemistry, peak temperature, and time available for the reaction.** The same physics governs pollutant formation in SI and CI engines.

### 1.3 The Four Key Pollutants (KTU-Anchored Inventory)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CO} &= \text{Incomplete oxidation of fuel carbon (rich mixtures, low T)} \\
\text{HC} &= \text{Unburnt / partially cracked fuel (crevices, walls, quenching)} \\
\text{NOx} &= \text{High-temperature oxidation of N}_2\text{ (Zeldovich mechanism)} \\
\text{PM} &= \text{Soot from rich zones + adsorbed HC (mainly CI engines)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU board examiner expects students to be able to map each **factor** (cause) to a specific **pollutant** (effect) and the corresponding **operating condition** (regime). Mastery of this 3-D mapping is the key to high scores in Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Master Equation — Equivalence Ratio (φ) and Lambda (λ)

The single most important parameter governing pollutant formation is the **normalized air–fuel ratio**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi \;(\text{Equivalence Ratio}) &= \dfrac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}} = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda \;(\text{Lambda, Excess Air}) &= \dfrac{1}{\phi} = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **λ = 1** → Stoichiometric (chemically correct air for complete combustion)
- **λ < 1 (φ > 1)** → **Rich** (excess fuel, deficit of O₂)
- **λ > 1 (φ < 1)** → **Lean** (excess air)

### 2.2 Stoichiometric Combustion of a Generic Hydrocarbon

For a fuel **CₓHᵧO_z N_w S_v** burning in air (O₂ + 3.76 N₂):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z\text{N}_w\text{S}_v \;+\; a\,(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2) \;\longrightarrow\; x\,\text{CO}_2 \;+\; \tfrac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \;+\; \tfrac{v}{2}\,\text{SO}_2 \;+\; \left(\tfrac{w}{2} + 3.76\,a\right)\text{N}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Oxygen atom balance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a = x + \dfrac{y}{4} + v - \dfrac{z}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Stoichiometric A/F by mass (using molecular weights M_fuel, M_air = 28.97 kg/kmol, M_N₂/M_O₂ ≈ 3.76):

$$
\begin{aligned}
(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{a \times 4.76 \times 28.97}{M_{\text{fuel}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3 Master Formula Cheat-Sheet (KTU Board Examiner's Favourite)

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Typical SI / CI Value | Units |
|---|-------------------|----------------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Excess air ratio | 0.8 – 1.6 | – |
| 2 | $\phi = 1/\lambda$ | Equivalence ratio | 0.6 – 1.4 | – |
| 3 | $(A/F)_{\text{stoich,gasoline}}$ | Stoichiometric A/F for gasoline | **14.7 : 1** | kg/kg |
| 4 | $(A/F)_{\text{stoich,diesel}}$ | Stoichiometric A/F for diesel | **14.5 : 1** | kg/kg |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{comb}} = 1 - \dfrac{[CO] + [HC]_{\text{m}}}{[CO_2]_{\text{max}}}$ | Combustion efficiency | 95 – 99 % | % |
| 6 | $\dfrac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} \;\propto\; \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT_{\text{peak}}}\right) [\text{O}_2]^{0.5}[\text{N}_2]$ | Zeldovich NOx rate | – | mol/(L·s) |
| 7 | $E_a(\text{NOx, thermal}) \approx \textbf{319 kJ/mol}$ | Activation energy (Zeldovich) | **319** | kJ/mol |
| 8 | $\dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{HC,crevice}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}}} \;\propto\; \dfrac{V_{\text{crevice}}}{V_{\text{cyl}}}$ | Crevice HC fraction | 1 – 5 % | – |
| 9 | $T_{\text{peak,isentropic}} \approx T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$ | Peak T (isochoric, no losses) | 2200 – 2800 K | K |
| 10 | $\text{NO}_{x,\text{adiabatic}} \propto T_{\text{peak}}^{0.5}\cdot\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{319{,}000}{8.314\,T_{\text{peak}}}\right)$ | Extended Zeldovich correlation | – | ppm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoid the `|` symbol in tables**: All absolute-value and conditional notations have been replaced with `\vert` / `\mid` to comply with markdown-table syntax. Eg., $A/F \;\vert_{\text{stoich}}$ instead of $A/F \;|\;_{\text{stoich}}$.

## 2.4 The "10-Factor Matrix" — Engine Variables → Pollutant Map

This is the **single most high-yield table for KTU ESE** on this topic. Examiners routinely ask for factor-by-pollutant mapping.

| **Factor** | **CO** | **HC** | **NOx** | **PM (Smoke)** |
|------------|:------:|:------:|:-------:|:--------------:|
| **Richer A/F (λ ↓)** | ↑↑↑ | ↑ | ↓ (slight) | ↑↑↑ (CI) |
| **Leaner A/F (λ ↑)** | ↓↓↓ | ↑ (misfire) | ↓ (at very lean) | ↓ |
| **Higher EGR rate** | ↑ | ↑ | ↓↓↓ | ↑ (PM) |
| **Higher peak temperature** | ↓ | – | ↑↑↑ | – |
| **Higher peak pressure** | – | – | ↑ | – |
| **Retarded spark (SI)** | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | n/a |
| **Advanced spark (SI)** | – | – | ↑ | n/a |
| **Higher engine load** | ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑↑ | ↑↑ |
| **Higher engine speed** | ↓ (time ↓) | ↑ (crevice) | ↑ | ↓ (SI), ↑ (CI) |
| **Wall quenching** | – | ↑↑ | – | – |
| **Higher compression ratio** | – | – | ↑ | ↑ |
| **Higher fuel aromaticity** | ↑ | ↑ | – | ↑↑ |
| **Higher fuel sulfur** | – | – | – | ↑↑↑ (sulfate PM) |
| **Greater valve overlap** | – | ↑ (blow-by HC) | – | – |
| **Deteriorated spark plug / injector** | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | ↓ (misfire) | ↑↑ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Cue:** When answering "Discuss the factors affecting X emissions", the **standard KTU 2024 marking scheme** expects you to mention at least 6 factors, link them to combustion chemistry, and (where possible) sketch the trend (↑/↓). A tabular answer with explanation easily secures full 7 marks per sub-part.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of the φ–λ Framework

The φ–λ framework is the **universal control handle** for engine calibration. It is used in:
- **Three-way catalytic converter (TWC) design** (operates only in λ = 1 ± 0.01 window).
- **Closed-loop lambda sensors** (zirconia / titania) for SI engines.
- **Diesel particulate filter (DPF)** control strategies (operate on lean side).
- **EGR rate modulation** (EGR effectively "replaces" air with inert gas, shifting local φ rich without globally leaning).
- **Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI)** stratified charge strategy (locally stoichiometric, globally lean).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric A/F for Iso-Octane (C₈H₁₈)

This derivation frequently appears as a 3-mark short problem in KTU Module 2.

**Step 1:** Write balanced chemical equation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} \;+\; a\,(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2) \;\longrightarrow\; 8\,\text{CO}_2 \;+\; 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \;+\; 3.76\,a\,\text{N}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply **Carbon balance** (8 C atoms on each side — satisfied by 8 CO₂).

**Step 3:** Apply **Hydrogen balance** — 18 H atoms on LHS = 2 × 9 = 18 H atoms on RHS (9 H₂O) ✓.

**Step 4:** Apply **Oxygen balance** — 2a atoms on LHS = 2(8) + 9 = 25 atoms on RHS.

$$
\begin{aligned}
2a = 25 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a = 12.5\;\text{kmol O}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Air supplied = $a \times (1 + 3.76) = 12.5 \times 4.76 = 59.5$ kmol of air.

**Step 6:** Mass of air = $59.5 \times 28.97 = 1723.7$ kg.
Mass of fuel = $(8 \times 12) + (18 \times 1) = 114$ kg.

**Step 7:** Stoichiometric A/F ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(A/F)_{\text{stoich,C8H18}} = \dfrac{1723.7}{114} = 15.12
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The accepted engineering value for **gasoline (not pure iso-octane) is 14.7** because commercial gasoline is a hydrocarbon mixture with some lighter fractions (C₇ and below) and some oxygen-bearing species. The KTU-accepted stoichiometric A/F for gasoline is therefore **14.7 : 1** and for diesel is **14.5 : 1**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Zeldovich NO Formation Mechanism

The KTU Module 2 syllabus explicitly lists the Zeldovich mechanism. The derivation below is the **canonical KTU board answer**.

**Step 1 — Reactions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O} + \text{N}_2 \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{NO} + \text{N} \quad (R_1) \\
\text{N} + \text{O}_2 \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{NO} + \text{O} \quad (R_2) \\
\text{N} + \text{OH} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{NO} + \text{H} \quad (R_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Rate-determining step** is R₁ (N₂ dissociation), with:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{1,f} = 1.8 \times 10^8 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{319{,}000}{RT}\right) \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/mol·s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state [N]** assumption (d[N]/dt = 0):

$$
\begin{aligned}
[\text{N}] = \dfrac{k_{1,f}\,[\text{O}][\text{N}_2]}{k_{1,b}\,[\text{NO}] + k_{2,f}\,[\text{O}_2] + k_{3,f}\,[\text{OH}]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Net rate of NO formation** (using pseudo-steady-state for N):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} = 2\,k_{1,f}\,[\text{O}][\text{N}_2]\;\dfrac{1 - (k_{1,b}[\text{NO}]^2)/(k_{1,f}[\text{O}][\text{N}_2][\text{O}_2])}{1 + (k_{1,b}[\text{NO}])/(k_{2,f}[\text{O}_2] + k_{3,f}[\text{OH}])}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Empirical shortcut used in engine calculations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
[\text{NO}_x] \;\approx\; \dfrac{k_1\,[\text{N}_2][\text{O}_2]^{0.5}}{k_2}\;\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{319{,}000}{RT}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Arrhenius-type** dependency that makes NOx so explosively sensitive to peak temperature: a 100 K rise in T_peak can **double or triple** NOx.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wall Quenching and Crevice Volume HC Mechanism

**Step 1 — Flame front thickness** δ ≈ 0.5 – 1.0 mm at stoichiometric.

**Step 2 — Quenching distance** (when δ > distance to wall, the flame cannot propagate):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_q \;\approx\; 1.1\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{S_u}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where α = thermal diffusivity, S_u = laminar burning velocity.

**Step 3 — HC mass trapped in crevice** (annulus between piston, head, gasket, and rings):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{HC,crevice}} = \rho_u \cdot V_{\text{crevice}} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{T_q}{T_u}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total HC from multiple sources:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{HC,total}} = m_{\text{wall}} + m_{\text{crevice}} + m_{\text{oil}} + m_{\text{deposit}} + m_{\text{misfire}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Typical percentage contribution in a port-injection SI engine:

| Source | % of total HC |
|--------|:-------------:|
| Crevice volume | 35 – 40 % |
| Wall quenching | 25 – 35 % |
| Oil layer absorption | 15 – 20 % |
| Deposits | 5 – 10 % |
| Misfire / partial-burn | 5 – 10 % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **one of the most frequently asked 7-mark questions in KTU past papers** ("Explain HC formation mechanism with neat diagram"). Memorize the **percentages of HC source contributions** and the **flame-quenching condition**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — CO Formation from Equilibrium Chemistry

**Step 1 — Global reaction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(water–gas shift)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium constant (depends only on T):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_p(T) = \dfrac{p_{\text{CO}_2}\,p_{\text{H}_2}}{p_{\text{CO}}\,p_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Implication for engines:**
- During **expansion and exhaust**, in-cylinder temperature drops within ~10 ms.
- The CO ⇌ CO₂ reaction is **"frozen"** (quenched) before equilibrium can re-establish with the lower temperature.
- Therefore **CO emission is essentially controlled by the time-temperature history in the first ~2 ms after TDC** when the flame is at peak temperature.
- High local **φ** (rich pockets) and low local T (during expansion) → **CO is locked in**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Writing "CO is formed because of insufficient air in the cylinder" — this is **only half-correct**. The full statement must include: "…and because the CO→CO₂ reaction freezes during expansion before reaching equilibrium at lower exhaust temperatures." A 1-mark deduction is applied if this nuance is missing.

## 3.5 PM (Soot) Formation in CI Engines

PM formation follows a **4-step sequence**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Pyrolysis:} \quad \text{Fuel} &\;\xrightarrow{\Delta}\; \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 \;+\; \text{H}_2 \\
\text{(ii) Nucleation:} \quad \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 &\;\rightarrow\; \text{Soot nuclei} \;(2\text{–}5\,\text{nm}) \\
\text{(iii) Surface growth:} \quad \text{Nuclei} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 &\;\rightarrow\; \text{Soot particles} \;(20\text{–}30\,\text{nm}) \\
\text{(iv) Aggregation:} \quad \text{Particles} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{Chain aggregates} \;(> 1\,\mu\text{m})
\end{aligned}
$$

PM is **promoted by**:
- Local rich zones (φ > 2 in the spray core)
- High cylinder pressure (favors soot surface growth)
- Low EGR
- High aromatic content of fuel
- Long ignition delay (more time for pyrolysis)
- **Reduced by** EGR, higher swirl, retarded injection, and oxygenated fuels (e.g., biodiesel, methanol blends).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Hierarchical Factors Influencing Pollutant Formation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FACTORS INFLUENCING POLLUTANT FORMATION] --> B[FUEL PROPERTIES]
    A --> C[ENGINE DESIGN PARAMETERS]
    A --> D[OPERATING CONDITIONS]
    A --> E[CONTROL & AFTERTREATMENT]

    B --> B1[Octane Cetane Number]
    B --> B2[Aromaticity and Sulfur]
    B --> B3[Volatility and Viscosity]
    B --> B4[Oxygenated Fuels]

    C --> C1[Combustion Chamber Geometry S V Ratio]
    C --> C2[Compression Ratio]
    C --> C3[Valve Timing Overlap]
    C --> C4[Injection System Type]
    C --> C5[Spark Plug Position]

    D --> D1[Air Fuel Ratio Lambda]
    D --> D2[Engine Speed RPM]
    D --> D3[Engine Load BMEP]
    D --> D4[Spark or Injection Timing]
    D --> D5[Coolant and Oil Temperature]

    E --> E1[EGR Rate]
    E --> E2[Turbo Boost Pressure]
    E --> E3[Inlet Air Humidity]

    B1 --> P1[POLLUTANTS]
    C1 --> P1
    D1 --> P1
    E1 --> P1
    B2 --> P1
    D3 --> P1

    P1 --> P1A[CO Carbon Monoxide]
    P1 --> P1B[HC Unburnt Hydrocarbons]
    P1 --> P1C[NOx Oxides of Nitrogen]
    P1 --> P1D[PM Particulate Matter]
    P1 --> P1E[CO2 and SO2]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (`B1`, `C1`, `D1`, `P1A`, etc.) and prefixed with letters. No reserved keywords are used as standalone node names. All labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text without bold/italic markdown.

## 4.2 Air–Fuel Ratio vs Emissions (SI Engine) — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AF[Air Fuel Ratio Lambda] --> STO[Stoichiometric Lambda = 1]
    AF --> RICHR[Rich Lambda less than 0.9]
    AF --> LEANR[Lean Lambda greater than 1.1]

    RICHR --> CO1[CO RISES sharply]
    RICHR --> HC1[HC rises]
    RICHR --> PM1[Particulate rises]

    STO --> NOX1[NOx PEAK maximum]
    STO --> CO2M[CO2 maximum and best fuel economy]

    LEANR --> CO2[CO falls to near zero]
    LEANR --> NOXR[NOx falls after lean limit]
    LEANR --> HCM[HC rises again due to misfire and slow burn]
```

## 4.3 SI vs CI Engine Pollutant-Formation Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SIENG[SI ENGINE Spark Ignition] --> SIMIX[Homogeneous Premixed Charge]
    SIMIX --> SICOM[Combustion Flame propagation]
    SICOM --> SIPOL[Pollutants: HC from wall and crevice, CO from rich zones, NOx from peak T]

    CIENG[CI ENGINE Compression Ignition] --> CIMIX[In Cylinder Heterogeneous Mixing]
    CIMIX --> CIDIFF[Diffusion Controlled Combustion]
    CIDIFF --> CIPOL[Pollutants: PM soot from rich spray core, NOx from high T, CO and HC from over lean zones]
```

## 4.4 In-Cylinder Pollutant Formation Zones (Cutaway Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CYL[Combustion Chamber] --> Z1[ZONE 1 Flame Front]
    CYL --> Z2[ZONE 2 Near Wall]
    CYL --> Z3[ZONE 3 Crevice Volume]
    CYL --> Z4[ZONE 4 Bulk Gases]

    Z1 --> Z1R[Reaction Zone: peak T = 2200 to 2800 K, NOx forms here]
    Z2 --> Z2R[Wall Quenching: T falls below 1000 K, HC trapped]
    Z3 --> Z3R[Crevice: flame cannot enter, HC fuel remains]
    Z4 --> Z4R[Post flame gases: CO freezes, CO2 stable]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This block-level architecture is a **physical schematic** (Mermaid-equivalent), since the topic involves spatial 3-D in-cylinder phenomena that cannot be rendered by Mermaid primitives. Each zone corresponds to a distinct pollutant-formation regime that the examiner expects you to label and explain in 7-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (PEMUT522 Model Paper)]
**"Define equivalence ratio (φ) and explain how it controls the formation of CO and NOx in an SI engine."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Equivalence ratio $\phi = (F/A)_{\text{actual}} \mid (F/A)_{\text{stoich}}$. It is the reciprocal of the excess-air ratio λ.
- **CO behaviour (1 mark):** As φ increases (richer mixture), CO rises sharply because of insufficient O₂ to complete the CO→CO₂ oxidation step. CO is also "frozen" in the expanding gas as temperature drops rapidly.
- **NOx behaviour (1 mark):** NOx peaks at slightly lean-to-stoichiometric mixtures (φ ≈ 0.8 – 1.0) where peak flame temperature is maximum. At very lean (φ < 0.7) or very rich (φ > 1.2), NOx falls sharply because peak temperature is suppressed.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (PEMUT522 Model Paper)]
**"What is crevice volume and how does it contribute to HC emissions in SI engines?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Crevice volume is the small enclosed volume between the piston crown, cylinder head, head gasket, and piston ring grooves — typically 1–3 % of clearance volume.
- **Mechanism (1 mark):** When the flame front arrives, the unburnt mixture is forced into the crevice where the flame cannot propagate (quenching). This fuel is expelled back into the cylinder late in expansion and exhausts as **unburnt HC**.
- **Quantitative contribution (1 mark):** Crevice volume accounts for ~35–40 % of total HC emissions in a port-injection SI engine — the single largest contributor.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — Option 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain the various factors affecting the formation of NOx in SI engines with a neat sketch of NOx vs equivalence ratio. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition of NOx and Zeldovich mechanism (2 marks):**
   NOx is mainly **NO** (with ~5 % NO₂) formed by **thermal (Zeldovich) NO** when N₂ and O₂ react at T > 1800 K:
   - O + N₂ ⇌ NO + N
   - N + O₂ ⇌ NO + O
   - Rate ∝ exp(−319 000 / RT) [O₂]^0.5 [N₂]

2. **Factors affecting NOx (4 marks):**
   - **Peak flame temperature:** NOx doubles for every ~75 K rise in T_peak. Peak T_peak occurs near φ ≈ 0.8 – 1.0.
   - **Equivalence ratio:** Maximum at φ ≈ 0.8 (slightly lean); falls off on both rich and lean sides.
   - **Residence time at high T:** Longer time above 1800 K → more NOx. Higher compression ratio and retarded spark increase NOx.
   - **EGR / residual gases:** Inert gas dilutes charge → lowers peak T → ↓ NOx dramatically.
   - **Load and speed:** Higher load → higher T_peak → ↑ NOx. Higher speed → less time at high T → ↓ NOx slightly.

3. **Neat sketch (1 mark):** NO ppm on y-axis; φ (or λ) on x-axis. Curve rises from φ=0.6, peaks at φ≈0.8, falls to φ>1.2.

**(b) Discuss the formation of CO and HC in IC engines and explain how they are affected by air-fuel ratio. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **CO Formation (3 marks):**
   - Global reaction: CO + ½O₂ ⇌ CO₂, but with finite rate.
   - **Rich mixtures (φ > 1):** Insufficient O₂ → CO rises sharply with richness.
   - **Lean mixtures (φ < 1):** Excess O₂ → CO approaches zero.
   - During expansion, the CO–H₂O water-gas shift reaction is **frozen** (kinetically limited at low T), locking in CO.

2. **HC Formation (3 marks):**
   - **Sources:** Wall quenching, crevice volume, oil absorption, deposits, misfire, and partial-burn.
   - **Flame quenching condition:** When flame thickness δ > distance to wall, the flame is extinguished and unburnt fuel remains.
   - **Effect of A/F:** HC is high both in very rich and very lean conditions. There is a U-shaped curve with minimum HC at slightly rich-to-stoichiometric (φ ≈ 0.9 – 1.0). At very lean (λ > 1.3), HC rises sharply due to misfire and slow flame propagation.

3. **Engineering relevance (1 mark):**
   - Three-way catalytic converters need **λ = 1 ± 0.01** to simultaneously oxidize CO and HC and reduce NOx.
   - GDI stratified operation locally stoich at spark plug, globally lean → reduces CO and HC in stratified mode.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common 1-mark deductions on this question:**
> 1. **Drawing the U-shaped HC curve without explaining the rising lean-side limb** (misfire reason). Students often sketch the curve and assume the examiner will infer it.
> 2. **Confusing λ and φ**: writing "CO is maximum at λ = 1" instead of "CO is maximum at φ > 1 (rich)".
> 3. **Omitting the "freezing" of CO during expansion** — losing 1 mark on the kinetics-vs-equilibrium distinction.

---

### QUESTION B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (PEMUT522 Model Paper)]

**(a) Compare the factors affecting pollutant formation in SI and CI engines in a tabular form with suitable explanations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Tabular comparison (5 marks):**

| Factor | SI Engine | CI Engine |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| **Mixture preparation** | Premixed (homogeneous) | Heterogeneous (diffusion-controlled) |
| **Dominant combustion mode** | Flame propagation | Spray auto-ignition |
| **A/F ratio operation** | Stoichiometric (λ = 1) | Always lean globally (λ = 1.3 – 2.5) |
| **CO formation** | High at rich part-throttle | Low (because of lean operation) |
| **HC formation** | Wall, crevice, oil; U-shaped vs A/F | Over-mixing, under-mixing, wall-quench on bowl walls |
| **NOx formation** | Thermal (Zeldovich) controlled by peak T | Same Zeldovich + "prompt NO" possible at high load |
| **PM (soot)** | Negligible (premixed) | High in rich spray core (φ > 2 locally) |
| **Main controlling parameter** | Spark timing, EGR | Injection timing, injection pressure, EGR, swirl |
| **Typical emission levels (g/km)** | CO: 1 – 2, HC: 0.1, NOx: 0.15, PM: 0 | CO: 0.5, HC: 0.1, NOx: 0.5 – 0.8, PM: 0.05 |

**Discussion of key contrasts (2 marks):**
- SI engines produce more **CO and HC** due to crevice/wall effects; CI engines produce more **PM and NOx** due to spray heterogeneity and high in-cylinder T.
- SI control = spark timing + 3-way catalyst; CI control = EGR + DPF + SCR + injection pressure.

**(b) Explain the Zeldovich mechanism for NOx formation and show, with a derivation, why NOx is exponentially sensitive to peak flame temperature. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Zeldovich reactions (2 marks):** O + N₂ ⇌ NO + N; N + O₂ ⇌ NO + O (and extended: N + OH ⇌ NO + H). The first step (N₂ dissociation) is rate-limiting.

2. **Activation energy E_a = 319 kJ/mol (1 mark):** Disclosed as a "high" E_a reaction.

3. **Rate expression (2 marks):**
   Rate ∝ [O][N₂] exp(−E_a / RT)

4. **Derivation of T-sensitivity (2 marks):**
   Taking the logarithmic derivative:
   ln(Rate) ∝ −E_a / RT = −319 000 / (8.314 × T)
   Differentiating w.r.t. T: d[ln(Rate)] / dT = +E_a / (RT²) = 319 000 / (8.314 × T²)
   For T = 2000 K: d[ln(Rate)] / dT ≈ 9.6 per 1000 K → 96 % increase per 100 K.
   **Hence a 100 K rise in peak T nearly doubles the NOx rate — the classic "exponential sensitivity" result.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common errors on Zeldovich questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention that Zeldovich NO is "thermal" NO** and not "prompt" or "fuel-bound" NO — examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Writing the rate expression as proportional to T alone**, not to exp(−E_a / RT). This is a serious conceptual error.
> 3. **Not stating that the rate-limiting step is N₂ dissociation** — losing 1 mark on the kinetics.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Equivalence ratio φ = (F/A)_actual / (F/A)_stoich**; λ = 1/φ. Stoichiometric A/F for gasoline = **14.7**, for diesel = **14.5**.
- [x] **CO is rich-mixture driven**; locked in by chemical "freezing" during expansion.
- [x] **HC has 5 sources** — wall quenching, crevice volume, oil absorption, deposits, misfire; U-shaped vs A/F.
- [x] **NOx is thermal (Zeldovich) NO**; rate ∝ exp(−319 000 / RT); peaks at φ ≈ 0.8.
- [x] **PM (soot) forms in CI** through 4 steps: pyrolysis → nucleation → surface growth → aggregation.
- [x] **S/V ratio (surface-to-volume)** of combustion chamber inversely affects HC (more wall → more HC).
- [x] **Retarded spark** → ↓ NOx but ↑ HC and CO; **advanced spark** → opposite.
- [x] **EGR** → ↓ NOx (by lowering peak T) but ↑ PM and HC.
- [x] **Higher aromaticity / sulfur in fuel** → ↑ PM and CO; **oxygenated fuels** → ↓ CO and PM.
- [x] **Higher load** → ↑ T_peak → ↑ NOx and PM; **higher speed** → ↓ time at high T → ↓ NOx.
- [x] **Valve overlap** → ↑ HC blow-by loss to exhaust.
- [x] **Knocking / abnormal combustion** → ↑ HC, ↑ NOx, possible engine damage.
- [x] **Cold start** → ↑ CO and HC (catalyst not yet at light-off T).
- [x] **High humidity in intake air** → ↓ NOx (steam dilution effect on peak T).
- [x] For **3-way catalyst** operation, λ must be held at **1.00 ± 0.01** — a 1 % error in λ can cause 50 % loss in conversion efficiency.

> [!TIP]
> **Final exam tip:** In any 7-mark "factors affecting" question, always conclude with **one quantitative statement** (e.g., "NOx doubles for every ~75 K rise in peak flame temperature" or "Crevice volume contributes ~35 – 40 % of total HC"). This single sentence can be the difference between a 6 and a 7.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Quality - Impact of Fuel Quality, Fuel Additives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuel Quality - Impact of Fuel Quality, Fuel Additives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fuel Quality — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Quality** is the aggregate measure of those physical, chemical, and combustion properties of a transportation fuel (gasoline, diesel, ethanol, CNG, LPG) that determine its **combustion efficiency, exhaust emission profile, engine durability, and storage stability**. In KTU 2024 terminology, it is quantified through standardized indices such as **Octane Number (ON)**, **Cetane Number (CN)**, **Reid Vapor Pressure (RVP)**, **Sulfur content (ppm by mass)**, **Aromatic content (% volume)**, **Calorific Value (MJ/kg)**, **Flash Point (°C)**, **Viscosity (cSt)**, and **Lubricity (HFRR wear scar, µm)**.

A fuel with "good quality" is one that:
- Ignites smoothly at the intended compression ratio (no knock in SI, no delay in CI)
- Burns almost completely (low CO, HC, PM)
- Contains minimal heteroatoms (S, N, metals) that form regulated pollutants
- Resists oxidation during storage (no gum, no color change)
- Lubricates the injection system adequately
- Is compatible with after-treatment devices (DPF, SCR, TWC, GPF)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe of a Cooked Dish"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of an engine cylinder as a small kitchen stove. The fuel is the dish being cooked. If you use stale, watery ingredients (poor quality fuel), the dish will smoke (PM), smell bad (HC, CO), and take longer to cook (poor combustion). If the ingredients are fresh, well-mixed, and have the right amount of moisture and spices (good quality fuel + additives), the dish cooks cleanly, gives a balanced flavor (low emissions), and leaves minimal residue on the pan (low deposits). **Fuel additives are exactly like the kitchen spices** — small quantities that drastically improve how the "dish" (combustion) turns out.

### 1.3 Key Quality Metrics at a Glance

| Property | Engine Type | Why It Matters | Typical Range |
|----------|-------------|----------------|---------------|
| **Octane Number (RON/MON)** | Spark Ignition (SI) | Resists knock | 87–98 RON |
| **Cetane Number (CN)** | Compression Ignition (CI) | Reduces ignition delay | 45–60 |
| **Sulfur content** | Both | Cuts SO₂ & PM | < 10–50 ppm |
| **Aromatics** | Both | Reduces PAH & soot | < 35 % v/v |
| **RVP (Reid Vapour Pressure)** | SI | Controls evaporative HC | 45–90 kPa |
| **Calorific Value** | Both | Energy density | 42–45 MJ/kg (diesel) |
| **Lubricity (HFRR)** | CI | Protects injectors | < 460 µm wear scar |
| **Flash Point** | CI | Safety, storage | > 55 °C (diesel) |
| **Density** | CI | Energy per stroke | 820–860 kg/m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI) fuel specifications (equivalent to Euro VI on the emission side) are the benchmark for this module. BS-VI gasoline mandates **RON 91 (regular) / 95 (premium)**, sulfur **< 10 ppm**, and aromatics **< 35 %**. BS-VI diesel mandates **CN ≥ 51**, sulfur **< 10 ppm**, poly-aromatics **< 7 %**, and **FAME 7 %** blending.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Fuel Quality Directly Governs Emissions

The combustion event in a cylinder is governed by three competing processes:
1. **Fuel-air mixing** (governed by volatility, viscosity, density)
2. **Auto-ignition chemistry** (governed by ON/CN)
3. **Burnout of carbonaceous matter** (governed by aromatics, sulfur, oxygenates)

Whenever any of these processes is *imperfect*, unburned or partially-burnt products escape as pollutants.

#### 2.1.1 Effect on Spark-Ignition (Petrol) Engines

- **Low Octane Number → Knocking combustion**
  - Auto-ignition of end-gas produces sharp pressure oscillations
  - Local temperatures exceed 2000 °C, generating **NOx spikes**
  - Mechanical stress damages piston, rings, spark plug
  - **Net effect:** Increased HC, CO, NOx; reduced fuel economy

- **High Sulfur Content**
  - Forms SO₂ → SO₃ → H₂SO₄ aerosols (acid rain)
  - "Poisons" the Three-Way Catalyst (TWC) — chemisorbed sulfur blocks the active sites
  - Catalyst light-off temperature rises by 30–60 °C
  - **Result:** Cold-start CO and HC emissions rise sharply

- **High Aromatic Content (BTEX — Benzene, Toluene, Ethyl-benzene, Xylene)**
  - High energy density but slow flame, high C/H ratio
  - Produces **Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAH)** — precursors of soot
  - Benzene itself is a class-1 carcinogen
  - **Lamp-black soot** deposits on spark plug, lambda sensor

- **High RVP (Reid Vapour Pressure)**
  - Too volatile → evaporative HC losses from tank, hot-soak losses from injectors
  - Contributes to **photochemical smog** (ground-level ozone)
  - Low RVP → cold-start difficulties, poor atomization

- **High Olefins**
  - Reactive double bonds → gum formation in tank, intake manifold
  - Increases NMOG (Non-Methane Organic Gases)

#### 2.1.2 Effect on Compression-Ignition (Diesel) Engines

- **Low Cetane Number → Long ignition delay**
  - Fuel accumulates in cylinder, then auto-ignites explosively (diesel knock)
  - Sudden pressure rise → **higher PM, noise, NOx**
  - Higher HC and white-smoke (unburnt fuel) emissions at start

- **High Sulfur Content**
  - Forms SO₂ → **sulfate particulate** (sulfuric acid mist)
  - Sulfate fraction of PM is significant (especially at high load)
  - In modern engines, even 10 ppm sulfur can degrade **DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter)** regeneration
  - Poisons the **SCR catalyst** (vanadia or Cu-zeolite) reducing NOx conversion by 20–40 %

- **High Poly-Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAH)**
  - Direct **soot precursors** — ring-structured carbon is hard to oxidize
  - Direct correlation: % PAH vs. **smoke number** and **BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption)**

- **Low Lubricity (after deep hydro-desulfurization)**
  - Wear of fuel injection pump, injectors
  - Increased leakage → power loss, higher HC

- **High FAME (Biodiesel) content (> 7 %)**
  - Solvent action cleans old deposits → may release them to filter
  - Higher NOx by 2–5 % due to oxygen content and cetane shift
  - Lower PM (because of in-cylinder oxygen)

- **High Density & Viscosity**
  - Poor atomization → larger droplet Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD)
  - Local fuel-rich zones → **soot formation**

#### 2.1.3 Effect on After-Treatment Devices

| After-Treatment Device | Sensitive to | Why |
|------------------------|-------------|-----|
| **TWC (Three-Way Catalyst)** | Sulfur, Pb, P | Permanent chemisorption poisons |
| **DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter)** | S, ash, lube-oil Zn, P | Clogs pores, prevents regeneration |
| **DOC (Diesel Oxidation Catalyst)** | S, P | Forms sulfates instead of oxidizing NO |
| **SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction)** | S, alkali metals | Kills active sites |
| **LNT/NOx Trap (Lean NOx Trap)** | S (most sensitive) | Requires de-sulfation cycle (~ 600 °C) |
| **GPF (Gasoline Particulate Filter)** | S, lube-oil ash | Direct particulate loading |

### 2.2 The Ten-Second Reasoning Chain for Emission Reduction

```
Fuel Property → Combustion Quality → Engine-out Emissions → After-Treatment Performance → Tailpipe Emissions
     (S)              (poor)               (high)                (deactivated)             (high)
     (CN)             (rough)              (high)                   (N/A)                  (high)
   (PAH)              (soot)               (high)                  (clog)                  (high)
```

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following relations are the most frequently tested in KTU University Exams. All quantities are in **SI units** unless explicitly noted.

#### 2.3.1 Air-Fuel Ratio & Equivalence Ratio

$$
\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoichiometric}}}
$$

$$
\phi < 1 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{lean mixture}
$$

$$
\phi > 1 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{rich mixture}
$$

For diesel: $\phi$ is always $< 1$ (excess air, $\lambda > 1$).

#### 2.3.2 Octane Number Relationship (Research vs. Motor)

$$
\text{ON} = \frac{\text{RON} + \text{MON}}{2}
$$

Anti-Knock Index (AKI) is what is shown on fuel pumps in India.

#### 2.3.3 Cetane Index (Empirical from Density & Distillation)

$$
\text{CI} = 454.74 - 1641.416\,D + 774.74\,D^{2} - 0.554\,T_{50} + 97.803\,(\log T_{50})^{2}
$$

where $D$ = density at 15 °C (g/mL) and $T_{50}$ = 50 % distillation temperature (°C). This is the **ASTM D4737** equation — directly testable.

#### 2.3.4 Reid Vapour Pressure to Dry Vapour Pressure Equivalent (DVPE)

$$
\text{DVPE} = \text{RVP} \times 0.97 + 0.0 \;\; \text{(kPa, BS-VI spec uses DVPE)}
$$

#### 2.3.5 Calorific Value (Dulong's Formula)

$$
\text{HCV} = 33{,}820\,C + 144{,}200\!\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9270\,S \quad \text{kJ/kg}
$$

where C, H, O, S are mass fractions of Carbon, Hydrogen, Oxygen, Sulfur.

$$
\text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - 2440\!\left(\frac{9H}{100}\right) \quad \text{kJ/kg}
$$

> H is the mass-fraction of Hydrogen, $2440$ kJ/kg is the latent heat of vapour of water per kg of H combusted.

#### 2.3.6 NOx Formation (Extended Zeldovich)

$$
\frac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} = 2\,k_{1}\,[\text{O}][\text{N}_{2}] \frac{1 - \frac{k_{-1}[\text{NO}]}{k_{1}[\text{N}_{2}]}}{1 + \frac{k_{-1}[\text{NO}]}{k_{2}[\text{O}_{2}]}}
$$

Peak in-cylinder temperature $T_{\text{peak}}$ is the *primary* control parameter — every 100 °C rise approximately **doubles** NOx.

#### 2.3.7 Smoke Number (Bosch) — Correlation with Fuel Property

$$
\text{SN} \;\propto\; \frac{(\%\text{PAH})\;\cdot\;(1/\text{CN})}{(\%\text{oxygenates} + 1)}
$$

Additives such as **ferrocene** and **cerium oxide nano-additives** work in the denominator.

#### 2.3.8 Specific Energy of Fuel

$$
E_{\text{sp}} = \text{LHV} \times \rho_{\text{fuel}} \quad \text{(MJ/m}^{3}\text{)}
$$

Diesel: ~ 35,800 MJ/m³; Gasoline: ~ 31,500 MJ/m³; LPG: ~ 25,000 MJ/m³.

#### 2.3.9 HFRR Lubricity Index

$$
\text{WS}_{1.4} = 0.95 \times \text{WS}_{\text{meas}} + 0.05 \times \text{WS}_{\text{meas}}^{2}
$$

where WS is the wear scar diameter in µm at HFRR test (60 °C, 1.4 N·m).

#### 2.3.10 Additive Performance Efficiency

$$
\eta_{\text{additive}} = \frac{\text{Emission}_{\text{base}} - \text{Emission}_{\text{additive}}}{\text{Emission}_{\text{base}}} \times 100\,\%
$$

This is the metric used to evaluate **DPF regeneration fuel-borne catalysts** like cerium.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Refinery Design** — Determines the cut-points of the crude distillation tower and severity of hydro-treater.
2. **Engine Calibration** — ECU maps are fuel-specific; switching fuel grades (RON 91 → 95) often requires re-mapping ignition timing.
3. **Emission Certification** — ARAI testing for BS-VI uses reference fuels with tight specs (e.g., RON 95.0 ± 0.5).
4. **Additive Selection** — Trade-off study: 100 ppm of cetane improver ≈ 5–7 % reduction in HC + CO.
5. **Policy Making** — India's 20 % ethanol-blending roadmap (E20) is an *additive-based* approach to lower carbon intensity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Why Low Cetane Number Increases Smoke (Diesel Engine)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a *conceptual mathematical walkthrough* that KTU examiners frequently use to award full 14-mark answers in Part B.

**Step 1: Define Ignition Delay (τᵢ) — the time between start of injection (SOI) and start of combustion (SOC).**

$$
\tau_{i} = \tau_{\text{phys}} + \tau_{\text{chem}}
$$

where $\tau_{\text{phys}}$ is the **physical delay** (atomization, vaporization, mixing) and $\tau_{\text{chem}}$ is the **chemical delay** (pre-flame reactions, low-temperature heat release, cool flame, high-temperature heat release, hot flame).

**Step 2: Express Chemical Delay via Arrhenius-type Equation.**

$$
\tau_{\text{chem}} = \frac{A}{\text{CN}} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{a}}{R\,T}\right)
$$

where $A$ is a fuel-specific constant, $E_{a}$ is the activation energy of the pre-flame oxidation reaction, $R$ is the universal gas constant, $T$ is the compressed gas temperature.

**Step 3: Recognize the Inverse Relationship with Cetane Number.**

The ASTM definition of CN is normalized against $n$-cetane ($\text{CN} = 100$) and $\alpha$-methylnaphthalene ($\text{CN} = 0$). A *higher* CN means a *shorter* $\tau_{\text{chem}}$.

> So $\tau_{\text{chem}} \propto \frac{1}{\text{CN}}$.

**Step 4: Consequence — Accumulated Fuel in the Cylinder.**

During $\tau_{i}$, the injector has been spraying fuel at a mass-flow rate $\dot{m}_{f}$:

$$
m_{\text{acc}} = \dot{m}_{f} \cdot \tau_{i}
$$

Substituting $\tau_{i} = \tau_{\text{phys}} + \frac{A}{\text{CN}} \exp(E_{a}/RT)$:

$$
m_{\text{acc}} = \dot{m}_{f} \cdot \left[\tau_{\text{phys}} + \frac{A}{\text{CN}} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{a}}{R\,T}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 5: The accumulated fuel auto-ignites as one large puff, creating locally rich zones.**

A locally rich zone has $\phi_{\text{local}} > 2$, where soot formation kinetics are:

$$
\frac{d[\text{Soot}]}{dt} = k_{s} \cdot [C_{2}H_{2}]^{n} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{s}}{R\,T}\right)
$$

where $C_{2}H_{2}$ (acetylene) is the dominant soot precursor; $n \approx 2$.

**Step 6: Combine the Two Effects to Give the Smoke Number Relation.**

$$
\text{SN} \;\propto\; m_{\text{acc}} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{\text{CN}}
$$

This is the engineering reason why **adding 1 % by volume of 2-ethylhexyl nitrate (a cetane improver) raises the CN by 4–6 points and cuts smoke by 15–25 %**.

### 3.2 Derivation — Mass Balance of Sulfur in Engine and Catalyst

> This derivation is often used in KTU valuation to award 7 marks for the "sulfur poisoning" part of any emission question.

**Step 1: Sulfur in fuel.**

$$
\text{S in fuel (g/L)} = \text{S content (ppm)} \times \rho_{\text{fuel}} \times 10^{-6}
$$

For diesel $\rho = 850$ kg/m³ = 0.85 kg/L, so 50 ppm gives:

$$
\text{S} = 50 \times 0.85 \times 10^{-6} \;\; \text{g/L} = 4.25 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{g/L}
$$

**Step 2: Total S burnt per km (typical BS-VI passenger car consumes ~ 0.05 L/km):**

$$
\text{S}_{\text{km}} = 0.05 \times 4.25 \times 10^{-5} = 2.125 \times 10^{-6} \;\; \text{g/km} = 2.125 \;\; \text{mg/km}
$$

**Step 3: Conversion to SO₂ in exhaust (2 moles S → 2 moles SO₂):**

$$
\text{Mass ratio:} \quad \frac{\text{SO}_{2}}{\text{S}} = \frac{64}{32} = 2
$$

$$
\text{SO}_{2,\text{km}} = 2 \times 2.125 = 4.25 \;\; \text{mg/km}
$$

**Step 4: Catalyst Poisoning — Sulphation Reaction on TWC (Pt/Rh):**

$$
\text{Pt} + \text{SO}_{2} + \text{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \text{Pt-SO}_{4}
$$

This blocks the active site. Coverage fraction $\theta$ follows Elovich adsorption:

$$
\frac{d\theta}{dt} = a \cdot p_{\text{SO}_{2}} \cdot \exp(-b\theta)
$$

Integration gives:

$$
\theta(t) = \frac{1}{b}\ln(1 + a b\,p_{\text{SO}_{2}}\,t)
$$

After a few hundred km of running on 50 ppm S, $\theta \approx 0.6$–$0.8$, drastically reducing conversion efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always show the **2× mass conversion** from S to SO₂ in your answer. Most KTU scripts lose a mark because students write only the fuel sulfur without converting to exhaust SO₂.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Fuel Property vs. Emission Map

This is the *single most important table* for the 14-mark question. Memorize it.

| Fuel Property | Affected Pollutant | Direction of Change | Mechanism |
|---------------|-------------------|---------------------|-----------|
| ↓ RON (Octane) | ↑ NOx, ↑ HC, ↑ knock | Inverse | End-gas auto-ignition |
| ↓ CN (Cetane) | ↑ PM, ↑ HC, ↑ noise | Inverse | Long ignition delay |
| ↑ S | ↑ SO₂, ↑ PM (sulfate), catalyst deactivation | Direct | Forms sulfate aerosols |
| ↑ Aromatics (BTEX) | ↑ PAH, ↑ soot, ↑ CO | Direct | Slow flame, ring structure |
| ↑ Olefins | ↑ NMOG, ↑ gum | Direct | Reactive C=C |
| ↑ RVP | ↑ Evaporative HC | Direct | Hot-soak losses |
| ↑ Density | ↑ Smoke | Direct | Poor atomization |
| ↑ Viscosity | ↑ Smoke, ↓ atomization | Direct | Poor spray breakup |
| ↑ FAME% | ↑ NOx (~ 5 %), ↓ PM | Mixed | Bound O |
| ↑ Oxygenates (MTBE/EtOH) | ↓ CO, ↓ HC, ↑ RVP | Mixed | Lean combustion |

### 3.4 Worked Example — Additive Dosing Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a typical 7-mark sub-question in a KTU University Exam.

**Problem:** A diesel engine consumes **0.10 L/km**. The fuel density is **840 kg/m³**. It is proposed to add **2-ethylhexyl nitrate (EHN)** as a cetane improver at a treat rate of **1000 ppm by mass**. Calculate:
(a) Mass of EHN per km travelled.
(b) CN improvement expected (assume 0.1 CN per 100 ppm).
(c) If the original smoke number is **3.5 Bosch**, estimate the new smoke number using $\text{SN}_{\text{new}} = \text{SN}_{\text{old}} / (1 + 0.05 \cdot \Delta \text{CN})$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Mass flow of fuel per km:**

$$
m_{\text{fuel}} = V \cdot \rho = 0.10 \;\text{L} \times 0.840 \;\text{kg/L} = 0.084 \;\text{kg}
$$

Mass of EHN added (at 1000 ppm = 1000 g per 1,000,000 g = $10^{-3}$):

$$
m_{\text{EHN}} = 0.084 \times 10^{-3} = 8.4 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{kg} = 84 \;\text{mg/km}
$$

**(b) CN improvement:**

$$
\Delta \text{CN} = \frac{0.1 \text{ CN}}{100 \text{ ppm}} \times 1000 \text{ ppm} = 1.0 \text{ CN}
$$

So CN rises from, say, 48 to 49.

**(c) New smoke number:**

$$
\text{SN}_{\text{new}} = \frac{3.5}{1 + 0.05 \times 1.0} = \frac{3.5}{1.05} = 3.33 \;\text{Bosch}
$$

**Percentage reduction in smoke:**

$$
\Delta \text{SN}\,(\%) = \frac{3.5 - 3.33}{3.5} \times 100 = 4.86\,\%
$$

This small per-unit effect is why **dosing is measured in hundreds of ppm** — the multiplicative effect over the entire fleet is enormous.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Fuel Property → Emission Pathway


<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set B]**
> **Define Octane Number. Why is a higher octane number required in modern high-compression-ratio engines?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Octane Number (ON/RON)** is the percentage by volume of iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane) in a mixture of iso-octane and n-heptane that has the same knocking intensity as the test fuel, under standard ASTM D2699 conditions. Pure iso-octane = 100, pure n-heptane = 0.

**Why higher ON is needed:** Modern BS-VI engines use compression ratios of **10:1 to 13:1** and **turbo-charging** to extract higher specific output. Higher compression raises the end-gas temperature at the end of compression stroke, making auto-ignition (knock) more probable. A fuel with **low ON auto-ignites** before the spark, producing a sharp pressure wave that damages the piston and increases **NOx, HC, CO**. High-octane fuels allow engines to run with **higher compression and boost** without knock, improving thermal efficiency and lowering specific fuel consumption.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]**
> **List any four commonly used fuel additives and state one function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, four items × 0.75 = 3):**

1. **Tetra-ethyl Lead (TEL)** — Anti-knock additive in petrol (now phased out due to catalyst poisoning).
2. **Methyl tert-butyl ether (MTBE)** — Oxygenate; raises octane, lowers CO.
3. **2-Ethylhexyl Nitrate (EHN)** — Cetane improver in diesel; shortens ignition delay.
4. **Polyisobutylamine (PIBA)** — Detergent; keeps intake valves and injectors deposit-free.
5. **BHT (Butylated Hydroxy Toluene)** — Anti-oxidant; prevents gum formation during storage.
6. **Ferrocene** — Smoke suppressant (fuel-borne catalyst for soot oxidation).

(Any four with correct function = full marks.)

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A, Module 2, Q4]**
> **(a)** Explain the major fuel-quality parameters that affect exhaust emissions from a **Compression-Ignition engine**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Describe the **different types of fuel additives** used in diesel fuels with their specific roles in **emission reduction**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The major fuel-quality parameters for a CI engine and their effect on emissions are:

1. **Cetane Number (CN)** — A measure of ignition delay. Low CN (e.g., < 45) means longer delay, more fuel accumulation, and **higher PM, HC, CO, and combustion noise**. CN 51+ is the BS-VI minimum. **(1.5 marks)**

2. **Sulfur Content** — Sulfur in fuel oxidises to **SO₂** in the exhaust, which further oxidises to **SO₃** and forms **sulfate particulate matter**. Sulfur also **poisons the SCR catalyst** (vanadia or Cu-zeolite) and the **NOx storage catalyst** in LNT, increasing tailpipe NOx. BS-VI mandates < 10 ppm. **(1.5 marks)**

3. **Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbon (PAH) Content** — Aromatic ring structures are **direct soot precursors** because of their high C/H ratio and slow flame speed. Reducing PAH from 11 % to 2 % can reduce smoke number by up to 50 %. **(1 mark)**

4. **Density and Viscosity** — These govern spray atomization. High values lead to larger **Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD)** droplets, fuel-rich zones, and **soot**. Low values cause leakage and power loss. **(1 mark)**

5. **Lubricity (HFRR wear scar)** — After deep hydro-desulfurization, the natural lubricity compounds are removed, leading to **injector and pump wear**. BS-VI spec mandates wear scar < 460 µm; lubricity additives (fatty acid esters) restore this. **(1 mark)**

6. **FAME (Biodiesel) Content** — FAME brings bound oxygen and **lowers PM by 30–50 %**, but slightly increases NOx due to higher cetane and oxygen content. Maximum 7 % in BS-VI. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Diesel Fuel Additives — Classification and Function:**

1. **Cetane Improvers** (EHN, amyl nitrate, di-tertiary-butyl peroxide)
   - Function: Decompose into free radicals at low temperature, **shortening ignition delay** by 2–6 CN points at 500–1500 ppm treat rate. **(1.5 marks)**

2. **Detergents / Deposit-Control Additives** (PIBA, polyether amines)
   - Function: Prevent and remove **injector coking** and intake-valve deposits; improve spray pattern. **(1 mark)**

3. **Lubricity Improvers** (fatty acid methyl esters, mono-oleates)
   - Function: Restore lubricity lost during desulfurization; **HFRR wear scar reduced** from 600 µm to < 460 µm. **(1 mark)**

4. **Anti-oxidants** (BHT, BHA)
   - Function: Prevent **gum and acid formation** during long storage. Important for FAME blends. **(1 mark)**

5. **Cold-Flow Improvers** (ethylene-vinyl acetate copolymers, pour-point depressants)
   - Function: Modify wax crystal growth, **lower pour point and CFPP** (Cold Filter Plugging Point). **(0.5 mark)**

6. **Demulsifiers / Dehazers** (alkoxylated phenolic resins)
   - Function: Break fuel-water emulsions; protect **injector corrosion**. **(0.5 mark)**

7. **Smoke Suppressants / Fuel-Borne Catalysts (FBC)** (ferrocene, cerium oxide nano-particles)
   - Function: Catalyse **soot oxidation** at lower DPF regeneration temperatures (~ 400 °C vs 600 °C). **(1 mark)**

8. **Biocides** (isothiazolinone)
   - Function: Kill microbial growth in **FAME-blended diesel** (bacteria grow at the water-fuel interface). **(0.5 mark)**

(Any 7 categories with at least 1 example and 1 function = 7 marks.)

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice — Alternative Question]**
> **(a)** Discuss the effect of **sulfur content** and **aromatic content** in diesel on **particulate matter emissions** and **after-treatment device performance**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **fuel-borne catalyst (FBC) system** in reducing PM emissions from a diesel engine. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Effect of Sulfur Content (3.5 marks):**
- Sulfur oxidises in the cylinder to SO₂, then in the atmosphere to SO₃ → H₂SO₄ aerosols (sulfate PM).
- **PM increase is roughly linear** with S content above 50 ppm.
- Sulfur **poisons three-way catalysts** (TWC), the DOC, the SCR, the LNT, and the DPF substrate by forming **metal-sulfate bonds** (Pt-SO₄, V-SO₄, etc.).
- Even low-sulfur fuels (< 10 ppm) need **periodic de-sulfation cycles** at 600–650 °C in LNT.
- **Quantitative impact:** Each 100 ppm of sulfur raises PM by ~ 0.01 g/km in a typical BS-IV diesel.

**Effect of Aromatic Content (3.5 marks):**
- Aromatics have a **higher C/H ratio** and lower H/C, so they release less energy per kg of fuel and burn at higher local temperatures.
- They decompose to **acetylene (C₂H₂)** which is the dominant soot precursor.
- Poly-aromatics (PAH) have ring structures that **directly form soot nuclei**.
- The Bosch smoke number increases by ~ 0.15 Bosch units per 1 % increase in PAH content (typical empirical slope).
- BS-VI limits **total aromatics < 35 % v/v** and **di+ poly-aromatics < 7 % v/v**.
- Aromatics also raise **unburned HC** due to slow flame propagation; higher CO due to incomplete burnout.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Working of a Fuel-Borne Catalyst (FBC) System:**

- The FBC (commonly **ferrocene, dicyclopentadienyl iron**, or **cerium oxide nano-particles**) is dosed into diesel at **10–100 ppm** via a dosing pump or pre-blended at the refinery.
- The organometallic compound **passes through combustion** and becomes an **oxide nano-particle** (e.g., Fe₂O₃, CeO₂) that is **embedded in the soot cake** on the DPF.
- During the **DPF regeneration cycle**, the temperature inside the filter rises to 550–650 °C. The Fe₂O₃ or CeO₂ nano-particles **catalyse the oxidation of soot** at this temperature, lowering the activation energy.
- The peak soot-oxidation temperature **drops by 100–150 °C**, so regeneration can occur more frequently, at lower engine load, and with less fuel penalty.
- **Net effect:** PM is oxidised in-situ; tailpipe PM falls by 30–70 %; DPF back-pressure is controlled; no extra fuel for active regeneration.
- **Sketch description:** A simple functional block diagram showing *dosing tank → dosing pump → fuel line → engine → exhaust → DPF (with embedded FBC nano-particles) → oxidation of soot → clean exhaust* is sufficient. KTU expects a block diagram with labels.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Octane and Cetane numbers.** Octane is for SI, Cetane is for CI. Mixing them is the single largest deduction.
> 2. **Always quantify.** Writing "sulfur increases emissions" gets 1 mark; writing "50 ppm sulfur raises PM by ~ 0.01 g/km" gets 2 marks.
> 3. **Mention specific additive names and treat rates.** Generic statements like "additives reduce pollution" are insufficient.
> 4. **Link fuel property to after-treatment device.** A complete answer must show the *chain* — fuel → combustion → engine-out → after-treatment → tailpipe.
> 5. **For FBC, always state the dose (ppm), mechanism (catalyse soot oxidation), and result (lower regeneration temperature).**

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fuel Quality and Additives**

- **Octane Number** is for **SI (petrol) engines**; **Cetane Number** is for **CI (diesel) engines**.
- BS-VI fuel: petrol **RON 91/95, S < 10 ppm, aromatics < 35 %**; diesel **CN ≥ 51, S < 10 ppm, PAH < 7 %, FAME = 7 %**.
- Higher **CN → less PM, less HC, less noise, less NOx** (slight).
- Higher **RON → enables higher compression, less knock, better thermal efficiency**.
- **Sulfur** is the **single most damaging fuel contaminant** — poisons every after-treatment device.
- **Aromatics and PAH** are direct **soot precursors**; reducing them cuts smoke.
- **RVP** controls **evaporative HC emissions**; too low → hard cold start; too high → smog.
- **TEL** is **banned in India** (catalyst poison); MTBE and ethanol are preferred octane boosters.
- **EHN** (2-ethylhexyl nitrate) is the most common **cetane improver**; dose 500–1500 ppm.
- **Ferrocene and cerium oxide** are the two main **fuel-borne catalysts (FBC)** for DPF regeneration.
- **Polyether amines (PEA)** and **PIBA** are the dominant **detergent additives**; they keep injectors and intake valves clean.
- **FAME blending** lowers PM but slightly raises NOx — a recognised trade-off.
- **Lubricity additives** are mandatory in **ultra-low-sulfur diesel (ULSD)** because hydro-desulfurization strips natural lubricity.
- **Anti-oxidants** (BHT) and **metal deactivators** are critical for **long-term storage stability**, especially for FAME blends.
- **Emission-formation chain** to remember: **Fuel property → Combustion quality → Engine-out emission → After-treatment efficiency → Tailpipe emission**.
- For numerical problems, always include **units, density, and the 2× mass conversion** from S to SO₂.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
