topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Role of Fuel Additives in Emission Reduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 2 — Role of Fuel Additives in Emission Reduction

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Additives** are chemical compounds (typically in concentrations of **50 to 10,000 ppm** by mass) blended into conventional gasoline and diesel fuels in small, precisely metered quantities to enhance fuel properties, improve combustion efficiency, protect engine components, and **reduce regulated exhaust emissions** (CO, HC, NOₓ, PM, CO₂) and unregulated toxic pollutants (PAH, aldehydes, benzene).

The primary role of additives in the **automotive pollution control** context is **in-cylinder emission reduction** — meaning they reduce pollutant formation *at the source* (during combustion) rather than treating emissions *post-combustion* via catalytic converters or DPF. This places them in the **"primary emission control"** category alongside fuel formulation, EGR, and engine calibration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Vitamins for the Engine"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Fuel as a Health Drink, Additives as Vitamins:**
> Imagine your body needs food (fuel) to function, but plain food alone may not give 100% nutrition. Vitamins (additives) are added in tiny amounts to make the body (engine) work more cleanly, efficiently, and with less "toxic waste" (exhaust emissions). Just as Vitamin C boosts immunity, a **detergent additive** keeps the engine's fuel injectors clean. Just as Iron improves blood oxygen transport, an **oxygenate additive** improves in-cylinder oxygen availability for cleaner combustion.

### 1.3 Why Fuel Additives Matter in KTU Context

The KTU 2024 Scheme places this topic under **Module 2: Sources and Effects of Automotive Pollution** (a foundational module preceding exhaust after-treatment). Understanding additive chemistry is essential because:

1. **It addresses pollution at the source** — pre-combustion strategy.
2. **It complements (does not replace) after-treatment** — Euro VI/BS-VI vehicles still rely on additives.
3. **It is a high-weightage KTU topic** — typically 7-14 marks in University exams.
4. **It bridges chemistry and mechanical engineering** — the only module where students must recall organic chemistry reactions.

### 1.4 Classification of Fuel Additives (Master Framework)

```
FUEL ADDITIVES
├── 1. Oxygenates (Alcohols, Ethers)        → Reduce CO, HC
├── 2. Detergents / Surfactants              → Reduce HC, PM
├── 3. Antioxidants                          → Reduce gums, deposits
├── 4. Anti-Knock Agents                     → Reduce HC, prevent pre-ignition
├── 5. Cetane Improvers                      → Reduce PM, HC, smoke
├── 6. Lubricity Improvers                   → Reduce PM (wear-derived)
├── 7. Metal Deactivators                    → Stabilize fuel
├── 8. Demulsifiers / Dehazers               → Water separation
├── 9. Anti-Foam Agents                      → Handling
└── 10. Cold Flow Improvers                  → Operability
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Of the above 10 categories, **Oxygenates, Detergents, Anti-Knock Agents, and Cetane Improvers** are the **four pillars** most frequently asked in University Exams. Students must master these four.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Each Additive Category — Detailed Mechanism

### 2.1.1 Oxygenates (MTBE, ETBE, Ethanol, Methanol)

**Chemical Identity:**
- **MTBE** — Methyl Tert-Butyl Ether, C₅H₁₂O, oxygen content = **18.2% by mass**
- **ETBE** — Ethyl Tert-Butyl Ether, C₆H₁₄O, oxygen content = **15.7% by mass**
- **Ethanol** — C₂H₅OH, oxygen content = **34.8% by mass**
- **Methanol** — CH₃OH, oxygen content = **49.9% by mass**

**Mechanism of Emission Reduction:**

$$ \text{Oxygenated Fuel} + \text{Bound Oxygen} \rightarrow \text{Leaner Local Equivalence Ratio} \rightarrow \text{More Complete Combustion} $$

**The "Why":**
1. In fuel-rich pockets (especially during cold start), incomplete combustion produces **CO and unburnt HC**. The oxygen in MTBE/ethanol acts as a **local oxygen reservoir**, ensuring that even in fuel-rich zones, oxygen is available.
2. Oxygenates have a **lower heating value** (gasoline ≈ 44 MJ/kg, ethanol ≈ 27 MJ/kg, MTBE ≈ 35 MJ/kg) — but they combust more completely.
3. They suppress the formation of **aromatic hydrocarbons** in the fuel blend (aromatics are precursors of PAH and soot).

**Emission Impact (typical, % reduction vs. baseline gasoline):**

| Pollutant | With 10% Ethanol | With 15% MTBE |
|-----------|------------------|---------------|
| CO        | ↓ 25-30%         | ↓ 15-20%      |
| HC (NMOG) | ↓ 10-15%         | ↓ 8-12%       |
| NOₓ       | ↑ 3-5% (slight)  | ↑ 2-4%        |
| CO₂       | ↓ 5-7% (net)     | ↓ 2-3%        |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** NOₓ often *increases* with oxygenates because higher combustion temperatures enhance thermal NOₓ formation (Zeldovich mechanism). KTU examiners frequently test this trade-off.

---

### 2.1.2 Detergents (Polyisobutylamine, PIBA; Polyetheramine, PEA)

**Chemical Identity:**
- **PIBA** — Polyisobutylamine, average MW = 800-1500 g/mol
- **PEA** — Polyetheramine, average MW = 1000-2000 g/mol

**Mechanism:**
1. Detergents are **surface-active molecules** (surfactants) with a polar "head" (amine group) and a non-polar "tail" (hydrocarbon chain).
2. The polar head **adsorbs onto metal surfaces** of intake valves, fuel injectors, and combustion chambers.
3. They form a **protective monolayer** that prevents deposit formation.
4. They also **solubilize existing deposits**, slowly cleaning engine components.

**Emission Impact (indirect):**
- Clean injectors → proper spray atomization → **↓ HC by 10-20%**
- Clean intake valves → no hot spots → **↓ HC by 5-10%**
- No combustion chamber deposits → **↓ PM, ↓ HC**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Detergents do NOT directly change combustion chemistry. They are **enablers** — by keeping the engine clean, they allow the engine's calibrated combustion to actually occur. This is a *highly testable conceptual point*.

---

### 2.1.3 Anti-Knock Agents (TEL, MMT, Ferrocene, Aromatic Amines)

**Chemical Identity:**
- **TEL** — Tetraethyl Lead, Pb(C₂H₅)₄ — **PHASED OUT globally** due to toxicity.
- **MMT** — Methylcyclopentadienyl Manganese Tricarbonyl, CH₃C₅H₄Mn(CO)₃
- **Ferrocene** — Fe(C₅H₅)₂
- **Aromatic Amines** — e.g., N-methylaniline, N,N-dimethyl-p-toluidine

**Mechanism (Why They Work):**

$$ \text{Knock} \leftarrow \text{Auto-ignition of end-gas ahead of flame front} $$

Anti-knock agents **decompose** in the end-gas to form **fine metal oxide particles** (e.g., MnO₂ from MMT, Fe₂O₃ from ferrocene) that act as **radical scavengers**, terminating the chain-branching reactions that lead to auto-ignition.

**Emission Impact (the KTU Trade-off Question):**

| Additive | HC | CO | NOₓ | PM/Soot |
|----------|----|----|-----|---------|
| TEL     | ↑  | ↑  | ↓  | ↑↑ (lead deposits) |
| MMT     | ↓  | ↔  | ↑  | ↑ (Mn particulates) |
| Ferrocene | ↓  | ↔  | ↑  | ↑ (Fe particulates) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Trend:** Anti-knock additives that contain heavy metals (Pb, Mn, Fe) are being **phased out** because the metal oxide deposits foul spark plugs, O₂ sensors, and catalytic converters, ultimately *increasing* lifecycle emissions. KTU exam questions often ask why we shifted to high-octane **refinery streams** (isooctane, alkylates) instead.

---

### 2.1.4 Cetane Improvers (2-EHN, DTBP, Amyl Nitrate)

**Chemical Identity:**
- **2-EHN** — 2-Ethylhexyl Nitrate, C₈H₁₇NO₃, the **most widely used** cetane improver
- **DTBP** — Di-Tertiary-Butyl Peroxide
- **Amyl Nitrate** — C₅H₁₁NO₃

**Mechanism:**
- Cetane number measures **ignition delay** — the time between fuel injection and start of combustion.
- 2-EHN **decomposes** at diesel-injection temperatures (~250-300°C) to form **free radicals (NO₂, alkyl radicals)**.
- These radicals **accelerate the pre-combustion chemistry**, shortening ignition delay.

$$ \text{C}_{8}\text{H}_{17}\text{NO}_{3} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{NO}_2 + \text{alkyl radicals} \rightarrow \text{Shorter Ignition Delay} \rightarrow \text{Smoother Combustion} $$

**Emission Impact:**
- **↓ PM (soot) by 15-25%** — better mixing before ignition
- **↓ HC by 10-15%** — more complete combustion
- **↓ CO by 10-20%** — better oxidation
- **↑ NOₓ by 2-5%** — slightly higher peak temperatures (trade-off)

---

### 2.1.5 Lubricity Improvers (Biodiesel FAME, Fatty Acid Esters)

**Context:** Ultra-low sulfur diesel (ULSD, <10 ppm S) has poor lubricity, causing injector and pump wear → metal debris → **PM increase**.

**Solution:** Blending **5-7% biodiesel (FAME)** restores lubricity. The fatty acid methyl esters form a **boundary lubrication film** on metal surfaces.

**Emission Impact:**
- **↓ PM-bound metals**
- Slight ↑ NOₓ (oxygen content of FAME)

---

### 2.1.6 Antioxidants (BHT, BHA, Amines)

**Mechanism:** Prevent formation of **gums and peroxides** during fuel storage. Gums clog fuel filters and injectors → **↑ HC emissions** over vehicle life.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates every quantitative relationship you need for KTU 2024 exams. Memorize the units — marks are lost for omitting them.**

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|-----------|---------------------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Oxygen Content of Fuel** | $$O_{\%} = \frac{16 \cdot (n_{\text{oxygen atoms per molecule})}{M_{\text{fuel}}}}{M_{\text{fuel}}} \times 100$$ | % by mass | Determines oxygenate loading for emission target |
| 2 | **Oxygenate Loading Limit** | $$\phi_{\text{ox}} = \frac{O_{\%,\text{additive}} \cdot x}{O_{\%,\text{blend}}}$$ | % vol | Regulatory limit (e.g., 10% ethanol, 15% MTBE) |
| 3 | **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of Blend** | $$LHV_{blend} = \sum_{i} x_i \cdot LHV_i$$ | MJ/kg | Energy density for fuel economy calculations |
| 4 | **Cetane Number Effect on Ignition Delay** | $$\tau_{id} \propto \frac{1}{CN}$$ | ms (qualitative) | 2-EHN dosing (typically 1000-3000 ppm) |
| 5 | **Octane Number Effect on Knock** | $$P_{\text{knock}} \propto \frac{1}{ON}$$ | bar (qualitative) | Anti-knock additive selection |
| 6 | **Emission Reduction Efficiency** | $$\eta_{red} = \frac{E_{baseline} - E_{additive}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100\%$$ | % | Quantifies additive performance |
| 7 | **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio** | $$AFR_{st} = \frac{2.67C + 8H - O + S}{12.01C + 1.008H + 16O + 32.06S}$$ | kg air / kg fuel | Determines if fuel is lean/rich |
| 8 | **Surface Tension of Detergent Layer** | $$\gamma_{\text{eff}} = \gamma_{metal} - \gamma_{adsorption}$$ | N/m | Predicts detergent adhesion to injector surface |
| 9 | **Particulate Matter (PM) Reduction** | $$\Delta PM = k \cdot [\text{Cetane Improver}]^{0.7}$$ | mg/m³ | Empirical correlation |
| 10 | **Antioxidant Effectiveness** | $$\ln \frac{[ROOH]_t}{[ROOH]_0} = -k_a \cdot [\text{antioxidant}] \cdot t$$ | s⁻¹ (kinetic) | Predicts fuel shelf life |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries** use additive blending to meet **BS-VI / Euro-VI** specifications.
- **OEMs** (e.g., Tata, Mahindra, Maruti) spec fuel additive packages for **warranty compliance**.
- **Fleet operators** (Buses, Trucks) use **cetane + lubricity** packages to extend **DPF service intervals**.
- **Racing fuels** use MMT or ferrocene derivatives to **boost octane** without resorting to lead.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Oxygen Content by Mass for an Oxygenate

**Problem:** Calculate the oxygen content (% by mass) of **MTBE** (methyl tert-butyl ether, C₅H₁₂O).

**Step 1 — Identify Molecular Formula and Atomic Masses**

The molecular formula of MTBE is **C₅H₁₂O**.

$$ \text{Atomic masses (per IUPAC standard):} $$
$$ M_C = 12.011 \text{ g/mol}, \quad M_H = 1.008 \text{ g/mol}, \quad M_O = 15.999 \text{ g/mol} $$

**Step 2 — Calculate Molar Mass of MTBE**

$$ M_{MTBE} = (5 \times 12.011) + (12 \times 1.008) + (1 \times 15.999) $$

Carrying out the multiplications:

$$ 5 \times 12.011 = 60.055 \text{ g/mol} $$
$$ 12 \times 1.008 = 12.096 \text{ g/mol} $$
$$ 1 \times 15.999 = 15.999 \text{ g/mol} $$

Summing these:

$$ M_{MTBE} = 60.055 + 12.096 + 15.999 = 88.150 \text{ g/mol} $$

**Step 3 — Calculate Mass of Oxygen in One Mole of MTBE**

The molecule contains **1 oxygen atom**, so:

$$ m_O = 1 \times 15.999 = 15.999 \text{ g per mole of MTBE} $$

**Step 4 — Calculate Mass Percentage of Oxygen**

The general formula for mass percentage is:

$$ O_{\%} = \frac{\text{Mass of element in one mole of compound}}{\text{Molar mass of compound}} \times 100\% $$

Substituting the values:

$$ O_{\%} = \frac{15.999}{88.150} \times 100\% $$

Performing the division:

$$ \frac{15.999}{88.150} = 0.18150 $$

Converting to percentage:

$$ O_{\%} = 0.18150 \times 100\% = 18.15\% \text{ by mass} $$

**Final Result:**

$$ \boxed{O_{\%,\text{MTBE}} \approx 18.15\% \text{ by mass}} $$

This matches the standard reference value of **18.2%** used in KTU textbooks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Blending Oxygenates to Meet a 2.7% Oxygen Mandate (US EPA Standard)

**Problem:** A refiner wants to produce gasoline with an oxygen content of **2.7% by mass** using **ethanol (O = 34.8%)** and **base gasoline (O = 0%)**. What volume % of ethanol is required? (Take density of ethanol = **789 kg/m³**, density of base gasoline = **740 kg/m³**.)

**Step 1 — Set Up the Mass Balance**

Let $x_E$ = mass fraction of ethanol in the blend.

$$ O_{\%,\text{blend}} = x_E \cdot O_{\%,\text{ethanol}} + (1 - x_E) \cdot O_{\%,\text{gasoline}} $$

Since $O_{\%,\text{gasoline}} = 0$:

$$ 2.7 = x_E \cdot 34.8 $$

Solving for $x_E$:

$$ x_E = \frac{2.7}{34.8} = 0.0776 $$

So ethanol must be **7.76% by mass** of the blend.

**Step 2 — Convert Mass Fraction to Volume Fraction**

Volume fraction is related to mass fraction by densities:

$$ \phi_E = \frac{x_E / \rho_E}{x_E / \rho_E + (1 - x_E) / \rho_G} $$

Substituting the values:

$$ \phi_E = \frac{0.0776 / 789}{0.0776 / 789 + 0.9224 / 740} $$

Calculate the numerator:

$$ \frac{0.0776}{789} = 9.835 \times 10^{-5} $$

Calculate the denominator (each term):

$$ \frac{0.9224}{740} = 1.2465 \times 10^{-3} $$

Summing:

$$ 9.835 \times 10^{-5} + 1.2465 \times 10^{-3} = 1.3449 \times 10^{-3} $$

Computing the ratio:

$$ \phi_E = \frac{9.835 \times 10^{-5}}{1.3449 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0731 $$

**Final Result:**

$$ \boxed{\phi_E \approx 7.3\% \text{ ethanol by volume}} $$

This corresponds to the well-known **E7** blend (7% ethanol) used in India and many countries.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cetane Number Improvement from 2-EHN Dosing

**Problem:** Baseline diesel has CN = **45**. Adding **1000 ppm of 2-EHN** increases CN by approximately **+5**. Adding **2000 ppm** increases CN by approximately **+9**. Model this as a power law and predict CN for **3000 ppm**.

**Step 1 — Assume Power-Law Relationship**

$$ \Delta CN = k \cdot C^n $$

where $C$ is the 2-EHN concentration in **ppm**, and $k, n$ are empirical constants.

**Step 2 — Use the Two Data Points to Solve for k and n**

For 1000 ppm:
$$ 5 = k \cdot (1000)^n $$

For 2000 ppm:
$$ 9 = k \cdot (2000)^n $$

**Step 3 — Divide the Two Equations to Eliminate k**

$$ \frac{9}{5} = \left(\frac{2000}{1000}\right)^n $$

$$ 1.8 = 2^n $$

Taking natural logarithms on both sides:

$$ \ln(1.8) = n \cdot \ln(2) $$

$$ n = \frac{\ln(1.8)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{0.5878}{0.6931} = 0.848 $$

**Step 4 — Back-Substitute to Find k**

$$ 5 = k \cdot (1000)^{0.848} $$

Compute $(1000)^{0.848}$:

$$ 1000^{0.848} = 10^{3 \times 0.848} = 10^{2.544} = 350.0 $$

Therefore:

$$ k = \frac{5}{350.0} = 0.01429 $$

**Step 5 — Predict CN Boost at 3000 ppm**

$$ \Delta CN = 0.01429 \cdot (3000)^{0.848} $$

Compute $(3000)^{0.848}$:

$$ 3000^{0.848} = (3 \times 10^3)^{0.848} = 3^{0.848} \times 10^{2.544} $$

$$ 3^{0.848} = e^{0.848 \cdot \ln 3} = e^{0.848 \times 1.0986} = e^{0.9316} = 2.539 $$

$$ 3000^{0.848} = 2.539 \times 350.0 = 888.6 $$

$$ \Delta CN = 0.01429 \times 888.6 = 12.7 $$

**Final Result:**

$$ \boxed{\Delta CN \approx +12.7 \text{ at 3000 ppm 2-EHN, giving a new CN of } 45 + 12.7 \approx 57.7} $$

This saturates near the practical limit of ~+15 CN, which is why 2-EHN dosing rarely exceeds **3000 ppm** in commercial diesel.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Additive vs. Pollutant Cross-Impact

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following matrix is the single most important KTU 2024 exam deliverable. Memorize the trends, not the exact numbers.**

| Additive Class | CO | HC | NOₓ | PM/Soot | Lead/Mn | Notes |
|----------------|----|----|-----|---------|---------|-------|
| **Oxygenates (MTBE, EtOH)** | ↓↓ | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ | — | Trade-off: better oxidation vs higher T |
| **Detergents (PIBA, PEA)** | ↓ | ↓↓ | ↔ | ↓ | — | Indirect, via deposit removal |
| **Anti-Knock (MMT, Ferrocene)** | ↔ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | Metals poison cat. converters |
| **Cetane Improvers (2-EHN)** | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | ↓↓ | — | Best for diesel PM reduction |
| **Lubricity (FAME)** | ↔ | ↔ | ↑ | ↓ (metal) | — | Sulfur-replacement additive |
| **Antioxidants (BHT)** | ↔ | ↓* | ↔ | ↔ | — | *Long-term effect via deposit control |

**Legend:** ↓↓ strong reduction, ↓ mild reduction, ↔ negligible, ↑ mild increase, ↑↑ strong increase

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"List any four fuel additives used in gasoline and write one function of each."**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Additive | Function |
|---|----------|----------|
| 1 | **MTBE** (Methyl Tert-Butyl Ether) | Increases oxygen content of fuel, promotes complete combustion, reduces **CO emissions** by 15-20%. |
| 2 | **PIBA** (Polyisobutylamine) | Detergent that prevents deposit formation on **intake valves and fuel injectors**, reducing long-term HC emissions. |
| 3 | **MMT** (Methylcyclopentadienyl Manganese Tricarbonyl) | Anti-knock additive that suppresses auto-ignition, allowing **higher compression ratios** and improved thermal efficiency. |
| 4 | **BHT** (Butylated Hydroxytoluene) | Antioxidant that prevents gum and peroxide formation during fuel storage, preserving fuel quality. |

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Explain how oxygenated fuels help in reducing CO emissions."**

**Model Answer:**

Oxygenated fuels like **MTBE, ETBE, and ethanol** contain chemically bound oxygen in their molecular structure. When blended with gasoline, this bound oxygen creates **locally lean conditions** in fuel-rich pockets of the combustion chamber. These fuel-rich pockets are the primary sites of **incomplete combustion**, where carbon cannot fully oxidize to CO₂ due to local oxygen starvation, forming **CO** instead. The bound oxygen from the oxygenate **supplements the intake air's oxygen** in these pockets, enabling CO to oxidize further to CO₂. The effect is most pronounced during **cold-start** and **transient operation** when the air-fuel mixture is typically richer. Quantitative studies show **10-15% ethanol blending reduces CO by 25-30%** in cold-start conditions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the role of **oxygenates** as fuel additives in reducing automotive emissions. Explain the mechanism with reference to **MTBE and ethanol**, including their oxygen content and typical emission reduction percentages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of **cetane improvers** in diesel engines. With a neat diagram, describe the **mechanism of 2-EHN action** and quantify its typical effect on PM, HC, and CO emissions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Oxygenates

**Definition:** Oxygenates are organic compounds containing oxygen atoms in their functional groups (hydroxyl, ether, ester) that are blended with gasoline to enhance combustion efficiency and reduce tailpipe emissions.

**Chemical Identity and Oxygen Content:**

| Oxygenate | Formula | Molecular Weight (g/mol) | Oxygen Content (% by mass) |
|-----------|---------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| Methanol  | CH₃OH   | 32.04                    | 49.9                       |
| Ethanol   | C₂H₅OH  | 46.07                    | 34.8                       |
| MTBE      | C₅H₁₂O  | 88.15                    | 18.2                       |
| ETBE      | C₆H₁₄O  | 102.18                   | 15.7                       |

**Mechanism of CO and HC Reduction:**

1. **Local Oxygen Enrichment:** During cold start and acceleration, the air-fuel mixture in certain cylinder regions is **fuel-rich** (φ > 1). In these regions, the available intake air is insufficient to fully oxidize all carbon to CO₂. Oxygenates provide **supplementary bound oxygen**, reducing the local equivalence ratio.

2. **Improved Flame Stability:** The polar oxygen-containing bonds in MTBE and ethanol have **lower bond dissociation energies** for certain steps, promoting **radical generation** (H•, OH•, O•) at lower temperatures. These radicals accelerate CO oxidation:
   
   $$ \text{CO} + \text{OH}{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}{\bullet} $$

3. **Aromatic Displacement:** Oxygenates **displace aromatics** (benzene, toluene, xylene) from the fuel blend. Aromatics are precursors of **polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAH)** and **soot**. Thus, oxygenate blending indirectly reduces PM and toxic emissions.

**Quantitative Emission Reductions (10% Ethanol vs. Baseline Gasoline):**

| Pollutant | Reduction |
|-----------|-----------|
| CO        | 25-30%    |
| NMHC      | 10-15%    |
| NOₓ       | +3 to +5% (slight increase) |
| Benzene   | 30-50%    |
| PM        | 20-30%    |

**Trade-off:** NOₓ increases marginally due to higher peak combustion temperatures (Zeldovich thermal NOₓ mechanism).

**Practical Applications:** E10 (10% ethanol) is mandated in India under the **Ethanol Blending Programme (EBP)**. MTBE was widely used in the 1990s but is now restricted in the US due to groundwater contamination concerns.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Cetane Improvers

**Definition:** Cetane improvers are chemical additives, typically **organic nitrates or peroxides**, that increase the **cetane number (CN)** of diesel fuel by shortening the **ignition delay period** between fuel injection and start of combustion.

**Common Cetane Improvers:**

1. **2-EHN (2-Ethylhexyl Nitrate)** — C₈H₁₇NO₃ — most widely used
2. **DTBP (Di-Tertiary-Butyl Peroxide)**
3. **Amyl Nitrate** — C₅H₁₁NO₃
4. **Cyclohexyl Nitrate**

**Mechanism of 2-EHN Action:**

1. **Thermal Decomposition:** When 2-EHN is injected into the hot compressed air in the cylinder (temperature ≈ 250-300°C), it decomposes:
   
   $$ \text{C}_8\text{H}_{17}\text{NO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta, \, 250-300°C} \text{NO}_2 + \text{alkyl radicals (R}{\bullet}\text{)} + \text{other products} $$

2. **Radical Pool Formation:** The NO₂ and alkyl radicals create a **pool of reactive species** that accelerate the pre-combustion chemistry of the diesel fuel.

3. **Ignition Delay Shortening:** The normal diesel ignition involves:
   
   $$ \text{Diesel} \rightarrow \text{Pyrolysis} \rightarrow \text{Hydroperoxides (ROOH)} \rightarrow \text{Radical chain branching} \rightarrow \text{Combustion} $$
   
   2-EHN provides NO₂ that converts ROOH to more reactive ROO• radicals faster, **accelerating** the chain-branching step.

4. **Effect on Combustion Phasing:** With shorter ignition delay, the **premixed combustion phase is reduced** and the **diffusion-controlled phase dominates**. This results in:
   - **More uniform combustion**
   - **Lower peak temperatures in the diffusion flame**
   - **Lower soot formation** (PM)

**Quantitative Effects (typical, 1000-3000 ppm 2-EHN):**

| Pollutant | Change |
|-----------|--------|
| PM (Soot) | ↓ 15-25% |
| HC        | ↓ 10-15% |
| CO        | ↓ 10-20% |
| NOₓ       | ↑ 2-5%   |
| BSFC      | ↓ 1-2%   |
| Smoke (Bosch) | ↓ 30-40% |

**Diagram (text representation):**

```
   ┌─────────────┐    Injection
   │ 2-EHN in   │ ─────────────►  Cylinder
   │ Diesel Fuel │                (250-300°C)
   └─────────────┘                       │
                                         ▼
                              ┌────────────────────┐
                              │ Thermal Decompose  │
                              │ → NO₂ + R• radicals│
                              └────────┬───────────┘
                                       │
                                       ▼
                              ┌────────────────────┐
                              │ Radical Pool       │
                              │ accelerates ROOH   │
                              │ → ROO• conversion  │
                              └────────┬───────────┘
                                       │
                                       ▼
                              ┌────────────────────┐
                              │ Shorter Ignition   │
                              │ Delay              │
                              └────────┬───────────┘
                                       │
                                       ▼
                              ┌────────────────────┐
                              │ More Diffusion     │
                              │ Combustion, Less   │
                              │ Premixed → Less PM │
                              └────────────────────┘
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare **lead-based and non-leaded anti-knock additives** with respect to their mechanism, effectiveness, and impact on automotive emissions. Justify why **TEL has been globally phased out**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of **detergent additives** in maintaining engine cleanliness and reducing emissions over the vehicle's life. Differentiate between **PIBA and PEA** chemistry. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Anti-Knock Additives

**Background:** Knocking (or "pinging") in SI engines is the auto-ignition of the end-gas ahead of the propagating flame front, caused by high pressure and temperature. It reduces efficiency and can damage engines. **Octane number (ON)** quantifies a fuel's resistance to knock.

**Lead-Based Anti-Knock: TEL (Tetraethyl Lead, Pb(C₂H₅)₄):**

- **Mechanism:** TEL decomposes at high temperatures to form **PbO and PbO₂ particles** that act as **radical scavengers**, terminating chain-branching reactions responsible for auto-ignition.
- **Effectiveness:** Very effective — **+10 to +15 ON** at 0.5-1.0 g Pb/L.
- **Drawback:** Lead forms **solid deposits** on spark plugs, valves, and catalytic converter surfaces. The deposits:
  1. Increase HC and CO emissions over time.
  2. **Poison catalytic converters** (irreversible).
  3. Toxic to humans (especially children — neurotoxicity).
- **Phase-Out:** Globally phased out between 1970s-2000s. In India, leaded petrol was banned in **2000**.

**Non-Leaded Alternatives:**

| Additive | Type | Effectiveness | Emission Impact |
|----------|------|---------------|-----------------|
| **MMT** (Methylcyclopentadienyl Mn Tricarbonyl) | Organo-metallic | +3 to +8 ON at 0.05-0.1 g Mn/L | Forms MnO₂ deposits; some cat. fouling |
| **Ferrocene** (Fe(C₅H₅)₂) | Organo-metallic | +2 to +6 ON | Forms Fe₂O₃ deposits; less toxic than Mn |
| **N-Methyl Aniline** | Aromatic amine | +5 to +10 ON | Cleaner combustion; no metal ash |
| **Methyl-Cyclopentadienyl Iron** | Organo-metallic | +4 to +7 ON | Similar to MMT but with Fe |
| **Refinery Streams** (isooctane, alkylate) | Processing | Variable | Best — no additive at all |

**Justification for Phasing Out TEL:**

1. **Toxicity:** Lead is a cumulative neurotoxin; even low-level exposure causes IQ reduction in children.
2. **Catalyst Poisoning:** Three-way catalysts (TWC) require unleaded fuel to function. Lead-poisoned catalysts cannot reduce NOₓ, CO, or HC.
3. **Modern Refining:** Catalytic cracking and alkylation can produce high-octane gasoline (>95 RON) without additives.
4. **Regulatory:** Vienna Convention (1979), EU Directive 98/70/EC, and Indian CPCB notifications.

**Emission Comparison (Lead vs. MMT vs. Aromatic Amine):**

| Metric | TEL | MMT | N-Methyl Aniline |
|--------|-----|-----|------------------|
| HC at light load | ↑ 15% | ↓ 5% | ↓ 8% |
| CO | ↑ 20% | ↔ | ↔ |
| NOₓ | ↓ 5% | ↑ 8% | ↑ 5% |
| PM | ↑ 30% (Pb ash) | ↑ 20% (Mn ash) | ↓ 5% |
| Cat. life | < 10,000 km | ~50,000 km | ~150,000 km |

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Detergent Additives

**Problem Statement:** Modern engines have **tight tolerances** in fuel injectors (clearances < 5 μm) and rely on **precise spray patterns**. Even **small deposits** (10-50 μm thick) on injector tips, intake valves, and combustion chambers can disrupt spray atomization, causing:
- **Increased HC emissions** (poor atomization → fuel impingement on walls → unburnt fuel)
- **Increased CO** (locally rich combustion)
- **Increased PM** (fuel-rich soot formation)
- **Reduced fuel economy** (5-10%)

**Function of Detergent Additives:**

Detergents are **amphiphilic molecules** — they have a **polar (hydrophilic) head** that bonds to metal surfaces and a **non-polar (hydrophobic) tail** that dissolves in fuel. This allows them to:
1. **Adsorb** on metal surfaces → form a **protective monolayer**
2. **Solubilize** existing deposits into fuel
3. **Prevent** new deposit formation by acting as a **boundary layer**

**Two Main Chemistries:**

**1. PIBA — Polyisobutylamine:**

$$ \text{Structure: } R - NH_2 \text{ where } R = \text{polyisobutyl chain (MW = 800-1500)} $$

- **Behavior:** Forms a **thicker, more tenacious film** on hot metal surfaces (intake valves at 200-300°C).
- **Strength:** Excellent **deposit prevention** for new engines; strong film at high temperatures.
- **Weakness:** **Poor deposit removal** for existing thick deposits; can form its own deposits at very high doses.

**2. PEA — Polyetheramine:**

$$ \text{Structure: } R - O - (\text{CH}_2\text{CHR'O})_n - NH_2 $$

- **Behavior:** More **surface-active**, with the polyether chain providing additional polarity.
- **Strength:** **Excellent deposit removal** ("clean-up" additive); effective at lower temperatures.
- **Weakness:** Slightly more expensive; can increase NOₓ in some applications.

**Quantitative Impact (PIBA at 200 ppm in gasoline):**

| Test Cycle | HC Reduction | CO Reduction | Fuel Economy |
|------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|
| New car (0-50,000 km) | ↓ 5-10% | ↓ 3-7% | ↑ 1-2% |
| Aged car (50,000+ km, no additive) | HC ↑ 30% | CO ↑ 25% | ↓ 8% |
| Aged car (with PIBA) | HC ↓ 15% vs. untreated | CO ↓ 12% | Restored |

**Real-World Application:**
- **Tier 3 (US EPA)** and **BS-VI (India)** standards effectively require detergent additives.
- **BMW, Toyota, Maruti Suzuki** specify "Top Tier Detergent Gasoline" with **3x the standard additive dose**.
- **Indian Oil's "XP95"** and **Hindustan Petroleum's "PoWer"** include premium additive packages.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — How Marks Are Lost**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Oxygenate" with "Octane Improver":** MTBE improves octane *and* oxygenates the fuel. Both effects exist, but in KTU answers, you must specify which mechanism is being discussed. Generic answers score poorly.
> 2. **Omitting the NOₓ Trade-off:** Almost every additive that reduces CO/HC/PM *increases* NOₓ. KTU examiners specifically test this trade-off. A 7-mark question that doesn't mention it loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting Units in Numerical Problems:** In Part B, "Calculate the oxygen content" without "g/mol" or "% by mass" attracts 1-2 mark deductions.
> 4. **Missing the "Why Phased Out" Angle:** Simply listing that TEL is toxic is insufficient. You must explain *catalyst poisoning* and *modern refinery alternatives*. KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes this.
> 5. **Mixing Up Detergent vs. Antioxidant:** Detergents clean *engine components*; antioxidants protect *fuel from oxidation*. These are commonly confused.
> 6. **Not Using Diagrams in 14-Mark Questions:** A 14-mark question without a labeled diagram (even a simple block diagram) typically loses 2-3 marks per KTU valuation patterns.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2: Fuel Additives**

### **Core Concepts to Memorize:**
- [ ] **MTBE oxygen content** = 18.2%; **Ethanol** = 34.8%; **Methanol** = 49.9%
- [ ] **2-EHN** is the most common cetane improver; **PIBA/PEA** are the most common detergents
- [ ] **TEL is globally phased out** due to lead toxicity and catalyst poisoning
- [ ] **E10 (10% ethanol)** is the standard oxygenate blend in India under EBP
- [ ] **Oxygenates reduce CO and HC, but slightly increase NOₓ** — this trade-off is the most-tested concept
- [ ] **Cetane improvers reduce PM, HC, CO, but slightly increase NOₓ**
- [ ] **Detergents work indirectly** — they keep the engine clean, allowing the engine's calibrated combustion to occur

### **Key Numerical Values to Remember:**
- [ ] Cetane number target for **BS-VI diesel**: ≥ 51
- [ ] Octane number target for **BS-VI petrol**: ≥ 91 RON
- [ ] 2-EHN typical dosing: **1000-3000 ppm**
- [ ] PIBA typical dosing: **50-300 ppm**
- [ ] Sulfur limit in BS-VI: **< 10 ppm** (necessitates lubricity additives)
- [ ] Aromatic content limit: **35% by volume** (BS-VI petrol)

### **Memorable Mnemonics:**
- **"O-H-C-K"** — **O**xygenates, **H**ydrocarbon detergents, **C**etane improvers, **K**nock suppressors (anti-knock)
- **"CO-HC-Down, NOx-Up"** — for oxygenates and cetane improvers (the standard trade-off)
- **"TEL = Toxic, Expensive, Lost"** — to remember why lead was phased out

### **Common Exam Traps to Avoid:**
- [ ] Don't say "MTBE reduces all emissions" — it increases NOₓ
- [ ] Don't confuse **cetane number** (diesel) with **octane number** (gasoline)
- [ ] Don't equate **anti-knock additives** with **oxygenates** — they are different classes
- [ ] Don't forget that **antioxidants** prevent **fuel degradation**, not **engine deposits**
- [ ] Don't claim that additives **replace** after-treatment devices — they **complement** them

### **One-Line Definitions (For 1-Mark Sub-Questions):**
- [ ] **Oxygenate:** *"A fuel-blending component containing chemically bound oxygen that promotes more complete combustion."*
- [ ] **Detergent Additive:** *"A surface-active agent that prevents and removes deposits from fuel system components."*
- [ ] **Cetane Improver:** *"A diesel fuel additive that shortens ignition delay by providing reactive radical species."*
- [ ] **Anti-Knock Additive:** *"A compound that suppresses auto-ignition in SI engines by terminating chain-branching reactions."*
- [ ] **Lubricity Improver:** *"An additive (typically FAME) that restores boundary lubrication to ultra-low-sulfur diesel."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emission Characteristics of Alternate Fuels - CNG, Biofuels, Hydrogen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Emission Characteristics of Alternate Fuels - CNG, Biofuels, Hydrogen

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternate Fuels** are defined as fuels that can be used as **substitutes for conventional petroleum-based fuels (gasoline and diesel)** in internal combustion engines, with the primary objective of **reducing tailpipe emissions**, **enhancing energy security**, and **improving sustainability** of the transportation sector.

The emission characteristics of these alternate fuels refer to the **qualitative and quantitative profile of regulated and unregulated exhaust species** released during combustion — specifically **CO, HC, NOx, PM, CO₂, SOx, aldehydes, and PAH (Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons)** — measured per unit distance or per unit fuel energy.

### 1.2 The Three Key Alternate Fuels in Focus

| Fuel | Chemical Identity | Carbon-Atom Status | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CNG (Compressed Natural Gas)** | Primarily **CH₄ (Methane, ~85-95%)** | Low C/H ratio (~0.25) | Underground reserves, biogas |
| **Biofuels** | **Ethanol (C₂H₅OH), Methanol (CH₃OH), Biodiesel (FAME)** | Renewable carbon | Biomass, vegetable oils |
| **Hydrogen (H₂)** | Pure molecular hydrogen | **Zero carbon** | Electrolysis, reforming, renewables |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Clean Plate Club"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine three friends eating dinner:
> - **Hydrogen** is the friend who eats only water — leaves **zero solid waste (CO₂)** on the plate. (Zero carbon, only H₂O exhaust).
> - **CNG (Methane)** is the friend who eats a simple, light salad — leaves very little waste and is mostly **clean-burning**, but still leaves a tiny bit (CO₂) since one carbon atom is involved.
> - **Biofuels** are the friend who brings their own lunch from home (renewable carbon) — the carbon they "ate" was already part of the atmosphere recently via photosynthesis, so it forms a **closed carbon cycle**, making it effectively carbon-neutral.

This analogy directly maps to the **carbon-to-hydrogen (C/H) ratio**, which is the single most important determinant of exhaust emissions:

- **Lower C/H ratio ⇒ Lower CO₂ emission per unit energy**
- **Hydrogen: 0, CNG: 0.25, Gasoline: 0.5, Diesel: 0.66**

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Emission Analysis

- **Brake-Specific Emissions (g/kWh)** — emissions normalized to engine brake power output.
- **Emission Index (EI) — g of pollutant per kg of fuel burned**.
- **Mass of CO₂ per unit energy released**: For gasoline ~**73.4 g/MJ**, CNG ~**56 g/MJ**, Hydrogen = **0 g/MJ**.
- **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)** by mass: Gasoline = **14.7:1**, CNG = **17.2:1**, Hydrogen = **34.3:1**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 CNG — Emission Characteristics Deep Dive

#### 2.1.1 Why CNG Burns Cleaner (The Molecular Logic)

Methane (CH₄) is the **simplest saturated hydrocarbon**. Its complete combustion follows:

$$CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O + Heat \quad (\Delta H_c = -802.3 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

Since there is **only one carbon atom per molecule**, the maximum theoretical CO₂ is minimal compared to long-chain gasoline (C₈H₁₈).

**Key Emission Outcomes for CNG:**

- **CO (Carbon Monoxide):** Drastically reduced (~**70-90% lower** than gasoline) due to higher H/C ratio and gaseous nature allowing better air-fuel mixing.
- **HC (Unburnt Hydrocarbons):** Lower (~**50-60% reduction**). However, **methane slip** — unburnt CH₄ — is a concern because CH₄ is a **potent greenhouse gas (GWP = 28-36 over 100 years)**.
- **NOx (Oxides of Nitrogen):** Generally **higher under stoichiometric conditions** due to higher flame temperature and elevated O₂ availability. Reduced by lean-burn technology and 3-way catalysts.
- **PM (Particulate Matter):** **Essentially zero** — CNG is a gas with no aromatic compounds, so no soot formation occurs.
- **CO₂:** ~**20-25% lower** per unit energy compared to gasoline.
- **Aldehydes (HCHO):** Slightly higher due to incomplete CH₄ oxidation, especially in cold start.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no soot in CNG?** Soot formation requires **polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAH)** as nucleation sites, which come from **aromatic rings** in the fuel. CNG has **zero aromatics**, hence **zero particulate mass emissions**.

### 2.2 Biofuels — Emission Characteristics Deep Dive

#### 2.2.1 Ethanol (C₂H₅OH)

Combustion: $C_2H_5OH + 3O_2 \rightarrow 2CO_2 + 3H_2O$

- **CO Reduction:** ~**30-50%** (oxygenated fuel promotes complete combustion).
- **HC Reduction:** ~**10-20%**.
- **NOx:** Variable — can **increase** due to higher flame temperature and **latent heat of vaporization** effects.
- **Aldehydes (Acetaldehyde):** **Significantly higher** — a known carcinogen.
- **CO₂:** Considered **carbon-neutral** (biogenic carbon).
- **PM:** Reduced in diesel blends.

#### 2.2.2 Biodiesel (FAME — Fatty Acid Methyl Esters)

- **PM Reduction:** ~**50-70%** (oxygen content ~10-11% in fuel molecule itself).
- **CO Reduction:** ~**40-50%**.
- **HC:** Reduced.
- **NOx:** Slightly **higher (~5-10%)** due to higher cetane, density, and bound oxygen.
- **Carbon Neutral:** Yes — life cycle CO₂ reduced by ~**50-80%**.
- **Sulfur:** Essentially zero (unlike fossil diesel).

#### 2.2.3 The Oxygenated Fuel Mechanism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bound Oxygen Theory:** Biofuels carry **oxygen atoms within the fuel molecule itself** (e.g., ethanol has one O, biodiesel has 2 O atoms in ester linkage). This **intramolecular oxygen** promotes complete combustion in fuel-rich zones, dramatically reducing **CO and PM** even where air-fuel mixing is imperfect.

### 2.3 Hydrogen — Emission Characteristics Deep Dive

#### 2.3.1 The Only Truly Zero-Carbon Fuel

$$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O + Energy \quad (\Delta H_c = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol at LHV})$$

- **CO₂: Absolute Zero** (no carbon in fuel).
- **CO: Zero** (no carbon).
- **HC: Zero** (no carbon).
- **PM: Zero** (no carbon).
- **NOx:** Only remaining concern. Can be **near-zero with lean operation** or low-temperature combustion strategies. Higher in stoichiometric conditions due to high flame temperature (~**2400 K**).
- **Unregulated emissions:** Trace **H₂O₂ (hydrogen peroxide)**, and minor **H₂ emissions** from crevice quenching.

#### 2.3.2 Hydrogen's NOx Challenge

> [!WARNING]
> Hydrogen's **adiabatic flame temperature is the highest** of all gaseous fuels (~**2400 K** for stoichiometric H₂-air). Since NOx formation follows the **Zeldovich mechanism** which is **exponentially sensitive to temperature**, hydrogen engines face a unique **NOx-versus-efficiency trade-off**. Solutions: **lean burn (λ > 1.5)**, **EGR**, and **water injection**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric AFR (mass) | $AFR_s = \dfrac{M_{air}}{M_{fuel}}$ | kg air/kg fuel | Engine calibration |
| 2 | Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | dimensionless | Combustion mode |
| 3 | Mass of CO₂ per kg fuel | $m_{CO_2} = \dfrac{44 \cdot N_C}{M_{fuel}}$ | kg CO₂/kg fuel | Carbon balance |
| 4 | Brake Specific Emission (BSE) | $BSE = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{pollutant}}{P_{brake}}$ | g/kWh | Emission regulation |
| 5 | Emission Index (EI) | $EI = \dfrac{mass\ of\ pollutant}{mass\ of\ fuel}$ | g/kg fuel | Combustion analysis |
| 6 | Zeldovich NOx | $NO_x \propto \exp\left(\dfrac{-E_a}{RT}\right)$ | ppm | Thermal NOx |
| 7 | Hydrogen LHV | $LHV_{H_2} = 119.96$ | MJ/kg | Energy content |
| 8 | CNG LHV (Methane) | $LHV_{CH_4} = 50.0$ | MJ/kg | Energy content |
| 9 | Ethanol LHV | $LHV_{C_2H_5OH} = 26.9$ | MJ/kg | Energy content |
| 10 | Biodiesel LHV | $LHV_{FAME} = 37.2$ | MJ/kg | Energy content |
| 11 | Carbon-to-Hydrogen Ratio | $C/H = \dfrac{n_C}{n_H}$ | dimensionless | CO₂ potential |
| 12 | Global Warming Potential (CH₄) | $GWP_{CH_4} = 28-36$ | vs CO₂ = 1 | Climate impact |
| 13 | Carbon Cycle Balance | $CO_{2,biogenic} = CO_{2,absorbed\ during\ growth}$ | kg | Net emissions |
| 14 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bt} = \dfrac{P_b}{\dot{m}_f \cdot LHV}$ | % | Engine performance |
| 15 | Equivalence air-fuel relation | $\lambda = 1/\phi$ | dimensionless | Mixture strength |

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **CNG**: Dominant in city buses, three-wheelers, and light commercial vehicles in India (Delhi, Ahmedabad) under **CNG Bharat Stage VI norms**.
- **Biofuels**: India's **Ethanol Blending Programme (E20 — 20% ethanol)** and **Biodiesel (B100)** mandate for agricultural sector.
- **Hydrogen**: **Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo FCEVs**; India's **National Hydrogen Mission (2021)** targets **5 million tonnes** annual production by 2030.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Each Fuel

The stoichiometric air-fuel ratio by mass is computed as:

$$AFR_s = \dfrac{M_{air}}{M_{fuel}} = \dfrac{2 \cdot (32 \cdot N_O)}{M_{fuel}}$$

where $M_{fuel}$ is the molecular weight of the fuel and $N_O$ is the number of oxygen atoms needed per molecule.

#### Step-by-Step Calculation:

**For Methane (CH₄) — CNG:**

Balanced equation: $CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$

O₂ required: **2 moles**
Mass of O₂: $2 \times 32 = 64$ g
Mass of air (with 23.2% O₂ by mass): $64 / 0.232 = 275.86$ g
Mass of CH₄: $12 + 4 = 16$ g

$$AFR_{s,CH_4} = \dfrac{275.86}{16} = 17.24$$

**For Ethanol (C₂H₅OH):**

Balanced equation: $C_2H_5OH + 3O_2 \rightarrow 2CO_2 + 3H_2O$

O₂ required: **3 moles**
Mass of O₂: $3 \times 32 = 96$ g
Mass of air: $96 / 0.232 = 413.79$ g
Mass of C₂H₅OH: $2(12) + 6(1) + 16 = 46$ g

$$AFR_{s,ethanol} = \dfrac{413.79}{46} = 9.00$$

**For Hydrogen (H₂):**

Balanced equation: $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$

O₂ required: **0.5 moles per mole of H₂**
Per kg H₂: moles of H₂ = $1000/2 = 500$ mol
O₂ needed = $500 \times 0.5 = 250$ mol
Mass of O₂ = $250 \times 32 = 8000$ g
Mass of air = $8000/0.232 = 34,482$ g

$$AFR_{s,H_2} = 34.48$$

**Summary Table:**

| Fuel | Stoichiometric AFR (mass) |
|---|---|
| Gasoline (C₈H₁₈) | 15.13 |
| Diesel (C₁₂H₂₆) | 14.6 |
| **CNG (CH₄)** | **17.24** |
| **Ethanol (C₂H₅OH)** | **9.00** |
| **Hydrogen (H₂)** | **34.48** |

### 3.2 Derivation 2: CO₂ Emission per Unit Energy (CO₂ Intensity)

The CO₂ emission per MJ of energy released is derived from:

$$CO_{2,intensity} = \dfrac{n_C \times 44 \times 1000}{M_{fuel} \times LHV_{fuel}} \quad \text{[g/MJ]}$$

#### Step-by-Step Calculation:

**For CNG (CH₄):**

$n_C = 1$ (one carbon per molecule)
$M_{fuel} = 16$ g/mol
$LHV = 50.0$ MJ/kg

$$CO_{2,intensity,CNG} = \dfrac{1 \times 44 \times 1000}{16 \times 50.0} = \dfrac{44,000}{800} = 55.0 \text{ g/MJ}$$

**For Ethanol (C₂H₅OH):**

$n_C = 2$
$M_{fuel} = 46$ g/mol
$LHV = 26.9$ MJ/kg

$$CO_{2,intensity,ethanol} = \dfrac{2 \times 44 \times 1000}{46 \times 26.9} = \dfrac{88,000}{1237.4} = 71.1 \text{ g/MJ}$$

**For Hydrogen (H₂):**

$n_C = 0$

$$CO_{2,intensity,H_2} = 0 \text{ g/MJ}$$

**For Comparison — Gasoline (C₈H₁₈):**

$n_C = 8$
$M_{fuel} = 114$ g/mol
$LHV = 44$ MJ/kg

$$CO_{2,intensity,gasoline} = \dfrac{8 \times 44 \times 1000}{114 \times 44} = \dfrac{352,000}{5016} = 70.2 \text{ g/MJ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Even though ethanol has **higher CO₂ intensity per MJ** than CNG, the **biogenic carbon** assumption means the net life-cycle emissions are still considered neutral. Hydrogen's zero in-cylinder CO₂ is offset by **upstream production emissions** unless produced via green electrolysis.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Lower Heating Value (LHV) Verification for Methane

The LHV of methane is computed from bond energies:

$$CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O_{(g)}$$

Energy balance:
- Bonds broken: 4 × C–H bonds = $4 \times 413 = 1652$ kJ/mol
- 2 × O=O bonds = $2 \times 498 = 996$ kJ/mol
- **Total input**: $1652 + 996 = 2648$ kJ/mol

- Bonds formed: 2 × C=O bonds = $2 \times 805 = 1610$ kJ/mol
- 4 × O–H bonds = $4 \times 463 = 1852$ kJ/mol
- **Total released**: $1610 + 1852 = 3462$ kJ/mol

$$\Delta H = 2648 - 3462 = -814 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$\Delta H \approx -802.3 \text{ kJ/mol (NIST standard value, accounts for zero-point energy)}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Brake-Specific Emissions Example

Given an engine produces **5 g CO per minute** at **10 kW brake power**, calculate **BSE_CO**:

$$BSE_{CO} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{CO}}{P_{brake}} = \dfrac{5 \text{ g/min}}{10 \text{ kW}}$$

Converting units:
$$\dot{m}_{CO} = 5 \text{ g/min} \times 60 \text{ min/hr} = 300 \text{ g/hr}$$

$$BSE_{CO} = \dfrac{300 \text{ g/hr}}{10 \text{ kW}} = 30 \text{ g/kWh}$$

### 3.5 Comparative Emission Matrix (vs Gasoline Baseline = 100%)

| Pollutant | Gasoline | CNG | Ethanol (E100) | Biodiesel (B100) | Hydrogen |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CO** | 100% | 10-30% | 50-70% | 50-60% | 0% |
| **HC (NMOG)** | 100% | 40-50% | 80-90% | 60-70% | 0% |
| **NOx** | 100% | 80-120% | 100-130% | 105-115% | 30-100% (lean) |
| **PM** | 100% | <5% | 20-40% | 20-40% | 0% |
| **CO₂ (well-to-wheel)** | 100% | 80-85% | 60-80% | 40-60% | 0-30% (renewable) |
| **SOx** | 100% | <5% | <10% | <5% | 0% |
| **Aldehydes** | Low | Moderate | High (CH₃CHO) | Moderate | Negligible |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Comparative Combustion Chemistry Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Alternate Fuel Selection] --> B{Carbon Content}
    B -->|Zero C| C[Hydrogen H2]
    B -->|Low C| D[CNG CH4]
    B -->|Renewable C| E[Biofuels]
    C --> C1[Product: H2O Only]
    C --> C2[NOx Concern: Thermal]
    D --> D1[Products: CO2 + H2O]
    D --> D2[Low PM, Low CO]
    E --> E1[Ethanol: CO2 + H2O + CH3CHO]
    E --> E2[Biodiesel: CO2 + H2O + PM less]
    C1 --> F[Emission Profile]
    C2 --> F
    D1 --> F
    D2 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Final Tailpipe: CO, HC, NOx, PM, CO2, Aldehydes]
    style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#333
```

### 4.2 Engine Emission Reduction Mechanism (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Fuel Input] --> B[Atomization and Mixing]
    B --> C[Combustion Chamber]
    C --> D{Equivalence Ratio}
    D -->|Stoichiometric lambda=1| E[3-Way Catalyst Active]
    D -->|Lean lambda greater than 1| F[Lean NOx Trap]
    D -->|Rich lambda less than 1| G[CO and HC Oxidation]
    C --> H[Tailpipe Emissions]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Post-Treatment: TWC, SCR, DPF, ASC]
    I --> J[Atmospheric Release]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#333
    style J fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#333
```

### 4.3 CNG Engine Subsystem Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CNG Storage Cylinder 200-250 bar] --> B[High Pressure Regulator]
    B --> C[Low Pressure Regulator]
    C --> D[Solenoid Valve]
    D --> E[CNG Mixer or Sequential Injector]
    F[Air Intake Filter] --> E
    E --> G[Intake Manifold]
    G --> H[Combustion Chamber SI Engine]
    H --> I[Exhaust Manifold]
    I --> J[3-Way Catalyst TWC]
    J --> K[Lambda Sensor O2]
    K --> D
    J --> L[Tailpipe]
    L --> M[Emissions: CO2, H2O, Low NOx, Zero PM]
    style A fill:#ffe1cc,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#333
    style M fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#333
```

### 4.4 Hydrogen Fuel Cell Vehicle Architecture (For Reference)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[H2 Tank 350-700 bar] --> B[Pressure Regulator]
    B --> C[Fuel Cell Stack PEM]
    D[Ambient Air] --> E[Air Compressor]
    E --> C
    C --> F[DC-DC Converter]
    F --> G[Li-ion Battery Buffer]
    G --> H[Electric Motor]
    H --> I[Wheels]
    C --> J[Water Vapor Exhaust]
    style C fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#333
    style J fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The above block diagrams show the **functional architecture** of emission control systems for each alternate fuel pathway. Note that the **Hydrogen Fuel Cell pathway** has **zero combustion** — emission is only **water vapor**, making it the cleanest option from a tailpipe perspective.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**"Why does CNG produce negligible particulate matter in SI engines?"** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
CNG consists primarily of methane (CH₄), a simple saturated hydrocarbon with **no aromatic compounds and no long carbon chains**. Particulate matter (soot) formation in IC engines requires **polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAH)** as nucleation precursors, which originate from **aromatic rings** in heavier fuels. Since CNG has a **C/H ratio of 0.25** (very low) and **zero aromatics**, the **incomplete combustion products do not form soot nuclei**, leading to **PM emissions <5% of gasoline levels**. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**"What is methane slip and why is it a climate concern despite CNG being a clean fuel?"** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
**Methane slip** refers to the **unburnt CH₄** that escapes through the tailpipe in CNG-fuelled engines, particularly during cold start and transient operation. While CH₄ is non-toxic, it is a **potent greenhouse gas with a Global Warming Potential (GWP) of 28-36 over 100 years** compared to CO₂. Thus, even small mass emissions of CH₄ contribute disproportionately to **climate forcing**. Three-way catalysts and oxidation catalysts are used to oxidize CH₄ to CO₂, but CH₄ is **chemically stable** and requires **higher light-off temperatures (~400-500°C)** to be effectively oxidized. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]*

**(a)** Compare the **emission characteristics of CNG, ethanol, and hydrogen** in SI engines, with specific reference to CO, HC, NOx, and PM. Highlight the underlying combustion chemistry responsible for the differences. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of stoichiometric calculations, determine the **mass of CO₂ produced per MJ of energy released** for gasoline (C₈H₁₈) and CNG (CH₄). Given: LHV of gasoline = 44 MJ/kg, LHV of methane = 50 MJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Comparative Emission Analysis (7 marks)

**Comparative Table:**

| Pollutant | CNG (CH₄) | Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) | Hydrogen (H₂) | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CO** | Very low (10-30% of gasoline) | Low (50-70%) | **Zero** | H₂ has no C; CNG has 1C/molecule; ethanol oxygenation helps |
| **HC** | Low (40-50%) but CH₄ slip | Moderate; acetaldehyde HCHO | **Zero** | H₂ fully combusts to H₂O |
| **NOx** | Higher (80-120%) | Slightly higher (100-130%) | **Moderate-High** (depends on λ) | All three have high flame temps |
| **PM** | **Negligible** (<5%) | Reduced (20-40%) | **Zero** | Absence of aromatics in CNG/H₂ |

**Underlying Chemistry:**

- **CNG**: $CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$. Only **1 carbon per molecule** → minimal CO₂; **no aromatics** → no PAH formation → no PM. Methane slip occurs in crevices due to low quenching distance interaction.

- **Ethanol**: $C_2H_5OH + 3O_2 \rightarrow 2CO_2 + 3H_2O$. **Intramolecular oxygen** assists oxidation in fuel-rich zones. **Acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO)** is an intermediate product from partial oxidation — a known unregulated emission.

- **Hydrogen**: $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$. **Zero carbon** → no CO₂, CO, HC, or PM. **NOx** arises purely from the **Zeldovich thermal mechanism** due to high adiabatic flame temperature (~2400 K).

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step scoring guide (7 marks):** 1 mark for CNG, 1 for Ethanol, 1 for H₂; 2 marks for table; 2 marks for chemistry equations.

#### Part (b) — Stoichiometric CO₂ Calculations (7 marks)

**Step 1: CO₂ produced per kg of fuel**

For **Gasoline (C₈H₁₈):**
- 1 mole of C₈H₁₈ (M = 114 g) → 8 moles of CO₂ (M = 8 × 44 = 352 g)

Per kg of gasoline:
$$\dfrac{352 \text{ g CO}_2}{114 \text{ g fuel}} \times 1000 = 3087.7 \text{ g CO}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

For **CNG (CH₄):**
- 1 mole of CH₄ (M = 16 g) → 1 mole of CO₂ (M = 44 g)

Per kg of CNG:
$$\dfrac{44 \text{ g CO}_2}{16 \text{ g fuel}} \times 1000 = 2750 \text{ g CO}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Step 2: CO₂ per MJ of energy (Intensity)**

For Gasoline:
$$\dfrac{3087.7 \text{ g/kg}}{44 \text{ MJ/kg}} = 70.17 \text{ g/MJ}$$

For CNG:
$$\dfrac{2750 \text{ g/kg}}{50 \text{ MJ/kg}} = 55.0 \text{ g/MJ}$$

**Step 3: Comparison and Interpretation**

$$\Delta CO_2 = 70.17 - 55.0 = 15.17 \text{ g/MJ}$$

$$\text{Reduction} = \dfrac{15.17}{70.17} \times 100\% = 21.6\%$$

**Conclusion:** CNG produces **~21.6% less CO₂ per unit energy** compared to gasoline, even before considering its biogenic/methane origins. This is a direct consequence of the lower C/H ratio (0.25 vs 0.44 for C₈H₁₈).

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step scoring (7 marks):** 1 mark for balanced equation, 1 for molar mass, 1 for per-kg CO₂ each fuel, 1 for LHV application, 1 for intensity calculation, 1 for percentage comparison, 1 for conclusion.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]*

**(a)** Discuss the **emission characteristics of biodiesel (B100)** in compression ignition engines, with specific emphasis on **NOx, PM, CO, and life-cycle CO₂**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain why **hydrogen engines** face a unique **NOx-versus-efficiency trade-off**, and discuss **two engineering strategies** to mitigate NOx in hydrogen-fuelled IC engines. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Biodiesel Emission Characteristics (7 marks)

**Biodiesel (FAME — Fatty Acid Methyl Esters)** has the general formula $R-COO-CH_3$, where R is a long hydrocarbon chain (C₁₅-C₂₁).

**Key Emission Outcomes:**

1. **PM Reduction (50-70%):** Biodiesel has **10-11% bound oxygen** in its molecular structure. This **intramolecular oxygen** allows more complete combustion in fuel-rich zones of the spray plume, eliminating **soot precursor formation**. **(2 marks)**

2. **CO Reduction (40-50%):** Improved oxidation efficiency due to fuel-bound oxygen and higher cetane number. **(1 mark)**

3. **NOx Increase (5-15%):** Biodiesel's higher **bulk modulus** leads to advanced injection timing, higher in-cylinder peak temperatures, and greater premixed combustion. Also, the **oxygen content** accelerates local high-temperature reactions, promoting **thermal NOx via the Zeldovich mechanism**. **(2 marks)**

4. **HC and CO₂:** HC reduced; CO₂ considered **biogenic/carbon neutral** since the plant feedstock absorbed equivalent CO₂ during growth. **Life-cycle CO₂ reduction: 50-80%** compared to fossil diesel. **(2 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's mark split:** Each pollutant category → 1-2 marks; bound oxygen concept → 1 mark; NOx increase mechanism → 1 mark; net conclusion → 1 mark.

#### Part (b) — Hydrogen Engine NOx Trade-off (7 marks)

**The Core Problem:**

Hydrogen's **adiabatic flame temperature at stoichiometric conditions is ~2400 K** — the **highest of all common gaseous fuels**. The Zeldovich mechanism for NOx formation is:

$$N_2 + O \rightleftharpoons NO + N$$
$$N + O_2 \rightleftharpoons NO + O$$
$$N + OH \rightleftharpoons NO + H$$

The rate of NO formation is **exponentially dependent on temperature**:
$$r_{NO} \propto \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

Therefore, hydrogen engines produce **high NOx in stoichiometric operation**. **(2 marks)**

**Efficiency-NOx Conflict:** Higher loads → higher peak temperatures → higher efficiency, but also **exponentially higher NOx**. **(1 mark)**

**Engineering Mitigation Strategies:**

**Strategy 1: Lean Burn Operation (λ = 1.5-2.5)**
- Excess air **absorbs combustion heat**, reducing peak flame temperature by **~200-400 K**.
- NOx reduces by **70-90%**.
- Trade-off: Power density decreases; requires larger displacement for equivalent output. **(2 marks)**

**Strategy 2: Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)**
- Inert exhaust gas (mainly CO₂, H₂O) **dilutes the charge** and **reduces specific heat capacity**, lowering peak temperatures.
- 10-20% EGR can cut NOx by **~50%**.
- EGR also suppresses knock in hydrogen SI engines. **(2 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often state that hydrogen produces "zero NOx" — this is **incorrect** for stoichiometric hydrogen engines. Hydrogen engines must be operated **lean** or with EGR to achieve low NOx.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Avoid the "Zero Emission" trap for hydrogen** — Hydrogen is **zero-carbon**, but NOT zero-emission due to NOx at stoichiometric conditions.
> 2. **Don't confuse biofuel CO₂ neutrality** — In-cylinder CO₂ is emitted; only **life-cycle** CO₂ is considered neutral due to biogenic carbon uptake during growth.
> 3. **Biodiesel NOx is HIGHER, not lower** — This is a common exam trap. Always mention the **bound oxygen + bulk modulus** reason.
> 4. **Methane slip is unburnt CH₄** — High GWP makes it climate-relevant even if it seems "natural."
> 5. **Always state LHV values** in numerical problems — examiners explicitly check for units and reference values.
> 6. **Stoichiometric AFR is mass-based** — Don't confuse with mole-based AFR.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Recap — KTU High-Yield Points**

- **CNG (CH₄):** Lowest C/H ratio among hydrocarbons; **zero PM, low CO, low HC**; concerns are **CH₄ slip** and slightly **higher NOx**. Stoichiometric AFR = **17.24**. CO₂ intensity = **55 g/MJ**.

- **Hydrogen (H₂):** **Zero carbon emissions**; only regulated pollutant is **NOx (thermal)**; solutions are **lean burn + EGR + water injection**. Stoichiometric AFR = **34.48**. LHV = **119.96 MJ/kg** (highest of all fuels).

- **Ethanol (C₂H₅OH):** Oxygenated; reduces **CO and PM**; produces **acetaldehyde** (toxic); slightly higher **NOx**; considered **carbon-neutral**. Stoichiometric AFR = **9.00**.

- **Biodiesel (FAME):** Reduces **PM by 50-70%** and **CO by 40-50%**; **NOx increases 5-15%** (KEE exam point); life-cycle CO₂ reduced by **50-80%**; **zero sulfur**; 10-11% bound oxygen in fuel.

- **C/H Ratio** is the master parameter for CO₂ potential: H₂ (0) < CNG (0.25) < Ethanol (0.33) < Gasoline (0.5) < Diesel (0.66).

- **GWP of methane = 28-36** → methane slip is a critical climate concern.

- **Zeldovich NOx mechanism** is exponentially temperature-dependent; lean operation and EGR are universal NOx reduction tools.

- **Bound oxygen in biofuel** = oxygen atoms within the fuel molecule that promote in-cylinder complete combustion in fuel-rich zones.

- **LHV values to memorize:** H₂ = 119.96, CH₄ = 50.0, Ethanol = 26.9, Biodiesel = 37.2, Gasoline = 44.0, Diesel = 42.5 (all in MJ/kg).

- **India-specific context:** **E20 (20% ethanol blending)** by 2025; **CNG as city bus fuel**; **National Hydrogen Mission 5 MTPA by 2030**.

- **Formula memory anchors:** $CO_{2,intensity} = (44 \times n_C \times 1000)/(M_{fuel} \times LHV)$ and $AFR_s = 4.773 \times N_O / M_{fuel}$ (where 4.773 = 32/0.232/2).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emission Measurement Techniques and Instruments - Procedure of Emission Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Emission Measurement Techniques and Instruments - Procedure of Emission Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Emission Measurement** is the standardized, scientifically controlled process of quantifying the mass, concentration, and chemical composition of regulated gaseous pollutants (CO, CO₂, HC, NOₓ) and particulate matter (PM) discharged from an internal combustion engine or motor vehicle, executed through certified sampling, conditioning, and analytical instrumentation in compliance with statutory test cycles such as the Indian Driving Cycle (IDC), Modified Indian Driving Cycle (MIDC), or World Motorcycle Test Cycle (WMTC).

In the context of KTU's PEMUT522 syllabus, the *Procedure of Emission Measurement* is the end-to-end methodology — beginning from the preparation of the test vehicle and dynamometer, through exhaust extraction and dilution, to the final computation of mass emissions per unit distance (g/km) or per unit energy (g/kWh).

The principal regulated pollutants measured are:

- **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** — product of incomplete combustion
- **Unburned Hydrocarbons (HC)** — released from crevices, walls, and fuel pockets
- **Oxides of Nitrogen (NOₓ)** — formed at high in-cylinder temperatures
- **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** — greenhouse gas indicator
- **Particulate Matter (PM)** — especially for diesel engines
- **Smoke Opacity** — visible black smoke from diesel engines

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Engine is the Patient; The Emission Analyzer is the Doctor"**

Imagine taking a patient (your engine) to a hospital for a complete health check-up. You cannot just glance at the patient and pronounce them healthy. You must:

1. **Prepare the patient** (warm-up, baseline conditions) → *Vehicle preconditioning*
2. **Run stress tests** (treadmill, ECG under load) → *Chassis dynamometer + driving cycle*
3. **Collect blood/air samples** through tubes → *Constant Volume Sampling (CVS)*
4. **Analyze samples in laboratory** (CO, O₂, glucose meters) → *NDIR, FID, Chemiluminescence analyzers*
5. **Cross-reference with healthy range** (CO < 2.3 g/km) → *Comparison with Bharat Stage VI limits*
6. **Issue a report** → *Type-Approval Certificate*

The **procedure of emission measurement** is precisely this clinical workflow applied to the exhaust pipe of a vehicle.

## 1.3 Why This Procedure Matters

> [!NOTE]
> Without a **standardized procedure**, two different labs testing the "same" vehicle could report drastically different emission values. The procedure eliminates ambiguity by fixing:
> - **Driver behavior** → Reproducible driving cycle
> - **Engine loading** → Dynamometer road-load simulation
> - **Sample handling** → Constant Volume Sampling (CVS)
> - **Analytical technique** → Approved analyzers with defined operating principles
> - **Boundary conditions** → Ambient temperature **20 °C to 30 °C**, humidity, atmospheric pressure corrections

The unified procedural framework ensures that emission compliance is verifiable, repeatable, and legally defensible.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Standardized Procedure — A 6-Stage Workflow

The Indian regulatory procedure (AIS-137 / CMVR / Bharat Stage VI) breaks emission measurement into the following sequential stages:

### **Stage 1 — Pre-Test Preparation**
- Vehicle soak at **20 °C – 30 °C** for minimum 6 hours
- Tyre pressure check (manufacturer spec ± 0.2 bar)
- Battery, fuel, lubricant, coolant verification
- Driver/operator training for cycle adherence
- Ambient **temperature, pressure, humidity** recording

### **Stage 2 — Dynamometer Setup**
- Chassis dynamometer alignment
- **Road load coefficient** determination:

$$
F = A + B \cdot v + C \cdot v^2
$$

where **F** is the tractive force (N), **v** is velocity (km/h), and **A, B, C** are coast-down coefficients representing rolling resistance, drivetrain friction, and aerodynamic drag respectively.

- Equivalent inertia mass selection (matches vehicle curb mass class)

### **Stage 3 — Driving Cycle Execution**
The vehicle is driven on the dynamometer rollers following a prescribed speed-vs-time trace:

| Cycle | Application | Duration | Distance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **IDC** (Indian Driving Cycle) | 2-Wheelers ≤ 150 cc | 1800 s | ~12 km |
| **MIDC** (Modified IDC) | 4-Wheelers (BS-VI) | 1220 s | ~11 km |
| **WMTC** (World Motorcycle Test Cycle) | Motorcycles | 1800 s | ~14 km |
| **NEDC / WLTP** | Passenger Cars (Europe) | 1220 s | ~10 km |

### **Stage 4 — Exhaust Sampling (CVS System)**
Raw exhaust is mixed with filtered ambient air inside a **Constant Volume Sampling (CVS)** tunnel. The diluted mixture is collected in **Tedlar bags** (one per phase of the cycle: Urban, Extra-Urban, or Cold-Start, Hot-Start).

### **Stage 5 — Analyzer Analysis**
- **CO, CO₂, HC** → Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) analyzer
- **HC (low concentration)** → Flame Ionization Detector (FID)
- **NOₓ** → Chemiluminescence Analyzer (CLA) or Non-Dispersive UV (NDUV)
- **PM** → Gravimetric filter weighing (47 mm Pallflex filters)
- **Smoke** → Opacimeter (Bosch / Hartridge)

### **Stage 6 — Computation & Reporting**
Bag concentrations are converted to **mass per km** (or **g/kWh** for engines) using the CVS total volume, pollutant density, and cycle distance.

## 2.2 The Five Major Analyzers — Working Principles

### **(A) NDIR — Non-Dispersive Infrared Analyzer**
- **Pollutants:** CO, CO₂, HC
- **Principle:** Each heteroatomic gas absorbs IR radiation at a unique wavelength (CO at **4.67 µm**, CO₂ at **4.26 µm**, HC near **3.4 µm**).
- **Construction:** IR source → rotating chopper → sample cell → reference cell → detector (microphone or solid-state).
- **Output:** Beer's Law relationship between absorbed energy and concentration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> NDIR is called "non-dispersive" because the IR beam is NOT split by a prism/grating — instead, **selective optical filters** or **gas-filled detection cells** isolate the desired wavelength.

### **(B) FID — Flame Ionization Detector**
- **Pollutants:** Total HC (excluding oxygenated compounds)
- **Principle:** A hydrogen flame burning in hydrocarbon-free air produces a baseline ion current. When HC molecules are introduced, carbon atoms are ionized (C + O → C⁺ + e⁻), causing current proportional to carbon mass.
- **Sensitivity:** Sub-ppm level (most sensitive HC analyzer)

### **(C) Chemiluminescence Analyzer (CLA)**
- **Pollutants:** NO, NO₂ (hence total NOₓ)
- **Principle:** NO + O₃ → NO₂* + O₂ → NO₂ + hν (light at **600–875 nm**)
- **Procedure:** Sample is split — one stream measures NO directly, the other passes through a **molybdenum converter** at **~315 °C** that reduces NO₂ to NO. The difference yields NO₂.

### **(D) NDUV — Non-Dispersive Ultraviolet Analyzer**
- **Pollutants:** NO, NO₂
- **Principle:** NO₂ absorbs UV at **400–450 nm**; similar Beer-Lambert application as NDIR but in UV range.

### **(E) Smoke Opacimeters**

| Type | Measurement Principle | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bosch Smoke Meter** | Filter-paper stains exhaust; reflects light off soot patch | **Bosch Number (0–10)** |
| **Hartridge Opacimeter** | Full-flow light extinction through exhaust plume | **Opacity (%) / k (m⁻¹)** |
| **AVL Smoke Meter** | Partial flow, light absorption | **Smoke number (FSN)** |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **must-memorize equations** for KTU university exams. Master the variables, units, and sign conventions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $m_{pollutant} = V_{mix} \times \rho_{pollutant} \times \frac{c_{pollutant}}{10^6}$ | Mass of pollutant from CVS bag | g |
| 2 | $E_{km} = \dfrac{m_{pollutant}}{D_{cycle}}$ | Mass emission per km | g/km |
| 3 | $k = -\dfrac{1}{L} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | Light absorption coefficient | m⁻¹ |
| 4 | $N = 100 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | Opacity percentage | % |
| 5 | $C_{corr} = C_{meas} \times \dfrac{P_0}{P} \times \dfrac{T}{T_0}$ | Concentration correction to standard conditions | ppm |
| 6 | $\rho_{NO_2} = \dfrac{M_{NO_2}}{V_{molar}}$ | Density of NO₂ for mass conversion | g/L |
| 7 | $F = A + Bv + Cv^2$ | Road load equation (chassis dyno) | N |
| 8 | $m_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \times 3.16$ | CO₂ from fuel mass (approx.) | g |
| 9 | $\eta_{c} = \dfrac{[CO_2]}{[CO_2] + [CO] + [HC]}$ | Combustion efficiency index | — |
| 10 | $Q_{CVS} = Q_{dilution} + Q_{exhaust}$ | Total CVS volumetric flow | m³/min |

> **Standard reference conditions:** T₀ = 273.15 K, P₀ = 101.325 kPa

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The procedure described here is the **backbone of the BS-VI / Euro 6 / EPA Tier 3 regulatory regime** worldwide. Engineers working in:
- **OEM test cells** (Tata, Maruti, Ashok Leyland, Hero)
- **Type-approval agencies** (ARAI, iCAT, VRDE)
- **Emission certification labs** (C-FAR)
- **Aftermarket retrofit industries** (DPF, SCR, EGR validation)

…all rely on this exact procedure daily. A graduate with mastery of these steps is immediately industry-ready for **powertrain calibration, aftertreatment validation, and homologation engineering** roles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass of Pollutant from CVS Bag Concentration

### Problem Statement
A vehicle is tested on the MIDC cycle. The CVS tunnel dilutes the exhaust with filtered ambient air. The diluted mixture is collected in a Tedlar bag. The **bag analyzer** reports the pollutant concentration in **ppm** (parts per million by volume). Derive the formula for the mass of pollutant emitted during the cycle.

### Given
- Diluted bag volume (at standard conditions): $V_{mix}$ in m³
- Measured bag concentration: $c$ in ppm (volume basis)
- Pollutant molar mass: $M$ in g/mol
- Molar volume at STP: $V_m = 0.02241$ m³/mol

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert ppm to volume fraction**

$$
c_{vol} = \frac{c}{10^6}
$$

**Step 2 — Volume of pollutant in the bag**

$$
V_{pollutant} = c_{vol} \times V_{mix} = \frac{c \times V_{mix}}{10^6}
$$

**Step 3 — Moles of pollutant present**

$$
n = \frac{V_{pollutant}}{V_m} = \frac{c \times V_{mix}}{10^6 \times V_m}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of pollutant (using molar mass M)**

$$
m = n \times M = \frac{c \times V_{mix} \times M}{10^6 \times V_m}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $V_m = 0.02241$ m³/mol and define $\rho = M / V_m$ (gas density at STP)**

$$
m = V_{mix} \times \rho \times \frac{c}{10^6}
$$

This is the master CVS equation.

**Step 6 — Numerical Check (for CO at 50 ppm, V_mix = 8 m³)**

$$
\rho_{CO} = \frac{28.01}{0.02241} = 1250 \text{ g/m}^3
$$

$$
m_{CO} = 8 \times 1250 \times \frac{50}{10^6} = 0.50 \text{ g}
$$

If the MIDC distance is 11.0 km:

$$
E_{CO} = \frac{0.50}{11.0} = 0.0455 \text{ g/km}
$$

This is well under the BS-VI limit of **1.0 g/km** for passenger cars.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Opacity from Light Extinction

### Problem Statement
A Hartridge opacimeter measures the intensity of light passing through a column of diesel exhaust. Derive the relationship between opacity, light absorption coefficient, and path length.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Beer-Lambert Law for a homogeneous gas column**

$$
I = I_0 \, e^{-kL}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the absorption coefficient k**

$$
k = -\frac{1}{L} \ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Define opacity N (% light blocked)**

$$
N = 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{I}{I_0}\right) = 100 \times \left(1 - e^{-kL}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Invert to obtain k from N**

$$
\frac{I}{I_0} = 1 - \frac{N}{100}
$$

$$
k = -\frac{1}{L} \ln\!\left(1 - \frac{N}{100}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (Bosch type, L = 0.10 m, N = 40%)**

$$
k = -\frac{1}{0.10} \ln(0.60) = -10 \times (-0.5108) = 5.11 \text{ m}^{-1}
$$

This is the linear absorption coefficient — directly comparable to the **BS-VI smoke limit of k ≤ 1.0 m⁻¹ for steady-state loaded testing**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mass Emission per kWh (For Engine Bench Testing)

For engines tested on an **engine dynamometer** (no driving cycle, no distance), emissions are reported as **g/kWh**:

$$
E_{kWh} = \frac{\dot{m}_{pollutant} \times 3600}{P_{brake} \times t}
$$

Equivalently, integrated over a test interval:

$$
E_{kWh} = \frac{m_{pollutant}}{W_{cycle}} = \frac{m_{pollutant}}{P_{brake} \times t}
$$

where $W_{cycle}$ is the brake work over the test cycle.

**Numerical Example (ESC test, 13-mode cycle):**
- Brake work over 13 modes = **47.7 kWh** (standard reference)
- Total NOₓ emitted = 6.5 g

$$
E_{NO_x} = \frac{6.5}{47.7} = 0.136 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

Compare with BS-VI heavy-duty limit of **0.46 g/kWh**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Analyzer Selection Decision Table

| Pollutant | Concentration Range | Preferred Analyzer | Alternative | Response Time |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CO** | 0.1 – 10 % | NDIR | Electrochemical (portable) | < 2 s |
| **CO₂** | 1 – 16 % | NDIR | — | < 2 s |
| **HC** | 1 – 10000 ppm | FID | NDIR (low range) | < 1 s |
| **NO** | 1 – 5000 ppm | CLA | NDUV, Electrochemical | < 3 s |
| **NO₂** | 1 – 1000 ppm | CLA (post-converter) | NDUV | < 3 s |
| **PM** | 0.1 – 100 mg/m³ | Gravimetric | — | minutes |
| **Smoke** | Opacity 0 – 100 % | Opacimeter (Hartridge) | Bosch filter-paper | < 1 s |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Procedure Quality Checks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU exam questions often test the **QC procedures** that must accompany emission measurement. Memorize these acceptance criteria.

| Quality Check | Acceptance Criterion | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Zero gas response** | Drift < 1 % of full scale in 30 min | Baseline stability |
| **Span gas response** | Within ± 2 % of certified value | Calibration accuracy |
| **System response time** | < 1.5 s for CO/CO₂, < 2 s for HC, < 3 s for NO | Transient cycle fidelity |
| **Sample line temperature** | 191 °C ± 5 °C for HC (FID) | Prevent HC condensation |
| **Ambient temperature** | 20 °C – 30 °C during test | Reference condition |
| **Bag leak check** | < 0.5 % volume loss per hour | Integrity of sample |
| **Analyzer warm-up** | Min 30 min before test | Sensor stabilization |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Emission Measurement Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Vehicle Preparation] --> B[Stage 2: Dynamometer Setup]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Driving Cycle Execution]
    C --> D[Stage 4: CVS Exhaust Sampling]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Bag Analysis via Analyzers]
    E --> F[Stage 6: Mass Emission Calculation]
    F --> G[Compare with BS-VI Limits]
    G --> H{Compliant?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Issue Type Approval]
    H -->|No| J[Rectify / Reject]

    A1[Soak 6 hrs at 25 C] -.-> A
    A2[Tyre Pressure Check] -.-> A
    A3[Fuel / Battery Check] -.-> A
    B1[Set Road Load F = A + Bv + Cv^2] -.-> B
    B2[Select Equivalent Inertia] -.-> B
    C1[Follow IDC / MIDC / WMTC] -.-> C
    C2[Driver Adheres to Trace within 2 km/h] -.-> C
    D1[Mix Exhaust with Dilution Air] -.-> D
    D2[Collect in Tedlar Bags] -.-> D
    E1[NDIR for CO and CO2] -.-> E
    E2[FID for HC] -.-> E
    E3[CLA for NOx] -.-> E
    E4[Opacimeter for Smoke] -.-> E
    F1[Apply m = V_mix x rho x c / 10^6] -.-> F
    F2[Divide by Distance D] -.-> F
```

## 4.2 CVS System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[Vehicle on Dyno] -->|Raw Exhaust| EP[Exhaust Probe]
    EP -->|Heated Line 191 C| CVST[Constant Volume Tunnel]
    DA[Dilution Air Filter] -->|Filtered Ambient Air| CVST
    CVST --> CF[Critical Flow Venturi]
    CF --> B1[Bag 1 Urban Phase]
    CF --> B2[Bag 2 Extra Urban Phase]
    B1 --> AN1[NDIR Analyzer]
    B2 --> AN1
    AN1 -->|CO and CO2 ppm| AN2[FID for HC]
    AN2 --> AN3[Chemiluminescence for NOx]
    AN3 -->|Concentrations| COMP[Computation Unit]
    COMP -->|Mass per km| REPORT[Final Report]
```

## 4.3 NDIR Optical Path — Functional Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IRS[IR Source] --> CH[Rotating Chopper]
    CH --> SC[Sample Cell with Exhaust Gas]
    SC --> RC[Reference Cell with N2]
    RC --> DET[Selective Detector with Gas Filter]
    DET --> AMP[AC Amplifier and Demodulator]
    AMP --> DSP[Linearizer and Output]
    DSP --> DISP[Concentration Display ppm]
```

## 4.4 Chemiluminescence NOₓ Analyzer — Functional Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Exhaust Sample] --> SP[Sample Splitter]
```
