```mermaid
    SP --> CH1[Chamber A: Direct NO]
    SP --> MC[Molybdenum Converter 315 C]
    MC --> CH2[Chamber B: Total NOx minus NO equals NO2]
    O3[Ozone Generator] --> CH1
    O3 --> CH2
    CH1 --> PMT1[Photomultiplier Tube]
    CH2 --> PMT1
    PMT1 --> AMP[Amplifier]
    AMP --> DIFF[Subtract A from B]
    DIFF --> NO2[NO2 Concentration Output]
    CH1 --> NO[NO Concentration Output]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`

**List the main components of an automotive exhaust gas analyzer used for measuring CO, HC and NOₓ emissions.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

An automotive exhaust gas analyzer (bench-type, as per AIS-137 / BS-VI procedure) consists of the following main components:

1. **Sample probe and heated sample line** — extracts raw or diluted exhaust and transports it at **191 °C ± 5 °C** to prevent HC condensation (1 mark)
2. **Sample conditioning unit** — includes particulate filter, water separator (chiller/condenser), and flow regulator to deliver clean, dry, isothermal sample to the analyzers (1 mark)
3. **Analyzer bank** — three analyzers: **NDIR** (for CO and CO₂), **FID** (for HC) and **Chemiluminescence Analyzer** (for NO/NO₂), each with built-in zero/span calibration gas inlet ports (1 mark)

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024 Style]`

**What is Constant Volume Sampling (CVS)? Why is it used in emission measurement procedures?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Constant Volume Sampling (CVS) is a dilution-based exhaust sampling technique in which the entire raw exhaust from the vehicle is mixed with a controlled, metered flow of filtered ambient dilution air inside a large mixing tunnel, such that the **total diluted flow rate (Q_CVS) remains constant** during the entire test cycle, regardless of engine speed or load variations.

**Purpose (1.5 marks):** CVS is used because (i) it prevents saturation of analyzers when raw exhaust concentration spikes occur during transient accelerations, (ii) it enables **bag-averaging of emissions** over a complete driving cycle, and (iii) it provides a known, fixed total volume (V_mix) that allows direct computation of mass emissions using the bag concentrations.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> Students often lose marks in Part B by: (a) drawing the diagram *without* labeling the parts, (b) writing the principle but skipping the **procedure steps**, (c) forgetting to mention **calibration/zero-span** checks, and (d) omitting units in numerical problems. Always follow the 4-quadrant answer format: **Principle + Construction + Procedure + Significance**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) analyzer** for measuring CO in exhaust gas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe in detail the **step-by-step procedure for emission measurement** of a four-wheeled passenger car under the Modified Indian Driving Cycle (MIDC) as per BS-VI regulations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — NDIR Analyzer Working (7 marks)

**1. Principle (2 marks):**
Carbon monoxide (CO) is a heteroatomic gas with a permanent electric dipole moment. It absorbs infrared (IR) radiation at a characteristic wavelength of **4.67 µm**. The intensity of absorption follows the **Beer-Lambert Law**:

$$
I = I_0 \, e^{-kCL}
$$

where $I_0$ is incident intensity, $I$ is transmitted intensity, $k$ is absorptivity, $C$ is concentration, and $L$ is optical path length. Hence, by measuring $I/I_0$, concentration $C$ is determined.

**2. Construction / Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**
- **IR Source** — a heated nichrome filament emitting broadband IR
- **Rotating Chopper** — modulates the IR beam into pulses (provides AC reference signal)
- **Sample Cell** — flow-through chamber through which the exhaust sample passes
- **Reference Cell** — sealed chamber filled with pure N₂ (no absorption)
- **Selective Detector** — dual-chamber design; the front chamber is filled with the gas of interest (CO), acting as a **narrow-band optical filter**, the rear chamber is filled with a non-absorbing gas
- **Condenser Microphone / Solid-State Sensor** — detects pressure or thermal changes
- **Amplifier and Display**

**3. Working (2 marks):**
- IR passes through both sample and reference cells simultaneously
- CO in the sample cell absorbs IR at 4.67 µm
- The detector's front chamber is filled with CO — it filters out the 4.67 µm wavelength
- Only the energy absorbed by CO in the sample cell reaches the rear chamber, creating a pressure differential
- The microphone converts this differential to an electrical signal proportional to CO concentration
- The chopper enables AC amplification for noise rejection

**4. Significance (1 mark):**
NDIR offers fast response (< 2 s), high selectivity, and ppm-level sensitivity for CO, CO₂, and HC — making it the **workhorse of automotive emission test cells**.

---

#### Part (b) — MIDC Procedure (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Pre-test Vehicle Preparation (1 mark):**
- Soak the vehicle for minimum 6 hours at ambient temperature **20 °C – 30 °C**
- Check tyre pressure (manufacturer spec), battery state, fuel, lubricants
- Pre-condition the vehicle by running one full MIDC cycle to warm up the engine (cold-start cycle is then performed from cold)

**Step 2 — Dynamometer Setup (1 mark):**
- Place vehicle on chassis dynamometer
- Determine **road-load coefficients (A, B, C)** by coast-down test:

$$
F = A + Bv + Cv^2
$$

- Select equivalent inertia mass class as per AIS-137
- Calibrate dynamometer load setting

**Step 3 — Cold-Start MIDC Execution (1.5 marks):**
- Engine started at t = 0 s
- Driver follows the prescribed speed-time trace of MIDC (**1220 seconds** total)
- Trace consists of three phases: **Urban (0–800 s)**, **Extra-Urban (800–1220 s)**, with controlled idling, accelerations, cruise, and decelerations
- Allowable speed tolerance: **±2 km/h**, cycle trace coverage ≥ 95 %

**Step 4 — CVS Sampling and Bag Collection (1.5 marks):**
- Raw exhaust continuously mixed with filtered ambient dilution air
- Critical flow venturi (CFV) or PDP (Positive Displacement Pump) maintains **Q_CVS constant**
- Sample collected in two Tedlar bags: **Bag 1 (Urban)**, **Bag 2 (Extra-Urban)**
- Maintain **heated line at 191 °C** to prevent HC condensation

**Step 5 — Analyzer Analysis (1 mark):**
- Bag samples drawn into NDIR (CO, CO₂), FID (HC), Chemiluminescence (NO, NO₂)
- Each analyzer calibrated before and after test using zero and span gases
- Record bag concentrations in ppm

**Step 6 — Mass Emission Computation and Compliance (1 mark):**

$$
m_{CO} = \frac{V_{mix,urban} \times \rho_{CO} \times c_{CO,urban} + V_{mix,extra} \times \rho_{CO} \times c_{CO,extra}}{10^6}
$$

$$
E_{CO} = \frac{m_{CO}}{D_{MIDC}} = \frac{m_{CO}}{11.0 \text{ km}}
$$

Compare with BS-VI limit: CO ≤ **1.0 g/km** for petrol passenger cars (4W).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Chemiluminescence Analyzer (CLA)** for NOₓ measurement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **procedure for measuring diesel smoke opacity** using the **Bosch smoke meter** and **Hartridge opacimeter**, including the free acceleration test. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Chemiluminescence Analyzer (7 marks)

**1. Principle (2 marks):**
NO molecules in the sample react with ozone (O₃) generated inside the analyzer, producing excited NO₂* and molecular oxygen. The excited NO₂* relaxes to ground state by emitting a photon:

$$
NO + O_3 \rightarrow NO_2^* + O_2
$$

$$
NO_2^* \rightarrow NO_2 + h\nu \quad (\lambda = 600 \text{ to } 875 \text{ nm})
$$

The emitted red light intensity is **directly proportional to NO concentration**.

**2. Construction (1.5 marks):**
- **Ozone generator** — high-voltage discharge converts O₂ in air to O₃
- **Reaction chamber** — sample mixes with O₃
- **Optical bandpass filter** — isolates 600–875 nm emission
- **Photomultiplier Tube (PMT)** — detects red photons, amplifies the signal
- **Molybdenum converter** at **~315 °C** — reduces NO₂ to NO (for total NOₓ measurement)

**3. Working — Two-Channel Operation (2.5 marks):**
- Sample is split into two streams
- **Stream A (Direct NO Channel):** Bypasses the converter; PMT reads the **red light from NO + O₃ reaction** → gives [NO]
- **Stream B (Total NOₓ Channel):** Passes through Mo converter where NO₂ → NO, then reacts with O₃; PMT reads total signal → gives [NOₓ]
- The analyzer electronically subtracts [NO] from [NOₓ] to obtain **[NO₂]**

**4. Interferences and Sensitivity (1 mark):**
- CO₂ and water vapor can absorb red light → must be conditioned
- Sensitivity: < 1 ppm NO
- Response time: < 3 s

---

#### Part (b) — Diesel Smoke Measurement (7 marks)

**1. Smoke Opacity Concepts (1 mark):**
Smoke is the **visible portion of diesel exhaust particulate**, measured as the percentage of light that is prevented from passing through the exhaust plume. The two most common instruments are Bosch and Hartridge.

**2. Bosch Smoke Meter — Procedure (2.5 marks):**
**Principle:** A measured volume of exhaust is drawn through a **white filter paper** (Whatman 4). The soot stains the paper, and a photometer measures the **reflected light** from the stained paper.

**Procedure (Free Acceleration Test as per IS 8118 / AIS-007):**
- Engine at idle, accelerator pedal fully depressed in < 1 second
- Hold fully open for **10 seconds** (or until max RPM reached)
- Release pedal; allow engine to return to idle for **5 seconds** (3 cycles)
- Collect sample in last 2 cycles (stops sampling in last 1–1.5 s of free acceleration)
- Measure black spot reflectance automatically
- Compute **Bosch Smoke Number (BSN)** on scale 0 to 10:

$$
BSN = 10 \times (1 - R_{stain} / R_{clean})
$$

**3. Hartridge Opacimeter — Procedure (2.5 marks):**
**Principle:** Light from a source is transmitted through the exhaust plume, and the reduction in intensity is measured in real-time.

**Procedure:**
- Engine coupled to a dynamometer (loaded steady-state test)
- Light source aligned with detector across exhaust stream
- Engine operated at **3 load-speed modes** (e.g., 25 %, 50 %, 100 % of rated speed at rated power)
- Read **opacity N (%)** or **absorption coefficient k (m⁻¹)** from display
- Convert to BSN or k-value as per regulation

**4. Significance (1 mark):**
BS-VI limit for heavy-duty diesel: smoke opacity ≤ **1.0 m⁻¹** (loaded steady state) and BSN ≤ **2.0** (free acceleration). Bosch is preferred for field/periodic testing, Hartridge for lab/development.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam**

- ✅ **Six stages of the procedure:** Prepare → Set Dyno → Run Cycle → CVS Sample → Analyze → Compute
- ✅ **CVS equation:** $m = V_{mix} \times \rho \times c / 10^6$ — derive on the spot if asked
- ✅ **Mass per km:** $E = m / D_{cycle}$; for engine bench: $E = m / W_{cycle}$ in g/kWh
- ✅ **MIDC distance:** ~11 km in 1220 s; IDC (2W): ~12 km in 1800 s
- ✅ **NDIR wavelengths:** CO = 4.67 µm, CO₂ = 4.26 µm, HC ≈ 3.4 µm
- ✅ **Heated sample line:** 191 °C ± 5 °C (mandatory for FID to avoid HC losses)
- ✅ **Chemiluminescence:** NO + O₃ → NO₂* → NO₂ + hν; **Mo converter @ 315 °C** for NO₂
- ✅ **Bosch Smoke Number:** scale 0–10, based on filter-paper reflectance
- ✅ **Hartridge k-value:** $k = -(1/L) \ln(I/I_0)$, in m⁻¹
- ✅ **Road load equation:** $F = A + Bv + Cv^2$ (N)
- ✅ **Calibration gases:** Zero gas (N₂) and Span gas (certified CO, C₃H₈, NO in N₂)
- ✅ **Ambient test conditions:** 20 °C – 30 °C, 50 %–90 % relative humidity
- ✅ **BS-VI limits (4W petrol):** CO ≤ 1.0 g/km, HC + NOₓ ≤ 0.10 g/km, PM ≤ 0.0045 g/km
- ✅ **CVS variants:** PDP (Positive Displacement Pump), CFV (Critical Flow Venturi), SSV (Subsonic Venturi)
- ✅ **Why CVS is mandatory:** Raw exhaust is too hot, too wet, and too concentrated for analyzers — dilution makes bag-averaging and transport feasible
- ✅ **Order of analyzers in line:** Sample → NDIR → FID → CLA (CLA is last as it requires O₃ and is more sensitive to interferences)
- ✅ **FID requires H₂ fuel and zero-grade air for the flame** — must be hydrocarbon-free

> **Last-Mile Tip:** In Part B answers, always pair the **principle equation** with a **real numerical value** (e.g., "For CO at 50 ppm in 8 m³ of CVS dilution air, mass = 0.50 g"). Examiners reward numerical literacy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Light Heavy-Duty Vehicles, Two Wheelers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Light Heavy-Duty Vehicles, Two Wheelers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Vehicle Classification by Mass & Application (KTU/CMV Rule Framework)

The Indian automotive regulatory framework (under the Central Motor Vehicle Rules – CMVR, 1989, and amended via GSR notifications by MoRTH) classifies road vehicles by **Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW)** and powertrain type, because each class produces a **fundamentally different exhaust signature**. Module 3 deals with the three categories that dominate India’s in-use fleet emissions: **Light-Duty Vehicles (LDV)**, **Heavy-Duty Vehicles (HDV)**, and **Two Wheelers (2W)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Set — Must Memorize**
> - **Light-Duty Vehicle (LDV):** A motor vehicle with **GVW ≤ 3,500 kg**, used primarily for passenger (cars, vans) or light goods transport.
> - **Heavy-Duty Vehicle (HDV):** A motor vehicle with **GVW > 3,500 kg**, including rigid trucks, tractors, trailers, and buses, predominantly powered by **Compression Ignition (CI / Diesel)** engines.
> - **Two Wheeler (2W):** A two-wheeled motor vehicle with engine displacement typically **≤ 500 cc**, powered almost universally by **Spark Ignition (SI / Petrol)** engines, with a small but growing Electric Vehicle (EV) segment.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lung Capacity" View

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy (Intuition):**
> Imagine a city’s traffic as a giant breathing organism. **Two Wheelers** are like *shallow, rapid breaths* — small engines sipping tiny amounts of air, but in **astronomical numbers** (over 220 million on Indian roads), so their *collective exhale* is huge. **Light-Duty Vehicles** are *medium breaths* — moderately sized engines with refined, computer-controlled combustion. **Heavy-Duty Vehicles** are *deep, grunting exhalations* — few in number, but each one *belches* massive volumes of NOₓ and Particulate Matter (PM) because of their large displacement diesel engines working under heavy load.

This analogy is critical for KTU exams: **the emission problem in India is a problem of fleet *volume* (2W), fleet *impact per unit* (HDV), and fleet *refinement* (LDV)**.

### 1.3 Why This Classification Matters for Pollution Control

| Vehicle Class | Dominant Engine | Dominant Pollutants | Primary Control Challenge |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Two Wheelers (2W)** | SI / Petrol (4-stroke, some 2-stroke) | HC, CO, PM (2-stroke), evaporative HC | Cost-sensitive; tight packaging; low-temperature catalyst operation |
| **Light-Duty Vehicles (LDV)** | SI Petrol / CI Diesel (BS-VI era) | CO, HC, NOₓ, PM (diesel), CO₂ | Three-way catalyst (TWC), Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF), Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) |
| **Heavy-Duty Vehicles (HDV)** | CI Diesel (large displacement, turbocharged) | NOₓ, PM, CO (smoke opacity) | High Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR), DPF, SCR with AdBlue/DEF, exhaust gas treatment volume |

### 1.4 Indian Regulatory Baselines (Bharat Stage VI = Euro 6)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** norms came into force on **1 April 2020** for all vehicle categories in India, equivalent to **Euro 6** (for LDV) and **Euro VI** (for HDV). Key test cycles:
> - **LDV:** Modified Indian Driving Cycle (MIDC) — equivalent to NEDC, with a cold-start bag + hot-start bag.
> - **HDV:** **WHSC (World Harmonised Steady-state Cycle)** + **WHTC (World Harmonised Transient Cycle)** with cold & hot start.
> - **2W:** **WMTC (World Motorcycle Test Cycle)** — class-1, 2, 3 based on Vmax.

### 1.5 Key Mass & Engine Metrics (Standardized Units)

- **GVW (Gross Vehicle Weight):** measured in **kg** or **tonnes**.
- **Engine Displacement:** measured in **cc (cm³)** or **litres (L)**.
- **Power:** **kW** (1 hp = 0.7457 kW).
- **CO₂ emission standard:** **g/km** for LDV & 2W; **g/kWh** for HDV (work-based).
- **NOₓ & PM for HDV:** **g/kWh** (Brake-Specific — measured on engine dynamometer).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Wheelers (2W) — Detailed Emission Behaviour

### 2.1.1 2-Stroke vs 4-Stroke Engine Emissions

The **2-stroke SI engine** (now phased out for new 2W under BS-VI for most categories) suffers from:
- **Short-circuiting of fresh charge** → unburnt HC loss (up to 25–30 % of fuel mass).
- **Oil carryover** → visible blue smoke, **PM (particulate matter)** emission.
- **No valve overlap control** → no internal EGR capability.

The **4-stroke SI engine** (current standard in 2W):
- Better combustion control, but suffers from **cold-start HC peaks** because the catalyst light-off temperature (≈ **250–300 °C**) is not reached for the first 60–120 seconds.
- Throttle-body injection / Port Fuel Injection (PFI) reduces wall-wetting → better cold-start HC.

### 2.1.2 Emission Control Devices for 2W

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — 2W After-Treatment Chain:**
> 1. **Oxidation Catalyst (OC):** Oxidises CO → CO₂ and HC → CO₂ + H₂O.
> 2. **Secondary Air Injection (SAI):** Pumps air into exhaust ports post-combustion to sustain catalyst light-off and oxidise HC/CO during cold start.
> 3. **Evaporative Canister (Charcoal):** Traps fuel vapours from the tank and purges them into the intake during running.

**2W-specific control philosophy:** Since the engine is small and cost-sensitive, **no NOₓ after-treatment is mandated** (NOₓ from small SI engines is naturally low due to lower in-cylinder temperatures and lean operation).

## 2.2 Light-Duty Vehicles (LDV) — Detailed Emission Behaviour

### 2.2.1 SI (Petrol) LDV Emission Control

The **Three-Way Catalyst (TWC)** is the heart of SI LDV emission control. It simultaneously oxidises **CO and HC** and reduces **NOₓ**, **but only when λ = 1 ± 0.01** (stoichiometric air–fuel ratio).

Supporting systems:
- **Lambda (λ) oxygen sensor / UEGO (Universal Exhaust Gas Oxygen)** — closed-loop feedback.
- **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** with **closed-loop stoichiometric control** at a target **AFR = 14.7** (stoichiometric).
- **Evaporative Emission Control System (EVAP)** — captures fuel-tank vapours in a charcoal canister.
- **On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II)** — monitors catalyst efficiency, O₂ sensor, misfire, EVAP leaks.

### 2.2.2 CI (Diesel) LDV Emission Control

Diesel LDV (a smaller share of the Indian market) requires different strategies because diesel engines operate **lean (λ > 1)** — TWC cannot reduce NOₓ under lean conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diesel LDV After-Treatment Chain (BS-VI):**
> 1. **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC):** Oxidises CO, HC, soluble organic fraction of PM.
> 2. **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF):** Traps soot; periodically regenerates at ~**600 °C** (passive via NO₂; active via post-injection).
> 3. **NOₓ Trap (LNT/NOₓ Storage Catalyst)** OR **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** with AdBlue/DEF (Diesel Exhaust Fluid = 32.5 % aqueous urea).
> 4. **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR):** Routes inert exhaust back to intake to lower in-cylinder peak temperatures → **reduces thermal NOₓ**.

## 2.3 Heavy-Duty Vehicles (HDV) — Detailed Emission Behaviour

HDV engines operate at **high BMEP (Brake Mean Effective Pressure)** for prolonged durations, producing **large absolute mass of pollutants**. BS-VI / Euro VI limits are set as **g/kWh** (work-based) measured on an **engine dynamometer** using **WHSC + WHTC cycles**.

### 2.3.1 HDV Emission Control Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — HDV BS-VI / Euro VI After-Treatment Train:**
>
> `Engine → Turbo → DOC → DPF → SCR (with AdBlue injection) → Tailpipe`
>
> - **DOC (Diesel Oxidation Catalyst):** NO → NO₂ (needed for DPF passive regen and SCR low-T operation).
> - **DPF (Wall-flow ceramic, SiC or cordierite):** Captures soot; > **99 %** filtration efficiency.
> - **SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction):** Urea (AdBlue) → NH₃; NH₃ + NOₓ → N₂ + H₂O at **200–500 °C**.
> - **ASC (Ammonia Slip Catalyst):** Catches any unreacted NH₃ exiting SCR.

### 2.3.2 The SCR Reaction Chemistry (Memorize for KTU)

**Step 1 — Urea hydrolysis (in exhaust at > 180 °C):**
$$(NH_2)_2CO + H_2O \rightarrow 2NH_3 + CO_2$$

**Step 2 — Selective catalytic reduction (vanadium or zeolite-based catalyst):**
$$4NH_3 + 4NO + O_2 \rightarrow 4N_2 + 6H_2O$$
$$4NH_3 + 2NO_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 3N_2 + 6H_2O$$
$$8NH_3 + 6NO_2 \rightarrow 7N_2 + 12H_2O$$

### 2.3.3 EGR for HDV

HDV EGR rates can reach **15–30 %** (mass of exhaust re-circulated as a fraction of fresh intake mass), substantially lowering NOₓ but at the cost of increased **PM** (handled by DPF).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL: Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value (NEVER the `\|` pipe in tables).**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Unit / Standard Value | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric, petrol) | $AFR_{stoich} = 14.7$ | dimensionless | Closed-loop λ = 1 target for TWC |
| 2 | Lambda (λ) | $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | dimensionless | λ = 1: TWC; λ > 1: lean; λ < 1: rich |
| 3 | CO₂ from fuel (carbon balance) | $m_{CO_2} = 3.16 \times m_{fuel}$ | kg | For petrol; for diesel ≈ **3.16** |
| 4 | Brake-Specific Emission (BSE) | $BSE = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{pollutant}}{P_{brake}}$ | g/kWh | HDV regulatory metric |
| 5 | Engine Displacement volume | $V_d = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot B^2 \cdot S \cdot n_{cyl}$ | m³ or L | Determines air-handling capacity |
| 6 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{it} = \dfrac{W_i}{m_f \cdot CV}$ | fraction or % | Higher η → lower CO₂ per kWh |
| 7 | BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption) | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_{brake}}$ | g/kWh | Lower is better; diesel ~ **200–220 g/kWh** |
| 8 | NOₓ reduction via EGR | $\Delta NO_x \approx (1 - r_{EGR})^{1.5}$ approx. | fraction | r_EGR is the EGR mass fraction |
| 9 | SCR efficiency (modern) | $\eta_{SCR} = \dfrac{[NO_x]_{in} - [NO_x]_{out}}{[NO_x]_{in}}$ | 80–95 % at 250–450 °C | Compliance with NOₓ ≈ **0.46 g/kWh** (Euro VI) |
| 10 | DPF filtration efficiency | $\eta_{DPF} \ge 99\,\%$ at steady state | % | Limits PM to ~ **0.01 g/kWh** |
| 11 | AdBlue dosing | $\dot{m}_{urea} \approx 0.05 \times \dot{m}_{fuel}$ | kg/h | ~ **5 % of fuel mass** |
| 12 | 2W Catalyst light-off temp | $T_{light-off}$ | **250–300 °C** | Cold-start HC peak duration |
| 13 | HDV BS-VI NOₓ limit (WHTC) | $NO_x \le 0.46$ | g/kWh | Euro VI / BS-VI |
| 14 | HDV BS-VI PM limit (WHTC) | $PM \le 0.01$ | g/kWh | Euro VI / BS-VI |
| 15 | LDV BS-VI CO₂ fleet target (India indicative) | ~ **130 g/km** | g/km | CAFC-like corridor |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fleet Operators & OEMs** use the **BSE (g/kWh)** metric to compare HDV engines across brands.
- **City planners** use **2W fleet-specific HC/CO factors** to model urban air quality.
- **Regulators (MoRTH / ARAI)** use **WHTC + WHSC** for HDV type-approval, **MIDC** for LDV, and **WMTC** for 2W.
- **Emission control engineers** design the **thermal management** of after-treatment — DPF regen, SCR light-off, TWC warm-up.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — CO₂ Mass Emission from Fuel Consumption (LDV / 2W)

This is the most frequently asked **2-mark and 7-mark derivation** in KTU ESE on this topic. We use the **carbon-balance method** — the entire carbon in the fuel leaves as CO₂, CO, HC, or PM, and under complete combustion we get pure CO₂.

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric reaction for a generic hydrocarbon fuel (assume C₈H₁₈, octane):**
$$C_8H_{18} + 12.5(O_2 + 3.76 N_2) \rightarrow 8CO_2 + 9H_2O + 47 N_2$$

**Step 2 — Mass of carbon per kg of fuel:**
For octane (C₈H₁₈), molecular weight = **8(12) + 18(1) = 114 g/mol**.
Mass fraction of C = (8 × 12) / 114 = **96 / 114 = 0.8421**.

**Step 3 — Mass of CO₂ produced per kg of fuel:**
Every kg of C yields (44/12) kg of CO₂ (because CO₂ MW = 44, C = 12).
$$m_{CO_2} \text{ per kg fuel} = 0.8421 \times \dfrac{44}{12} = 0.8421 \times 3.667$$
$$m_{CO_2} \text{ per kg fuel} = 3.088 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Generic formula for any hydrocarbon CₓHᵧ:**
$$\text{Carbon mass fraction} = \dfrac{12x}{12x + y}$$
$$m_{CO_2} \text{ per kg fuel} = \dfrac{12x}{12x+y} \times \dfrac{44}{12} = \dfrac{44x}{12x + y}$$

**Step 5 — For typical petrol (assumed C₈H₁₈ average):**
$$m_{CO_2} \text{ per kg fuel} = \dfrac{44 \times 8}{96 + 18} = \dfrac{352}{114} = 3.088 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{kg fuel}$$

**Step 6 — For typical diesel (C₁₂H₂₆ average):**
$$\text{Carbon fraction} = \dfrac{12 \times 12}{144 + 26} = \dfrac{144}{170} = 0.8471$$
$$m_{CO_2} \text{ per kg fuel} = 0.8471 \times 3.667 = 3.106 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{kg fuel}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conventional engineering shortcut:** Because the carbon mass fraction of all petroleum fuels lies between 0.84 and 0.87, engineers universally use **3.16 kg CO₂ per kg of petrol** and **3.18 kg CO₂ per kg of diesel** as a simple working rule (an average of the above).

## 3.2 Derivation — Brake-Specific Emission (BSE) and Engine-Out to Tailpipe Conversion

**Problem context (typical KTU numerical):** A BS-VI heavy-duty diesel engine has the following test data on WHTC:
- Brake power: **P_b = 280 kW**
- Engine-out NOₓ: **8.0 g/kWh** (before after-treatment)
- SCR efficiency: **95 %**
- Fuel consumption: **210 g/kWh**

**Step 1 — Engine-out NOₓ per hour:**
$$\dot{m}_{NO_x, \text{out}} = 8.0 \text{ g/kWh} \times 280 \text{ kW} = 2240 \text{ g/h}$$

**Step 2 — Tailpipe NOₓ after SCR (95 % efficiency):**
$$\eta_{SCR} = 0.95$$
$$\dot{m}_{NO_x, \text{tail}} = \dot{m}_{NO_x, \text{out}} \times (1 - \eta_{SCR})$$
$$\dot{m}_{NO_x, \text{tail}} = 2240 \times (1 - 0.95) = 2240 \times 0.05 = 112 \text{ g/h}$$

**Step 3 — Tailpipe BSE:**
$$BSE_{NO_x, \text{tail}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{NO_x, \text{tail}}}{P_b} = \dfrac{112}{280} = 0.40 \text{ g/kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Compliance check with Euro VI / BS-VI NOₓ limit (0.46 g/kWh):**
$$0.40 \text{ g/kWh} \le 0.46 \text{ g/kWh} \quad \checkmark \text{ COMPLIANT}$$

**Step 5 — Annual CO₂ equivalent (for fleet analysis):**
$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = 210 \text{ g/kWh} \times 280 \text{ kW} = 58800 \text{ g/h} = 58.8 \text{ kg/h}$$
$$\dot{m}_{CO_2} = 58.8 \times 3.18 = 187.0 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{h}$$

## 3.3 Derivation — Cold-Start HC Penalty for 2W

A typical BS-VI 2W with catalyst light-off at **T = 280 °C** reaches this temperature in **τ ≈ 90 s** after start. The cold-start HC spike averages **HC_cs ≈ 2.0 g/s** for the first 90 s, and the hot-running HC is **HC_h ≈ 0.05 g/s**.

**Step 1 — HC during cold-start phase:**
$$m_{HC, cs} = HC_{cs} \times \tau = 2.0 \times 90 = 180 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — HC during hot phase (say 1800 s for a city ride):**
$$m_{HC, hot} = HC_{h} \times t_{hot} = 0.05 \times 1800 = 90 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Total HC over the trip:**
$$m_{HC, total} = 180 + 90 = 270 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4 — Fraction due to cold start:**
$$f_{cs} = \dfrac{180}{270} = 66.7\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion (high-yield for KTU):** About **two-thirds** of 2W HC emissions come from the **first 90 seconds** of operation. This is why **secondary air injection, exhaust manifold design, and catalyst placement close to the exhaust port** are the focus of 2W emission engineering.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Control Strategy vs Vehicle Class

| Pollutant | 2W (SI) | LDV Petrol (SI) | LDV Diesel (CI) | HDV Diesel (CI) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CO** | OC, ECU closed-loop | TWC, λ-control | DOC | DOC |
| **HC** | OC + SAI | TWC + EVAP | DOC | DOC |
| **NOₓ** | Low; passive control | TWC (stoich) | LNT or SCR + EGR | SCR + High-EGR + EGR cooling |
| **PM** | Negligible (4-stroke) | TWC | DPF + DOC | DPF + DOC |
| **Evaporative HC** | Charcoal canister | EVAP canister | EVAP canister | EVAP canister (limited) |
| **CO₂** | Engine efficiency | Engine efficiency + hybridization | Engine efficiency | Engine efficiency + SCR thermal mgmt |

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — HDV Annual Emission Inventory

**Problem (KTU-type):** A logistics fleet of 50 HDV trucks, each running 1,500 hours/year with BS-VI emission factors, calculate annual NOₓ and PM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** P_b = 280 kW; BS-VI NOₓ = **0.46 g/kWh**; BS-VI PM = **0.01 g/kWh**; hours per truck = 1,500; trucks = 50.

**Step 1 — Annual work per truck:**
$$W_{annual} = 280 \text{ kW} \times 1500 \text{ h} = 420{,}000 \text{ kWh/truck}$$

**Step 2 — Annual NOₓ per truck:**
$$m_{NO_x, truck} = 0.46 \times 420{,}000 = 193{,}200 \text{ g} = 193.2 \text{ kg/truck}$$

**Step 3 — Annual PM per truck:**
$$m_{PM, truck} = 0.01 \times 420{,}000 = 4{,}200 \text{ g} = 4.2 \text{ kg/truck}$$

**Step 4 — Annual fleet NOₓ:**
$$m_{NO_x, fleet} = 193.2 \times 50 = 9{,}660 \text{ kg} = 9.66 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

**Step 5 — Annual fleet PM:**
$$m_{PM, fleet} = 4.2 \times 50 = 210 \text{ kg/year}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **emission inventory methodology** that KTU expects you to know — multiply BSE (g/kWh) by total cumulative work (kWh) summed over the fleet to obtain the **absolute emission load in tonnes/year** for city or regional air-shed planning.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HDV BS-VI After-Treatment Architecture (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Diesel CI] --> B[Turbocharger]
    B --> C[EGR Cooler & Mixer]
    C --> D[DOC Oxidation Catalyst]
    D --> E[DPF Particulate Filter]
    E --> F[SCR Module with AdBlue Injector]
    F --> G[ASC Ammonia Slip Catalyst]
    G --> H[Tailpipe]
    I[AdBlue Tank 32.5 percent Urea] -->|Dosed| F
    J[ECU Engine Control Unit] -.->|Sensors & Control| A
    J -.->|Urea dosing control| F
    style A fill:#ffd9a3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    style G fill:#c2f0c2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

```mermaid
    B -->|Purge| C[Carburettor or EFI Throttle]

    E --> F[Secondary Air Injection SAI]
    F --> G[Oxidation Catalyst OC]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Architecture: 2W vs LDV vs HDV

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Two_Wheeler_2W
        TW1[Fuel Tank] --> TW2[Carb/EFI] --> TW3[SI Engine] --> TW4[OC + SAI]
    end
    subgraph Light_Duty_LDV_Petrol
        LD1[FUEL TANK] --> LD2[EVAP Canister] --> LD3[PFI] --> LD4[SI Engine] --> LD5[TWC + UEGO]
    end
    subgraph Light_Duty_LDV_Diesel
        LDD1[FUEL TANK] --> LDD2[HP Pump] --> LDD3[CI Engine] --> LDD4[DOC + DPF + LNT] --> LDD5[Tailpipe]
    end
    subgraph Heavy_Duty_HDV
        HD1[FUEL TANK] --> HD2[HP CRDi] --> HD3[CI Engine Turbo] --> HD4[DOC] --> HD5[DPF] --> HD6[SCR + AdBlue] --> HD7[ASC] --> HD8[Tailpipe]
    end
    style TW4 fill:#e6b3cc
    style LD5 fill:#f4cccc
    style LDD4 fill:#b3d9ff
    style HD5 fill:#b3d9ff
    style HD6 fill:#c2f0c2
```

## 4.5 SCR Reaction Module — Function Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Exhaust with NO and NO2] --> B[SCR Inlet Temp 200 to 500 C]
    B --> C[Urea Injector sprays AdBlue]
    C --> D[Hydrolysis to NH3]
    D --> E[NH3 adsorbed on zeolite or V2O5 catalyst]
    E --> F[NO and NO2 + NH3 reaction]
    F --> G[N2 and H2O out]
    H[Slip NH3] --> I[ASC cleanup]
    style E fill:#c2f0c2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#ffe599,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU:** Each block in the above diagrams represents a **distinct engineering subsystem** with its own operating temperature window, control variable, and failure mode. In exam answers, always mention the **purpose** of the block in one line — this fetches full marks.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A is typically **1-mark / 2-mark / 3-mark short answers** requiring 3–5 line crisp answers. Two representative 3-mark questions are given below with model answers.

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**"With neat sketches, explain the working of a Three-Way Catalytic Converter used in petrol Light-Duty Vehicles."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (Examiner-Grade):**
- A TWC is an after-treatment device fitted in the exhaust stream of an SI engine, between the exhaust manifold and tailpipe. *(1 mark)*
- It contains a **ceramic honeycomb substrate (cordierite)** coated with **platinum (Pt), palladium (Pd) and rhodium (Rh)**. *(1 mark)*
- When λ = 1 (stoich AFR = 14.7, controlled by UEGO + ECU), the catalyst simultaneously oxidises **CO → CO₂, HC → CO₂ + H₂O**, and reduces **NOₓ → N₂**, achieving > **95 %** conversion efficiency once light-off (~ 250–300 °C) is reached. *(1 mark)*

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**"Why is EGR not effective in two-wheelers, but very effective in heavy-duty diesel vehicles?"** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- **2W engines are small SI, naturally low-NOₓ** because of low in-cylinder temperatures; EGR adds complexity and cost for marginal benefit. *(1 mark)*
- **2W engines are air-cooled, two-stroke legacy** → re-circulated hot exhaust causes **lubrication issues, throttle plate fouling, and packaging constraints** in the tight under-bonnet space of a 100–150 cc vehicle. *(1 mark)*
- **HDV CI engines operate at high BMEP and high in-cylinder T (NOₓ ∝ T²)**; cool EGR (15–30 % rate) directly lowers flame temperature → **30–50 % NOₓ reduction** is achievable, and the HDV has a large, water-cooled engine bay to absorb the complexity. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question is **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two completely independent alternatives (Or Option) are provided. Students answer **either** the first set **or** the second.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**A.(a) Explain the BS-VI emission norms for Heavy-Duty Diesel Vehicles (HDV) in India. With a neat block diagram, describe the after-treatment system used in BS-VI HDV engines.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**1. BS-VI / Euro-VI emission limits for HDV (WHTC + WHSC):** *(3 marks)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Euro VI / BS-VI key limits (memorize):**
> - NOₓ: **0.46 g/kWh** (90 % reduction from Euro IV = 5.0 g/kWh)
> - PM: **0.01 g/kWh** (97 % reduction from Euro IV = 0.4 g/kWh)
> - CO: **1.5 g/kWh**
> - HC: **0.13 g/kWh**
> - NH₃: **10 ppm** (new, SCR-related limit)
> - **In-Service Conformity (ISC)** + **On-Board Diagnostic (OBD)** + **PEMS (Portable Emission Measurement System)** for real-driving emissions.

**2. After-treatment block diagram description (Engine → DOC → DPF → SCR → ASC → Tailpipe):** *(4 marks)*

(i) **DOC (Diesel Oxidation Catalyst)** — Oxidises CO, HC, and converts NO to NO₂ (the latter is essential for DPF passive regeneration and SCR low-temperature activity). Operating window: 200–400 °C.

(ii) **DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter)** — Wall-flow ceramic (SiC or cordierite) capturing **> 99 %** of soot. Regeneration is either **passive** (NO₂-based at 300–400 °C) or **active** (post-injection raising exhaust to 600 °C).

(iii) **SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction)** — AdBlue (32.5 % aqueous urea) is dosed before the SCR brick. Urea hydrolyses to NH₃ which reacts with NO/NO₂ on a **vanadium (V₂O₅/WO₃/TiO₂)** or **Cu-zeolite / Fe-zeolite** catalyst at 200–500 °C to produce N₂ + H₂O. Efficiency **80–95 %**.

(iv) **ASC (Ammonia Slip Catalyst)** — Catches excess NH₃ that didn't react in the SCR, oxidising it back to N₂ to keep slip < **10 ppm**.

**3. Engine-side measures:** High-pressure common rail, **EGR (15–30 %)** with EGR cooler, VGT (Variable Geometry Turbo), precise electronic management — all work synergistically with after-treatment.

---

**A.(b) Two Wheeler BS-VI emission control measures are limited to oxidation catalyst, secondary air injection, and EVAP. Justify why NOₓ after-treatment is NOT mandated for 2W. What is the role of the on-board diagnostic (OBD) system in 2W emission control?** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**1. Why NOₓ after-treatment is NOT used in 2W:** *(4 marks)*

- **Naturally low NOₓ from 4-stroke SI small engines** — small displacement (≤ 150 cc) and low BMEP means peak in-cylinder temperatures are modest; thermal NOₓ (Zeldovich mechanism) is **proportional to T²–T³**, so reducing T naturally suppresses NOₓ formation. *(1 mark)*
- **Lean operation (λ ≈ 1.0–1.05)** of small 2W engines — TWC cannot be used because stoichiometric control is hard to maintain cost-effectively; LNT is too expensive and bulky. *(1 mark)*
- **Cost-sensitivity** of the 2W segment in India (price point ₹ 70,000–1,50,000) — an additional SCR system would add ₹ 5,000–8,000 per vehicle, unacceptable. *(1 mark)*
- **Packaging constraint** — 2W has minimal under-seat volume; an SCR brick + AdBlue tank cannot be physically accommodated. *(1 mark)*

**2. Role of OBD in 2W BS-VI:** *(3 marks)*

- **Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL):** Warns the rider of emission system faults (e.g., catalyst degradation, O₂ sensor failure, misfire). *(1 mark)*
- **DTC (Diagnostic Trouble Code) storage:** Faults are logged in ECU memory, retrievable via service tool; this enables **In-Use Compliance** of emission standards. *(1 mark)*
- **Real-time monitoring of catalyst efficiency** (OBD-II for 2W uses simplified single-sensor or dual-sensor O₂ sensor logic) to ensure the OC remains functional throughout the vehicle's life. *(1 mark)*

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**B.(a) Differentiate between the emission control approaches for Light-Duty Petrol (SI) and Light-Duty Diesel (CI) vehicles. Explain why a Three-Way Catalyst (TWC) cannot be used in a diesel LDV.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**1. Differences in emission control:** *(5 marks)*

| Aspect | LDV Petrol (SI) | LDV Diesel (CI) |
|---|---|---|
| **A/F operation** | Stoichiometric, λ = 1.0 | Lean, λ = 1.3–1.7 |
| **HC control** | TWC oxidation | DOC oxidation |
| **CO control** | TWC oxidation | DOC oxidation |
| **NOₓ control** | TWC reduction (stoich) | LNT or SCR + EGR |
| **PM control** | Negligible (no soot) | DPF + DOC |
| **Closed-loop control** | Yes (UEGO + ECU) | Open-loop / model-based |
| **Evaporative** | EVAP canister (large) | EVAP canister (smaller) |
| **OBD scope** | Full OBD-II (10+ monitors) | Simplified OBD (DPF, SCR, EGR) |

**2. Why TWC fails in diesel LDV:** *(2 marks)*
- TWC reduces NOₓ via **CO and HC acting as reductants** — these are *scarce* in lean diesel exhaust, so the reduction reaction stalls. *(1 mark)*
- Excess O₂ in lean diesel exhaust (λ > 1) **also re-oxidises any reduced species**, annihilating the NOₓ → N₂ conversion. Hence, lean NOₓ strategies (LNT, SCR) are mandatory for diesel LDV. *(1 mark)*

---

**B.(b) With neat sketch, explain the working of a Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) system for NO_x control in BS-VI heavy-duty diesel vehicles. What is AdBlue and how is it stored & dosed?** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**1. Working of SCR (with reference to the block diagram from Section 4.5):** *(4 marks)*

- Hot exhaust (200–500 °C) enters the SCR module. *(0.5 mark)*
- A precision **urea dosing injector** sprays a calibrated quantity of AdBlue (32.5 % urea in 67.5 % de-mineralised water) into the exhaust stream. *(0.5 mark)*
- High temperature causes **urea hydrolysis:** $(NH_2)_2CO + H_2O \rightarrow 2NH_3 + CO_2$. *(1 mark)*
- NH₃ adsorbs onto the catalyst surface (V₂O₅/WO₃/TiO₂ for high-temp; Cu/Fe-zeolite for low-temp) and reacts with NO/NO₂:
$$4NH_3 + 4NO + O_2 \rightarrow 4N_2 + 6H_2O$$ *(1 mark)*
- The catalytic surface and exhaust temperature together give **80–95 % NOₓ reduction efficiency** when dosed correctly. *(1 mark)*

**2. AdBlue — definition, storage, dosing:** *(3 marks)*

- **Definition:** AdBlue (also called **DEF — Diesel Exhaust Fluid**) is a **32.5 % by weight** aqueous solution of high-purity urea in **de-mineralised water**, conforming to **ISO 22241**. *(1 mark)*
- **Storage:** A separate insulated plastic tank (typically 15–80 L depending on HDV class), located near the diesel tank; refill interval typically 4,000–8,000 km. *(1 mark)*
- **Dosing:** ECU-driven **airless or air-assisted urea injector** mounted in the exhaust pipe upstream of the SCR. ECU uses **NOₓ sensor feedback (pre- and post-SCR)** to maintain a dosing ratio of about **5 g AdBlue per 100 g fuel**; under-dosing → excess NOₓ, over-dosing → NH₃ slip (caught by ASC). *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1–2 marks each):**
> 1. Writing **"AdBlue"** without expanding it as **"32.5 % aqueous urea (DEF)"** — costs 0.5 mark.
> 2. Confusing **SCR (NH₃-based)** with **LNT/NOₓ trap (uses HC-rich regeneration cycle)** — examiner cuts 2 marks if you interchange.
> 3. Forgetting **ASC** in the HDV after-treatment chain — examiners specifically look for it because it relates to the new BS-VI NH₃ limit of 10 ppm.
> 4. In 2W answers, writing "TWC used in 2W" — INCORRECT; only **OC + SAI + EVAP** are used. Marks deducted.
> 5. Stating "Euro 6 limits apply to HDV" — WRONG; the correct term is **Euro VI** (Roman numeral) for HDV; "Euro 6" is for LDV.
> 6. Omitting the **catalyst light-off temperature** (250–300 °C) in TWC / OC answers — examiner expects this to justify cold-start HC spike discussion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Bullet-Point Digest:**

- **2W** = small SI, < 500 cc, **OC + SAI + EVAP**, no NOₓ after-treatment, TWC not used.
- **LDV** = GVW ≤ 3,500 kg; petrol (TWC) or diesel (DOC + DPF + LNT/SCR + EGR).
- **HDV** = GVW > 3,500 kg; CI diesel; **DOC + DPF + SCR + ASC**, AdBlue dosed, high EGR.
- **Bharat Stage VI** = **1 April 2020**; equivalent to Euro 6 (LDV) and Euro VI (HDV).
- **WHTC** (transient) + **WHSC** (steady-state) for HDV; **MIDC** for LDV; **WMTC** for 2W.
- **BSE (g/kWh)** is the work-based metric for HDV; **g/km** for LDV and 2W.
- **BS-VI HDV limits:** NOₓ = 0.46 g/kWh; PM = 0.01 g/kWh; CO = 1.5 g/kWh; HC = 0.13 g/kWh; NH₃ = 10 ppm.
- **CO₂ from fuel:** ~ **3.16 kg/kg petrol**, ~ **3.18 kg/kg diesel** (carbon balance shortcut).
- **SCR reaction:** Urea → NH₃ → reduces NOₓ to N₂ + H₂O at 200–500 °C; efficiency 80–95 %.
- **DPF efficiency:** > 99 %; regenerates at 600 °C (active) or 300–400 °C (passive via NO₂).
- **EGR for HDV:** 15–30 % mass; lowers peak flame T → thermal NOₓ falls.
- **TWC works only at λ = 1 ± 0.01**; hence petrol LDV uses UEGO + ECU closed-loop.
- **AdBlue = 32.5 % aqueous urea (ISO 22241)**, dosed ≈ 5 % of fuel mass.
- **ASC** = Ammonia Slip Catalyst — final stage in HDV BS-VI exhaust train.
- **Cold-start HC in 2W:** ~ 67 % of total HC emitted in first 90 s (catalyst not yet at light-off).
- **2-stroke 2W** = historical, mostly phased out; main issue = short-circuiting HC + crankcase oil PM.
- **4-stroke 2W** = current norm; uses port injection / carburettor with OC + SAI.
- **OBD-II** for LDV; **OBD** simplified for 2W; **ISC + PEMS** for HDV in-service compliance.
- **EGR is not used in 2W** — packaging, oil fouling, cost outweigh marginal NOₓ benefit.
- **Diesel LDV needs LNT or SCR** — TWC fails because lean exhaust lacks reductants and excess O₂ re-oxidises reduced species.
- **3.16 factor for CO₂** is a **direct carbon-balance** derivation, not an assumption — examiners will award full credit only if the derivation shows: fuel mass → C fraction → C × 44/12.
- **Indian in-use fleet contribution:** 2W = highest *number* but lower per-unit emissions; HDV = lowest *number* but highest per-unit NOₓ and PM; LDV = mid-range with the most mature control technology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for the HDV after-treatment chain — remember as:** **"DDS-A"**
> **D**OC → **D**PF → **S**CR → **A**SC → Tailpipe.
> Mnemonic for LDV Petrol: **"T.U.E."** → **T**WC, **U**EGO, **E**VAP. Mnemonic for 2W: **"O.S.E."** → **O**C, **S**AI, **E**VAP.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crankcase, Evaporative, Refuelling and On-Road Emissions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Crankcase, Evaporative, Refuelling and On-Road Emissions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Non-Exhaust & Non-Tailpipe Emissions

In automotive pollution control, emission sources are broadly classified into **tailpipe (exhaust) emissions** and **non-tailpipe emissions**. The four major non-tailpipe sources addressed in this module are **crankcase emissions**, **evaporative emissions**, **refuelling emissions**, and **on-road (off-cycle) emissions**. Together, these can constitute **20–30%** of the total hydrocarbon (HC) emissions from a typical light-duty gasoline vehicle in pre-controlled states.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The module focuses on identifying emission sources that are *not* directly measured by tailpipe testing (e.g., the Indian Driving Cycle IDC or Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI) test cycles). Understanding their mechanisms is mandatory for the design of modern Pollution Control Devices (PCDs).

---

### 1.2 Crankcase Emissions (Blow-by Gases)

**Formal Definition (KTU Terminology):**
Crankcase emissions, also known as **blow-by gases**, are the unburned and partially burned combustion gases (HC, CO, NOx, and partially oxidized products) that leak past the piston rings into the engine crankcase during the compression and power strokes of an internal combustion engine.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Pressure Sneak" Effect:**
Imagine squeezing a water-filled syringe. The water has nowhere to go except out the nozzle. Now imagine a worn-out syringe where the rubber plunger does not seal perfectly — water leaks backward past the plunger. That is exactly what happens in an engine: high cylinder pressure during combustion (typically **10–60 bar** in gasoline and up to **200 bar** in diesel) forces a fraction of the combustion mixture **backward past the piston rings** into the crankcase.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Pollutants in Blow-by:** Hydrocarbons (HC) — the dominant species (~70–80% of crankcase emissions by mass), Carbon Monoxide (CO), and Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs) including carcinogenic **Benzo(a)pyrene**.

**Historical Context:**
- Pre-1960s: Blow-by gases were **vented to atmosphere** through a road-draft tube.
- 1960s onward: **Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV)** systems became mandatory in the US (California first, 1961) and later globally.

---

### 1.3 Evaporative Emissions (EVAP)

**Formal Definition (KTU Terminology):**
Evaporative emissions are the **hydrocarbon vapours** released to the atmosphere from the fuel system of a motor vehicle, arising from fuel volatility, diurnal temperature fluctuations, hot-soak conditions, and permeation through polymeric fuel system components (hoses, tanks, seals).

The three classical sub-categories (as defined by the US EPA and adopted in BIS/AIS standards):

| Sub-Category | Trigger Mechanism | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| **Diurnal Losses** | Daily ambient temperature swings (e.g., 15°C → 35°C) | Largest contributor (50–70% of EVAP) |
| **Hot-Soak Losses** | Engine-off heat soakback into fuel lines/carburettor | ~20–30% of EVAP |
| **Running Losses** | Vapour generation during vehicle operation | Smallest (~5–10%) |

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Soda Can" Effect:**
Have you ever opened a warm can of soda and felt vapours escape? Gasoline in your fuel tank behaves similarly. On a hot day, the fuel behaves like a sealed pressure cooker; fuel molecules with the lowest boiling points escape as vapour. Without a sealed system, those vapours leak out through every hose joint and seal — your car is essentially "sweating" hydrocarbons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BS-VI Compliance Note:** Indian BS-VI norms (since April 2020) adopt the United Nations **UN Regulation No. 83 (Revision 4)** test cycle, which includes a **SHED (Sealed Housing for Evaporative Determination)** test limiting evaporative emissions to **0.5 g/test** for light-duty vehicles.

---

### 1.4 Refuelling Emissions

**Formal Definition (KTU Terminology):**
Refuelling emissions are the **hydrocarbon vapours displaced from a vehicle's fuel tank** when liquid gasoline is pumped in at a filling station. As fuel enters the tank, it displaces the saturated hydrocarbon–air mixture present in the tank's ullage (vapour) space.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Sink Filling with Displaced Air" Effect:**
Picture pouring water into a glass already full of air. The air must go somewhere — it bubbles out. When you pump fuel into your car, the fuel displaces the fuel vapours sitting in the empty space above the fuel. If these vapours are not captured, they are expelled into the station forecourt atmosphere — directly into the breathing zone of the refueller.

**Quantitative Significance:**
A typical light-duty vehicle can release **1–2 g of HC per refuelling event**, contributing to urban air quality degradation, especially in congested metropolitan areas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Control Strategy:** Two approaches exist: (1) **Stage II vapour recovery** at the pump (vacuum-assisted hose recovery to underground tank), and (2) **Onboard Refuelling Vapour Recovery (ORVR)** — integrated into the vehicle's EVAP system.

---

### 1.5 On-Road (Off-Cycle) Emissions

**Formal Definition (KTU Terminology):**
On-road emissions, also called **off-cycle emissions**, are those pollutants generated by real-world driving conditions that are **not captured** by standardized laboratory test cycles (e.g., IDC, NEDC, WLTP). They arise from aggressive acceleration, high-speed driving, hard braking, cold starts, idling, and accessory loads (AC, power steering).

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Standardized Test vs. Reality" Gap:**
Think of it like a car manufacturer advertising a fuel efficiency figure from a controlled wind-tunnel test, but in real city traffic you observe much higher consumption. Similarly, an engine calibrated to pass a laboratory test (with warmed-up, steady-state conditions) may emit 5–10 times more pollutants during aggressive urban driving or short cold trips.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Relevance:** Real Driving Emissions (RDE) regulations in the EU (and likely future Indian Bharat Stage VII) use **Portable Emissions Measurement Systems (PEMS)** to measure on-road emissions during actual driving — bridging the gap between laboratory and real-world emission factors.

---

### 1.6 Quick Comparison Table

| Emission Type | Primary Pollutant | Primary Source | Control Device |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Crankcase** | HC | Piston ring leakage | PCV Valve |
| **Evaporative** | HC (VOCs) | Fuel tank & lines | EVAP Canister + Sealed System |
| **Refuelling** | HC (Vapours) | Fuel tank ullage | ORVR / Stage II Recovery |
| **On-Road** | NOx, CO, HC, PM | Real-world operation | PEMS-based conformity factors |

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Crankcase Emission Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

The formation of blow-by gases follows a four-stage mechanism:

1. **Combustion Pressure Build-up:** During the power stroke, peak cylinder pressure in a gasoline engine reaches **30–60 bar** (SI) and **80–200 bar** (CI/diesel).
2. **Ring-Bore Interface Leakage:** High-pressure combustion gases flow **in reverse direction** through the micro-gap between piston rings and cylinder bore (gap size ~5–10 µm).
3. **Crankcase Pressure Rise:** Accumulation of gases pressurizes the crankcase (designed for near-atmospheric pressure), causing oil aeration, foaming, and oil degradation.
4. **Venting Requirement:** To prevent seal failures and oil leaks, the crankcase MUST be vented. The vent pathway is either:
   - **Atmospheric** (illegal in modern vehicles), or
   - **Closed-loop** via PCV back to the intake manifold.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quantitative Rule of Thumb (KTU Numerical):** Blow-by flow rate $\dot{m}_{bb} \approx 0.5\% - 1.0\%$ of total airflow for a worn engine; <0.1% for a new, well-sealed engine.

---

### 2.2 Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV) System

The PCV system is a **calibrated, metered air flow** from crankcase to intake manifold.

**Operational Logic (Bulleted Steps):**

- A **PCV valve** is installed in the line connecting the crankcase to the intake manifold (typically upstream of the throttle body).
- The valve contains a **spring-loaded poppet** that meters flow based on manifold vacuum.
- At **idle** (high manifold vacuum): The spring compresses, restricting flow — preventing excessively lean operation.
- At **cruise/WOT** (low vacuum): The poppet opens fully, maximizing blow-by extraction.
- A **fresh air inlet** (clean air breather) supplies filtered air to the crankcase, ensuring one-way sweep flow.

**PCV Mass Flow Equation (for analysis):**

$$
\dot{m}_{PCV} = C_d \cdot A_{valve} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_{crank} \cdot \Delta P_{manifold}}
$$

Where:
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (typically **0.6–0.8**)
- $A_{valve}$ = effective valve flow area (m²)
- $\rho_{crank}$ = crankcase gas density (~**1.2 kg/m³** at standard conditions)
- $\Delta P_{manifold}$ = manifold vacuum relative to crankcase (Pa)

---

### 2.3 Evaporative Emission (EVAP) System — Detailed Logic

The EVAP system **traps and stores fuel vapours** in a charcoal canister for later combustion.

**Key Components:**

1. **Carbon Canister** — typically contains **~500–1000 grams of activated carbon** (pellet or monolith form), with surface area **800–1500 m²/g**.
2. **Purge Solenoid Valve** — ECU-controlled; opens during engine operation to draw stored vapours into the intake.
3. **Vapour-Liquid Separator** — prevents liquid fuel from reaching the canister.
4. **Tank Pressure Control Valve** — seals the system, only opens for purging/venting.
5. **Leak Detection Pump (LDP)** — mandatory on OBD-II compliant vehicles, detects system leaks as small as **0.5 mm**.

**EVAP Mass Balance Equation (Diurnal Test Simulation):**

$$
m_{HC,evap} = \frac{P \cdot V_{ullage} \cdot M_{HC}}{R \cdot T_{final}} - \frac{P \cdot V_{ullage} \cdot M_{HC}}{R \cdot T_{initial}}
$$

For a closed tank with constant pressure (above atmospheric, vented at set pressure):

$$
m_{HC,evap} = \frac{(P_{final} - P_{initial}) \cdot V_{ullage} \cdot M_{HC}}{R \cdot T_{avg}}
$$

---

### 2.4 Refuelling Emissions — Displacement Logic

When a volume $V_f$ of liquid fuel enters a tank of ullage volume $V_u$:

**Pre-condition:** Ullage is saturated with fuel vapour at mole fraction $y_{HC}$ (typically **0.4–0.6** for gasoline at 30°C).

**Volume Displaced:**

$$
V_{displaced} = V_f \cdot \frac{\rho_{liq}}{\rho_{vap,HC}}
$$

**Mass of HC Released (if uncontrolled):**

$$
m_{HC,refuel} = y_{HC} \cdot \frac{P \cdot V_f}{R \cdot T} \cdot M_{HC} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{T_{slosh}}{\Delta T_{operation}}\right)
$$

**With ORVR Recovery (Efficiency $\eta_{ORVR}$):**

$$
m_{HC,controlled} = m_{HC,refuel} \cdot (1 - \eta_{ORVR})
$$

ORVR efficiency is typically **95–98%**.

---

### 2.5 On-Road Emissions — Conformity Factor (CF) Approach

The European RDE regulation uses a **Conformity Factor (CF)** to compare real-world to laboratory emissions:

$$
CF = \frac{e_{real,road}}{e_{lab,cycle}}
$$

Where:
- $e_{real,road}$ = emission (g/km) measured by PEMS during real driving
- $e_{lab,cycle}$ = emission (g/km) measured on the WLTP/IDC test cycle

**Current EU Limits (as of 2024):**
- **NOx CF:** ≤ 1.43 (with margin of 1.5)
- **PN (Particle Number) CF:** ≤ 1.5
- **CO CF:** Not yet legislated but under observation

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters for India:** Many studies (e.g., IIT Delhi, ARAI reports) show in-use NOx from BS-VI diesel vehicles can be **2–5× higher** than the type-approval cycle values, motivating India's push toward PEMS-based RDE adoption in future BS-VII norms.

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Reference)

| Concept | Governing Equation | Typical Values / Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Blow-by flow** | $\dot{m}_{bb} = \rho \cdot A_{gap} \cdot v_{leak}$ | $0.1\%-1.0\%$ of total airflow |
| **PCV flow** | $\dot{m}_{PCV} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2\rho \Delta P}$ | $C_d \in [0.6, 0.8]$ |
| **Diurnal HC mass** | $m_{HC} = \dfrac{P V_u M_{HC}}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_i} - \dfrac{1}{T_f} \right)$ | $M_{HC} \approx 100$ g/mol (gasoline equiv.) |
| **Canister capacity** | $m_{stored} = q_{carbon} \cdot X_{loading}$ | $q_{carbon} \approx 0.4-0.6$ g HC/g carbon |
| **Refuelling HC (uncontrolled)** | $m_{HC} = y_{HC} \cdot \dfrac{P V_f M_{HC}}{RT}$ | $y_{HC} \approx 0.5$ at 30°C |
| **Refuelling HC (ORVR)** | $m_{HC,ctrl} = m_{HC} \cdot (1 - \eta_{ORVR})$ | $\eta_{ORVR} \approx 0.95$ |
| **Conformity Factor** | $CF = e_{road} / e_{lab}$ | $CF_{NOx} \le 1.43$ (EU) |
| **Canister purge ratio** | $\eta_{purge} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{HC,purged}}{\dot{m}_{HC,stored}}$ | Target $\ge 0.95$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gas Constant Reminder:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). Always convert temperatures to **Kelvin** (K) when applying ideal gas equations in EVAP/refuelling calculations.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

These non-tailpipe emissions are now the **new regulatory battleground**. Automakers must:
- Calibrate **PCV flow** to prevent oil contamination of intake valves (especially in direct-injection engines, the well-known "low-speed pre-ignition" / LSPI risk).
- Design **fuel systems** with multi-layer polymer barriers (e.g., Selar® RF) to reduce permeation by **80–90%**.
- Integrate **ORVR** in regions that mandate it (e.g., USA since 1998, EU partial adoption).
- Submit to **In-Service Conformity (ISC)** testing and **PEMS-based RDE** for type approval.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Crankcase Blow-by Mass Flow Rate

**Starting Point (Newtonian Flow Through Annular Gap):**

The piston ring/cylinder bore interface forms an annular clearance of width $h$ (typically **5–10 μm**) and circumference $\pi D$, where $D$ is the bore diameter.

**Step 1:** Velocity profile of laminar Couette-Poiseuille flow between two parallel surfaces (one stationary bore, one moving ring):

$$
u(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu} \cdot \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \cdot (y^2 - h \cdot y) + U \cdot \left(1 - \frac{y}{h}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Integrating velocity across the gap to get volumetric flow per unit circumference:

$$
Q_{linear} = \int_0^h u(y) \, dy
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting and integrating:

$$
Q_{linear} = -\frac{h^3}{12\mu} \cdot \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{U \cdot h}{2}
$$

**Step 4:** Multiplying by circumference $\pi D$ to get total volumetric flow:

$$
\dot{V}_{bb} = \pi D \left( -\frac{h^3}{12\mu} \cdot \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{U h}{2} \right)
$$

**Step 5:** Approximating $\partial p / \partial x \approx -\Delta P / L_{ring}$, where $L_{ring}$ is the axial ring contact length:

$$
\dot{V}_{bb} = \pi D \left( \frac{h^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L_{ring}} + \frac{U h}{2} \right)
$$

**Step 6:** Converting volumetric to mass flow using crankcase gas density $\rho_{cg}$:

$$
\dot{m}_{bb} = \rho_{cg} \cdot \dot{V}_{bb} = \rho_{cg} \cdot \pi D \left( \frac{h^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L_{ring}} + \frac{U h}{2} \right)
$$

**Engineering Insight:** The cubic dependence on $h$ is critical — tripling ring clearance increases blow-by by **27×**. This is why ring wear dramatically elevates crankcase emissions.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Diurnal Evaporative Emission Mass

**Step 1 — Initial State (Morning, $T_i$):**
The tank ullage of volume $V_u$ contains saturated HC vapour at partial pressure $P_{HC,i} = y_i \cdot P_{atm}$.

By ideal gas law:

$$
n_{HC,i} = \frac{P_{HC,i} \cdot V_u}{R \cdot T_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Final State (Afternoon, $T_f$):**
Assuming closed system (sealed tank), and increased vapour pressure (Raoult's Law for multi-component fuel):

$$
P_{HC,f} = \sum_{j} x_j \cdot P_j^{sat}(T_f)
$$

The total moles of HC in vapour:

$$
n_{HC,f} = \frac{P_{HC,f} \cdot V_u}{R \cdot T_f} \cdot \left( \frac{V_u}{V_u - \Delta V} \right)
$$

For a vented tank (pressure relief at $P_{max}$), assuming the relief valve maintains constant pressure, the escaped mass is:

$$
\Delta n_{HC} = \frac{P_{HC} \cdot V_{escaped}}{R \cdot T_f}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass Released:**

$$
m_{HC,diurnal} = \Delta n_{HC} \cdot M_{HC} = \frac{P_{HC} \cdot V_{escaped} \cdot M_{HC}}{R \cdot T_f}
$$

Where $V_{escaped} = V_{liq,expanded}(T_f) - V_{liq,expanded}(T_i)$, driven by thermal expansion of liquid fuel.

**Step 4 — Liquid Volume Expansion:**

$$
V_{liq}(T) = V_0 \left[ 1 + \beta_f (T - T_{ref}) \right]
$$

Where $\beta_f$ = volumetric thermal expansion coefficient of gasoline $\approx$ **$9.5 \times 10^{-4}$ /K**.

**Step 5 — Final Diurnal Emission Expression:**

$$
m_{HC,diurnal} = \frac{P_{HC} \cdot V_0 \cdot \beta_f \cdot (T_f - T_i) \cdot M_{HC}}{R \cdot T_f}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Refuelling Emission with ORVR Capture

**Step 1 — Volume of Fuel Pumped:**
Let $V_f$ = volume of fuel dispensed, $\rho_{liq}$ = liquid fuel density (~**750 kg/m³** for gasoline).

**Step 2 — Mass of Fuel:**
$m_{fuel} = V_f \cdot \rho_{liq}$

**Step 3 — Volume of Ullage Displaced:**
Ullage space initially $V_u$ is reduced by the volume of liquid entering:
$V_{displaced} = V_f$ (assuming incompressible liquid; vapour volume = liquid volume displaced)

**Step 4 — Moles of Vapour Displaced (Uncontrolled Case):**
Using ideal gas law with $y_{HC}$ as HC mole fraction in ullage:

$$
n_{HC,disp} = \frac{y_{HC} \cdot P \cdot V_f}{R \cdot T}
$$

**Step 5 — Mass of HC Released (Uncontrolled):**

$$
m_{HC,unc} = n_{HC,disp} \cdot M_{HC} = \frac{y_{HC} \cdot P \cdot V_f \cdot M_{HC}}{R \cdot T}
$$

**Step 6 — ORVR Capture Efficiency:**
ORVR captures vapour via a venturi at the filler neck, routing it to the carbon canister. Capture efficiency $\eta_{ORVR}$:

$$
m_{HC,ORVR} = m_{HC,unc} \cdot (1 - \eta_{ORVR})
$$

**Step 7 — Numerical Example (KTU-Type Problem):**
Given: $V_f = 40$ L, $T = 303$ K, $P = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa, $y_{HC} = 0.5$, $M_{HC} = 100$ g/mol, $\eta_{ORVR} = 0.95$.

$$
m_{HC,unc} = \frac{0.5 \times 1.013 \times 10^5 \times 40 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 \times 10^{-3}}{8.314 \times 303}
$$

**Evaluating Numerator:**

$$
\text{Num} = 0.5 \times 1.013 \times 10^5 \times 0.040 \times 0.100 = 202.6 \text{ Pa·m³}
$$

**Evaluating Denominator:**

$$
\text{Den} = 8.314 \times 303 = 2519.14 \text{ J/mol}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
m_{HC,unc} = \frac{202.6}{2519.14} = 0.0804 \text{ kg} = 80.4 \text{ g per refuel}
$$

**With ORVR:**

$$
m_{HC,ORVR} = 80.4 \times (1 - 0.95) = 4.02 \text{ g per refuel}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a typical numerical expected in KTU Module 3 problems. Note the molar mass of gasoline is often approximated as a hydrocarbon mixture equivalent to **C₈H₁₈** (octane, $M = 114$ g/mol) or simply assumed as **100 g/mol** for simplified problems.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Emission Control

| Parameter | Crankcase | Evaporative | Refuelling | On-Road |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dominant Pollutant** | HC | HC (VOCs) | HC (Vapours) | NOx, CO, HC, PM |
| **Source Location** | Piston ring interface | Tank, lines, canister | Fuel tank ullage | Real-world drive cycle |
| **Test Method** | PCV flow measurement | SHED test (UN R83) | Refuelling simulation | PEMS / RDE |
| **Key Control** | PCV valve | Activated carbon canister | ORVR venturi | Engine calibration, SCR, EGR |
| **Regulatory Limit (BS-VI)** | <0.15 g/km | 0.5 g/test | Captured >95% | No fixed limit; CF-based |
| **Efficiency Range** | 90–99% | 95–99% | 95–98% (with ORVR) | 60–90% (depends on cycle) |
| **Failure Mode** | PCV valve stuck | Canister saturation | Venturi blockage | Calibration drift, sensor aging |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PCV (Positive Crankcase Ventilation) System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FRESH AIR FILTER] --> B[CRANKCASE BREATHER]
    B --> C[CRANKCASE OIL PAN]
    C --> D[PCV VALVE SPRING LOADED POPPET]
    D --> E[INTAKE MANIFOLD]
    E --> F[THROTTLE BODY]
    F --> G[ENGINE CYLINDERS]
    G -->|BLOW BY LEAKAGE| C
    H[VACUUM SIGNAL FROM MANIFOLD] --> D
    I[FUEL AND AIR MIXTURE] --> G
    G --> J[EXHAUST TO CATALYTIC CONVERTER]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- Fresh air enters the crankcase (blue/clean path) to sweep out blow-by gases.
- Blow-by follows the **dotted return path** from cylinders back to crankcase (counter-flow to fresh air).
- The PCV valve meters flow based on **manifold vacuum** — high vacuum at idle, low vacuum at WOT.
- Final integration point: vapours re-enter the engine and are combusted, not released to atmosphere.

---

### 4.2 Evaporative (EVAP) System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T1[FUEL TANK VAPOUR SPACE ULLAGE] --> P1[TANK PRESSURE CONTROL VALVE]
    P1 --> C[ACTIVATED CARBON CANISTER 500G]
    C --> V1[VENT VALVE ATMOSPHERIC]
    C --> S1[PURGE SOLENOID VALVE ECU CONTROLLED]
    S1 --> M1[INTAKE MANIFOLD]
    M1 --> ENG[ENGINE COMBUSTION CHAMBER]
    ENG --> EX[EXHAUST]
    FL[FUEL LINES] --> T1
    FIL[FUEL FILLER NECK] --> T1
    LDP[LEAK DETECTION PUMP OBD II] -.-> C
    LDP -.-> T1
```

**Subsystem Roles:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Tank Pressure Control Valve** | Seals tank; opens for purge |
| **Activated Carbon Canister** | Adsorbs HC vapours at rest |
| **Vent Valve** | Allows air ingress to displace purge |
| **Purge Solenoid** | ECU modulates HC flow to engine |
| **Leak Detection Pump** | Tests system integrity for OBD-II |

---

### 4.3 ORVR (Onboard Refuelling Vapour Recovery) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PUMP[FUEL DISPENSER PUMP] --> FILL[FILLER NECK WITH VENTURI]
    FILL --> TANK[FUEL TANK]
    FILL -->|VAPOUR SUCTION| LINE[ORVR VAPOUR LINE]
    LINE --> CHECK[CHECK VALVE]
    CHECK --> CAN[CARBON CANISTER STORAGE]
    CAN --> PURGE[PURGE VALVE]
    PURGE --> INTM[INTAKE MANIFOLD]
    TANK -->|ULLAGE VAPOUR| FILL
```

**Operating Sequence:**
1. **Refuelling begins** — filler gun inserted.
2. **Venturi Effect** — as liquid fuel flows past the narrow filler neck, a low-pressure region forms, drawing tank vapours through the ORVR line.
3. **Check Valve Prevents Backflow** — vapours cannot re-enter the fuel filler.
4. **Vapours Stored in Canister** — for later purging during engine operation.

---

### 4.4 On-Road Emission Measurement (PEMS / RDE) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VEH[TEST VEHICLE] --> GAS[EXHAUST GAS PEMS UNIT]
    GAS -->|RAW SIGNAL| PROC[ONBOARD DATA PROCESSOR]
    PROC -->|GPS SYNC| GPS[GPS MODULE FOR TRIP DETECTION]
    PROC -->|EMISSION G PER KM| CLOUD[TELEMATICS CLOUD STORAGE]
    CLOUD --> ANA[POST PROCESSING SOFTWARE]
    ANA --> CF[CONFORMITY FACTOR CF E ROAD OVER E LAB]
    CF --> DEC[RDE COMPLIANCE DECISION PASS OR FAIL]
    DEC --> REG[REGULATORY REPORTING TO ARAI OR EU TYPE APPROVAL]
```

**Explanation of Block Roles:**
- **PEMS Unit:** Senses exhaust flow, O₂, CO, CO₂, NOx (and PM/PN if equipped), computes instantaneous mass emission rates.
- **GPS Module:** Provides vehicle speed, altitude, and trip classification (urban / rural / motorway) for normal trip validation.
- **Conformity Factor:** Final ratio determines whether the vehicle's real-world emissions are within regulatory tolerance (e.g., 1.43 for NOx).

---

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Consolidated Module 3)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NT1[NON TAILPIPE EMISSION SOURCES]
        E1[CRANKCASE BLOW BY]

    end
    E1 --> C1 --> R1
    E2 --> C2 --> R2
    E3 --> C3 --> R3
    E4 --> C4 --> R4

```

> **Why Block Diagrams Matter in KTU Exams:** The 2024 KTU valuation scheme awards marks for **schematic clarity** in long-answer questions. A well-labelled flow diagram showing emission source → control technology → test method → outcome can fetch 3–4 marks by itself, even if the supporting numerical solution has minor errors.
