## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define crankcase emissions. With a neat sketch, explain the working of a Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV) system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Crankcase emissions, also called **blow-by gases**, are the unburned and partially burned combustion gases that escape past the piston rings into the engine crankcase during the compression and power strokes. **(1 Mark)**

A PCV system routes these gases back to the intake manifold instead of releasing them to atmosphere. **Working principle:** A spring-loaded PCV valve meters the flow of blow-by gases from the crankcase to the intake manifold based on manifold vacuum. At idle (high vacuum), the valve restricts flow to prevent a lean mixture. At higher loads (low vacuum), the valve opens fully, allowing maximum blow-by extraction. **(1 Mark)** Fresh air is drawn into the crankcase through a breather filter, ensuring one-way sweep flow. The captured gases are then re-combusted in the engine, eliminating atmospheric HC release. **(1 Mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**List the three categories of evaporative emissions. Briefly explain the function of an activated carbon canister.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three categories of evaporative emissions are:
1. **Diurnal losses** — caused by daily ambient temperature variation.
2. **Hot-soak losses** — fuel vapours generated after engine shutoff due to residual heat.
3. **Running losses** — vapours produced during vehicle operation. **(1 Mark)**

An **activated carbon canister** stores fuel vapours by adsorption on its high-surface-area carbon bed (typically **800–1500 m²/g** of surface area). **(1 Mark)** During engine operation, the ECU opens a purge solenoid valve, and manifold vacuum draws the stored hydrocarbons into the intake manifold for combustion. This process regenerates the canister and prevents atmospheric release. **(1 Mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the mechanisms of formation and control of **evaporative emissions** from a gasoline vehicle. Describe the construction and working of an activated carbon canister with a labelled diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle fuel tank has a usable volume of **50 L** and an ullage volume of **8 L** at reference temperature. The fuel is gasoline with average molar mass **$M_{HC} = 100$ g/mol**, density **$\rho_{liq} = 750$ kg/m³**, and thermal expansion coefficient **$\beta_f = 9.5 \times 10^{-4}$ /K**. The HC mole fraction in ullage at $30°C$ is **$y_{HC} = 0.45$**. Calculate the diurnal HC emission mass when temperature rises from **$30°C$ to $40°C$** at atmospheric pressure of **$1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa**. Assume the tank vent releases vapour at constant pressure. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Mechanism and Control (7 Marks):**

- **Formation mechanisms (2 Marks):** Evaporative emissions arise from (i) **diurnal breathing losses** due to temperature-induced fuel expansion, (ii) **hot-soak** after engine shutdown, (iii) **running losses** during high-temperature operation, and (iv) **permeation** through polymeric fuel system components (hoses, seals, tank walls made of HDPE or multilayer fluoropolymers).
- **Control strategy (1 Mark):** A **closed fuel system** with an activated carbon canister that captures and stores vapours.
- **Canister construction (2 Marks):** The canister contains **500–1000 g of activated carbon** (typically pellet form, sometimes honeycomb monolith), arranged in a sealed container with three ports: tank inlet, atmospheric vent, and purge line to engine. The carbon's high surface area (800–1500 m²/g) provides enormous adsorption capacity.
- **Working (1 Mark):** During parking/soak, vapours flow into the canister and adsorb. During driving, the ECU opens the purge solenoid, drawing stored HC into the intake manifold for combustion, regenerating the canister.
- **Diagram (1 Mark):** A labelled block/flow diagram showing fuel tank → canister → purge valve → intake manifold.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**

- $V_{ullage} = V_u = 8$ L = $8 \times 10^{-3}$ m³
- $M_{HC} = 100$ g/mol = $0.1$ kg/mol
- $\beta_f = 9.5 \times 10^{-4}$ /K
- $y_{HC} = 0.45$
- $T_i = 30°C = 303$ K
- $T_f = 40°C = 313$ K
- $P = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Step 1:** Compute the increase in liquid fuel volume due to thermal expansion.

$$
\Delta V = V_{liq} \cdot \beta_f \cdot \Delta T
$$

For a full 50 L tank:

$$
\Delta V = 50 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 10 = 4.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2:** This expansion displaces vapour out of the ullage space.

$$
V_{escaped} = 4.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3:** Moles of HC in escaped vapour (using $T_{avg} = (T_i + T_f)/2 = 308$ K):

$$
n_{HC} = \frac{y_{HC} \cdot P \cdot V_{escaped}}{R \cdot T_{avg}}
$$

$$
n_{HC} = \frac{0.45 \times 1.013 \times 10^5 \times 4.75 \times 10^{-4}}{8.314 \times 308}
$$

**Numerator:** $0.45 \times 1.013 \times 10^5 \times 4.75 \times 10^{-4} = 21.65$ Pa·m³

**Denominator:** $8.314 \times 308 = 2560.71$ J/mol

$$
n_{HC} = \frac{21.65}{2560.71} = 8.456 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 4:** Mass of HC emitted:

$$
m_{HC} = n_{HC} \cdot M_{HC} = 8.456 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1 = 8.456 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{HC} \approx 0.846 \text{ g/day}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes in Part (b):**
> - **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert L to m³ (multiply by $10^{-3}$), g to kg (multiply by $10^{-3}$), or °C to K (add 273.15). A single conversion error can cause 2-mark deduction.
> - **Wrong $T$ Choice:** Using $T_i$ or $T_f$ instead of $T_{avg}$ in the ideal gas denominator. Use **$T_{avg} = (T_i + T_f)/2$** for an isobaric process.
> - **Ignoring $y_{HC}$:** Treating displaced vapour as 100% HC instead of 45%. This will inflate the answer by a factor of 2.2×.
> - **Not Using $\beta_f$:** Some students skip the thermal expansion step and assume the expansion is zero.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** What are **on-road (off-cycle) emissions**? Why do they differ from laboratory-measured cycle emissions? Discuss the **Real Driving Emissions (RDE)** test procedure with the help of a block diagram, and explain the **Conformity Factor (CF)** approach. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A driver refuels his car with **40 L of gasoline** at a station where ambient temperature is **30°C** and pressure is **1.013 × 10⁵ Pa**. The ullage HC mole fraction is **0.50**, average HC molar mass is **100 g/mol**, and the ORVR system has an efficiency of **96%**. Calculate:
  (i) The uncontrolled refuelling HC mass released.
  (ii) The mass of HC captured by ORVR.
  (iii) The final HC mass released to the atmosphere. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — On-Road Emissions and RDE (7 Marks):**

- **Definition (1 Mark):** On-road emissions (or off-cycle emissions) are pollutants released by vehicles during **real-world driving conditions** that are not represented in standardized laboratory test cycles.
- **Why they differ (2 Marks):** Real driving includes aggressive accelerations, high engine loads, cold-start events, high-speed cruising, gradient changes, and accessory loads (AC, power steering) that laboratory cycles simplify or omit. Modern engines are also **calibrated** to pass specific test cycles (e.g., WLTP, IDC), which may be sub-optimal for actual driving.
- **RDE Test Procedure (2 Marks):** The vehicle is driven on public roads with a **Portable Emissions Measurement System (PEMS)** installed. The trip must follow a **normal driving** envelope: a balanced mix of urban (low speed), rural (medium speed), and motorway (high speed) segments, with moderate dynamics (no extreme accelerations). Trip altitude must remain within a specified range, and the trip duration typically 90–120 minutes.
- **Conformity Factor (2 Marks):** $CF = e_{road}/e_{lab}$. The EU currently sets $CF_{NOx} \le 1.43$ and $CF_{PN} \le 1.5$ for RDE-compliant vehicles. If CF exceeds these limits, the vehicle fails type approval.
- **Block diagram (1 Mark):** Vehicle → PEMS → Data Logger → GPS → Cloud → CF Calculation → Compliance Decision.

**Part (b) — Refuelling Emission Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $V_f = 40$ L = $0.040$ m³
- $T = 30°C = 303$ K
- $P = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $y_{HC} = 0.50$
- $M_{HC} = 100$ g/mol = $0.1$ kg/mol
- $\eta_{ORVR} = 0.96$

**(i) Uncontrolled HC Mass:**

$$
m_{HC,unc} = \frac{y_{HC} \cdot P \cdot V_f \cdot M_{HC}}{R \cdot T}
$$

$$
m_{HC,unc} = \frac{0.50 \times 1.013 \times 10^5 \times 0.040 \times 0.1}{8.314 \times 303}
$$

**Numerator:** $0.5 \times 1.013 \times 10^5 \times 0.040 \times 0.1 = 202.6$ Pa·m³·kg/mol (numerical value)

**Denominator:** $8.314 \times 303 = 2519.14$ J/mol

$$
m_{HC,unc} = \frac{202.6}{2519.14} = 0.0804 \text{ kg} = 80.4 \text{ g}
$$

**(ii) Mass Captured by ORVR:**

$$
m_{captured} = m_{HC,unc} \times \eta_{ORVR} = 80.4 \times 0.96 = 77.18 \text{ g}
$$

**(iii) Mass Released to Atmosphere:**

$$
m_{released} = m_{HC,unc} - m_{captured} = 80.4 - 77.18 = 3.22 \text{ g}
$$

**OR equivalently:**

$$
m_{released} = m_{HC,unc} \times (1 - \eta_{ORVR}) = 80.4 \times 0.04 = 3.216 \text{ g}
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{HC,unc} = 80.4 \text{ g}, \quad m_{captured} = 77.18 \text{ g}, \quad m_{released} = 3.22 \text{ g}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes in Part (b):**
> - **Mixing Units:** Using $M_{HC}$ in g/mol but forgetting to keep other terms consistent. Always express the **final answer in the unit requested** (g or kg). KTU often marks the unit explicitly.
> - **Forgetting to Multiply by $M_{HC}$:** A common error is computing moles and stopping there, without converting to mass. This loses 1.5 marks.
> - **ORVR Efficiency Interpretation:** Some students subtract 96% from 100% as 96 g instead of 0.96 kg. Always treat efficiency as a **dimensionless ratio**.
> - **Significant Figures:** Present the final answer to **3 significant figures** for full marks; over-precise answers may also lose a mark if intermediate rounding is inconsistent.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Crankcase, Evaporative, Refuelling and On-Road Emissions**

- ☐ **Crankcase emissions** = blow-by gases past piston rings; controlled by **PCV valve** routing vapours to intake manifold.
- ☐ **Blow-by flow** depends on the **cube of ring clearance** — wear is exponentially impactful.
- ☐ **Three categories of evaporative emissions:** Diurnal, Hot-Soak, Running losses.
- ☐ **Activated carbon canister** has surface area **800–1500 m²/g**; capacity typically **0.4–0.6 g HC per g carbon**.
- ☐ **SHED test** is the standard for measuring EVAP emissions; BS-VI limit is **0.5 g/test** for light-duty vehicles.
- ☐ **Refuelling emissions** originate from **ullage displacement**; ORVR efficiency is typically **95–98%**.
- ☐ **On-road (off-cycle) emissions** are measured by **PEMS-based RDE**; **Conformity Factor (CF)** = real/lab emission ratio.
- ☐ **Key Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); always use **absolute temperature (Kelvin)** in ideal gas calculations.
- ☐ **HC Molar Mass for gasoline:** Approximate as **100 g/mol** unless a specific component is given (e.g., octane = 114 g/mol, benzene = 78 g/mol).
- ☐ **Thermal expansion coefficient of gasoline** $\beta_f \approx 9.5 \times 10^{-4}$ /K — critical for diurnal emission problems.
- ☐ **Permeation control** uses multi-layer fuel tanks and fluoropolymer barriers; reduces emissions by **80–90%**.
- ☐ **OBD-II Leak Detection Pump** can detect EVAP system leaks as small as **0.5 mm**.
- ☐ **ORVR check valve** prevents backflow of stored vapours during non-refuelling operation.
- ☐ **PCV valve poppet** uses **spring-loaded** mechanism; flow is **vacuum-modulated**.
- ☐ **Peak cylinder pressures:** SI engines **30–60 bar**, CI engines **80–200 bar** — driving force for blow-by.
- ☐ **RDE Conformity Factors (EU 2024):** $CF_{NOx} \le 1.43$, $CF_{PN} \le 1.5$.
- ☐ **Perfluorinated membranes** (e.g., Selar® RF) reduce permeation emissions by 80–90% in modern fuel systems.
- ☐ **For numerical problems:** Always (a) draw a free-body / flow diagram, (b) list givens with units, (c) show step-by-step substitution, (d) box the final answer with correct units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laboratory Testing Procedures, NDIR Analysers, Chemiluminescence Analysers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laboratory Testing Procedures, NDIR Analysers, Chemiluminescence Analysers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Laboratory Testing Procedures for Automotive Emissions

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> **Laboratory Testing Procedures** are standardized, controlled-environment experimental methodologies employed to quantify regulated exhaust emissions (CO, HC, NOx, PM, CO₂) from in-use and prototype vehicles. These procedures operate on a **Constant Volume Sampling (CVS)** principle where the entire mass of exhaust is diluted with filtered ambient air at a fixed volumetric rate, and a proportional sample is drawn through analyzers for gravimetric and spectroscopic determination in accordance with test cycles such as **FTP-75 (Federal Test Procedure)**, **ECE-15 (EEC Directive 70/220)**, and **Bharat Stage (BS-VI/Euro 6)** protocols.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus (Module 3):** The three pillars of laboratory emissions measurement are:
> 1. **Sample Acquisition** – CVS, Critical Flow Venturi (CFV), Partial Flow Dilution (PFD)
> 2. **Component Separation & Detection** – NDIR (CO, CO₂, HC), Chemiluminescence (NOx), FID (HC), MFD (PM)
> 3. **Quantification & Reporting** – Mass calculation via integration over test cycle

---

### 1.2 NDIR Analyser (Non-Dispersive Infrared Analyser)

> **Formal Definition:** An NDIR analyser is a **non-dispersive** (i.e., uses no prism/grating to separate wavelengths) gas analyser that operates on the principle of **selective infrared absorption** by heteronuclear diatomic and polyatomic molecules. The IR source emits broadband radiation, and the detector is filled with the target gas, making it selectively sensitive to a specific absorption band (Beer-Lambert regime).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytes Measured by NDIR:** CO (4.67 µm), CO₂ (4.26 µm), HC (3.3–3.5 µm CH stretch), NO (5.3 µm), SO₂ (7.4 µm), N₂O (4.5 µm).
> **Why "Non-Dispersive"?** Unlike FTIR (which uses an interferometer to disperse light into a spectrum), NDIR uses a **fixed optical filter** (gas-filled detector cell) to isolate the wavelength of interest. This makes the instrument rugged, fast, and ideal for online vehicle testing.

### 1.3 Chemiluminescence Analyser (CLA)

> **Formal Definition:** A Chemiluminescence Analyser is a gaseous detection instrument that quantifies **nitric oxide (NO)** and, by catalytic conversion, **total oxides of nitrogen (NOx = NO + NO₂)**. It exploits the **gas-phase chemiluminescent reaction** between NO and excess ozone (O₃) producing NO₂ in an excited electronic state. The relaxation of excited NO₂ to its ground state emits a broadband photon centred near **λ ≈ 600–875 nm (red to near-IR)**, which is detected by a photomultiplier tube (PMT).

$$ \text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{NO}_2^{*} + \text{O}_2 $$

$$ \text{NO}_2^{*} \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + h\nu \quad (\lambda \approx 600\text{–}875 \text{ nm}) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Modern CLA instruments must comply with detection limits of **≤ 1 ppm** NO and linearity of **± 2%** of full scale (per CPCB/ARAI standards). NO₂ is measured by passing the sample through a **molybdenum (Mo) or stainless-steel catalytic converter** at **~ 350 °C** that quantitatively reduces NO₂ → NO before the reaction cell.

---

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | Why It Works |
|---|---|---|
| NDIR Analyser | A **"scent detector"** like a sniffer dog picking out one smell in a complex cocktail | Each gas absorbs IR at a unique wavelength — like a unique fingerprint |
| Beer-Lambert Law | A pair of **sunglasses** — the more concentrated the dye (gas), the darker (less light reaches the eye) | Light attenuation scales with path length and concentration |
| Chemiluminescence | A **"glowing firefly"** — chemical reaction produces light directly, no flame needed | NO + O₃ reaction inherently emits photons (no external light source) |
| Constant Volume Sampling (CVS) | A **"diluting juice concentrate"** — measuring the whole glass instead of a sip | Captures the entire exhaust mass-flow for accurate integrated emission calculation |
| Chassis Dynamometer | A **"treadmill for cars"** | The car drives on rollers simulating road load (inertia + friction) |

---

### 1.5 Why These Procedures Matter (Engineering Context)

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Relevance:** Under BS-VI / Euro 6 norms, every new vehicle model must undergo **Whole Vehicle Type Approval (WVTA)** at agencies such as **ARAI (India), UTAC (France), TÜV (Germany), JARI (Japan)**. The lab test report — captured in the **Type Approval Certificate** — defines whether a vehicle is legally saleable. A **1%** analytical error in NDIR can translate to a vehicle failing the CO limit (~ **1.0 g/km** for petrol cars under BS-VI).

**Typical Measured Pollutant Ranges (Petrol SI Engine, FTP-75):**

| Pollutant | Symbol | Typical Range | KTU Standard Limit (BS-VI Petrol) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon Monoxide | CO | 0.5 – 3.0 g/km | **≤ 1.0 g/km** |
| Total Hydrocarbons | THC | 0.05 – 0.2 g/km | **≤ 0.1 g/km** |
| Non-Methane HC | NMOG | 0.03 – 0.1 g/km | **≤ 0.068 g/km** |
| Oxides of Nitrogen | NOx | 0.02 – 0.1 g/km | **≤ 0.06 g/km** |
| Particulate Matter | PM | 0.002 – 0.005 g/km | **≤ 0.0045 g/km** |

> **Standard Reference Metrics (Bolded):** **FTP-75 (US)**, **NEDC (EU pre-2017)**, **WLTP (EU 2017+)**, **IM240 (US Inspection)**, **ASM (Acceleration Simulation Mode – India)**, **IDC (Indian Driving Cycle)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle of NDIR Analyser (Block-Level Theory)

The NDIR analyser comprises **five sub-systems**, each with a definite physical role:

1. **IR Source (Black-Body Emitter)** – A heated filament (~ 600–800 °C) producing broadband IR radiation, often modulated by a **mechanical chopper** at 100–400 Hz to enable AC detection.
2. **Sample Cell (Absorption Chamber)** – A gas-tight tube (path length L = 0.1 – 0.5 m) through which the exhaust sample flows continuously.
3. **Wavelength Selector (Optical Filter)** – Either a **narrow-band interference filter** (modern micro-NDIR) or a **gas-filled reference cell** (classic Luft-detector design).
4. **Detector (Pneumatic or Solid-State)** – Measures IR intensity at the absorption band. Two main types:
   - **Luft Detector (Microphone-based):** Uses a sealed reference gas chamber; absorbed IR heats the gas → pressure wave → microphone (capacitive).
   - **Thermopile/Pyroelectric Detector:** Solid-state; IR heats a thermopile generating a voltage.
5. **Signal Processor** – Lock-in amplifier synchronous with chopper; converts transmittance to concentration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Non-Dispersive" matters:** The detector is itself filled with the gas of interest (e.g., pure CO at the CO-cell). When IR of the CO absorption band reaches the detector, the gas inside absorbs energy, producing a temperature/pressure signal. The detector is *self-tuning* — no external monochromator is required. This makes NDIR robust to vibrations, ideal for **Portable Emissions Measurement Systems (PEMS)** and chassis-dyno use.

---

## 2.2 Operational Principle of Chemiluminescence Analyser

The CLA has **four functional blocks**:

1. **Ozone Generator** – Corona discharge (≈ 5 kV AC) in dry O₂ or air → O₃ at ~ 1–5% concentration.
2. **Reaction Chamber (Mixing Cell)** – Sample NO + excess O₃ → excited NO₂*; visible red glow visible in dark.
3. **Optical Assembly** – Bandpass filter (600–875 nm) + PMT (photomultiplier tube) cooled to ~ 5 °C to reduce dark noise.
4. **NO₂ → NO Converter (Mo catalyst at 350 °C)** – For total NOx measurement; sample stream is split, one half bypasses converter (measures NO only), other half passes through (measures NOx). NOx − NO = NO₂ by difference.

> **KTU Key Fact:** The **limit of detection (LOD)** of a modern CLA is **0.5 ppb** (parts per billion) and the **response time (T₉₀)** is **< 2 seconds** for online CVS systems.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe symbol `\vert` is used in place of `|` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. All equations are rendered in LaTeX.

| # | Equation / Concept | LaTeX Form | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Beer-Lambert Law (NDIR Core)** | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-k(\lambda) \cdot C \cdot L}$ | $I$ in W/m², $C$ in mol/m³, $L$ in m | Attenuation of IR intensity through absorbing gas |
| 2 | **Transmittance** | $T = \dfrac{I}{I_0} = e^{-k C L}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of transmitted to incident IR |
| 3 | **Absorbance** | $A = -\log_{10}(T) = k C L$ | dimensionless (AU) | Linear in concentration (for $A < 1$) |
| 4 | **Specific Absorptivity** | $k(\lambda) = \varepsilon(\lambda) \cdot \ln(10)$ | m²/mol | Wavelength-dependent property of gas |
| 5 | **CL Photon Flux** | $\Phi = \eta \cdot k_r \cdot [NO][O_3] \cdot V$ | photons/s | Chemiluminescent output rate |
| 6 | **CL Quantum Yield** | $\Phi_{QY} = \dfrac{\text{photons emitted}}{\text{molecules reacted}}$ | dimensionless | Efficiency: ≈ 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻² for NO+O₃ |
| 7 | **PMT Photocurrent** | $i_{PMT} = G \cdot e \cdot \Phi \cdot \eta_{QE}$ | amperes | $G$: gain 10⁶, $\eta_{QE}$: quantum efficiency |
| 8 | **CVS Mass Emission** | $m_{pol} = \dfrac{1}{d_{test}} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} c(t) \cdot \dot{V}(t) \, dt$ | g/km | Mass of pollutant per km |
| 9 | **CVS Dilution Ratio** | $DR = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{diluted}}{\dot{V}_{exhaust}}$ | dimensionless | Typically 8–15 for light-duty vehicles |
| 10 | **CVS Mass Flow** | $\dot{m}_{CVS} = \rho_{air} \cdot \dot{V}_{CVS}$ | kg/s | Diluted stream at standard $T = 25\,°C$ |
| 11 | **Standard Volume Correction** | $V_{std} = V_{meas} \cdot \dfrac{P_{std}}{P_{meas}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{meas}}{T_{std}}$ | m³ at NTP | Corrects sample bag volume to reference conditions |
| 12 | **NDIR Sensitivity Limit** | $C_{min} = \dfrac{A_{noise}}{k L}$ | ppm | $A_{noise} = 10^{-4}$ AU typical |
| 13 | **CLA Linearity Check** | $R^2 \geq 0.998$ for 5-point calibration | dimensionless | Per ISO 12039 / CPCB standards |
| 14 | **Coulometric NO₂** | $Q = n_{NO_2} \cdot z \cdot F$ | coulombs | Alternative wet-chemistry method (deprecated) |
| 15 | **PMT Background Counts** | $N_{bg} = N_{dark} + N_{stray}$ | counts/s | Reduced by cooling PMT to 5 °C |

> **Standard Reference Conditions:** **T = 25 °C (298.15 K)**, **P = 101.325 kPa**, **STP = 0 °C (273.15 K), 101.325 kPa**, **NTP = 20 °C, 101.325 kPa**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **NDIR** is the *workhorse* of automotive emissions benches due to its **simultaneous multi-gas capability** (single analyser with rotating filter wheel measures CO, CO₂, HC in < 1 s).
- **Chemiluminescence** is *the only* technique mandated by **US EPA 40 CFR Part 1065** and **CPCB/ARAI** for regulatory NOx measurement, owing to its ppb-level sensitivity (essential for low-emission BS-VI diesels targeting 0.06 g/km NOx).
- **Portable variants** (PEMS — Portable Emissions Measurement System) for Real-Driving Emissions (RDE) integrate both techniques in a single rack, e.g., **Horiba OBS-ONE**, **Sensors SEMTECH-DS**, **AVL M.O.V.E**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Use-Case:** A diesel sedan (BS-VI) on a chassis dyno accumulates ≈ **2 million data points** during an FTP-75 cycle (1372 s × 1 Hz × 3 bags × 5 pollutants). The NDIR + CLA combo processes this in < 10 minutes with R² > 0.99 linearity per pollutant.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Beer-Lambert Law (NDIR Working Equation)

### Step 1: Differential Form of Absorption

Consider a thin slab of gas of thickness $\mathrm{d}x$ containing a uniform concentration $C$ of absorbing molecules. The reduction in intensity $\mathrm{d}I$ as the beam traverses this slab is proportional to the incident intensity $I(x)$, the concentration $C$, and the slab thickness $\mathrm{d}x$.

$$ \mathrm{d}I = -k(\lambda) \cdot C \cdot I(x) \cdot \mathrm{d}x $$

> **Logic:** More molecules in the path → more photons absorbed. The proportionality constant $k(\lambda)$ is the specific absorptivity (m²/mol) and is a function of wavelength.

### Step 2: Separation of Variables

Divide both sides by $I(x)$:

$$ \dfrac{\mathrm{d}I}{I} = -k(\lambda) \cdot C \cdot \mathrm{d}x $$

### Step 3: Integration over the Cell Length

Integrate from $x = 0$ (entry, $I = I_0$) to $x = L$ (exit, $I = I$):

$$ \int_{I_0}^{I} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}I'}{I'} = -k(\lambda) \cdot C \cdot \int_{0}^{L} \mathrm{d}x $$

$$ \ln(I) - \ln(I_0) = -k(\lambda) \cdot C \cdot L $$

$$ \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) = -k(\lambda) \cdot C \cdot L $$

### Step 4: Exponentiate to Get Exponential Attenuation

$$ \dfrac{I}{I_0} = e^{-k(\lambda) C L} $$

$$ I = I_0 \cdot e^{-k(\lambda) C L} $$

### Step 5: Define Transmittance and Absorbance

$$ T = \dfrac{I}{I_0} = e^{-k C L} $$

$$ A = -\log_{10}(T) = k C L \cdot \log_{10}(e) $$

$$ \boxed{A = \varepsilon(\lambda) \cdot C \cdot L} $$

where $\varepsilon(\lambda) = k(\lambda) \cdot \log_{10}(e) = k(\lambda) / 2.3026$ is the **molar absorptivity** (L · mol⁻¹ · cm⁻¹).

### Step 6: Invert to Solve for Concentration (NDIR Working Equation)

$$ \boxed{C = \dfrac{A}{\varepsilon(\lambda) \cdot L} = \dfrac{-\log_{10}(I/I_0)}{\varepsilon(\lambda) \cdot L}} $$

> **Logic:** The detector measures $I$, the electronics knows $I_0$ (reference), path length $L$ is fixed by the cell design, and $\varepsilon(\lambda)$ is tabulated. Therefore, **C is uniquely determined from absorbance A**.

### Step 7: Validity Range of Beer-Lambert Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Beer-Lambert Law is **linear in C only for $A < 1$** (i.e., $T > 0.1$). For high-concentration ranges (e.g., CO at 5–10%), the analyser is operated in a **multi-pass Herriott cell** (path length extended to 5–20 m by mirrors) to keep $A < 1$. **Failure to obey** this constraint causes *pressure broadening* and *saturation* non-linearities.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Chemiluminescent Photon Rate from First-Order Kinetics

### Step 1: Reaction Rate

The NO + O₃ reaction is first-order in both [NO] and [O₃]:

$$ \text{Rate} = -\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[NO]}{\mathrm{d}t} = k_r \cdot [NO] \cdot [O_3] $$

where $k_r \approx 1.8 \times 10^{-14}$ cm³·molecule⁻¹·s⁻¹ at 298 K (NIST recommended).

### Step 2: Chemiluminescent Branching Fraction

Only a fraction $\Phi_{QY}$ of the reacting molecules produce an excited state NO₂* that subsequently emits a photon:

$$ \text{Emission Rate} = \Phi_{QY} \cdot k_r \cdot [NO] \cdot [O_3] \cdot V $$

where $V$ is the reaction chamber volume.

### Step 3: PMT Photocurrent Calculation

The PMT measures the photon flux after optical filtering:

$$ i_{PMT} = G \cdot e \cdot \Phi_{QY} \cdot k_r \cdot [NO] \cdot [O_3] \cdot V \cdot \eta_{QE} \cdot \Omega/4\pi $$

> **Logic:** $G$ = dynode gain (≈ 10⁶), $e$ = electron charge (1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C), $\eta_{QE}$ = photocathode quantum efficiency (~ 15% at 700 nm), $\Omega/4\pi$ = solid angle collection factor.

### Step 4: Calibration

A **gas divider** dilutes a known NO standard (e.g., 1000 ppm NO in N₂) to multiple concentration points (50, 100, 200, 500 ppm). The PMT current $i_{PMT}$ vs. [NO] is plotted — a straight line. From the slope:

$$ \boxed{[NO] = \dfrac{i_{PMT}}{S \cdot k_r \cdot [O_3] \cdot V \cdot \eta_{QE}}} $$

where $S$ is the slope sensitivity (amperes per ppm).

### Step 5: NO₂ by Difference

$$ \boxed{[NO_2] = [NO_x] - [NO]} $$

where $[NO_x]$ is the signal when the Mo-converter is active.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: CVS Mass Emission Calculation

> **Problem:** A BS-VI petrol car is tested on a chassis dynamometer over the FTP-75 cycle. The CVS volumetric flow is $\dot{V}_{CVS} = 12 \, \text{m}^3/\text{min}$ at standard conditions. The CO concentration in the CVS bag is $c_{CO} = 60 \, \text{ppm}$ (averaged). The total distance simulated is $d = 17.77 \, \text{km}$. Calculate the **CO mass emission in g/km**.

### Solution

**Step 1: Total CVS Volume Per Test**

$$ V_{CVS,total} = \dot{V}_{CVS} \times t_{test} = 12 \, \dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{min}} \times 22.86 \, \text{min} = 274.32 \, \text{m}^3 $$

> **Note:** FTP-75 total duration = 1877 s ≈ 31.3 min (cold-start 505 s + stabilized 867 s + hot-start 505 s). The 22.86 min here represents the post-stabilization phase.

**Step 2: Convert ppm to Volume Fraction**

$$ c_{CO} = 60 \times 10^{-6} \, \dfrac{\text{m}^3 \, CO}{\text{m}^3 \, CVS} $$

**Step 3: Calculate Volume of CO at NTP**

$$ V_{CO} = c_{CO} \times V_{CVS,total} = 60 \times 10^{-6} \times 274.32 = 0.01646 \, \text{m}^3 $$

**Step 4: Convert to Mass Using Ideal Gas Law**

Using $P = 101.325$ kPa, $T = 298.15$ K, $M_{CO} = 28.01$ g/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K):

$$ m_{CO} = \dfrac{P \cdot V_{CO} \cdot M_{CO}}{R \cdot T} = \dfrac{101325 \times 0.01646 \times 28.01}{8.314 \times 298.15} $$

**Step 5: Numerator**

$$ 101325 \times 0.01646 = 1667.81 $$

$$ 1667.81 \times 28.01 = 46714.6 $$

**Step 6: Denominator**

$$ 8.314 \times 298.15 = 2478.83 $$

**Step 7: Mass of CO**

$$ m_{CO} = \dfrac{46714.6}{2478.83} = 18.846 \, \text{g} $$

**Step 8: Mass Emission per km**

$$ \boxed{m_{CO,km} = \dfrac{m_{CO}}{d} = \dfrac{18.846}{17.77} = 1.0605 \, \text{g/km}} $$

> **Interpretation:** This is **slightly above** the BS-VI petrol limit of **1.0 g/km**, so the vehicle would **fail** the type approval. A reduction of 6% in catalyst light-off temperature would bring it within compliance.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: NDIR vs CLA vs Other Techniques

| Parameter | NDIR | Chemiluminescence (CLA) | FID (Flame Ionization) | FTIR | Mass Spectrometer |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Target Gases | CO, CO₂, HC, NO, SO₂ | NO, NO₂ (via converter) | HC (total) | All IR-active | All |
| Detection Limit | 1–10 ppm | 0.5 ppb (NO) | 50 ppb C | 1–100 ppm | 1 ppb |
| Response Time (T₉₀) | 1–3 s | 1–2 s | < 1 s | 5–10 s | 1 s |
| Linearity | ± 2% FS | ± 1% FS | ± 1% FS | ± 2% FS | ± 0.5% |
| Interferences | H₂O, CO₂ cross-sensitivity | NO₂ converter efficiency | CO₂, O₂ | Spectral overlap | Charge competition |
| Cost (USD) | 8,000–25,000 | 15,000–40,000 | 10,000–20,000 | 50,000+ | 100,000+ |
| Portability | High (PEMS) | Medium | High | Low | Low |
| **KTU Regulatory Status** | **Mandated for CO, CO₂** | **Mandated for NOx** | **Mandated for THC** | Research | Reference lab |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram A: NDIR Analyser — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[IR Source Filament 700C] --> A2[Mechanical Chopper 200Hz]
    A2 --> A3[Sample Cell Path L 0.3m]
    A3 --> A4[Optical Bandpass Filter 4.67um]
    A4 --> A5[Luft Detector CO filled]
    A5 --> A6[Capacitive Microphone]
    A6 --> A7[Lock in Amplifier]
    A7 --> A8[Microprocessor A log I over I0]
    A8 --> A9[Display C ppm]
    B1[Reference Cell Sealed CO] -.-> A5
    C1[Exhaust Sample In] --> A3
    C2[Sample Out to Pump] --> A3
    A8 --> D1[RS232 CAN Output to CVS Data Logger]
```

> **Reading Guide:**
> - **Solid arrows** → signal/light path
> - **Dotted arrows** → reference path (for differential measurement)
> - **A1 → A9** traces a single IR photon: emission → chop → absorption → detection → processing → display
> - **C1/C2** are the gas inlet/outlet; the pump draws sample continuously

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram B: Chemiluminescence Analyser — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Exhaust Sample from CVS] --> V1[Three Way Solenoid Valve]
    V1 --> P1[Path 1 NO only bypass Mo converter]
    V1 --> P2[Path 2 NOx through Mo at 350C NO2 to NO]
    P1 --> RC1[Reaction Chamber]
    P2 --> RC1
    O3G[Ozone Generator Corona 5kV] --> O3D[O3 Dryer Silica Gel]
    O3D --> RC1
    RC1 --> OPT[Optical Filter 600 to 875nm]
    OPT --> PMT[PMT Photomultiplier Cooled 5C]
    PMT --> AMP[Amplifier and Discriminator]
    AMP --> DAQ[Data Acquisition 10Hz]
    RC1 --> V2[Vacuum Pump 2 L per min]
    V2 --> EXH[Exhaust to Lab Vent]
    DAQ --> PROC[Processor computes NO and NO2 by difference]
    PROC --> DISP[Display NOx ppm and NO ppm]
```

> **Reading Guide:** The dual-path design (P1, P2) enables *simultaneous* measurement of NO and NOx, with NO₂ derived as the difference — eliminating time-skew errors.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram C: Complete Laboratory CVS Test Cell Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VEH[Test Vehicle on Chassis Dyno] --> TAIL[Tailpipe]
    TAIL --> DIL[Dilution Tunnel CFV]
    DIL --> AIR[Filtered Ambient Air Blower]
    AIR --> DIL
    DIL --> CVS[Constant Volume Pump]
    CVS --> SPL[Solenoid Sample Splitter]
    SPL --> B1[Bag 1 Cold Start]
    SPL --> B2[Bag 2 Stabilized]
    SPL --> B3[Bag 3 Hot Start]
    B1 --> NDIR
    B2 --> NDIR
    B3 --> NDIR
    B1 --> CLA
    B2 --> CLA
    B3 --> CLA
    B1 --> FID
    B2 --> FID
    B3 --> FID
    NDIR --> DLOG[Data Logger]
    CLA --> DLOG
    FID --> DLOG
    DLOG --> CALC[Mass Emission Calculator g per km]
    CALC --> RPT[Compliance Report]
```

> **Engineering Insight:** Note the **three bags** correspond to the three phases of FTP-75: cold-start transient, stabilized operation, and hot transient. The result is *mass-weighted* using a formula:
>
> $$ m_{total} = 0.43 \cdot m_1 + 0.57 \cdot m_2 + m_3 $$
>
> (Cold start weighted higher to reflect real-world urban cold driving.)

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram D: Signal Processing & Calibration Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Daily Start of Test]) --> CAL[Zero Gas Calibration N2 99.999 percent]
    CAL --> ZC[Verify Zero Drift less than 1 percent FS]
    ZC --> SPAN[Span Gas Calibration 500 ppm CO 300 ppm NO]
    SPAN --> LIN[Linearity Check 5 points R2 greater than 0.998]
    LIN --> LEAK[Leak Check Less than 1 percent per min]
    LEAK --> READY[System Ready Green Light]
    READY --> TEST[Test Sequence FTP75 Start]
    TEST --> ACQ[Acquire Data 1 Hz]
    ACQ --> END([Test End])
    END --> POST[Post test Span Drift Check]
    POST --> JUDG{Drift less than 2 percent?}
    JUDG -->|Yes| VAL[Data Valid]
    JUDG -->|No| REJ[Reject and Recalibrate]
    VAL --> RPT2[Issue Report]
```

> **QA Note:** Per **40 CFR 1065**, if the post-test span drift exceeds **± 2%**, the run is invalid and must be repeated after full re-calibration. This is the *single most common* cause of re-tests at ARAI.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions (Direct Recall)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State the Beer-Lambert Law as applied to an NDIR analyser and explain why the detector is termed "non-dispersive."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Beer-Lambert Law governing NDIR is $A = \varepsilon(\lambda) C L$, where $A$ is absorbance, $\varepsilon(\lambda)$ is the molar absorptivity, $C$ is concentration, and $L$ is the optical path length. The detector is termed **"non-dispersive"** because the wavelength of interest is selected *not* by a prism or grating (which would *disperse* the light) but by a **gas-filled reference cell** at the detector that is itself selectively sensitive to the target gas's absorption band. The detector acts as its own optical filter, making the instrument rugged, fast-responding, and well-suited to online vehicle emissions measurement.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the principle of chemiluminescence as used in NOx analysers. Write the governing reaction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A chemiluminescence analyser (CLA) measures NOx by the reaction of nitric oxide (NO) with excess ozone (O₃) in a reaction chamber. The reaction produces electronically excited NO₂*, which relaxes to its ground state by emitting a photon in the **600–875 nm** red-to-near-IR range, detected by a cooled photomultiplier tube (PMT). The governing reactions are:

$$ \text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{NO}_2^{*} + \text{O}_2 $$

$$ \text{NO}_2^{*} \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + h\nu $$

Total NOx is measured by passing the sample through a **Mo catalyst converter (350 °C)** which reduces NO₂ to NO, and NO₂ is computed as the difference between NOx and NO.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024]
**(a)** Describe, with a neat block diagram, the working of an **NDIR analyser** for measuring CO and CO₂ in automotive exhaust. State Beer-Lambert's law and explain the role of the gas-filled detector. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chassis-dynamometer CVS test of a BS-VI petrol vehicle over the FTP-75 cycle gives a CVS total flow of **8.5 m³/min** and an average CO concentration in the dilution tunnel of **42 ppm**. The total simulated distance is **17.77 km**. Calculate the **CO mass emission in g/km** and state whether the vehicle complies with the BS-VI limit of **1.0 g/km**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer A (a) — 7 marks**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
Refer to **Section 4.1 Mermaid Diagram A** for the complete block-level architecture. The flow is:

1. **IR Source (1 mark):** A heated tungsten filament (~700 °C) emits broadband IR radiation spanning 2–10 µm.
2. **Chopper (0.5 mark):** A rotating mechanical chopper modulates the IR at 200 Hz for AC lock-in detection, eliminating 1/f noise and DC drift.
3. **Sample Cell (1 mark):** The modulated beam passes through a gas-tight cell of path length L = 0.1–0.5 m carrying the exhaust sample continuously drawn by a pump. CO absorbs at **4.67 µm** and CO₂ at **4.26 µm**.
4. **Wavelength Filter (0.5 mark):** An interference filter centred on the target absorption band selects the relevant wavelength.
5. **Luft Detector (1 mark):** A sealed reference cell filled with the same gas (CO or CO₂) acts as a highly selective detector. IR at the absorption wavelength is absorbed by the gas inside, heating it → pressure rise → capacitive microphone detects the pressure wave. Differential comparison with a sealed reference arm cancels common-mode drift.

**Beer-Lambert Law (2 marks):**
The transmitted intensity is:

$$ I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\varepsilon(\lambda) C L} $$

$$ A = \varepsilon(\lambda) \cdot C \cdot L $$

Solving for concentration:

$$ C = \dfrac{A}{\varepsilon(\lambda) \cdot L} = \dfrac{-\log_{10}(I/I_0)}{\varepsilon(\lambda) \cdot L} $$

The **gas-filled detector's role**: by filling the detector cell with the *target gas*, only the wavelength absorbed by that gas produces a pressure response. This is the *non-dispersive* principle — no external dispersing element (prism/grating) is needed because the detector is inherently wavelength-selective.

**Limitations (1 mark):** NDIR cannot measure homonuclear diatomics (N₂, O₂, H₂) since these have no IR-active vibrational modes. Water vapour causes cross-interference and must be removed by a **Nafion dryer** or **cold trap (1–4 °C)** before the cell.

---

### **Model Answer A (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $\dot{V}_{CVS} = 8.5$ m³/min, $c_{CO} = 42$ ppm, $d = 17.77$ km.

**Step 1: CVS Test Duration for FTP-75 (1 mark)**

$$ t_{test} = 1877 \text{ s} = 31.28 \text{ min} $$

**Step 2: Total CVS Volume (1 mark)**

$$ V_{CVS} = 8.5 \times 31.28 = 265.88 \, \text{m}^3 $$

**Step 3: Volume of CO (1 mark)**

$$ V_{CO} = 42 \times 10^{-6} \times 265.88 = 0.01117 \, \text{m}^3 $$

**Step 4: Mass of CO via Ideal Gas Law (3 marks)**

Using $P = 101.325$ kPa, $T = 298.15$ K, $M_{CO} = 28.01$ g/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K):

$$ m_{CO} = \dfrac{P V_{CO} M_{CO}}{R T} = \dfrac{101325 \times 0.01117 \times 28.01}{8.314 \times 298.15} $$

**Numerator:** $101325 \times 0.01117 \times 28.01 = 31,694.1$

**Denominator:** $8.314 \times 298.15 = 2478.83$

$$ m_{CO} = \dfrac{31694.1}{2478.83} = 12.786 \, \text{g} $$

**Step 5: Mass Emission per km (1 mark)**

$$ m_{CO,km} = \dfrac{12.786}{17.77} = 0.7195 \, \text{g/km} $$

**Conclusion:** $0.72 \, \text{g/km} < 1.0 \, \text{g/km}$ → **The vehicle COMPLIES** with the BS-VI CO limit. ✅

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**(a)** Explain with a block diagram the working of a **chemiluminescence analyser (CLA)** for NOx measurement. Describe the role of the **Mo-converter** and the principle of NO₂ quantification by difference. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **NDIR and chemiluminescence analysers** with respect to (i) target gases, (ii) detection principle, (iii) detection limit, (iv) linearity, and (v) typical cost. State one engineering limitation of each. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer B (a) — 7 marks**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.2 Mermaid Diagram B**. Key stages:

1. **Ozone Generation (1 mark):** Dry air or O₂ passes through a corona discharge cell (~5 kV AC), generating O₃ at ~1–5% concentration. The O₃ is dried in a silica-gel trap to prevent quenching.
2. **Reaction Chamber (1.5 marks):** Sample NO mixes with excess O₃ in a low-volume reaction cell. The reaction $\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{NO}_2^* + \text{O}_2$ produces excited NO₂*. As NO₂* relaxes, it emits a red/near-IR photon ($h\nu$ at 600–875 nm). A vacuum pump (2 L/min) maintains low pressure to maximize mean free path and reduce quenching.
3. **Optical Detection (1.5 marks):** A bandpass optical filter selects the 600–875 nm range; a **thermoelectrically cooled PMT** (–5 to 5 °C) detects the photons, reducing dark noise. The PMT output current is proportional to [NO].
4. **Mo-Converter for Total NOx (1 mark):** A separate sample stream is routed through a heated stainless-steel or Mo catalyst at **~ 350 °C**, which quantitatively reduces NO₂ → NO. The analyser measures [NOx] = [NO] + [NO₂] in this stream.
5. **NO₂ by Difference (0.5 mark):** $[NO_2] = [NOx] - [NO]$. Two solenoid valves alternate the sample path every second, enabling quasi-simultaneous measurement.

---

### **Model Answer B (b) — 7 marks**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Aspect | NDIR | Chemiluminescence (CLA) |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Target Gases** | CO, CO₂, HC, NO, SO₂, N₂O | NO, NO₂ (via Mo converter) |
| **(ii) Detection Principle** | Selective IR absorption (Beer-Lambert) | Chemiluminescent reaction of NO + O₃ |
| **(iii) Detection Limit** | 1–10 ppm (gas-dependent) | 0.5 ppb (NO), 1 ppb (NOx) |
| **(iv) Linearity** | ± 2% of full scale | ± 1% of full scale (after calibration) |
| **(v) Typical Cost (USD)** | 8,000–25,000 (benchtop) | 15,000–40,000 (benchtop) |

**Limitations (2 marks):**

- **NDIR Limitation:** Cannot measure homonuclear diatomics (N₂, O₂, H₂) because these have **no permanent dipole moment change** during vibration and therefore are IR-inactive. Also, severe water-vapour cross-interference at high humidity requires sample drying (Nafion membrane or cold trap).
- **CLA Limitation:** Requires a high-purity **O₂ or dry air supply** for the ozone generator, and the Mo converter's **conversion efficiency** (typically 95–98%) must be regularly validated; incomplete conversion leads to under-reporting of NO₂.

---

## **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in NDIR/CLA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Beer-Lambert Formula (2 marks):** Students often write $I = I_0 e^{-kCx}$ but forget to mention that **C is the molar concentration** and **L is the path length** — units must be SI-consistent. Examiner deducts 1 mark if units are not stated.
>
> 2. **NDIR "Non-Dispersive" — High-weightage (2 marks):** Do NOT confuse with FTIR (which IS dispersive). The correct answer: the **detector itself** is filled with the target gas, acting as a built-in filter. Many students incorrectly say "no prism" without explaining the gas-cell mechanism.
>
> 3. **Chemiluminescence Reaction (1 mark):** A common error is writing the wrong products. Correct: $\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{NO}_2^{*} + \text{O}_2$ (NOT $\text{N}_2 + \text{O}_2$). The asterisk (*) on NO₂ indicating the excited state is often missed — full 1 mark depends on it.
>
> 4. **NO₂ by Difference (1 mark):** Students forget the Mo converter role. You MUST mention: Mo at **350 °C** reduces NO₂ to NO; the analyser measures NO in both modes; NO₂ = NOx − NO.
>
> 5. **Mass Emission Calculation (CVS):** Three places students lose marks:
>    - Forget the **NTP correction** (T = 298.15 K, not 273.15 K unless STP is specified)
>    - Mix up g/km with g/mile (1 mile = 1.609 km)
>    - Forget the **molar mass** of CO = 28.01 g/mol (sometimes written as 28 — the examiner may allow either but 28.01 is rigorous)
>
> 6. **Block Diagrams:** The block diagram must be a *flow diagram with arrows* showing the signal/gas flow. A simple list of components will earn only 1/3 marks. **No diagram = full 3 marks lost** in 7-mark sub-parts.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **NDIR = Non-Dispersive InfraRed** — uses *gas-filled detector* as built-in optical filter; **cannot** measure N₂, O₂, H₂ (homonuclear).
- ✅ **Beer-Lambert Law:** $A = \varepsilon(\lambda) C L$; valid only for $A < 1$ (transmittance > 10%).
- ✅ **CLA = ChemiLuminescence Analyser** — measures NO via NO + O₃ reaction emitting red light at **600–875 nm**.
- ✅ **NO₂ quantification:** Use **Mo converter at 350 °C**; NO₂ = NOx − NO.
- ✅ **CVS** = Constant Volume Sampling; flow is held constant; mass emission = $\dfrac{\rho \cdot c \cdot \dot{V} \cdot t}{d}$.
- ✅ **Reference conditions:** NTP = 20 °C, 101.325 kPa; STP = 0 °C, 101.325 kPa; SATP = 25 °C, 101.325 kPa.
- ✅ **PMT** is cooled to ~ 5 °C to reduce dark noise; gain G ≈ 10⁶.
- ✅ **Calibration sequence:** Zero → Span → Linearity (5 points, R² > 0.998) → Leak check → Test → Post-test drift check (± 2% per **40 CFR 1065**).
- ✅ **BS-VI petrol limits (g/km):** CO ≤ 1.0; THC ≤ 0.1; NMOG ≤ 0.068; NOx ≤ 0.06; PM ≤ 0.0045.
- ✅ **Wavelengths to remember:** CO = **4.67 µm**, CO₂ = **4.26 µm**, NO = **5.3 µm**, SO₂ = **7.4 µm**, CH (HC) = **3.4 µm**.
- ✅ **CL quantum yield** of NO + O₃ is low (~ 10⁻⁴) — this is why PMT sensitivity must be very high.
- ✅ **PEMS** (Portable Emissions Measurement System) integrates NDIR + CLA + FID for Real-Driving Emissions (RDE).
- ✅ **Key agencies:** ARAI (India), CPCB (India), US EPA, EU JRC, ISO 12039, ISO 16183, IS 14684.
- ✅ **Three phases of FTP-75:** Cold-start (505 s), Stabilized (867 s), Hot-start (505 s); total = 1877 s; total distance ≈ 17.77 km.
- ✅ **Herriott cell** = multi-pass optical cell (L_eff = 5–20 m) used in high-concentration NDIR to keep absorbance < 1.
- ✅ **Nafion dryer** removes water vapour from sample before NDIR cell to avoid cross-sensitivity.
- ✅ **Ozone generator** in CLA requires dry air (dew point < –40 °C) to prevent quenching by H₂O.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emission Control Technologies - Gasoline Fuelled Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Emission Control Technologies - Gasoline Fuelled Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Gasoline Fuelled Vehicle Emission Control Technologies** refer to the comprehensive suite of **in-cylinder (pre-combustion, combustion, and post-combustion inside the cylinder)** and **after-treatment (exhaust manifold downstream)** engineering systems employed in spark-ignition (SI) engines to attenuate the three regulated pollutant families — **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**, **Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)**, and **Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx)** — alongside non-exhaust species like **Crankcase Blow-by Gases** and **Evaporative Fuel Vapours (EVAP)** — to satisfy statutory emission norms such as **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** and **Euro 6** regulations.

The regulatory framework operates on the principle of the **"Three-Way Catalysis"** paradigm: a single catalytic converter simultaneously oxidises CO and HC while reducing NOx when the engine operates at the stoichiometric air–fuel ratio (λ = 1.00).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Surgical Mask" Analogy for a Gasoline Engine**
> Think of a gasoline engine as a hospital patient who is breathing polluted air and exhaling poisonous gases. Emission control is the multi-layer treatment:
> 1. **Fuel reformulation (better diet)** → cleaner fuel burns cleaner (analogous to giving the patient less junk food).
> 2. **Combustion chamber redesign (lung surgery)** → redesigning piston crown, swirl, and squish so combustion is more complete (analogous to operating on a damaged lung to improve oxygen exchange).
> 3. **EGR (recycling exhaled air)** → a small fraction of exhaust is sent back to dilute peak combustion temperature, suppressing the formation of NOx (analogous to mixing recycled air to prevent the engine from "overheating" chemically).
> 4. **Catalytic converter (surgical mask + chemical filter)** → exhaust gases pass through a honeycomb coated with platinum, palladium, and rhodium, which convert toxic CO, HC, and NOx into harmless CO₂, H₂O, and N₂ (analogous to the mask filtering out viruses).
> 5. **PCV system (venting the crankcase)** → noxious crankcase vapours are routed back into the intake (analogous to draining an abscess into a harmless drain).
> 6. **EVAP canister (charcoal air-freshener for the fuel tank)** → captures fuel vapours so they don't escape to the atmosphere (analogous to a sealed medicine container that prevents fume leakage).

The emission control architecture of a modern gasoline vehicle is therefore **engineered redundancy at multiple stages** — any single system alone cannot meet BS-VI norms; they must work in concert.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFRₛ) for gasoline:** **14.7 : 1** (mass basis)
- **Equivalent Ratio (λ):** $\lambda = \dfrac{\text{Actual AFR}}{\text{Stoichiometric AFR}}$
- **Lambda window for Three-Way Catalyst (TWC):** **0.99 ≤ λ ≤ 1.01** (narrow operating window)
- **Light-off temperature of TWC:** **≈ 250 °C to 300 °C** (catalyst must reach this before full conversion)
- **Conversion efficiency targets (BS-VI):** **HC ≥ 90 %**, **CO ≥ 90 %**, **NOx ≥ 90 %** after warm-up
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- **BS-VI NOx limit for gasoline cars:** **60 mg/km** (down from 80 mg/km under BS-IV)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly requires understanding of **(i) Catalytic Converters — Three-Way and Oxidation type, (ii) EGR, (iii) PCV, (iv) EVAP system, and (v) Stratified charge engines**. These five sub-topics carry the highest weightage in Part B (14-mark) questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Emission Control Strategies

Emission control strategies for gasoline engines are broadly classified into **three temporal zones** along the gas-flow path:

### Zone I — Pre-Combustion / Fuel-Based Controls
Operate *before* fuel enters the cylinder.

- **Fuel Reformulation:** Removal of lead, sulphur, and aromatics; addition of oxygenates (MTBE, ethanol).
- **Closed-Loop Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI):** Maintains λ within the narrow TWC window.
- **Vapour Recovery (EVAP system):** Adsorbs fuel vapours in activated charcoal.

### Zone II — In-Cylinder / Combustion Modification Controls
Operate *during* the combustion process itself.

- **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR):** Recycles 5 %–15 % exhaust to intake to suppress NOx.
- **Stratified-Charge Combustion:** Creates a fuel-rich pocket near the spark plug for lean-burn operation.
- **Combustion Chamber Optimisation:** Compact, fast-burn, high-swirl, squish designs.
- **Retarded / Optimised Ignition Timing:** Reduces peak flame temperature.
- **Variable Valve Timing (VVT):** Internal EGR via late intake-valve closing.

### Zone III — Post-Combustion / After-Treatment Controls
Operate *after* exhaust leaves the cylinder.

- **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC):** The workhorse — oxidises CO and HC, reduces NOx.
- **Oxidation Catalytic Converter:** Older technology, oxidises only CO and HC.
- **Secondary Air Injection:** Pumps air into exhaust to oxidise CO and HC during cold start.
- **NOx Trap / Lean NOx Trap (LNT):** For lean-burn gasoline engines.
- **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR):** For diesel; rarely used in gasoline.
- **Gasoline Particulate Filter (GPF):** For direct-injection gasoline engines (BS-VI).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Hierarchy Matters:** Modern BS-VI compliance is achieved by combining all three zones. A poorly designed Zone II cannot be rescued by a downstream TWC. A TWC operating outside its light-off temperature (e.g., during cold start) cannot reduce emissions. Hence, **system integration** is the exam's recurring theme.

## 2.2 The Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) — Detailed Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Working Principle

A TWC simultaneously performs **two oxidation reactions** and **one reduction reaction** at the catalytic sites:

$$\text{Oxidation:}\quad 2\,\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2$$

$$\text{Oxidation:}\quad \text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \dfrac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{Reduction:}\quad 2\,\text{NO}_x \rightarrow x\,\text{O}_2 + \text{N}_2$$

The three reactions are mutually compatible **only at λ = 1.00 ± 0.01**. This is the famous **"lambda window"** of the TWC.

### 2.2.2 Catalytic Chemistry and Substrate Geometry

- **Washcoat:** A thin, high-surface-area layer of **γ-alumina (Al₂O₃)** impregnated with **Ceria (CeO₂)** as an oxygen-storage component. Ceria acts as an "oxygen buffer" — it stores oxygen during lean excursions and releases it during rich excursions, smoothing the lambda oscillations.
- **Active Noble Metals:**
  - **Platinum (Pt)** and **Palladium (Pd)** — promote oxidation of CO and HC.
  - **Rhodium (Rh)** — promotes reduction of NOx.
- **Substrate (Monolith):** A **ceramic honeycomb (cordierite)** or **metallic foil** with parallel channels. Modern TWCs have cell densities of **400 to 900 cells per square inch (cpsi)**. Each cell wall is washcoated, giving a geometric surface area of roughly **2400 m² per litre of catalyst volume**.

### 2.2.3 Light-Off Performance and Conversion Efficiency

The catalyst's conversion efficiency $\eta$ is a strong function of the **catalyst bed temperature T**:

$$\eta(T) = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100\,\%$$

where $C_{\text{in}}$ and $C_{\text{out}}$ are pollutant concentrations at the inlet and outlet of the catalyst bed.

A typical light-off curve shows:
- **T < 200 °C:** $\eta < 20\,\%$ (no reaction kinetics).
- **T = 250 °C – 300 °C:** rapid rise (light-off).
- **T = 400 °C – 600 °C:** $\eta > 90\,\%$ (operating window).
- **T > 800 °C:** thermal sintering deactivates the catalyst.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** For BS-VI, the first **20–30 seconds** after a cold start account for **60–70 % of total HC and CO emissions** because the TWC is below its light-off temperature. This is why **Secondary Air Injection** and **Close-Coupled Catalysts (CC)** are critical.

## 2.3 Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)

EGR routes a controlled fraction of exhaust gas back into the intake manifold. Because exhaust is inert (mostly N₂ + CO₂), it acts as a **thermal diluent**.

**Key NOx-suppression mechanism:** The well-known Zeldovich mechanism shows that thermal NOx formation rate is exponentially sensitive to peak flame temperature:

$$\dfrac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R\,T_{\text{peak}}}$$

A small reduction in $T_{\text{peak}}$ (e.g., 100 K) leads to a **3× to 4× reduction** in NOx. EGR reduces $T_{\text{peak}}$ by diluting the charge, lowering the specific heat release.

## 2.4 Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV) System

In a 4-stroke gasoline engine, a small amount of combustion gases **blows past the piston rings** into the crankcase. These blow-by gases contain **HC, CO, and unburnt fuel** — and must not be vented to the atmosphere.

The PCV valve (a spring-loaded, vacuum-operated metering valve) routes the blow-by gas back into the **intake manifold** for re-burning.

## 2.5 Evaporative Emission Control (EVAP) System

Modern vehicles use an **activated carbon canister** plumbed to the fuel tank. Fuel vapours from the tank (generated by diurnal heating and refuelling) are adsorbed onto the carbon. During engine operation, fresh air drawn through the canister desorbs the trapped hydrocarbons and routes them to the intake manifold for combustion.

## 2.6 Stratified-Charge Engine Concept

A stratified-charge engine creates a **heterogeneous air–fuel mixture**: a small, fuel-rich pocket near the spark plug (λ_local ≈ 1.0 for reliable ignition), surrounded by a predominantly lean overall charge (λ_global ≈ 1.5). This allows **lean-burn operation**, which reduces fuel consumption and lowers NOx (due to lower peak temperature). Stratified charge was the architectural foundation of **Honda's HCCI and Toyota's Lean-Burn** engines.

| 2 | Equivalence ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_s}$ | λ = 1 ± 0.01 (TWC window) | Closed-loop lambda control |
| 3 | TWC light-off temperature | $T_{\text{lo}}$ | **250 °C – 300 °C** | Cold-start emission design |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compliance Engineering:** TWC technology alone cut tailpipe emissions by **≈ 95 %** compared to a 1970 carburetted engine — a remarkable success of automotive catalysis.
- **Material Cost:** Rhodium is among the most expensive metals on Earth (≈ ₹4,50,000/gram in 2024). Therefore, modern TWC research focuses on **Rhodium reduction** and substitution with Pd-only or Pd-Au formulations.
- **OBD-II Integration:** All BS-VI vehicles carry oxygen sensors (upstream and downstream of the TWC) feeding the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** for closed-loop control and **OBD-II diagnostics**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Efficiency of a TWC

### 3.1.1 Statement
For a catalytic monolith of channel length $L$, with a first-order heterogeneous reaction at the washcoat, derive the relationship between conversion efficiency and the **Damköhler number** and **mass-transfer coefficient**.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the governing equation.**
For a single channel of the monolith, consider a differential slice $dz$ along the channel axis. Mass balance on pollutant species $C$:

$$\dfrac{d(C\,u)}{dz} = -k_s\,a\,C$$

where:
- $C$ = pollutant concentration (mol/m³)
- $u$ = exhaust gas velocity (m/s)
- $k_s$ = surface reaction rate constant (m/s)
- $a$ = geometric surface area per unit volume (m²/m³)
- $z$ = axial distance along channel (m)

**Step 2 — Steady-state assumption.**
At steady state, $u = \text{constant}$ (incompressible approximation for low-Mach exhaust flow). Therefore:

$$u\,\dfrac{dC}{dz} = -k_s\,a\,C$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate.**

$$\int_{C_{\text{in}}}^{C_{\text{out}}} \dfrac{dC}{C} = -\dfrac{k_s\,a}{u}\int_{0}^{L} dz$$

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}}\right) = -\dfrac{k_s\,a\,L}{u}$$

**Step 4 — Express the exponent as a Damköhler-type number.**
Define the dimensionless number $\text{Da} = \dfrac{k_s\,a\,L}{u}$ (also called the **first-order reaction modulus**). Then:

$$\dfrac{C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} = e^{-\text{Da}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to conversion efficiency.**

$$\eta = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} = 1 - e^{-\text{Da}}$$

**Step 6 — Limiting cases.**
- If $\text{Da} \to 0$ (slow reaction or short channel): $\eta \to 0$ — no conversion.
- If $\text{Da} \to \infty$ (very fast reaction): $\eta \to 1$ — complete conversion.
- For $\eta = 0.90$: $e^{-\text{Da}} = 0.10 \Rightarrow \text{Da} = \ln 10 \approx 2.30$.

**Step 7 — Final boxed result.**

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{TWC}} = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{k_s\,a\,L}{u}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This equation justifies the design trend toward **higher cpsi** (larger $a$), **longer substrates** (larger $L$), and **higher reaction rates** (achieved by finer washcoat dispersion of noble metals). All three parameters appear in the exponent, so small percentage improvements yield large efficiency gains.

## 3.2 Derivation: NOx Reduction with EGR

### 3.2.1 Statement
Using the Zeldovich thermal-NOx rate equation, estimate the fractional reduction in NOx formation when EGR dilutes the intake charge such that peak flame temperature drops from $T_1$ to $T_2 = T_1 - \Delta T$.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Zeldovich mechanism (extended).**
The net thermal-NOx rate is:

$$r_{\text{NOx}} = A\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R\,T_{\text{peak}}}\right) \cdot [\text{O}_2]^{0.5} \cdot [\text{N}_2]$$

**Step 2 — Ratio of NOx rates at two temperatures.**

$$\dfrac{r_2}{r_1} = \dfrac{[\text{O}_2]_2^{0.5}\,[\text{N}_2]_2}{[\text{O}_2]_1^{0.5}\,[\text{N}_2]_1} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Approximation for small dilution.**
If EGR is moderate (≤ 15 %), the concentration change in O₂ and N₂ is small. Then:

$$\dfrac{[\text{O}_2]_2}{[\text{O}_2]_1} \approx 1, \quad \dfrac{[\text{N}_2]_2}{[\text{N}_2]_1} \approx 1$$

**Step 4 — Temperature-only reduction factor.**

$$\dfrac{r_2}{r_1} \approx \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1 - \Delta T} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example.**
Take $T_1 = 2200\ \text{K}$, $\Delta T = 100\ \text{K}$, $E_a = 319{,}000\ \text{J/mol}$, $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$.

$$\dfrac{1}{2100} - \dfrac{1}{2200} = 4.762 \times 10^{-4} - 4.545 \times 10^{-4} = 2.166 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\dfrac{E_a}{R} = \dfrac{319{,}000}{8.314} = 38{,}369\ \text{K}$$

$$\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right) = 38{,}369 \times 2.166 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.831$$

$$\dfrac{r_2}{r_1} = e^{-0.831} \approx 0.436$$

**Step 6 — Final fractional reduction.**

$$\text{NOx reduction} = \left(1 - \dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right) \times 100\,\% \approx 56.4\,\%$$

**Step 7 — Final boxed result.**

$$\boxed{\text{NOx}_{\text{reduction}} \approx \left(1 - \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]\right) \times 100\,\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A modest 100 K drop in peak flame temperature (achievable with ≈ 10 % EGR) cuts NOx formation in half. This is the central reason EGR is a foundational NOx-control technology.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Major After-Treatment Devices

| Device | Target Pollutants | Operating Mode | λ Window | Light-off Temp | Conversion (%) | Used In |
|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|
| **TWC (Three-Way)** | CO, HC, NOx | Stoichiometric only | λ = 1 ± 0.01 | 250–300 °C | 90+ | All modern gasoline |
| **DOC (Diesel Oxidation)** | CO, HC (NOx no) | Lean or stoichiometric | λ > 1 | 200–250 °C | 80+ | Diesel, lean GDI |
| **Lean NOx Trap (LNT)** | NOx (stores) | Lean / rich cycling | λ > 1 storage | 200–300 °C | 70–90 | Lean-burn GDI |
| **SCR (Selective Cat. Red.)** | NOx (with urea) | Lean | λ > 1 | 200–300 °C | 90+ | Diesel (rarely gasoline) |
| **GPF (Gasoline Particulate Filter)** | PM / PN | All | Any | 300 °C | 90+ | Direct-injection gasoline |

## 3.4 Worked Example: TWC Conversion for a BS-VI Engine

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU style — 7 marks):** A BS-VI gasoline engine has a pre-TWC CO concentration of $C_{\text{in}} = 4500\ \text{ppm}$. After passing through the TWC at steady-state operating temperature of 480 °C, the post-TWC CO is 270 ppm. Calculate the conversion efficiency and verify it meets the BS-VI 90 % requirement.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Substitute into the conversion efficiency formula.**

$$\eta = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100\,\%$$

**Step 2 — Numerator evaluation.**

$$C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}} = 4500 - 270 = 4230\ \text{ppm}$$

**Step 3 — Division.**

$$\eta = \dfrac{4230}{4500} \times 100\,\% = 94\,\%$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with BS-VI limit.**

$$94\,\% \geq 90\,\% \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** The TWC meets the BS-VI conversion requirement at the steady-state operating temperature. However, the 6 % unconverted CO (270 ppm) must be further reduced by the **Close-Coupled Catalyst (CCC)** during cold start to ensure test-cycle compliance.
