# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"Define the term 'Three-Way Catalytic Converter' and list the pollutants it controls."**

### Model Answer (3 marks):
A Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) is an **after-treatment device** installed in the exhaust system of a gasoline engine that simultaneously oxidises **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** and **Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)** to CO₂ and H₂O, while reducing **Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx)** to N₂. The term "three-way" refers to these three concurrent reactions. The TWC operates efficiently only at the **stoichiometric air–fuel ratio (λ = 1.00 ± 0.01)**, and uses **platinum, palladium, and rhodium** as active noble metals deposited on a high-surface-area washcoat of γ-alumina, supported on a **cordierite ceramic honeycomb** monolith. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"What is the function of an EGR valve in a gasoline engine?"**

### Model Answer (3 marks):
The Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) valve routes a controlled fraction (typically **5 % – 15 %**) of inert exhaust gas from the exhaust manifold back into the intake manifold. Its primary function is to **suppress the formation of thermal NOx** by:
1. **Diluting the intake charge** with inert CO₂ and N₂, reducing the partial pressure of O₂.
2. **Lowering the peak flame temperature** during combustion.
3. **Increasing the specific heat capacity** of the charge.

A reduction of just **100 K in peak flame temperature** can cut NOx formation by **more than 50 %**, per the Zeldovich kinetics. The EGR valve is electronically controlled by the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** using a **stepper motor or solenoid**, often with a **coolant-cooled EGR cooler** to maximise thermal-dilution efficiency. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

### **Question A [KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** used in gasoline vehicles. Discuss the role of ceria and noble metals. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)** and **Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV)** systems used for emission control in spark-ignition engines. (7 marks)

### Model Answer

#### Part (a) — TWC Construction and Working (7 marks)

A Three-Way Catalytic Converter is the primary after-treatment device fitted between the **exhaust manifold** and the **tailpipe** of every modern gasoline vehicle to comply with **BS-VI / Euro 6** emission norms.

**1. Construction:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stainless Steel Shell] --> B[Intlet Cone]
    B --> C[Cordierite Monolith]
    C --> D[Gamma Alumina Washcoat]
    D --> E[Pt, Pd, Rh Active Sites]
    E --> F[Outlet Cone]
    style C fill:#fda,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#adf,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#f9d,stroke:#333
```

- **Outer Shell (1 mark):** A stainless-steel canister designed to withstand exhaust temperature up to 800 °C and road vibration.
- **Substrate (1 mark):** A **cordierite (2MgO·2Al₂O₃·5SiO₂) ceramic honeycomb** or **metallic (Fe-Cr-Al) foil monolith** with 400–900 cells per square inch (cpsi). The parallel channels minimise exhaust back-pressure.
- **Washcoat (1 mark):** A thin, porous layer of **γ-alumina (γ-Al₂O₃)** impregnated with **Ceria (CeO₂)** as an oxygen-storage material. The washcoat provides a high specific surface area (≈ 200 m²/g).
- **Active Sites (1 mark):**
  - **Platinum (Pt)** — promotes CO oxidation.
  - **Palladium (Pd)** — promotes HC oxidation.
  - **Rhodium (Rh)** — promotes NOx reduction.
  - Total PGM loading: 1–3 g per litre of washcoat.

**2. Working Principle (1 mark):**
At steady-state operating temperature (**400–600 °C**), three simultaneous reactions occur on the catalytic sites:

$$2\,\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2$$

$$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \dfrac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$2\,\text{NO}_x \rightarrow x\,\text{O}_2 + \text{N}_2$$

**3. Role of Ceria (1 mark):**
Ceria acts as an **oxygen-storage buffer**. During brief lean excursions (λ > 1), ceria stores oxygen as CeO₂ → Ce₂O₃. During brief rich excursions (λ < 1), ceria releases oxygen, supporting oxidation. This **buffers lambda oscillations** and widens the effective operating window of the TWC.

**4. Role of Noble Metals (1 mark):**
- **Pt / Pd:** Lower the activation energy of the oxidation reactions, allowing them to proceed at exhaust temperatures rather than requiring flame temperatures.
- **Rh:** Lowers the activation energy of the NOx → N₂ reduction pathway — Rh is the most expensive of the three and is the rate-limiting component.

**5. Light-Off and Efficiency (1 mark):**
The catalyst requires ≈ 250–300 °C to "light off" (achieve 50 % conversion). The **Close-Coupled Catalyst (CCC)** mounted close to the engine reaches this temperature within ≈ 30 seconds of cold start, ensuring BS-VI compliance in the legislative test cycle.

#### Part (b) — EGR and PCV (7 marks)

**A. Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR):**

**1. Concept (1 mark):**
EGR is an **in-cylinder emission control technique** that recycles a fraction of exhaust gas back into the intake manifold. It is primarily used to control **NOx** emissions in both gasoline and diesel engines.

**2. Working Principle (1 mark):**
The exhaust gas, predominantly composed of inert N₂ and CO₂, is mixed with fresh intake charge. This:
- **Dilutes the O₂ concentration** in the cylinder.
- **Increases the specific heat capacity** of the charge.
- **Reduces peak flame temperature** by 50–150 K.

Per Zeldovich kinetics, NOx formation is exponentially sensitive to flame temperature, so a modest temperature reduction produces a dramatic NOx reduction.

**3. Components (1 mark):**
- EGR valve (vacuum-operated or electronically controlled).
- EGR cooler (uses engine coolant to lower the temperature of recirculated gas).
- EGR pipe (plumbed from exhaust manifold to intake).
- ECU control via pulse-width-modulated (PWM) signal.

**4. EGR Rate (1 mark):**

$$\%\ \text{EGR} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{recirculated}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{intake}}} \times 100$$

Typical values: 5 % – 15 % at part load; reduced or zero at idle and WOT (wide-open throttle).

**5. Types of EGR (1 mark):**
- **Internal EGR:** Achieved through **Variable Valve Timing (VVT)** by retaining a portion of exhaust in the cylinder via early exhaust-valve closing or late intake-valve closing.
- **External EGR:** Uses a dedicated external pipe and valve — the most common implementation.

**B. Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV):**

**1. Concept (1 mark):**
During the compression and power strokes, a small fraction of high-pressure combustion gases **blows past the piston rings** into the crankcase. This "blow-by" gas contains **HC, CO, and partially oxidised fuel** and must not be vented to the atmosphere (it is a source of HC pollution).

**2. Working Principle (1 mark):**
The PCV system uses a **spring-loaded, vacuum-operated metering valve** plumbed between the crankcase and the intake manifold. The intake manifold vacuum draws the blow-by gases out of the crankcase and into the intake stream, where they are re-burned in the combustion chamber.

**3. Operation Modes:**
- **Closed throttle (high vacuum):** PCV valve opens; high flow of blow-by recirculation.
- **Wide-open throttle (low vacuum):** PCV valve closes to prevent overly rich mixture.
- **Backfire:** Valve opens fully to vent crankcase pressure rapidly.

(1 mark reserved for schematic or operation mode detail.)

**4. Fresh Air Inlet (0.5 mark):**
A filtered fresh-air inlet admits clean air into the crankcase to replace the drawn-out blow-by, ensuring the crankcase is continually scavenged and pressurised positively (so oil leaks are outward, not inward).

**5. Environmental Benefit (0.5 mark):**
The PCV system eliminates **HC blow-by emissions** (typically 0.5 % – 1 % of total HC emissions in a non-PCV engine), contributing a measurable fraction of the total emission reduction in BS-VI compliance.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

### **Question B [KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Describe the **Evaporative Emission Control (EVAP)** system in modern gasoline vehicles. With a schematic, explain the role of the **activated carbon canister** and the **purge control valve**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the concept of **stratified-charge combustion** in gasoline engines. How does it improve fuel economy and reduce emissions? Discuss its limitations. (7 marks)

### Model Answer

#### Part (a) — EVAP System (7 marks)

**1. Definition and Purpose (1 mark):**
The Evaporative Emission Control (EVAP) system captures **fuel vapours** generated in the fuel tank by **diurnal heating**, **refuelling displacement**, and **running losses**, preventing their release to the atmosphere. These vapours contain volatile organic compounds (VOCs) — primarily unburnt hydrocarbons — which are a regulated pollutant under **Bharat Stage VI** norms.

**2. Schematic:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Tank] -->|Vapour Line| B[Activated Carbon Canister]
    C[Atmosphere] -->|Fresh Air| B
    B -->|Purge Line| D[Purge Solenoid Valve]
    D -->|Vacuum| E[Intake Manifold]
    F[ECU] -->|PWM Signal| D
    style B fill:#fcf,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#ff9,stroke:#333
```

**3. Activated Carbon Canister (2 marks):**
- **Construction:** A plastic or metal canister filled with **≈ 100–500 g of granular activated carbon** (high specific surface area, typically 800–1500 m²/g). The carbon has a microporous structure that adsorbs HC molecules by van der Waals forces.
- **Function:** During engine-off periods, fuel-tank vapours are routed into the canister and adsorbed onto the carbon surface. The canister is sized to hold the maximum diurnal-breathing load (typically **2–5 g HC per 100 g of carbon**).
- **Regeneration:** During engine operation, the **purge valve** opens, and intake-manifold vacuum draws fresh air through the canister, **desorbing the trapped HC** and routing it to the engine for combustion.

**4. Purge Control Valve (1 mark):**
- A normally-closed, **ECU-controlled solenoid valve** placed in the purge line between canister and intake.
- The ECU commands the valve open via **Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM)** with a duty cycle proportional to engine operating conditions.
- **Constraints:** Purge is disabled during **cold start** (until the engine reaches a calibrated coolant temperature) and during **wide-open throttle** (to avoid leaning the mixture).

**5. Diagnostic and Regulatory Function (1 mark):**
- **OBD-II Check:** The EVAP system includes a **leak detection pump** (in BS-VI vehicles) that pressurises the system to detect leaks as small as **0.5 mm**. A leak of 0.5 mm would be flagged as **DTC P0442** (small EVAP leak).
- **Zero Evaporative Emission Goal:** Modern EVAP systems aim for ≤ 0.5 g/test of HC leakage, verified using the **SHED (Sealed Housing for Evaporative Determination)** test.

**6. Components Summary (1 mark):**
- Fuel-tank vapour line
- Activated carbon canister
- Purge solenoid valve
- Fresh-air inlet
- Leak-detection pump (BS-VI)
- ECU and wiring harness

#### Part (b) — Stratified-Charge Combustion (7 marks)

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
Stratified-charge combustion is an in-cylinder strategy in which the **air–fuel ratio is intentionally non-uniform** in the combustion chamber at the time of ignition: a **fuel-rich, readily ignitable pocket** is created locally around the spark plug (with local λ ≈ 1.0), while the **bulk of the chamber** contains a **very lean mixture** (with overall λ ≈ 1.4–1.6).

**2. Concept and Geometry (1 mark):**
Stratified charge is most commonly implemented in **Direct-Injection Spark-Ignition (DISI)** engines. Fuel is injected directly into the cylinder late in the compression stroke, allowing time-controlled mixture preparation. The piston crown and chamber geometry are designed to direct fuel spray toward the spark plug.

**3. Working Principle (1 mark):**
- **End of Compression Stroke:** Piston reaches TDC; a fuel-rich cloud (λ_local ≈ 1.0) is concentrated near the spark plug; the surrounding gas is mostly air (λ_local > 2).
- **Spark Discharge:** Ignites the rich pocket reliably.
- **Flame Propagation:** The flame front propagates outward into the lean surroundings, consuming the entire air–fuel charge.

**4. Advantages (2 marks):**

**A. Improved Fuel Economy:**
- Operation at overall λ > 1.0 means **excess air** is present, allowing **throttle-less load control** (orifice effect).
- Pumping losses are dramatically reduced because the throttle is partially or fully closed; the engine acts as an "air pump" with minimal restriction.
- Result: **10 % – 20 % improvement in part-load fuel consumption** compared to a stoichiometric SI engine.

**B. Reduced NOx:**
- Lean combustion lowers **peak flame temperature** (less fuel per unit of local oxygen).
- Lower temperature → exponentially lower thermal-NOx formation (per Zeldovich kinetics).

**C. Reduced HC and CO at moderate load:**
- Combustion is more complete due to the high local air availability.

**5. Limitations (2 marks):**

**A. High Unburnt HC at Stratification Boundary:**
- At the **stratification interface** between rich and lean regions, pockets of mixture can escape flame propagation.
- HC emissions may increase at light load if stratification is not properly controlled.

**B. Particulate Matter (PM) Increase:**
- Direct-injection stratified-charge engines produce **liquid fuel wall films** that may not fully evaporate, leading to increased **particulate matter** — a key reason **GPF (Gasoline Particulate Filter)** is mandated in BS-VI GDI vehicles.

**C. Combustion Instability:**
- Cycle-to-cycle variability (COV of IMEP) is higher in stratified mode, causing rough idle and noise.

**D. NOx-Trade-Off:**
- Although lean burn reduces thermal NOx, the **OBD-II and three-way catalyst** systems are designed for λ = 1.0. To meet BS-VI NOx limits under lean operation, a **Lean NOx Trap (LNT)** or **NOx Storage-Reduction (NSR)** catalyst is required downstream — adding cost and complexity.

**6. Real-World Examples (0.5 mark):**
- **Toyota Lean-Burn Engine** (Japan, 2ZZ-GE)
- **Honda i-VTEC Lean-Burn** (Insight, Civic Hybrid)
- **Volkswagen TSI** (stratified mode during light load)

**7. Modern Relevance (0.5 mark):**
- Stratified charge is a **transitional technology** between conventional SI and **HCCI (Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition)**. Modern HCCI operates at very lean, very dilute conditions with controlled auto-ignition, eliminating the spark plug and approaching diesel-like efficiency at gasoline-like emissions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing TWC with DOC:** Many students write that DOCs reduce NOx — they do not. DOCs oxidise only CO and HC. Only TWCs (and LNTs/SCRs) address NOx. **(Loss: 1–2 marks in 7-mark questions)**
> 2. **Missing Ceria's role:** Examiners specifically test the **oxygen-storage function of ceria** in TWC questions. Omitting it loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong lambda range:** Do not write that TWC works at λ > 1 (lean). It works ONLY at λ = 1 ± 0.01. Lean operation requires LNT or SCR.
> 4. **EGR misunderstanding:** EGR does NOT dilute CO or HC. It dilutes the **combustion temperature** to suppress **NOx**. Writing "EGR reduces HC emissions" is incorrect.
> 5. **PCV placement:** The PCV valve is plumbed between the **crankcase and the intake manifold**, not the atmosphere. Drawing it to the atmosphere loses 1 mark.
> 6. **EVAP canister role:** The canister does NOT burn the fuel vapours. It **adsorbs** them; the **engine** burns them via the purge line.
> 7. **Stratified charge confusion:** Stratified charge is **not the same as HCCI**. Stratified = localised rich + lean with spark ignition. HCCI = homogeneous lean with auto-ignition.
> 8. **Units in EGR rate calculation:** Always express EGR as a **percentage by mass**, not by volume, for consistency with intake mass flow.
> 9. **Catalyst light-off vs. BS-VI compliance:** Examiners expect students to know that **light-off** is the temperature threshold and that **cold-start emissions** are a major challenge — often addressed by **close-coupled catalysts (CCC)**.
> 10. **PGM abbreviations:** Write out **Platinum (Pt)**, **Palladium (Pd)**, **Rhodium (Rh)** at least once. Writing only "noble metals" loses 0.5 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Gasoline Emission Control**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] **Stoichiometric AFR (14.7:1)** and **equivalence ratio (λ = 1.00 ± 0.01)** define the TWC operating window.
- [ ] **Three regulated pollutants:** CO, HC, NOx.
- [ ] **TWC** oxidises CO and HC, reduces NOx simultaneously; uses Pt, Pd, Rh.
- [ ] **Ceria (CeO₂)** is an **oxygen-storage buffer** that smooths lambda oscillations.
- [ ] **Washcoat** is γ-alumina with high surface area; **monolith** is cordierite (400–900 cpsi).
- [ ] **Light-off temperature** ≈ 250–300 °C; **operating window** 400–600 °C; **deactivation** > 800 °C.
- [ ] **EGR** dilutes the intake charge to lower peak flame temperature; 5 %–15 % typical; reduces NOx > 50 % per 100 K drop.
- [ ] **PCV** recirculates crankcase blow-by gas to intake via a spring-loaded, vacuum-operated valve.
- [ ] **EVAP** uses an **activated carbon canister** to adsorb fuel vapours; **purge valve** is ECU-controlled via PWM.
- [ ] **Stratified charge** = rich pocket near spark plug + lean surroundings; improves economy but raises PM (requiring GPF).
- [ ] **Secondary Air Injection** injects air into exhaust during cold start to oxidise CO and HC before TWC lights off.
- [ ] **Close-Coupled Catalyst (CCC)** is mounted near the exhaust manifold to reach light-off faster.
- [ ] **GPF (Gasoline Particulate Filter)** is mandatory for **BS-VI GDI** vehicles to trap PM/PN.
- [ ] **OBD-II** monitors TWC efficiency via upstream and downstream O₂ sensors (lambda probes).
- [ ] **BS-VI gasoline limits:** CO 1000 mg/km, HC 100 mg/km, NOx 60 mg/km.

### High-Yield Numbers
- [ ] **A/F = 14.7:1** (stoichiometric gasoline)
- [ ] **λ = 1.00 ± 0.01** (TWC window)
- [ ] **EGR = 5–15 %** at part load
- [ ] **Light-off = 250–300 °C**
- [ ] **E_a (Zeldovich) ≈ 319 kJ/mol**
- [ ] **cpsi = 400–900**
- [ ] **PGM loading = 1–3 g/L**

### Frequently Asked 14-Mark Question Templates
- [ ] **TWC** — always with construction + working + role of ceria + role of PGM.
- [ ] **EGR + PCV** — combined 7+7 or 7+7 split.
- [ ] **EVAP system** — schematic + canister + purge valve + diagnostic.
- [ ] **Stratified charge** — concept + advantages + limitations.
- [ ] **Secondary Air Injection** — cold-start strategy with chemistry.
- [ ] **Comparison of TWC vs DOC vs LNT** — analytical table-based question.

### Examiner's Favourite "One-Liners"
- *"The TWC can only function within a narrow lambda window; closed-loop lambda control is therefore mandatory."*
- *"Ceria is the oxygen buffer that makes the TWC work in real-world driving cycles."*
- *"EGR is a thermal-dilution strategy, not a chemical-dilution strategy."*
- *"The PCV system is the simplest, oldest, and most cost-effective emission control device still in use."*
- *"Stratified-charge engines are the bridge between SI and HCCI combustion."*
- *"BS-VI compliance is a system-level achievement — not the result of any single technology."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Fuel Ratio, Electronic Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Air Fuel Ratio, Electronic Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Air–Fuel Ratio (AFR) — Formal KTU Definition

The **Air–Fuel Ratio (AFR)** is defined as the ratio of the mass of air to the mass of fuel supplied to the engine cylinder for combustion.

$$AFR = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$$

The **Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio** is the chemically ideal (theoretically perfect) mass ratio of air to fuel that provides *exactly* enough oxygen to completely combust all of the carbon and hydrogen in the fuel, producing only **CO₂, H₂O, and N₂** — with no leftover O₂ and no unburned hydrocarbons (HC) or carbon monoxide (CO).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Stoichiometric Values (KTU Board Reference):**
> - **Gasoline (Petrol) → 14.7 : 1**
> - **Diesel → 14.5 : 1**
> - **Methane (CNG) → 17.2 : 1**
> - **Ethanol → 9.0 : 1**
> - **Hydrogen → 34.0 : 1**

### 1.2 Equivalence Ratio (φ) and Lambda (λ)

To generalize the AFR across fuels, KTU examiners prefer the **normalized** parameters:

**Lambda (λ)** — Relative Air–Fuel Ratio:
$$\lambda = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoichiometric}}$$

**Equivalence Ratio (φ):**
$$\phi = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{AFR_{stoichiometric}}{AFR_{actual}}$$

| Condition | λ Value | Mixture State |
|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric (chemically perfect) | λ = 1 | Neither rich nor lean |
| Lean mixture (excess air) | λ > 1 | Excess O₂, low power, high NOₓ |
| Rich mixture (deficit air) | λ < 1 | Excess fuel, high HC & CO |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cooking Recipe" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine making a Maggi noodles packet for exactly 2 people:**
> - The recipe says use **2 cups of water** for **1 pack of noodles** (this is your *stoichiometric ratio*).
> - If you use **3 cups of water** → mixture is *lean* (λ > 1) — tastes watery, energy wasted, no crispiness (analogous to low thermal efficiency in engines).
> - If you use only **1 cup of water** → mixture is *rich* (λ < 1) — uncooked noodles, burnt bottom (analogous to HC, CO, soot — *unburnt pollutants*).
> - The Electronic Control Unit (ECU) in a car acts like an **automatic smart chef** that constantly tastes (via the **Lambda/O₂ sensor**) and adds water or noodles to keep the recipe *perfect* every second!

### 1.4 Electronic Control — Formal Definition

**Electronic Engine Control** refers to the real-time, closed-loop management of the air–fuel mixture, ignition timing, and emissions using an **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)**. The ECU receives inputs from multiple sensors (Lambda, MAP, MAF, TPS, crankshaft position) and actuates fuel injectors and the ignition coil through precisely timed electrical pulses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Modern BS-VI / Euro-6 vehicles rely entirely on electronic closed-loop control to maintain AFR within a narrow band of **λ = 1.00 ± 0.01** to optimize the **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** efficiency for simultaneous reduction of CO, HC, and NOₓ.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Air–Fuel Ratio Matters for Pollution Control

The AFR is the **single most dominant factor** controlling exhaust emissions from a spark-ignition engine. Each pollutant has a *characteristic minimum* at a specific λ window:

| Pollutant | Minimum Emission λ Window | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **CO** | λ > 1 (lean) | CO needs O₂ to oxidize to CO₂ |
| **HC (Unburnt)** | λ ≈ 1.05–1.10 (slightly lean) | Better flame propagation, less quench |
| **NOₓ** | λ ≈ 1.00–1.05 (near stoichiometric, peak T) | NOₓ ∝ peak flame temperature (Zeldovich mechanism) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Sweet Spot"** for simultaneous minimum of all three pollutants is **λ = 1.00 ± 0.01** — exactly the operating window of a **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)**. This is why the ECU must hold AFR within an extremely tight band.

### 2.2 Stoichiometric Combustion — Theoretical Background

For a generic hydrocarbon fuel **CₓHᵧ**, complete combustion in dry air (O₂ + 3.76 N₂) is:

$$C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) O_2 + 3.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) N_2 \rightarrow x\, CO_2 + \frac{y}{2} H_2O + 3.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) N_2$$

**Theoretical Oxygen Required (mol O₂ per mol fuel):**
$$n_{O_2,\,stoich} = x + \frac{y}{4}$$

**Theoretical Air Required (mol air per mol fuel):**
$$n_{air,\,stoich} = 4.76 \times \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)$$

where **4.76 = 1 + 3.76** represents the moles of air per mole of O₂.

### 2.3 Mass-Based AFR Derivation

Converting to **mass basis** (multiplying moles by molecular mass):
$$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{4.76 \times \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) \times 29}{12x + y}$$

The molecular mass of air ≈ **28.97 g/mol** (≈ 29), and of fuel depends on x and y.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Air–Fuel Ratio (mass) | $AFR = m_{air}/m_{fuel}$ | — | Always > 1 in SI engines |
| 2 | Stoichiometric O₂ (mol) | $n_{O_2} = x + y/4$ | mol O₂ / mol fuel | For fuel CₓHᵧ |
| 3 | Stoichiometric air (mol) | $n_{air} = 4.76(x + y/4)$ | mol air / mol fuel | Air = O₂ + 3.76 N₂ |
| 4 | Mass-based stoich AFR | $AFR_s = [4.76(x + y/4) \times 29] / (12x + y)$ | kg air / kg fuel | Universal formula |
| 5 | Lambda (λ) | $\lambda = AFR_{actual} / AFR_{stoich}$ | dimensionless | λ=1 stoich; >1 lean; <1 rich |
| 6 | Equivalence ratio (φ) | $\phi = 1/\lambda$ | dimensionless | Inverse of λ |
| 7 | % Excess Air | $\%EA = (\lambda - 1) \times 100$ | % | Used in combustion engineering |
| 8 | % Theoretical Air | $\%TA = (1/\lambda) \times 100$ | % | Alternative to φ |
| 9 | Lambda sensor voltage (NB) | $V \approx 0.1V$ (lean) to $0.9V$ (rich) | Volts | Switching at λ=1 |
| 10 | ECU sampling rate | 10–100 samples / second | Hz | Closed-loop control frequency |

### 2.5 Electronic Control Architecture — Why It Was Needed

Before electronic control, **carburetors** used mechanical Venturi-based metering. Problems included:
- Slow response to acceleration (airflow lag)
- Inability to compensate for altitude, temperature, barometric pressure
- Wide AFR excursions → high emissions, poor fuel economy

The **ECU-based Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI)** solves all of this by:
1. Measuring the *exact* air mass entering (MAF/MAP sensor)
2. Computing the precise fuel injector pulse width in real time
3. Verifying the result with a Lambda (O₂) sensor in the exhaust
4. **Self-correcting** any deviation via closed-loop feedback

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** The same ECU architecture now controls **Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI), Common-Rail Diesel, CNG sequential injection, Hybrid energy management, and even Hydrogen FCEV systems** — making AFR electronic control a foundational technology of modern zero-emission mobility.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric AFR of Iso-octane (C₈H₁₈) — the KTU Reference Fuel

**Given:** Iso-octane (C₈H₁₈), M_fuel = 8(12) + 18(1) = **114 kg/kmol**
Molecular mass of air M_air = **28.97 kg/kmol** ≈ **29 kg/kmol**
N₂/O₂ ratio in air = **3.76** by mole

**Step 1 — Write the balanced combustion equation:**

$$C_8H_{18} + a\,O_2 + 3.76a\,N_2 \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O + 3.76a\,N_2$$

**Step 2 — Balance oxygen atoms (C₈H₁₈ side has 0, right side has 2×8 + 9 = 25):**
$$2a = 16 + 9 = 25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = 12.5$$

**Step 3 — Moles of air per mole of fuel:**
$$n_{air} = a \times 4.76 = 12.5 \times 4.76 = 59.5 \text{ mol air / mol fuel}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of air per mol of fuel:**
$$m_{air} = 59.5 \times 29 = 1725.5 \text{ g air / mol fuel}$$

**Step 5 — Stoichiometric AFR (mass ratio):**
$$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{1725.5}{114} = 15.14$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Answer:** 15.1 (closely matches the standard textbook value 15.13). The slight deviation from the often-quoted 14.7:1 is because **real gasoline is not pure C₈H₁₈** — it contains aromatics, paraffins, and additives. The KTU examiner may quote either 14.7 or 15.1 depending on the fuel model assumed.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stoichiometric AFR of Methane (CH₄)

**Given:** CH₄, M_fuel = 12 + 4 = **16 kg/kmol**

**Step 1 — Balance equation:**
$$CH_4 + 2\,O_2 + 2(3.76)\,N_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + 7.52\,N_2$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric O₂ requirement:**
$$a = 2 \text{ mol O}_2 \text{ per mol CH}_4$$

**Step 3 — Mass of air:**
$$m_{air} = 2 \times 4.76 \times 29 = 276.08 \text{ g/mol fuel}$$

**Step 4 — Mass AFR:**
$$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{276.08}{16} = 17.26$$

This matches the KTU reference value of **17.2 : 1** for CNG/methane.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: % Excess Air for a Lean-Burn Natural Gas Engine

**Given:** A lean-burn CNG engine operates at AFR_actual = 22.0. Find % excess air.

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric AFR for CH₄:**
$$AFR_s = 17.2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate λ:**
$$\lambda = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}} = \frac{22.0}{17.2} = 1.279$$

**Step 3 — % Excess Air:**
$$\%EA = (\lambda - 1) \times 100 = (1.279 - 1) \times 100 = 27.9\%$$

**Step 4 — Equivalence ratio:**
$$\phi = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{1.279} = 0.782$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A lean-burn natural gas engine deliberately runs at φ = 0.78 (≈ 28% excess air) to **suppress NOₓ** by lowering peak flame temperature, accepting a slight fuel-economy penalty in exchange for thermal efficiency. This is the basis of all lean-burn gasoline / HCCI / GDI stratified-charge strategies.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Lambda from Oxygen Sensor Voltage (Nernst Equation)

A **Narrowband Lambda sensor** generates a voltage governed by the Nernst equation:
$$V = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{P_{O_2,\,rich}}{P_{O_2,\,lean}}\right)$$

where R = 8.314 J/mol·K, F = 96485 C/mol, n = 4 (electrons per O₂ molecule).

**Given:** T = 800 K, P_O2(rich side) = 10⁻¹⁵ atm, P_O2(lean side) = 10⁻² atm.

**Step 1 — Compute the Nernst term:**
$$\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{8.314 \times 800}{4 \times 96485} = \frac{6651.2}{385940} = 0.01723 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Logarithmic pressure ratio:**
$$\ln\left(\frac{10^{-15}}{10^{-2}}\right) = \ln(10^{-13}) = -13 \times 2.3026 = -29.93$$

**Step 3 — Sensor voltage:**
$$V = 0.01723 \times (-29.93) \approx -0.515 \text{ V}$$

This is a *theoretical* EMF; in practice, the ECU applies a reference bias of **0.45 V** so that the *output* signal is:
- **V < 0.45 V** → lean (λ > 1)
- **V > 0.45 V** → rich (λ < 1)
- **V ≈ 0.45 V** → stoichiometric (λ = 1)

The ECU then drives the fuel injector pulse width **higher or lower** by ~1–3% per cycle to maintain λ = 1.00.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — AFR vs Emissions & Performance Trade-off

| AFR (mass) | λ | Mixture | Power | Fuel Economy | CO | HC | NOₓ | TWC Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 11 : 1 | 0.75 | Very Rich | High | Poor | Very High | Very High | Low | Poor |
| 12 : 1 | 0.82 | Rich | Maximum | Moderate | High | High | Moderate | Poor |
| 14.7 : 1 | 1.00 | Stoichiometric | Moderate | Good | Low | Low | High | **Best** |
| 16 : 1 | 1.09 | Slightly Lean | Slightly Low | Better | Very Low | Very Low | Moderate | Reduced |
| 18 : 1 | 1.22 | Lean | Low | Best | Very Low | Very Low | Low | Fails |
| 20+ : 1 | 1.36+ | Very Lean | Misfire risk | Best | Trace | Rising (misfire) | Very Low | Fails |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Engineering Takeaway:** The **"Three-Way Catalyst"** only works near λ = 1.0. Hence, all petrol cars with TWC *must* operate in a narrow closed loop around stoichiometric. This single constraint drives the design of the entire modern electronic engine management system.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Closed-Loop Electronic Air–Fuel Control — Functional Block Architecture

The diagram below maps the **interactions between sensors, ECU, actuators, and exhaust feedback** that constitute modern electronic air–fuel ratio control.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Accelerator Pedal] --> B[Throttle Position Sensor TPS]
    C[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> D[Mass Air Flow Sensor MAF]
    D --> E[Engine Cylinder Combustion]
    B --> E
    F[Manifold Absolute Pressure MAP] --> E
    E --> G[Exhaust Manifold]
    G --> H[Lambda Oxygen Sensor]
    H --> I[Three Way Catalytic Converter TWC]
    I --> J[Tailpipe Emissions]
    H --> K[Electronic Control Unit ECU]
    B --> K
    D --> K
    F --> K
    L[Crankshaft Position Sensor CKP] --> K
    M[Coolant Temperature Sensor CTS] --> K
    K --> N[Fuel Injector Pulse Width Control]
    N --> O[Fuel Rail and Injectors]
    O --> E
    K --> P[Ignition Coil Timing Control]
    P --> E
    K --> Q[Idle Air Control Valve IACV]
    Q --> D
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Sensors (left/center feed):** TPS, MAF, MAP, CKP, CTS, Lambda — all feed **voltage/frequency signals** to the ECU.
- **ECU (center):** The "brain" runs algorithms (often a **PI / PID controller**) at ~100 Hz to compute fuel injector pulse width.
- **Actuators (right output):** Fuel injectors, ignition coil, IACV — execute the ECU commands.
- **Feedback (top loop):** Lambda sensor measures the *result* of the last injection cycle and corrects the next cycle within milliseconds.

### 4.2 Lambda Sensor Operating Point — Voltage Switching Characteristic

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[Lambda equals 1.00 Stoichiometric] --> S1[Sensor Voltage equals 0.45 V]
    L2[Lambda greater than 1.00 Lean] --> S2[Sensor Voltage less than 0.45 V approx 0.1 V]
    L3[Lambda less than 1.00 Rich] --> S3[Sensor Voltage greater than 0.45 V approx 0.9 V]
    S2 --> ECU1[ECU reduces fuel injector pulse width]
    S3 --> ECU2[ECU increases fuel injector pulse width]
    S1 --> ECU3[ECU holds current pulse width]
```

**Key takeaway:** The narrowband Lambda sensor acts as a **binary switch** that rapidly dithers the AFR around λ = 1.00, producing a characteristic "saw-tooth" O₂ sensor trace on an oscilloscope. The **frequency of switching** is itself a diagnostic indicator of catalyst health (a healthy TWC dampens the oscillations).

### 4.3 ECU Closed-Loop PID Control Logic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Target Lambda equals 1.00] --> B[Summing Junction]
    C[Actual Lambda from Sensor] --> B
    B --> D[Error Signal e of t]
    D --> E[PID Controller Proportional Integral Derivative]
    E --> F[Fuel Injector Pulse Width Correction]
    F --> G[Engine Combustion]
    G --> C
    G --> H[Exhaust and TWC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PID?** The proportional term gives *immediate* correction, the integral term eliminates *steady-state offset* (so AFR never lingers at λ = 0.99), and the derivative term *anticipates* rapid transients such as sudden throttle opening. Together, they keep AFR inside a band of **±1% of stoichiometric**, which is mandatory for TWC to convert >95% of CO, HC, and NOₓ simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the Air–Fuel Ratio (AFR) and explain the significance of the stoichiometric ratio for a petrol engine. State the standard value.**

**Model Answer:**

The Air–Fuel Ratio is the ratio of the mass of air to the mass of fuel supplied to the engine for combustion: $AFR = m_{air}/m_{fuel}$.

The **stoichiometric ratio** is the chemically ideal mass ratio at which *all* fuel is completely combusted with *exactly* the available oxygen, leaving no free O₂, CO, or unburnt HC. For petrol, the standard value is **14.7 : 1**, meaning 14.7 kg of air is required to burn 1 kg of petrol completely.

**Significance:** Operating close to this ratio is essential for a **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** to simultaneously oxidize CO/HC and reduce NOₓ. Deviation in either direction sharply increases emissions and reduces fuel economy.

*(3 marks: 1 — definition, 1 — stoichiometric significance, 1 — standard value + role of TWC)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is Lambda (λ)? Explain how the Lambda sensor enables closed-loop AFR control.**

**Model Answer:**

**Lambda (λ)** is the normalized air–fuel ratio, defined as:

$$\lambda = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoichiometric}}$$

- λ = 1 → stoichiometric
- λ > 1 → lean (excess air)
- λ < 1 → rich (deficit air)

The **Lambda (O₂) sensor**, mounted in the exhaust manifold, generates a voltage based on the difference in oxygen partial pressure between the exhaust gas and ambient air (Nernst principle). For a *narrowband* sensor, the output switches abruptly:
- **~0.1 V (lean, λ > 1)**
- **~0.9 V (rich, λ < 1)**
- **~0.45 V (stoichiometric, λ ≈ 1)**

The ECU continuously reads this voltage. If it detects a lean signal, the ECU **increases the injector pulse width** (more fuel); if rich, it **decreases** pulse width. This real-time correction constitutes **closed-loop control** and keeps λ pinned at 1.00 ± 0.01.

*(3 marks: 1 — λ definition, 1 — sensor voltage behaviour, 1 — closed-loop feedback action)*

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the stoichiometric air–fuel ratio by mass for **methane (CH₄)** and for **iso-octane (C₈H₁₈)**. Take the molecular mass of air as 29 kg/kmol. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A four-cylinder, four-stroke petrol engine operates at 3000 rpm with a volumetric efficiency of 85%. The air density is 1.2 kg/m³ and the engine displacement is 1.5 L. If the engine runs at λ = 1.05, calculate the **mass of fuel consumed per second** and the **brake-specific fuel consumption (BSFC)** if the brake power is 50 kW. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (a)**

**Step 1 — Combustion equation for methane:**
$$CH_4 + 2\,O_2 + 2(3.76)\,N_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + 7.52\,N_2$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric O₂:** 2 mol O₂ / mol CH₄

**Step 3 — Mass of air per mol CH₄:**
$$m_{air} = 2 \times 4.76 \times 29 = 276.08 \text{ kg air / kmol CH}_4$$

**Step 4 — Mass of CH₄ per kmol = 16 kg**

**Step 5 — Stoichiometric AFR for CH₄:**
$$AFR_{s,\,CH_4} = \frac{276.08}{16} = 17.26$$

**Step 6 — Combustion equation for iso-octane:**
$$C_8H_{18} + 12.5\,O_2 + 12.5(3.76)\,N_2 \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O + 47\,N_2$$

**Step 7 — Mass of air per mol C₈H₁₈:**
$$m_{air} = 12.5 \times 4.76 \times 29 = 1725.5 \text{ kg air / kmol fuel}$$

**Step 8 — Mass of C₈H₁₈ = 114 kg/kmol**

**Step 9 — Stoichiometric AFR for C₈H₁₈:**
$$AFR_{s,\,C_8H_{18}} = \frac{1725.5}{114} = 15.14$$

**Conclusion:** CH₄ has a higher stoichiometric AFR (17.26) than C₈H₁₈ (15.14) because methane has a higher H/C ratio and needs proportionally more oxygen per unit mass.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (b)**

**Step 1 — Air mass flow rate:**
Displacement V_d = 1.5 L = 0.0015 m³
At 3000 rpm, 4-stroke → cycles/sec N = 3000/(2×60) = 25 cycles/s
Theoretical air volume flow:
$$\dot{V}_{air,th} = V_d \times N = 0.0015 \times 25 = 0.0375 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Actual air mass flow rate:**
$$\dot{m}_{air} = \eta_{vol} \times \rho_{air} \times \dot{V}_{th} = 0.85 \times 1.2 \times 0.0375 = 0.03825 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3 — Actual AFR at λ = 1.05:**
$$AFR_{actual} = \lambda \times AFR_{stoich} = 1.05 \times 14.7 = 15.435$$

**Step 4 — Fuel mass flow rate:**
$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{AFR_{actual}} = \frac{0.03825}{15.435} = 2.478 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kg/hr:**
$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = 2.478 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 8.92 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 6 — BSFC:**
$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_{brake}} = \frac{2.478 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}}{50 \text{ kW}} = 4.956 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/kJ}$$

Converting to the standard g/kWh:
$$BSFC = 4.956 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.6 \times 10^6 = 178.4 \text{ g/kWh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Fuel consumption ≈ 8.92 kg/hr, BSFC ≈ 178.4 g/kWh. This is a reasonable value for a lightly loaded naturally aspirated petrol engine.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **closed-loop electronic air–fuel control system** of a modern MPFI petrol engine. List the major sensors and actuators. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the **Nernst equation**, derive the expression for the EMF of a Lambda oxygen sensor. A narrowband O₂ sensor at 700 K shows a voltage of 0.18 V when λ = 1.08. Estimate the voltage at λ = 0.95 (rich). Assume the rich-side O₂ partial pressure is roughly 1000 times lower than the lean side. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (a)**

A closed-loop electronic air–fuel control system has the following components:

**1. Input Sensors (feed signals to ECU):**
- **TPS** (Throttle Position Sensor) — driver's demand
- **MAF/MAP** (Mass Air Flow / Manifold Absolute Pressure) — measures intake air mass
- **CKP/CMP** (Crankshaft/Camshaft Position) — engine speed and valve timing
- **CTS** (Coolant Temperature Sensor) — engine temperature
- **IAT** (Intake Air Temperature) — air density compensation
- **Lambda (O₂) Sensor** — feedback from exhaust

**2. ECU (Electronic Control Unit):**
- Microcontroller running **PID algorithm** at ~100 Hz
- Lookup tables (maps) for base injector pulse width vs RPM and load
- Closed-loop correction factor based on Lambda sensor

**3. Output Actuators:**
- **Fuel injectors** — solenoid-operated; ECU controls pulse width (ms)
- **Ignition coil** — dwell and spark timing
- **Idle Air Control Valve (IACV)** — bypass air control
- **TWC** (passive exhaust after-treatment)

**4. Feedback Loop:**
Lambda sensor compares exhaust O₂ with ambient, generates a Nernst EMF; ECU interprets the voltage and adjusts pulse width by ~1–3% per cycle. Result: λ maintained at **1.00 ± 0.01**, TWC efficiency >95%, BS-VI compliance.

**Block diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the complete mermaid block architecture.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (b)**

**Step 1 — Nernst Equation for the O₂ sensor:**

A Lambda sensor is a galvanic cell of the form:
$$Pt, O_2(P_1) \mid ZrO_2 \text{ (solid electrolyte)} \mid O_2(P_2), Pt$$

The Nernst EMF is:
$$V = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)$$

where:
- R = 8.314 J/mol·K
- T = absolute temperature
- n = 4 electrons per O₂ molecule (O₂ + 4e⁻ → 2O²⁻)
- F = 96485 C/mol
- P₁, P₂ = partial pressures of O₂ on rich and lean sides

**Step 2 — Plug in values for lean condition (T = 700 K, λ = 1.08, V = 0.18 V):**
$$0.18 = \frac{8.314 \times 700}{4 \times 96485} \ln\left(\frac{P_{O_2,lean}}{P_{O_2,rich}}\right)$$
$$0.18 = 0.01508 \times \ln(r)$$

where r is the pressure ratio. This gives $\ln(r) = 11.94$ → r ≈ 1.56 × 10⁵ (theoretical). In real narrowband sensors, the biasing circuit normalizes this.

**Step 3 — For rich condition (λ = 0.95), the O₂ partial pressure on the rich side is 1000× lower:**
$$\ln\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)_{rich} = \ln\left(\frac{1.56 \times 10^5}{1000}\right) = \ln(156) = 5.05$$

**Step 4 — Voltage at λ = 0.95:**
$$V_{rich} = 0.01508 \times 5.05 = 0.0762 \text{ V}$$

**Conclusion:** As the mixture goes rich, O₂ partial pressure drops, and the Nernst EMF changes. In practice, with a **0.45 V reference bias**, the actual sensor output rises to ~0.85–0.9 V in rich conditions, allowing the ECU to detect and correct the rich excursion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Mixing up λ and φ:** Many students write λ = AFR_stoich / AFR_actual. Correct formula is **λ = AFR_actual / AFR_stoich**. This single error costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the 4.76 factor (O₂ + 3.76 N₂):** Several students compute air required as just the O₂ mass. The KTU board expects *air* (with N₂), not *oxygen*. Always multiply by 4.76 or by 32/23.2% ≈ 1/0.232.
> 3. **Using M_air = 28 instead of 29:** Use **28.97 ≈ 29 kg/kmol** for accuracy.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions:** Always state the fuel formula and the molecular mass of air explicitly in derivations.
> 5. **In closed-loop diagrams, missing the feedback arrow from Lambda sensor back to ECU:** Examiners specifically check the *loop*. A one-way arrow diagram will lose 2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing Nernst equation constants:** n = 4 (not 2) for O₂ sensor. Getting this wrong doubles/halves the voltage.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✦

- ✦ **AFR (mass)** = m_air / m_fuel; standard value for petrol = **14.7 : 1**; for diesel ≈ **14.5 : 1**; for CNG ≈ **17.2 : 1**.
- ✦ **Stoichiometric combustion** assumes only CO₂, H₂O, and N₂ in products — no O₂, CO, or HC remaining.
- ✦ **Universal stoichiometric AFR formula:** $AFR_s = [4.76(x + y/4) \times 29] / (12x + y)$ for fuel CₓHᵧ.
- ✦ **Lambda λ = 1** → stoichiometric; **λ > 1** → lean (more air); **λ < 1** → rich (more fuel).
- ✦ **Equivalence ratio φ = 1/λ** — preferred term in research papers.
- ✦ **% Excess Air = (λ − 1) × 100** — common in combustion engineering problems.
- ✦ The **"sweet spot"** for simultaneous CO + HC + NOₓ control is **λ = 1.00 ± 0.01**, achievable only with **closed-loop electronic control + Three-Way Catalyst**.
- ✦ **ECU-based EFI** replaces carburetors because it offers real-time, sensor-driven, closed-loop correction across all operating conditions (cold start, idle, cruise, acceleration, altitude).
- ✦ **Lambda sensor** is the heart of closed-loop control — works on **Nernst principle** with n = 4 electrons per O₂ molecule.
- ✦ **Major sensors:** TPS, MAF, MAP, CKP, CMP, CTS, IAT, Lambda.
- ✦ **Major actuators:** Fuel injectors, ignition coil, IACV, TWC.
- ✦ **PID controller** in the ECU: P = immediate correction, I = removes steady-state offset, D = anticipates transients.
- ✦ **Modern legislation:** BS-VI / Euro-6 / EPA Tier-3 mandates AFR held within ±1% of stoichiometric for the entire useful life of the catalyst — impossible without electronic control.
- ✦ **Lean-burn engines** (CNG, GDI stratified, HCCI) deliberately operate at λ > 1 to suppress NOₓ but cannot use a TWC — they require **NOₓ traps or SCR** for emission compliance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Catalytic Convertors - Two-Way and Three-Way Convertors, Catalytic Wear and Poisoning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Catalytic Convertors - Two-Way and Three-Way Convertors, Catalytic Wear and Poisoning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Catalytic Converter?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **catalytic converter** is an exhaust emission control device installed in the vehicle's exhaust system that uses chemical catalysts (typically platinum, palladium, and rhodium) supported on a ceramic or metallic honeycomb substrate to convert harmful exhaust pollutants—carbon monoxide (CO), unburned hydrocarbons (HC), and oxides of nitrogen (NOx)—into less harmful gases such as carbon dioxide (CO₂), water vapor (H₂O), and nitrogen (N₂) via simultaneous oxidation and reduction reactions, without being consumed in the process.

In simple, plain English for a student reading it for the very first time:

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Hospital for Dirty Exhaust Gases":**
> Think of the catalytic converter as a **chemical "filter hospital"** sitting in the tailpipe of your car. The exhaust gases leaving the engine are like "sick patients" carrying poisonous substances (CO, HC, NOx). As these gases pass through the converter, they flow over a special "medicine-coated surface" (the catalyst), which chemically transforms the dangerous gases into safer ones (CO₂, H₂O, N₂) — just like medicine cures a patient. The "medicine" (catalyst) is **not used up**; it only speeds up the chemical reaction. Over time, however, the medicine can become "contaminated" or "worn out" — this is called **catalyst poisoning and wear**.

## 1.2 Historical Context and Regulatory Push

The catalytic converter became mandatory in the United States from **1975** onwards (after the Clean Air Act amendments) and progressively in Europe, India (BS-VI norms from 2020), and globally. Modern KTU 2024 syllabus vehicles operating on petrol (gasoline) engines universally use **three-way catalytic converters (TWC)** as the cornerstone of their after-treatment system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics Used in the Industry:**
> - **Light-off temperature:** The temperature at which the catalyst becomes 50% efficient. Typical values are between **250 °C to 400 °C** depending on the precious metal formulation.
> - **Conversion efficiency (η):** Expressed as a percentage of pollutant converted. Modern TWCs achieve **>95% efficiency** for all three regulated pollutants once fully warmed up.
> - **Space velocity (SV):** The volumetric flow rate of exhaust gas divided by the volume of the substrate. Units: **hr⁻¹ (per hour)**. Typical values range from **30,000 to 100,000 hr⁻¹**.

## 1.3 Classification of Catalytic Converters

The two principal categories relevant to the KTU syllabus are:

| **Type** | **Pollutants Targeted** | **Era / Application** |
|---|---|---|
| **Two-Way (Oxidation) Catalytic Converter** | CO and HC only | Pre-1981 vehicles, early emission norms |
| **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** | CO, HC, and NOx simultaneously | All modern BS-VI / Euro 6 petrol vehicles |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of a Catalytic Converter

A modern catalytic converter consists of three functionally distinct layers:

1. **The Substrate (Core Structure):**
   - Made of either **ceramic (cordierite)** or **stainless steel foil**.
   - Shaped as a **honeycomb monolith** with thousands of parallel microscopic square channels (typically **400–600 cells per square inch, cpsi**).
   - The honeycomb geometry provides a **high surface area-to-volume ratio** (approx. **2,400 m²/m³**) without creating excessive exhaust back-pressure.

2. **The Washcoat:**
   - A porous, high-surface-area layer of **aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃), silicon dioxide (SiO₂), cerium oxide (CeO₂), and zirconium oxide (ZrO₂)**.
   - Applied over the substrate walls, increasing the effective catalytic surface area enormously.

3. **The Active Catalyst (Precious Metals):**
   - **Platinum (Pt)** — Promotes oxidation of CO and HC.
   - **Palladium (Pd)** — Excellent oxidation catalyst, more heat resistant than Pt.
   - **Rhodium (Rh)** — The only precious metal capable of efficiently reducing NOx to N₂.
   - Typical loading: **1–2 grams of precious metals per converter**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CeO₂ and ZrO₂ are added to the washcoat:**
> Cerium oxide acts as an **oxygen storage component (OSC)**. During rich burn (excess fuel, oxygen-deficient exhaust), it releases stored O₂; during lean burn (excess air), it absorbs O₂. This buffering action allows the TWC to function efficiently across the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (λ = 1) window.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Two-Way (Oxidation) Catalytic Converter

The two-way converter, as the name suggests, performs only **two chemical reactions** — both of which are **oxidation reactions**:

$$2\,\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt/Pd}} 2\,\text{CO}_2$$

$$4\,\text{HC} + 5\,\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt/Pd}} 4\,\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

- The two-way converter **cannot reduce NOx** because it lacks rhodium and operates only in an oxidizing exhaust environment.
- It requires the engine to run slightly **lean (λ > 1)** so that excess oxygen is available for the oxidation reactions.
- It is largely **obsolete in modern vehicles** but is still asked in KTU examinations for conceptual clarity.

## 2.3 Working Principle — Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)

The TWC is the heart of modern petrol-engine emission control. It performs **three simultaneous reactions**:

**Oxidation Reactions (Platinum and Palladium catalysed):**

$$2\,\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2$$

$$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Reduction Reaction (Rhodium catalysed):**

$$2\,\text{NO}_x \longrightarrow x\,\text{O}_2 + \text{N}_2$$

For NO (the dominant NOx species):

$$2\,\text{NO} \xrightarrow{\text{Rh}} \text{N}_2 + \text{O}_2$$

$$2\,\text{NO} + 2\,\text{CO} \xrightarrow{\text{Rh}} \text{N}_2 + 2\,\text{CO}_2$$

### 2.3.1 The Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (λ Window)

The TWC operates efficiently **only in a very narrow air-fuel ratio window** centered at the stoichiometric point (λ = 1, i.e., **14.7 : 1 by mass for petrol**).

| **Condition** | **λ value** | **TWC Performance** |
|---|---|---|
| Rich mixture | λ < 1 | Good reduction of NOx, poor oxidation of CO/HC |
| Stoichiometric | λ = 1 | **Optimal — >95% conversion for all three pollutants** |
| Lean mixture | λ > 1 | Good oxidation of CO/HC, poor reduction of NOx |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Closed-Loop Lambda Sensor (O₂ Sensor) is mandatory:**
> Because the efficient window is so narrow (only ±1% of λ = 1), a **Heated Exhaust Gas Oxygen (HEGO) sensor** is placed before the TWC. It continuously measures the residual oxygen and feeds the signal to the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)**, which fine-tunes the fuel injection duration every few milliseconds to keep the engine oscillating tightly around λ = 1.

### 2.3.2 Conversion Efficiency of TWC (η)

$$\eta_{\text{pol}} = \frac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$$

where $C_{\text{in}}$ and $C_{\text{out}}$ are the pollutant concentrations at the inlet and outlet of the converter, respectively.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU 2024 Examination — Master These:**

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $\eta = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | Conversion efficiency of a pollutant | % |
| $SV = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{\text{exhaust}}}{V_{\text{substrate}}}$ | Space velocity | hr⁻¹ |
| $\lambda = \dfrac{(\text{AF})_{\text{actual}}}{(\text{AF})_{\text{stoichiometric}}}$ | Relative air-fuel ratio (equivalence ratio inverse) | dimensionless |
| $A/F_{\text{stoich}} = 14.7$ | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for petrol | kg air / kg fuel |
| $T_{\text{light-off}} = 250 \text{ to } 400$ | Catalyst light-off temperature range | °C |
| $\text{Number of cells} \propto 400 \text{–}1200$ | Cell density of substrate (cpsi) | cells/in² |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:**
> When asked to compare two-way vs three-way converters in Part B (14-mark) questions, **always** mention: (a) the chemistry of reactions, (b) the requirement of λ = 1 control, (c) the role of rhodium (Rh) as the unique NOx-reducing catalyst, and (d) the necessity of the upstream oxygen sensor for closed-loop control. Missing any of these four points typically costs 2–3 marks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Catalytic converters are critical in achieving **BS-VI (India), Euro 6 (Europe), Tier 3 (USA)** emission norms. The TWC alone, when properly maintained, can reduce:

- CO emissions by **~95%**
- HC emissions by **~90–95%**
- NOx emissions by **~85–95%**

Beyond automobiles, similar catalytic after-treatment principles are used in **CNG buses, two-wheelers, industrial furnaces, and stationary power generation engines**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio of Petrol

A representative petrol hydrocarbon is **octane (C₈H₁₈)**. The complete combustion reaction is:

$$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + a\,\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,a\,\text{N}_2 \longrightarrow b\,\text{CO}_2 + c\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3.76\,a\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step 1: Balance carbon atoms.**
Each octane molecule has 8 carbon atoms, so the CO₂ produced must have 8 carbon atoms.
$$b = 8$$

**Step 2: Balance hydrogen atoms.**
Octane has 18 hydrogen atoms, so H₂O must contain 9 molecules.
$$2c = 18 \quad \Rightarrow \quad c = 9$$

**Step 3: Balance oxygen atoms.**
The O atoms on the right side = $2(8) + 9 = 25$
The O atoms on the left side = $2a$
$$2a = 25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = 12.5$$

**Step 4: Mass of air (with 23% O₂ by mass, 21% by volume).**
Molecular masses: O₂ = 32, N₂ = 28, Air = 29 (approx.)

$$\text{Mass of O}_2 = 12.5 \times 32 = 400\,\text{g}$$
$$\text{Mass of N}_2 = 12.5 \times 3.76 \times 28 = 1316\,\text{g}$$
$$\text{Mass of air} = 400 + 1316 = 1716\,\text{g}$$
$$\text{Mass of octane} = 8(12) + 18(1) = 114\,\text{g}$$

**Step 5: Compute the A/F ratio.**
$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{1716}{114} = 15.05 \approx 14.7 \text{ (for commercial petrol blend)}$$

> Commercial petrol is a mixture of many hydrocarbons (paraffins, aromatics, olefins), and the practical stoichiometric ratio accepted by KTU textbook convention is **A/F = 14.7 : 1** (or equivalently, equivalence ratio φ = 1).

## 3.2 Derivation: Catalyst Light-Off Characteristic

The catalytic conversion efficiency follows a **sigmoidal (S-shaped) curve** with respect to temperature. A widely used empirical model is:

$$\eta(T) = \frac{\eta_{\max}}{1 + e^{-k(T - T_{50})}}$$

where:
- $\eta_{\max}$ = maximum conversion efficiency (asymptotic, e.g., 98%)
- $T_{50}$ = temperature at 50% conversion (light-off temperature)
- $k$ = steepness factor (depends on catalyst formulation)

**Why this matters:** Below the light-off temperature, the catalyst is essentially "asleep" — almost no conversion happens. Once exhaust temperature rises past $T_{50}$, conversion ramps up rapidly. This is the engineering rationale behind **heated catalysts** and **close-coupled converters** mounted near the engine exhaust manifold to reduce light-off time.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Two-Way vs Three-Way Converter

| **Parameter** | **Two-Way (Oxidation) Converter** | **Three-Way Converter (TWC)** |
|---|---|---|
| Pollutants reduced | CO, HC only | CO, HC, NOx |
| Chemical reactions | Oxidation only | Oxidation + Reduction |
| Catalysts used | Pt, Pd | Pt, Pd, Rh |
| A/F ratio requirement | Lean (λ > 1) acceptable | Strict λ = 1 ± 1% |
| Oxygen sensor required | No (open-loop) | **Yes (closed-loop HEGO sensor mandatory)** |
| Oxygen storage component | Not required | **CeO₂/ZrO₂ washcoat required** |
| Conversion efficiency | ~70–80% (CO/HC) | **>95% for all three pollutants** |
| Year of introduction | 1975 | ~1981 onwards |
| Modern application | Obsolete in cars | Universal in modern BS-VI petrol vehicles |

## 3.4 Derivation: Effect of Space Velocity on Conversion Efficiency

The space velocity relates the exhaust flow rate to substrate volume:

$$SV = \frac{\dot{V}_{\text{exhaust}}}{V_{\text{substrate}}} \quad [\text{hr}^{-1}]$$

The relation between conversion efficiency and space velocity is approximately:

$$\eta \propto \frac{1}{1 + k \cdot SV}$$

This means:
- **Higher SV** (small converter, high exhaust flow) → **lower conversion efficiency** (residence time is too short for full reaction).
- **Lower SV** (larger converter, low flow) → higher efficiency but excessive back-pressure and cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Design Rule of Thumb:** A well-designed passenger car TWC has an SV in the range of **50,000–80,000 hr⁻¹** at cruise conditions, balancing efficiency, packaging, and back-pressure.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Three-Way Catalytic Converter System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Combustion Chamber] -->|Hot exhaust gases| B[Exhaust Manifold]
    B --> C[Heated Oxygen Sensor HEGO]
    C -->|Lambda feedback signal| D[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    D -->|Adjusts fuel injection pulse| A
    C --> E[Three Way Catalytic Converter]
    E --> F[Ceramic Honeycomb Substrate]
    E --> G[Alumina Washcoat with CeO2 ZrO2]
    E --> H[Pt Pd Oxidation Catalysts]
    E --> I[Rh Reduction Catalyst]
    E -->|Cleaned exhaust| J[Tailpipe to Atmosphere]
    F -.->|High surface area support| H
    G -.->|Oxygen storage buffering| H
    G -.->|Oxygen storage buffering| I
```

## 4.2 Internal Construction Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OuterShell[Outer Stainless Steel Shell] --> InletCone[Inlet Cone Diffuser]
    InletCone --> IntumescentMat1[High Temperature Intumescent Mounting Mat]
    IntumescentMat1 --> SubstrateBlock[Cordierite Ceramic Honeycomb Substrate 400 600 cpsi]
    SubstrateBlock --> WallCoating[Porous Alumina Washcoat Al2O3 plus CeO2 plus ZrO2]
    WallCoating --> PreciousMetals[Active Catalyst Layer Pt 1 to 2 g plus Pd plus Rh]
    PreciousMetals --> Channels[Thousands of Parallel Microchannels]
    Channels --> OutletCone[Outlet Cone]
    OutletCone --> OutletPipe[Tailpipe Exit]
    IntumescentMat2[Second Intumescent Mat Layer] --> OutletPipe
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — TWC Chemical Reactions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In[Exhaust Inlet CO plus HC plus NOx] --> OxBranch[Oxidation Zone Pt and Pd Catalyst]
    In --> RedBranch[Reduction Zone Rh Catalyst]
    OxBranch -->|CO plus O2| CO2Out[CO2]
    OxBranch -->|HC plus O2| H2OOut[H2O]
    RedBranch -->|NOx plus CO| N2Out[N2]
    RedBranch -->|NOx| CO2Out
    CO2Out --> Out[Cleaned Exhaust Outlet]
    H2OOut --> Out
    N2Out --> Out
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Catalyst Degradation Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Fresh Catalyst High Efficiency] --> ThermalDegrad[Thermal Degradation]
    Start --> ChemicalPoison[Chemical Poisoning]
    Start --> MechanicalDegrad[Mechanical Degradation]
    ThermalDegrad --> Sintering[Precious Metal Sintering Particle Growth]
    ThermalDegrad --> PhaseChange[Washcoat Phase Change Alumina to Alpha Alumina]
    Sintering --> ReducedSA[Reduced Active Surface Area]
    PhaseChange --> ReducedSA
    ChemicalPoison --> LeadP[Lead Poisoning Pb from Leaded Fuel]
    ChemicalPoison --> SulfurP[Sulfur Poisoning S from Fuel]
    ChemicalPoison --> PhosP[Phosphorus Poisoning P from Engine Oil]
    ChemicalPoison --> ManganeseP[Manganese Poisoning Mn from MMT Additive]
    LeadP --> BlockedSites[Blocked Active Catalyst Sites]
    SulfurP --> BlockedSites
    PhosP --> BlockedSites
    ManganeseP --> BlockedSites
    MechanicalDegrad --> Vibration[Road Vibration and Thermal Cycling]
    MechanicalDegrad --> MeltCollapse[Substrate Melt and Collapse]
    Vibration --> Cracks[Cracking of Substrate]
    MeltCollapse --> ChannBlock[Blocked Exhaust Channels]
    ReducedSA --> EndState[Converter Efficiency Drop]
    BlockedSites --> EndState
    Cracks --> EndState
    ChannBlock --> EndState
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:**
> When asked to "draw and explain the working of a TWC," start with the inlet arrow showing raw exhaust composition, branch it into oxidation and reduction zones, show the catalyst metals, and end with the cleaned exhaust components. Always label **λ = 1 control loop** with the HEGO sensor arrow.
