# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the three pollutants controlled by a three-way catalytic converter and name the precious metal responsible for the reduction of NOx.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three pollutants are **carbon monoxide (CO)**, **unburned hydrocarbons (HC)**, and **oxides of nitrogen (NOx)**. The precious metal responsible for the reduction of NOx to N₂ is **Rhodium (Rh)**. While Platinum and Palladium catalyse the oxidation of CO and HC, only Rhodium has the unique capability to dissociate the strong N–O bond of NOx and release free nitrogen.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by the "light-off temperature" of a catalytic converter? State its typical range.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **light-off temperature** is defined as the exhaust temperature at which the catalytic converter achieves **50% conversion efficiency** of the regulated pollutants. Below this temperature, the catalytic reactions are too slow to be significant, and the converter is essentially inactive. The typical light-off temperature range for modern TWCs is between **250 °C and 400 °C**, depending on the precious metal formulation and washcoat composition. This is why **close-coupled converters** are placed near the exhaust manifold — to reach light-off as quickly as possible after a cold start.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer ONE full question from a choice of two. Both alternatives are provided below for parallel practice.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of neat chemical equations, explain the working of a **three-way catalytic converter**. Why is the air-fuel ratio strictly maintained at the stoichiometric value of 14.7:1? Discuss the role of the **oxygen storage component** in the washcoat. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the various **mechanisms by which a catalytic converter loses its efficiency over time** (catalyst poisoning and wear). Name at least four catalyst poisons and the source of each. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Working of TWC and λ = 1 Control (7 marks):**

The three-way catalytic converter simultaneously performs oxidation and reduction reactions on the exhaust gases. The reactions are:

*Oxidation of carbon monoxide:*

$$2\,\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt, Pd}} 2\,\text{CO}_2$$

*Oxidation of unburned hydrocarbons:*

$$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \tfrac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt, Pd}} x\,\text{CO}_2 + \tfrac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

*Reduction of oxides of nitrogen:*

$$2\,\text{NO} \xrightarrow{\text{Rh}} \text{N}_2 + \text{O}_2$$

$$2\,\text{NO} + 2\,\text{CO} \xrightarrow{\text{Rh}} \text{N}_2 + 2\,\text{CO}_2$$

**Why A/F = 14.7:1 is mandatory:**

The TWC achieves >95% conversion of all three pollutants **only within a narrow window** of the air-fuel ratio (λ = 1 ± 1%). If the mixture is rich (excess fuel), there is insufficient oxygen for the oxidation of CO and HC. If the mixture is lean (excess air), the reducing environment is lost and NOx cannot be converted. Therefore, the **HEGO (λ) sensor** upstream of the TWC sends continuous feedback to the **ECU**, which modulates the fuel injector pulse width to keep the engine oscillating tightly around the stoichiometric point. (4 marks for chemistry + control loop)

**Role of the Oxygen Storage Component (OSC):**

The washcoat contains **cerium oxide (CeO₂)**, often stabilized with **zirconium oxide (ZrO₂)**. CeO₂ stores oxygen during lean excursions (λ > 1) by the reaction:

$$2\,\text{CeO}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Ce}_2\text{O}_3 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$$

and releases it during rich excursions (λ < 1) by the reverse reaction. This **buffers** the local oxygen concentration at the catalyst surface, extending the effective operating window and significantly improving NOx, CO, and HC conversion efficiency during transient driving conditions. (1 mark for OSC explanation)

---

**(b) — Catalyst Poisoning and Wear (7 marks):**

Catalyst degradation mechanisms are broadly classified into three categories:

**1. Thermal Degradation (2 marks):**
- **Sintering:** At sustained high exhaust temperatures (above **800 °C**), the nano-sized precious metal particles migrate and coalesce into larger clusters. This **reduces the active surface area** and hence the conversion efficiency.
- **Washcoat phase change:** The gamma-alumina (γ-Al₂O₃) washcoat can transform into the more stable but low-surface-area **alpha-alumina (α-Al₂O₃)** at temperatures above **1000 °C**, permanently reducing the catalyst's effectiveness.

**2. Chemical Poisoning (3 marks):**
Contaminants chemically bond to the active sites of the catalyst, rendering them inactive. Major poisons and their sources:

| **Poison** | **Source** |
|---|---|
| **Lead (Pb)** | Leaded petrol (now banned in India), historical emissions |
| **Sulfur (S)** | Sulfur content in petrol and diesel fuel (forms sulfates) |
| **Phosphorus (P)** | Engine lubricating oil (enters via blow-by gases) |
| **Manganese (Mn)** | MMT (methylcyclopentadienyl manganese tricarbonyl) fuel additive |
| **Silicon (Si)** | Silicone-based engine sealants or oil contamination |
| **Zinc (Zn)** | Zinc dialkyldithiophosphate (ZDDP) anti-wear additive in engine oil |

**3. Mechanical Degradation (2 marks):**
- **Substrate cracking** due to repeated thermal cycling and road vibration.
- **Channel blockage** from carbon deposits, oil contamination, or physical impact.
- **Melt-down** of the ceramic substrate under severe overheating (e.g., from a misfiring engine dumping unburned fuel into the hot converter).

The combined effect of these mechanisms is a **permanent or semi-permanent loss of conversion efficiency**, requiring eventual replacement of the catalytic converter.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 – Adapted]**

**(a)** Compare in detail the **construction, working, and limitations** of a two-way oxidation catalytic converter with that of a three-way catalytic converter. (7 marks)

**(b)** What is the function of the **ceramic honeycomb substrate** in a catalytic converter? Why is **cordierite** the most widely used substrate material? Explain with reference to the **400–600 cpsi** cell density. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Two-Way vs Three-Way Converter (7 marks):**

| **Feature** | **Two-Way (Oxidation)** | **Three-Way (TWC)** |
|---|---|---|
| Year of introduction | 1975 | ~1981 onwards |
| Reactions | Oxidation only | Oxidation + Reduction |
| Catalysts | Pt, Pd | Pt, Pd, Rh |
| Pollutants addressed | CO, HC | CO, HC, NOx |
| A/F control | Open-loop; lean acceptable | Closed-loop; λ = 1 ± 1% |
| Conversion efficiency | ~70–80% (CO, HC) | >95% (all three) |
| Oxygen sensor | Not required | **HEGO sensor mandatory** |
| Oxygen storage (CeO₂) | Not required | Required for buffering |
| Modern applicability | **Obsolete** in passenger cars | **Universal** in BS-VI vehicles |

**Limitations of two-way converter:**
- Cannot reduce NOx — this is the single biggest drawback.
- Cannot meet modern Euro 6 / BS-VI / Tier 3 norms.
- Open-loop control leads to high CO/HC slip during transient operation.

**Advantages of TWC:**
- Single unit addresses all three regulated pollutants.
- High conversion efficiency once warmed up.
- Compatible with closed-loop electronic fuel injection systems.

(2 marks for tabular comparison + 5 marks for narrative elaboration with chemical equations)

---

**(b) — Ceramic Honeycomb Substrate (7 marks):**

**Function:**
The substrate is the **structural backbone** of the catalytic converter. It provides the mechanical support for the washcoat and the precious metal catalysts, while offering a high surface area for the exhaust gases to contact the catalytically active material.

**Why cordierite (2MgO·2Al₂O₃·5SiO₂)?**

Cordierite is the preferred substrate material due to its unique combination of properties:

- **Low thermal expansion coefficient** (~1×10⁻⁶ /°C) — survives rapid thermal cycling without cracking.
- **High melting point** (~1400 °C) — withstands extreme exhaust temperatures.
- **Low thermal conductivity** — keeps heat inside the converter for better light-off.
- **Adequate mechanical strength** at operating temperatures.
- **Cost-effective** compared to metallic substrates.

**Why 400–600 cpsi (cells per square inch)?**

The cell density determines the trade-off between:
- **Surface area** for catalytic reactions (higher cpsi → more area → more conversion).
- **Back-pressure** on the engine (higher cpsi → more flow restriction → power loss).
- **Clogging tendency** (very high cpsi > 1200 can plug with soot/oil).

A density of **400 cpsi** is standard for normal road cars, while **600 cpsi** (or even higher, 900–1200 cpsi in ultra-thin-wall substrates) is used in high-performance or low-emission applications. The wall thickness of cordierite substrates is now as low as **2–3 mil (50–75 µm)** in modern ultra-thin-wall designs, allowing more open frontal area and lower back-pressure while still maintaining structural integrity. (7 marks — 2 marks for substrate function, 3 marks for cordierite properties, 2 marks for cpsi discussion)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Catalytic Converter Questions (As Per KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing two-way with three-way** — many students write "three-way" but list only two reactions. Always state the number of reactions explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting Rhodium's unique role** — Rh is the *only* precious metal that reduces NOx. Writing "platinum reduces NOx" costs at least 1 mark.
> 3. **Omitting the closed-loop oxygen sensor** — without mentioning the HEGO sensor and ECU feedback, the explanation of λ = 1 control is incomplete.
> 4. **Mixing up poisoning and wear** — poisoning is **chemical** contamination of active sites; wear is **physical/thermal** degradation of the substrate and washcoat. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 5. **Missing the oxygen storage role of CeO₂** — this is a high-yield KTU point often asked as a 2-mark sub-question.
> 6. **Writing chemical equations without balancing them** — every CO, HC, and NOx equation must be stoichiometrically balanced.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ A catalytic converter is an **after-treatment device** — it does not prevent pollutant formation, it converts pollutants into harmless gases.
- ⭐ **Two-way converter** → only oxidation, only CO and HC, no Rh, obsolete.
- ⭐ **Three-way converter (TWC)** → oxidation + reduction, all three pollutants, **Pt + Pd + Rh** mandatory.
- ⭐ **Rhodium (Rh)** is the **only** metal that reduces NOx to N₂.
- ⭐ TWC requires **λ = 1 ± 1%** control via the upstream **HEGO sensor** and ECU.
- ⭐ **CeO₂ (cerium oxide)** in the washcoat is the **oxygen storage component (OSC)** that buffers O₂ during transient operation.
- ⭐ **Cordierite ceramic honeycomb** is the standard substrate; **400–600 cpsi** is the typical cell density.
- ⭐ Light-off temperature is **250–400 °C**; close-coupled mounting reduces cold-start emissions.
- ⭐ **Catalyst poisons**: Lead (Pb), Sulfur (S), Phosphorus (P), Manganese (Mn), Silicon (Si), Zinc (Zn).
- ⭐ **Catalyst wear**: Thermal sintering, washcoat phase change (γ → α alumina), substrate cracking/melting, channel blockage.
- ⭐ Space velocity **SV = 50,000–80,000 hr⁻¹** is the design sweet spot for modern TWCs.
- ⭐ **Conversion efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ must reach **>95%** for BS-VI compliance.
- ⭐ The **stoichiometric A/F ratio for petrol is 14.7:1** (or 14.6:1 for some references).
- ⭐ Modern vehicles use **OBD-II (On-Board Diagnostics)** to monitor TWC efficiency by comparing pre- and post-cat oxygen sensor signals.
- ⭐ **No leaded fuel** can be used in vehicles equipped with catalytic converters — even one tank of leaded petrol can permanently poison the catalyst.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diesel Fuelled Vehicles, Diesel Particulate Filter, Exhaust After Treatment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Diesel Fuelled Vehicles, Diesel Particulate Filter, Exhaust After Treatment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Diesel Fuelled Vehicles — Formal Definition

A **diesel-fuelled vehicle** is a compression-ignition (CI) engine-powered automobile that ignites fuel through the heat of compressed air (no spark plug), operating on the **Diesel Cycle** with a high compression ratio (typically **14:1 to 22:1**). The combustion is heterogeneous, premixed-leaning, and diffusion-controlled, producing fundamentally different pollutant profiles compared to spark-ignition engines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Diesel vehicles are emphasized because they emit **significantly higher NOₓ and Particulate Matter (PM)** than petrol vehicles, but **lower CO and unburnt HC** due to lean-burn operation. Their control requires dedicated after-treatment hardware: **DOC + DPF + SCR/LNT**.

### 1.2 Pollutant Formation in Diesel Engines — Conceptual Intuition

**Analogy — "The Incomplete Campfire"**

Imagine throwing wet leaves, dry twigs, and paper into a campfire in bursts. The dry twigs burn cleanly (CO₂), the paper produces smoke (HC/CO), and the wet leaves puff out unburnt carbon particles (PM/soot). A diesel engine is just a tiny, controlled version of this:

- **Excess air** (λ > 1, lean) → complete oxidation → very low CO and HC.
- **High in-cylinder temperatures (>1800 K)** → atmospheric N₂ reacts with O₂ → **NOₓ**.
- **Fuel-rich pockets in diffusion flame** → incomplete combustion → **Particulate Matter (soot)**.
- **High pressure and high compression** → some fuel sprays onto cold cylinder walls → **wall-quench HC**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR):** 18:1 to 70:1 (vs. petrol's stoichiometric 14.7:1)
> - **Equivalence Ratio (φ):** 0.2 to 0.8 (always lean, φ < 1)
> - **Lambda (λ):** 1.1 to 5.0
> - **Compression Ratio (r):** 14:1 to 22:1
> - **Cetane Number (CN):** 40–55 (ignition quality of diesel)

### 1.3 Why Diesel Vehicles Need Special After-Treatment

Unlike petrol engines that operate at the stoichiometric point (λ = 1, the "three-way catalyst sweet spot"), diesel engines operate **lean** (λ > 1). The excess oxygen in the exhaust stream makes traditional three-way catalysts ineffective. Hence, **specialized exhaust after-treatment devices** are mandatory for meeting BS-VI / Euro-VI emission norms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Board Exam Favourite):**
> - Petrol (SI) → λ = 1 → **Three-Way Catalyst (TWC)** can reduce NOₓ, oxidize CO and HC.
> - Diesel (CI) → λ > 1 → TWC fails; needs **DOC + DPF + SCR** (or LNT) combination.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pollutant Formation Mechanisms in Diesel Engines

#### A. Oxides of Nitrogen (NOₓ)
- **Primary species:** NO (≈90–95%), NO₂ (≈5–10%), N₂O (trace).
- **Formation pathways:**
  1. **Thermal (Zeldovich) NOₓ** — dominant in diesel. Requires T > 1800 K.
     - Reaction: N₂ + O₂ → 2NO
     - N₂ + O → NO + N
     - N + O₂ → NO + O
     - N + OH → NO + H
  2. **Fuel-bound NOₓ** — from nitrogen content in fuel (minor for petrodiesel, significant for biodiesel).
  3. **Prompt NOₓ** (Fenimore) — negligible in diesel.

#### B. Particulate Matter (PM / Soot)
- **Composition:** Solid carbon (soot) + adsorbed hydrocarbons (SOF) + sulfates + water.
- **Formation Steps:**
  1. **Pyrolysis** of fuel in fuel-rich zones → formation of PAHs (Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons).
  2. **Nucleation** of PAHs into solid carbon particles (10–30 nm).
  3. **Coagulation / Surface growth** → larger particles (30–500 nm).
  4. **Aggregation** → chain-like soot clusters.
  5. **Adsorption** of heavy HC onto soot (wet soot).

#### C. Carbon Monoxide (CO)
- Formed during **incomplete combustion** in fuel-rich zones within the diffusion flame.
- Diesel engines emit **very low CO** (typically 0.5–3 g/km) because overall operation is lean.

#### D. Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)
- Sources: **wall quenching**, crevice volumes, **over-mixed** regions where temperature is too low to ignite.
- Diesel HC emissions: **0.1–0.5 g/km** (very low).

#### E. Sulfur Dioxide (SO₂)
- Formed from sulfur in fuel: S + O₂ → SO₂
- Modern ultra-low sulfur diesel (ULSD, < 10 ppm S) has drastically reduced SO₂.

### 2.2 Exhaust After-Treatment Devices for Diesel

#### A. Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC)
- **Function:** Oxidizes CO, HC (and SOF) to CO₂ and H₂O; also raises exhaust temperature for DPF regeneration.
- **Catalyst:** Platinum (Pt) and/or Palladium (Pd) on washcoat (Al₂O₃, CeO₂).
- **Operating window:** 200–500°C; light-off temperature ~250°C.
- **Reactions:**
  - CO + ½O₂ → CO₂
  - CₓHᵧ + (x + y/4)O₂ → xCO₂ + (y/2)H₂O
  - HC + NO → CO₂ + H₂O + N₂ (secondary, minor)

> [!WARNING]
> **DOC Pitfall:** DOC can oxidize SO₂ to SO₃ (forming sulfate PM) if fuel sulfur is high. This is why **ULSD is mandatory** for advanced after-treatment.

#### B. Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)
- **Function:** Physically traps soot/PM from exhaust gas with **>99% efficiency**.
- **Construction:** Porous ceramic (cordierite, SiC) honeycomb with **alternately plugged channels** (wall-flow design).
- **Filtration mechanism:** Exhaust gas forced through porous walls; PM deposited on wall surface.

##### DPF Regeneration Methods
| Regeneration Type | Trigger Condition | Method | Typical Temp |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Passive** | During normal driving | NO₂-assisted oxidation (CRT) | 250–400°C |
| **Active (Thermal)** | ΔP exceeds threshold | Post-injection of fuel → DOC oxidizes → heat | 600–650°C |
| **Active (Fuel-borne catalyst)** | Continuous | Fuel additive (e.g., cerium) lowers soot ignition T | 400–500°C |
| **Forced (Service)** | Manual / DTC | Burn-off in workshop with heat gun | 650°C+ |
| **CRT (Continuously Regenerating Trap)** | Steady-state highway | Uses NO₂ from upstream DOC to oxidize soot | 250–450°C |

- **Pressure Drop Equation:**
$$\Delta P_{DPF} = \frac{\mu \cdot v \cdot L}{k_{wall}} + K \cdot \frac{\rho \cdot v^2}{2} \cdot (1-\varepsilon_{cake})$$
where μ = exhaust viscosity, v = gas velocity, L = wall thickness, k_wall = wall permeability, ε_cake = cake porosity.

- **Loading capacity:** 5–10 g/L of soot before ΔP triggers regeneration.

#### C. Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)
- **Function:** Reduces NOₓ to N₂ and H₂O using **ammonia (NH₃)** as reducing agent.
- **Reducing agent source:** **AdBlue®** (32.5% urea solution in deionized water).
- **Reactions:**
  - (NH₂)₂CO + H₂O → 2NH₃ + CO₂ (urea decomposition, ~200°C)
  - 4NH₃ + 4NO + O₂ → 4N₂ + 6H₂O (Standard SCR)
  - 2NH₃ + NO + NO₂ → 2N₂ + 3H₂O (Fast SCR — preferred at low T)
  - 8NH₃ + 6NO₂ → 7N₂ + 12H₂O (NO₂ SCR)
- **Catalyst:** Cu-zeolite, Fe-zeolite, or V₂O₅/WO₃/TiO₂.
- **Operating window:** 200–500°C; efficiency 80–95%.
- **NH₃ Slip Control:** Critical — excess NH₃ is toxic; controlled by dosing strategy.

#### D. Lean NOₓ Trap (LNT) / NOₓ Storage Reduction (NSR)
- **Function:** Adsorbs NOₓ during lean operation, releases and reduces during brief rich pulse.
- **Storage material:** BaO, BaCO₃, or K₂O on washcoat.
- **Cycle:** Lean (60–90 s) → adsorb NOₓ; Rich (5–10 s) → release + reduce with HC/CO/H₂.
- **Equations:**
  - Lean: BaO + 2NO₂ + ½O₂ → Ba(NO₃)₂
  - Rich: Ba(NO₃)₂ + 5H₂ → BaO + N₂ + 5H₂O

#### E. Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) — In-Cylinder Control
- **Function:** Recirculates a portion of exhaust gas back to the intake manifold to lower combustion temperature and reduce NOₓ.
- **EGR Rate:** % of exhaust gas recirculated (typically 5–30%).
- **Effect:** Lowers peak flame temperature from ~2000 K to ~1500 K → reduces thermal NOₓ formation.
- **Complications:** Increases PM (more soot) → must balance with DPF.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric Diesel) | (C₁₂H₂₃) + 17.5O₂ → 12CO₂ + 11.5H₂O → AFR = 14.5 | — | Theoretical minimum O₂ demand |
| 2 | Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{stoich}}{(A/F)_{actual}}$ | dimensionless | Lean: φ < 1; Rich: φ > 1 |
| 3 | Lambda (λ) | $\lambda = \frac{1}{\phi} = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | dimensionless | Lean: λ > 1 |
| 4 | NOₓ formation (Zeldovich) rate | $\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = k_1[O][N_2] + k_2[N][O_2] + k_3[N][OH]$ | mol/(m³·s) | Thermal NOₓ kinetics |
| 5 | Activation energy (Zeldovich) | E_a ≈ 319 kJ/mol | kJ/mol | High T sensitivity |
| 6 | NOₓ reduction (Thermal) | ΔNOₓ ∝ 1/T_exhaust (for given load) | ppm | T control via EGR |
| 7 | DPF collection efficiency | $\eta_{DPF} = 1 - \frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}$ | % | PM removal (>99%) |
| 8 | DPF pressure drop | $\Delta P = \frac{\mu v L}{k_w} + \frac{K \rho v^2}{2}$ | Pa | Soot loading indicator |
| 9 | SCR efficiency | $\eta_{SCR} = \frac{[NO_x]_{in} - [NO_x]_{out}}{[NO_x]_{in}} \times 100$ | % | 80–95% typical |
| 10 | Stoichiometric urea dosing | $m_{urea} = \frac{[NO_x] \times V_{exh} \times M_{urea}}{2 \times M_{NO_x} \times \eta_{SCR}}$ | kg/h | AdBlue injector |
| 11 | EGR rate | $EGR\% = \frac{\dot{m}_{EGR}}{\dot{m}_{total\,intake}} \times 100$ | % | 5–30% typical |
| 12 | Soot oxidation by NO₂ | C + 2NO₂ → CO₂ + 2NO (passive regen) | — | CRT mechanism |
| 13 | Soot oxidation by O₂ | C + O₂ → CO₂ (active regen, T > 600°C) | — | Thermal regen |
| 14 | BS-VI Diesel NOₓ limit | 80 mg/km | mg/km | India regulation |
| 15 | BS-VI Diesel PM limit | 4.5 mg/km | mg/km | India regulation |
| 16 | BS-VI Diesel CO limit | 500 mg/km | mg/km | India regulation |
| 17 | BS-VI Diesel HC+NOₓ limit | 170 mg/km (HC+NOₓ) | mg/km | India regulation |
| 18 | PM mass distribution | 60–80% soot, 5–15% SOF, 5–10% sulfate, rest ash | % | PM composition |
| 19 | Cetane Number effect | Higher CN → shorter ignition delay → lower HC, lower peak T | — | Fuel quality |
| 20 | Compressibility of PM cake | $\varepsilon_{cake} = 1 - \frac{m_{soot}}{\rho_{soot} \cdot V_{cake}}$ | dimensionless | Cake porosity |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Zeldovich (Thermal) NOₓ Formation Mechanism

**Premise:** At high in-cylinder temperatures, atmospheric N₂ and O₂ dissociate and recombine to form NO. This is the **rate-limiting step** in diesel NOₓ formation.

**Step 1: Identify the elementary reactions (Extended Zeldovich Mechanism)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(R1)} \quad N_2 + O \xrightleftharpoons[k_{-1}]{k_1} NO + N \\
&\text{(R2)} \quad N + O_2 \xrightleftharpoons[k_{-2}]{k_2} NO + O \\
&\text{(R3)} \quad N + OH \xrightleftharpoons[k_{-3}]{k_3} NO + H
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: State the assumptions**
- Steady-state for N atom concentration: d[N]/dt = 0
- [O] and [OH] assumed in partial equilibrium
- The rate-limiting step is the dissociation of N₂ (R1 forward), which has the highest activation energy.

**Step 3: Write the rate expression for [NO]**

The net rate of NO formation from R1 and R2 (ignoring R3 for simplicity) is:

$$
\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = k_1[O][N_2] - k_{-1}[NO][N] + k_2[N][O_2] - k_{-2}[NO][O]
$$

**Step 4: Apply steady-state for [N]**
Setting d[N]/dt = 0:

$$
k_1[O][N_2] = k_{-1}[NO][N] + k_2[N][O_2] - k_{-3}[N][OH]
$$

Solving for [N] (assuming R3 small):

$$
[N] = \frac{k_1[O][N_2]}{k_{-1}[NO] + k_2[O_2]}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute back into the rate equation**
After algebraic manipulation and simplification (using the partial equilibrium of O and O₂ from dissociation: O₂ ⇌ 2O, with K_p = [O]²/[O₂]):

$$
\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = 2 k_1 [O][N_2] \cdot \left( \frac{1 - \frac{[NO]^2}{K_c [O_2][N_2]}}{1 + \frac{k_{-1}[NO]}{k_2[O_2]}} \right)
$$

**Step 6: Identify the dominant forward rate**

In diesel combustion, [NO] is much below equilibrium, so the denominator term with [NO] in numerator is small. The rate reduces to:

$$
\frac{d[NO]}{dt} \approx 2 k_1 [O][N_2]
$$

**Step 7: Express the rate constant using Arrhenius equation**

$$
k_1 = A_1 \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)
$$

For the N₂ + O reaction: **A₁ ≈ 1.8 × 10⁸ m³/(mol·s)**, **E_a ≈ 319 kJ/mol**.

**Step 8: Use partial equilibrium for [O]**

From O₂ ⇌ 2O equilibrium:

$$
[O] = \sqrt{K_p(T) \cdot [O_2]}
$$

where $K_p(T) = A \exp(-E_{O_2}/RT)$ with E_O₂ ≈ 494 kJ/mol.

**Step 9: Final Zeldovich rate expression**

$$
\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = 2 A_1 [N_2] \sqrt{K_p [O_2]} \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)
$$

**Conclusion:** NOₓ formation is **exponentially sensitive** to temperature. Reducing peak flame temperature by even 100 K (via EGR) can cut NOₓ by 50–70%. This is the foundation for EGR-based NOₓ control in diesel engines.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: SCR Stoichiometric Urea Dosing Rate

**Premise:** Compute the AdBlue® injection rate required to reduce NOₓ in exhaust from concentration [NOₓ]_in to target [NOₓ]_out at a given exhaust flow rate.

**Step 1: NOₓ removal requirement (mass balance)**

$$
\dot{m}_{NO_x,removed} = \dot{V}_{exh} \cdot \left( [NO_x]_{in} - [NO_x]_{out} \right)
$$

where [NOₓ] is in kg/m³ and $\dot{V}_{exh}$ is in m³/s.

**Step 2: Apply the "Standard SCR" reaction stoichiometry**

$$
4 NH_3 + 4 NO + O_2 \rightarrow 4 N_2 + 6 H_2 O
$$

Stoichiometry: **1 mole NO requires 1 mole NH₃**.

**Step 3: NH₃ mass required**

$$
\dot{m}_{NH_3} = \frac{\dot{m}_{NO_x,removed}}{M_{NO_x}} \cdot M_{NH_3} = \dot{m}_{NO_x,removed} \cdot \frac{17}{30}
$$

where M_NO = 30 g/mol, M_NH₃ = 17 g/mol.

**Step 4: Convert NH₃ to Urea (AdBlue®) requirement**

Urea hydrolyses: (NH₂)₂CO + H₂O → 2NH₃ + CO₂.
Moles: **1 mole urea produces 2 moles NH₃.**
M_urea = 60 g/mol, M_NH₃ = 17 g/mol.

$$
\dot{m}_{urea,stoich} = \frac{\dot{m}_{NH_3}}{2} \cdot \frac{M_{urea}}{M_{NH_3}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{NH_3} \times 60}{2 \times 17} = 1.7647 \cdot \dot{m}_{NH_3}
$$

**Step 5: Account for AdBlue® is 32.5% urea by mass**

$$
\dot{m}_{AdBlue} = \frac{\dot{m}_{urea,stoich}}{0.325} = \frac{1.7647}{0.325} \cdot \dot{m}_{NH_3} \approx 5.43 \cdot \dot{m}_{NH_3}
$$

**Step 6: Real-world dosing (NSR — Normalized Stoichiometric Ratio)**

Actual dosing uses **NSR ≈ 1.0 to 1.1** (slightly more for margin, but not >1.05 to avoid NH₃ slip):

$$
\dot{m}_{AdBlue,actual} = NSR \cdot \dot{m}_{AdBlue,stoich}
$$

**Step 7: Compact final formula**

$$
\dot{m}_{AdBlue} = \frac{5.43 \times \dot{V}_{exh} \times [NO_x]_{in} \times \eta_{SCR} \times M_{NH_3}}{M_{NO_x}}
$$

**Numerical Example:** For a 2.0 L diesel engine, exhaust flow = 0.05 m³/s, [NOₓ]_in = 500 ppm = 0.0005 × 2.05 kg/m³ ≈ 1.025 × 10⁻³ kg/m³, target efficiency 90%:

$$
\dot{m}_{AdBlue} = 5.43 \times 0.05 \times (1.025 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.9 \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s} = 0.9 \text{ kg/h}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Diesel Pollutants vs. Control Technologies

| Pollutant | Formation Mechanism | In-Cylinder Control | After-Treatment | Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NOₓ** | Thermal Zeldovich at T > 1800 K | EGR, retarded injection, Miller cycle | SCR (with AdBlue), LNT | SCR: 80–95% |
| **PM (Soot)** | Fuel-rich diffusion flame, PAH nucleation | Common-rail high pressure, swirl, multi-injection | DPF (wall-flow), CRT | DPF: >99% |
| **CO** | Incomplete combustion in rich zones | Lean operation, better mixing | DOC | DOC: 70–90% |
| **HC** | Wall quench, crevice, over-mixing | Wall temperature control, low sac volume | DOC | DOC: 60–80% |
| **SO₂** | Sulfur in fuel oxidation | Use ULSD (< 10 ppm) | LNT (storage) | ULSD dependent |
| **CO₂** | Complete combustion (greenhouse) | Thermal efficiency, hybridization | None | Reduced via efficiency |
| **N₂O** | Side reaction in SCR/LNT | Catalyst formulation | Optimized dosing | LNT operation dependent |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem — BS-VI Compliance Check

**Problem:** A BS-VI compliant diesel car emits the following: CO = 350 mg/km, HC = 50 mg/km, NOₓ = 60 mg/km, PM = 3.0 mg/km. The DPF efficiency is 99% and SCR efficiency is 90%. Compute the **engine-out emissions** and verify compliance.

**Step 1: Engine-out PM (reverse DPF)**

$$
PM_{engine-out} = \frac{PM_{tailpipe}}{1 - \eta_{DPF}} = \frac{3.0}{1 - 0.99} = 300 \text{ mg/km}
$$

**Step 2: Engine-out NOₓ (reverse SCR)**

$$
NO_{x,engine-out} = \frac{NO_{x,tailpipe}}{1 - \eta_{SCR}} = \frac{60}{1 - 0.90} = 600 \text{ mg/km}
$$

**Step 3: CO and HC are not affected by DPF/SCR (DOC only)**

Assuming DOC efficiency of 80% for CO and 70% for HC:

$$
CO_{engine-out} = \frac{350}{1 - 0.80} = 1750 \text{ mg/km}
$$

$$
HC_{engine-out} = \frac{50}{1 - 0.70} \approx 167 \text{ mg/km}
$$

**Step 4: Verify compliance with BS-VI limits**

| Pollutant | Tailpipe | BS-VI Limit | Compliant? |
|---|---|---|---|
| CO | 350 mg/km | 500 mg/km | ✅ Yes |
| HC+NOₓ | 50 + 60 = 110 mg/km | 170 mg/km | ✅ Yes |
| NOₓ | 60 mg/km | 80 mg/km | ✅ Yes |
| PM | 3.0 mg/km | 4.5 mg/km | ✅ Yes |

**Conclusion:** The vehicle complies with BS-VI. Engine-out NOₓ is 600 mg/km, which is **7.5× higher** than the limit, justifying the need for SCR.

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Diesel After-Treatment System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Engine CI Diesel] --> TUR[EGR Cooler]
    TUR --> DOC[Diesel Oxidation Catalyst DOC]
    DOC --> DPF[Diesel Particulate Filter DPF]
    DPF --> SCR[Selective Catalytic Reduction SCR]
    SCR --> ASC[Ammonia Slip Catalyst ASC]
    ASC --> MUFF[Muffler]
    MUFF --> EXH[Tailpipe Exhaust]

    ADB[AdBlue Tank 32.5 Percent Urea] --> INJ[Urea Injector]
    INJ --> SCR

    ECU[Engine Control Unit ECU] -. Controls dosing .-> INJ
    ECU -. Controls EGR valve .-> TUR
    ECU -. Reads sensors .-> DOC
    ECU -. Reads pressure drop .-> DPF
    ECU -. Reads NOx sensor .-> SCR
```

### 4.2 DPF Wall-Flow Filtration Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INLET[Inlet Channel Plugged]
        EXG[Exhaust Gas In PM Loaded]
    end
    subgraph WALL[Porous Ceramic Wall SiC or Cordierite]
        FILT[Filtration through pores]
    end
    subgraph OUTLET[Outlet Channel Plugged]
        CLEAN[Clean Exhaust Gas Out]
    end
    SOOT[Soot Cake deposited on inlet channel walls]

    EXG --> FILT
    FILT --> CLEAN
    FILT -.-> SOOT
    SOOT -.-> REGEN[Regeneration Burn off at 600 to 650 C]
```

### 4.3 SCR Chemistry Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UREA[AdBlue Urea Solution 32.5 percent] --> SPRAY[Atomized Spray at Mixer]
    SPRAY --> THERM[Thermal Decomposition 200 C]
    THERM --> NH3[NH3 Released]
    NH3 --> STDSCR[Standard SCR Reaction]
    NO[NO from Exhaust] --> STDSCR
    NO2[NO2 from Exhaust] --> FASTSCR[Fast SCR Reaction Preferred]
    FASTSCR --> N2[Pure N2 Gas]
    STDSCR --> N2
    FASTSCR --> H2O[Water H2O]
    STDSCR --> H2O
    NH3 -. Excess .-> SLIP[NH3 Slip]

```

### 4.4 Diesel Pollutant Formation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FUEL[Diesel Fuel Injector] --> INJCYL[In-Cylinder Combustion]
    AIR[Charge Air Intake] --> INJCYL
    INJCYL --> COMB[Combustion Process]
    COMB --> NOX[NOx Formation T greater than 1800 K]
    COMB --> SOOT[Soot Formation in rich diffusion flame]
    COMB --> CO2[CO2 Complete Combustion]

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Explain the formation of NOₓ in a diesel engine using the Zeldovich mechanism."**

**Model Answer:**
NOₓ in diesel engines is predominantly formed through the **thermal (Zeldovich) mechanism**, which involves three elementary reactions between atmospheric nitrogen and oxygen at high temperatures (>1800 K):

$$N_2 + O \rightarrow NO + N \quad \text{(R1)}$$
$$N + O_2 \rightarrow NO + O \quad \text{(R2)}$$
$$N + OH \rightarrow NO + H \quad \text{(R3)}$$

The rate-limiting step is R1, which requires breaking the strong N≡N triple bond (energy ≈ 941 kJ/mol). The activation energy is **≈ 319 kJ/mol**, making the reaction **exponentially sensitive to temperature**. The net rate is:

$$\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = 2 A_1 [N_2] \sqrt{K_p [O_2]} \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

In a diesel engine, the peak flame temperature of ~2000–2500 K in fuel-rich diffusion flame zones drives significant NOₓ formation, which is then reduced by EGR and SCR in modern after-treatment systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "NOₓ forms because of high pressure." **NOₓ is fundamentally a high-TEMPERATURE phenomenon**, not high pressure. Pressure has only a minor effect. Don't lose 1 mark here.

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"Compare Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC) and Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF) with respect to their function, mechanism, and operating conditions."**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | DOC | DPF |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Oxidizes CO, HC, SOF to CO₂, H₂O | Physically traps soot/PM (>99%) |
| **Mechanism** | Catalytic oxidation (Pt/Pd) | Mechanical wall-flow filtration |
| **Target Pollutants** | CO, HC, SOF (gaseous) | Particulate Matter (solid) |
| **Operating Temperature** | 200–500°C (light-off ~250°C) | Continuous; regeneration 600–650°C |
| **Byproduct** | Heat, NO₂ (for CRT) | Soot cake → requires regen |
| **Maintenance** | None typically | Periodic regeneration (active/passive) |

DOC is the **upstream** device; DPF is **downstream**. The NO₂ generated by DOC actively oxidizes soot in the DPF via the **CRT (Continuously Regenerating Trap)** mechanism: $C + 2NO_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2NO$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often say "DPF oxidizes CO and HC." **DPF does NOT oxidize gases — it ONLY traps particulates.** CO and HC are handled by the DOC. Avoid this confusion; it costs 2 marks.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete exhaust after-treatment system for a BS-VI diesel vehicle. Discuss the role of each component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the standard SCR reaction mechanism and compute the AdBlue® dosing rate for a diesel engine with the following data: exhaust flow = 0.08 m³/s, NOₓ inlet = 600 ppm, target NOₓ outlet = 60 ppm, SCR efficiency = 90%, exhaust temperature = 350°C. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram of BS-VI Diesel After-Treatment System (7 Marks):**

The complete after-treatment chain in a modern BS-VI diesel vehicle consists of:
1. **EGR System (In-cylinder control):** Routes 5–30% of cooled exhaust gas back to the intake manifold, lowering peak combustion temperature from ~2000 K to ~1500 K, reducing thermal NOₓ formation.
2. **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC):** Placed immediately after the turbocharger. Contains Pt/Pd catalysts that oxidize CO → CO₂ and HC → H₂O/CO₂. Also produces NO₂ which assists DPF regeneration.
3. **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF):** Wall-flow ceramic (SiC/cordierite) honeycomb with alternately plugged channels. Filters PM with >99% efficiency. Requires periodic regeneration (passive via NO₂, or active via post-injection).
4. **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR):** Uses AdBlue® (32.5% urea) injected upstream. NOₓ + NH₃ → N₂ + H₂O on a Cu-zeolite or V₂O₅/TiO₂ catalyst. Efficiency 80–95%.
5. **Ammonia Slip Catalyst (ASC):** Final guard catalyst that oxidizes any excess NH₃ to N₂ to prevent toxic release.
6. **ECU + Sensors:** Closed-loop control via upstream/downstream NOₓ sensors, DPF ΔP sensor, exhaust temperature sensors.

**Order:** Engine → EGR → DOC → DPF → SCR → ASC → Tailpipe.

**(b) Derivation and Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Standard SCR Reaction**
$$4NH_3 + 4NO + O_2 \rightarrow 4N_2 + 6H_2O$$

**Step 2: Urea Decomposition**
$$(NH_2)_2CO + H_2O \rightarrow 2NH_3 + CO_2$$

**Step 3: Compute exhaust NOₓ mass flow**

Convert 600 ppm to kg/m³: $[NO_x] = 600 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{M_{NO_x}}{V_m} = 600 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{30}{22.4 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.804 \text{ kg/m³}$ (at STP; adjust for 350°C by factor T/273 ≈ 1.28 → ≈ 0.628 kg/m³ for 350°C)

For simplicity, use 0.804 kg/m³ at STP approximation.

**Step 4: Compute NOₓ to be removed (at 90% efficiency)**

$$
\dot{m}_{NO_x,removed} = 0.08 \times 0.804 \times 0.90 = 0.0579 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 5: Compute NH₃ required (1:1 mole ratio with NO)**

$$
\dot{m}_{NH_3} = 0.0579 \times \frac{17}{30} = 0.0328 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 6: Convert to Urea (1 mole urea → 2 moles NH₃)**

$$
\dot{m}_{urea} = 0.0328 \times \frac{60}{2 \times 17} = 0.0579 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 7: Convert to AdBlue® (32.5% urea solution)**

$$
\dot{m}_{AdBlue} = \frac{0.0579}{0.325} = 0.178 \text{ kg/s} \approx 0.18 \text{ kg/s} = 642 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Answer: AdBlue® dosing rate ≈ 0.18 kg/s (≈ 642 kg/h)**, with practical NSR = 1.0–1.1.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **regeneration methods** of a Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF). Compare passive, active, and forced regeneration strategies. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A diesel engine operates at compression ratio 18:1, λ = 1.5, with 2.0 L displacement and 2500 rpm. Calculate: (i) equivalence ratio φ, (ii) excess air factor in percentage, (iii) theoretical air required per kg of fuel, (iv) comment on combustion regime. Assume stoichiometric AFR for diesel = 14.5. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DPF Regeneration Methods (7 Marks):**

| Type | Trigger | Method | Temp | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Passive (CRT)** | Highway driving, low ΔP | DOC upstream oxidizes NO → NO₂; NO₂ oxidizes soot (C + 2NO₂ → CO₂ + 2NO) | 250–450°C | Continuous |
| **Active (Thermal)** | ΔP > threshold (~10 kPa) | ECU commands late post-injection; DOC oxidizes unburnt HC → exotherm → 600–650°C → soot burns with O₂ | 600–650°C | 5–15 min |
| **Active (Fuel-borne catalyst)** | Continuous | Cerium/iron additive in fuel reduces soot ignition T to 400°C | 400–500°C | Continuous |
| **Forced (Service)** | DPF fully loaded, fault code | Workshop burn-off using heat gun/oven | 650–700°C | Hours |
| **Active (Electrical)** | Modern (BMW, Mercedes) | Resistive coil heating element in DPF | 600°C | 5–10 min |

**CRT Mechanism (Detailed):**
- Upstream DOC: $2NO + O_2 \rightarrow 2NO_2$ (at 250–400°C)
- In DPF: $C + 2NO_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2NO$ (NO recycled)
- Net: Soot continuously oxidized at low temperature → **no fuel penalty**.

**Active Regeneration Sequence:**
1. ΔP sensor detects >10 kPa → ECU triggers regen.
2. Late post-injection of diesel into exhaust stroke.
3. DOC oxidizes HC → raises exhaust T to 600°C.
4. Soot cake ignites: $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$.
5. ΔP drops → regen ends; ECU returns to normal mode.

**(b) Calculations (7 Marks):**

Given: r = 18:1, λ = 1.5, V_d = 2.0 L, N = 2500 rpm, (A/F)_stoich = 14.5.

**(i) Equivalence Ratio φ:**
$$
\phi = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{1.5} = 0.667
$$

**(ii) Excess Air Factor:**
$$
\text{Excess Air \%} = (\lambda - 1) \times 100 = (1.5 - 1) \times 100 = 50\%
$$

**(iii) Theoretical Air per kg of Fuel:**
$$
(A/F)_{actual} = \lambda \times (A/F)_{stoich} = 1.5 \times 14.5 = 21.75 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}
$$

**(iv) Combustion Regime Comment:**
- φ = 0.667 < 1 → **lean combustion regime**.
- 50% excess air → low CO, low HC, **high NOₓ (due to high peak T) and moderate PM**.
- Compression ratio 18:1 → typical for modern CI engines (pre-DPF era was 22:1, reduced to limit PM and mechanical stress).
- This regime mandates **DPF + SCR** for BS-VI compliance.
- Indicated work: $W_i = p_{mi} V_d N / 2 = $ (calculable if p_mi given).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A & B:**
> 1. **Don't mix up NO and NO₂ in SCR!** NO₂ actually undergoes the **Fast SCR** ($2NH_3 + NO + NO_2 \rightarrow 2N_2 + 3H_2O$), which is faster than Standard SCR. Many students ignore NO₂.
> 2. **Don't confuse EGR and SCR.** EGR is an **engine modification** (in-cylinder); SCR is **exhaust after-treatment** (post-combustion). Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 3. **Always state operating temperatures** for catalysts — vague answers lose marks.
> 4. **For numerical problems, show units at every step** — a missing unit in the final answer = 1 mark deduction in KTU valuation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Diesel engine compression ratio:** 14:1 to 22:1 (modern BS-VI: ~16:1 to reduce PM and mechanical stress).
- [x] **Diesel operates LEAN:** λ > 1, φ < 1, AFR = 18–70.
- [x] **Diesel pollutant profile:** Low CO & HC; **High NOₓ and PM** (the two key pollutants).
- [x] **NOₓ formation** is via the **Zeldovich (thermal) mechanism** — exponential with temperature; reduces with EGR, SCR, LNT.
- [x] **PM formation** is via **PAH nucleation + coagulation** in fuel-rich diffusion flame zones; reduces with DPF (>99% efficiency).
- [x] **DOC:** Oxidizes CO, HC, SOF → CO₂, H₂O; also produces NO₂ for DPF passive regen.
- [x] **DPF:** Wall-flow ceramic filter; >99% PM capture; requires regeneration (passive via CRT / active via post-injection at 600–650°C).
- [x] **SCR:** Uses AdBlue® (32.5% urea); reduces NOₓ to N₂ + H₂O with 80–95% efficiency; Standard, Fast, and NO₂-SCR pathways.
- [x] **LNT/NSR:** Adsorbs NOₓ in lean, reduces in brief rich pulse; alternative to SCR for smaller vehicles.
- [x] **EGR:** In-cylinder technique; 5–30% exhaust recirculation reduces peak T → thermal NOₓ ↓ but PM ↑.
- [x] **BS-VI limits (Diesel):** CO = 500 mg/km, HC+NOₓ = 170 mg/km, NOₓ = 80 mg/km, PM = 4.5 mg/km.
- [x] **Engine-out NOₓ is 5–10× higher than tailpipe** — this gap is closed by SCR.
- [x] **AdBlue® dosing:** ~1.7647 g urea per g NH₃; ~5.43 g AdBlue per g NH₃ (32.5% solution).
- [x] **ULSD (Ultra-Low Sulfur Diesel):** Mandatory for advanced after-treatment; sulfur poisons DOC and SCR catalysts.
- [x] **Order of after-treatment devices:** Engine → EGR → DOC → DPF → SCR → ASC → Tailpipe.
- [x] **DPF cannot oxidize gases** — only DOC oxidizes CO/HC; DPF ONLY filters PM.
- [x] **Ammonia Slip Catalyst (ASC)** prevents toxic NH₃ release to atmosphere — must be the last device before tailpipe.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crankcase Emission and Control, Evaporative Emission and Control, Fuel Dispensing and Distribution Emissions and Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Crankcase Emission and Control, Evaporative Emission and Control, Fuel Dispensing and Distribution Emissions and Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Crankcase Emissions

### Formal Definition
Crankcase emissions are the unburned hydrocarbons (HC) and combustion blowby gases that escape past the piston rings into the engine crankcase during the combustion and compression strokes. In a four-stroke spark-ignition (SI) engine, the high pressure developed in the combustion chamber forces a portion of the combustion gases (containing unburned fuel, CO, NOₓ, and water vapor) past the piston rings into the crankcase. These gases are collectively termed **blowby emissions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Crankcase emissions were historically vented to the atmosphere through a road draft tube (positive crankcase ventilation or open system), releasing roughly **20–25% of total HC emissions** of an uncontrolled SI engine. They are now classified as a **Tier-1 evaporative-loss category** under all modern emission regulations (Euro, EPA, BS-VI).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Pressure Cooker Analogy
Imagine a pressure cooker with a slightly loose rubber gasket. Every time pressure builds up inside the cooker, a small amount of steam and odor escapes around the seal into the surrounding body of the cooker instead of being vented through the safety valve. Now imagine the body of the cooker (the crankcase) is full of hot oil (lubricating oil). The escaped steam mixes with the oil, contaminates it, and produces a foul smell.

Similarly, in an engine, high-pressure combustion gases (the "steam") leak past the piston rings (the "gasket") into the crankcase, mix with the lubricating oil, dilute it, and form harmful vapors. If left uncontrolled, these vapors are pushed out of the crankcase into the atmosphere — that is crankcase emission.

> [!NOTE]
> **Consequences of Uncontrolled Crankcase Emissions:**
> 1. Release of unburned hydrocarbons (HC) — direct air pollution
> 2. Dilution and contamination of lubricating oil
> 3. Formation of sludge and acids that corrode engine internals
> 4. Increased oil consumption
> 5. Reduced engine life

### System of Control — Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV)
The **Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV)** system is the standard control strategy. The blowby gases are drawn from the crankcase by engine manifold vacuum and routed back into the intake system to be re-burned in the combustion chamber. This is a **closed-loop** system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Standard:** Modern emission norms (Bharat Stage VI / Euro 6) treat crankcase emissions as **zero** at the tailpipe because of PCV — the gases are not vented to the atmosphere. The control efficiency of a PCV system is **~95–99%**.

---

## 1.2 Evaporative Emissions (EVAP)

### Formal Definition
Evaporative emissions are the volatile organic compounds (VOCs) released into the atmosphere from the fuel system of a motor vehicle through evaporation rather than combustion. Major sources include:
- **Fuel tank** (running losses, hot-soak losses, diurnal breathing losses)
- **Carburetor** (in older vehicles) or fuel injector lines
- **Fuel hoses, seals, and gaskets**
- **Charcoal canister** purge system (in modern systems)

### Conceptual Analogy — The Open Soda Bottle
Have you ever opened a warm bottle of soda and seen the vapors hiss out? The same thing happens in a fuel tank. Gasoline is a mixture of light hydrocarbons that easily vaporize, especially when:
- The sun heats the car (hot-soak / diurnal losses)
- The engine runs and heats surrounding components (running losses)

Without a control system, these vapors would leak straight into the atmosphere. The EVAP (Evaporative Emission Control) system acts like a **"vapor sponge"** — it traps the vapors in an activated charcoal canister when the engine is off and then sucks them into the engine for burning when the engine is running.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Key Terminology:**
> - **Diurnal Losses:** Vapor losses due to daily temperature swings (ambient temperature rise causes fuel to expand and vaporize).
> - **Hot-Soak Losses:** Vapor losses that occur after the engine is shut off (residual heat in the engine and exhaust system continues to heat the fuel system).
> - **Running Losses:** Vapor losses that occur while the engine is running (heat from the engine bay vaporizes fuel).

---

## 1.3 Fuel Dispensing and Distribution Emissions

### Formal Definition
Fuel dispensing and distribution emissions are the hydrocarbon (HC) vapors released during the **refueling of vehicles** and the **storage and transfer of gasoline** at terminals, bulk plants, service stations, and underground storage tanks. This includes:
- **Stage I controls:** Vapor recovery during fuel transfer from tanker to underground storage tank.
- **Stage II controls:** Vapor recovery at the vehicle fuel-tank filler neck during refueling.
- **Stage II onboard refueling vapor recovery (ORVR):** Vehicle-mounted carbon canister that captures vapors displaced during refueling.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital IV Bottle
When an IV bottle empties, air enters to replace the liquid — otherwise a vacuum forms and the flow stops. When you fill a fuel tank with gasoline, the air + fuel vapor inside the tank must go *somewhere*. If the gas station pump doesn't suck the displaced vapors back, they puff out into the atmosphere at the nozzle. Stage II vapor recovery is like attaching a second tube to the IV bottle that **sucks the displaced air back**, returning it to the system instead of polluting the room.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standards & Units:**
> - Refueling emissions were once a major source of urban HC pollution. The US EPA estimated that a single uncontrolled refueling could release **1–1.5 grams of HC** per gallon dispensed.
> - **Stage II** controls can reduce refueling emissions by **~95%**.
> - **ORVR (Onboard Refueling Vapor Recovery)** achieves equivalent or better control without requiring special nozzles at fuel stations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Crankcase Emissions — Origin and Mechanism

### 2.1.1 Mechanism of Blowby
The piston rings are designed to seal the combustion chamber from the crankcase. However, due to the **high cyclic pressure differential** between the combustion chamber (peak ~40–60 bar in SI engines) and the crankcase (~1 atm), a small fraction of gases leaks past the rings.

**Contributing factors to blowby:**
1. Ring flutter at high RPM
2. Wear and aging of ring/cylinder wall interface
3. Insufficient ring tension
4. Thermal expansion mismatches
5. Cylinder bore distortion
6. Low oil viscosity at high temperature (reduced hydrodynamic sealing)

### 2.1.2 Composition of Blowby Gases
| Component | Approx. Volume % |
|-----------|-----------------|
| Unburned Hydrocarbons (HC) | 70–85% |
| Carbon Monoxide (CO) | 3–5% |
| Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) | 5–10% |
| Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ) | 0.5–2% |
| Water Vapor (H₂O) | 5–8% |
| Particulate Matter (PM) | trace |

### 2.1.3 The PCV System — Operating Principle

The **Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV)** system uses the intake manifold vacuum (which is sub-atmospheric during engine operation due to throttle restriction) to draw blowby gases from the crankcase.

**PCV System Components:**
1. **PCV Valve** — A calibrated, spring-loaded check valve that regulates flow.
2. **Fresh Air Intake Hose** — Draws filtered fresh air into the crankcase.
3. **Crankcase Ventilation Hose** — Routes blowby gases to the intake manifold.
4. **Oil Separator** — Removes oil droplets before the gases reach the intake.

**PCV Valve Operation Modes:**

| Engine Condition | Manifold Vacuum | PCV Valve Status | Function |
|------------------|-----------------|------------------|----------|
| Idle / Deceleration | High vacuum | Partially open | Modest flow, prevents backfire |
| Part throttle | Medium vacuum | Open | Normal blowby evacuation |
| Wide open throttle (WOT) | Low vacuum | Fully open | Maximum flow |
| Backfire | Pressure pulse | Fully closed | Prevents flame propagation to crankcase |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** If the PCV valve sticks **open**, the engine runs lean (excess air drawn in), causing rough idle, hesitation, and increased NOₓ. If it sticks **closed**, crankcase pressure builds up, pushing oil past seals and gaskets (oil leaks) and increasing hydrocarbon emissions.

---

## 2.2 Evaporative Emissions — Detailed Sources

### 2.2.1 The Three Categories of Evaporative Losses

| Loss Type | Trigger | Temperature Range | Dominant Source |
|-----------|---------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Diurnal** | Daily ambient temperature swing (sun heating) | 0–40 °C | Fuel tank breathing |
| **Hot-Soak** | After engine shutdown, residual heat vaporizes fuel | 40–80 °C (engine bay) | Engine bay fuel lines, carburetor float bowl, fuel injector fuel rail |
| **Running Losses** | During engine operation | 60–100 °C | All heat-exposed fuel system components |

### 2.2.2 The EVAP System Architecture

**Components of a typical modern EVAP system:**

1. **Activated Carbon Canister**
   - Contains ~500 g to 2 kg of activated charcoal (high surface area ~1000 m²/g)
   - Adsorbs fuel vapors when engine is off
   - Stored vapors are purged into the engine via controlled purge flow

2. **Purge Solenoid Valve (Purge Control Valve)**
   - ECM-controlled duty cycle
   - Opens during closed-loop engine operation to draw vapors into intake

3. **Vent Valve (Canister Close Valve)**
   - Seals the canister from the atmosphere during certain conditions (e.g., leak detection)

4. **Fuel Tank Pressure Sensor**
   - Measures pressure in the fuel tank for EVAP leak detection

5. **Vapor Lines / Hoses**
   - Connect fuel tank → canister → intake manifold

### 2.2.3 EVAP System Operating Logic

> **Closed Engine / Cold Vehicle (Adsorption Phase):**
> - Engine off, fuel tank heats up (diurnal / hot-soak)
> - Vapors flow from fuel tank → into the activated carbon canister
> - HC molecules are trapped (adsorbed) in carbon pores
> - Vent to atmosphere is open to allow displacement of clean air
> - **No release to atmosphere**

> **Engine Running (Purge Phase):**
> - ECM opens the purge solenoid valve
> - Intake manifold vacuum pulls fresh air through the canister
> - Air flow **desorbs** the trapped HC from the carbon
> - HC-laden air is drawn into the engine and **burned** in the combustion chamber
> - Vapors are converted to CO₂ and H₂O

### 2.2.4 Adsorption Capacity — Engineering Numbers

The carbon canister's adsorption capacity depends on:
- Mass of activated carbon
- Carbon's specific surface area
- Fuel vapor composition (light hydrocarbons are adsorbed more easily)
- Temperature (lower temperature = more adsorption)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Canister Working Capacity:** A typical automotive canister holds **~50–100 grams of fuel vapor** at saturation, sufficient to absorb diurnal + hot-soak losses over a 24–48 hour parked period.

---

## 2.3 Fuel Dispensing and Distribution Emissions

### 2.3.1 The Refueling Process — Vapors Generated

When a fuel tank is filled, the liquid gasoline displaces the vapor space inside the tank. This creates a positive pressure inside the tank. If not captured, the **fuel-rich vapor** is pushed out through the filler neck into the atmosphere.

**Volume balance during refueling:**

$$V_{\text{vapor displaced}} = V_{\text{fuel dispensed}} \times \left(\frac{\rho_L}{\rho_V}\right)_{\text{effective}}$$

Where:
- $V_{\text{vapor displaced}}$ = volume of HC vapor released
- $V_{\text{fuel dispensed}}$ = volume of liquid gasoline dispensed
- $\rho_L$ = density of liquid gasoline (~ 0.74 kg/L)
- $\rho_V$ = effective density of vapor mixture

**Simplified Rule of Thumb (Engineering Approximation):**
> For every **1 liter of gasoline** dispensed, approximately **1 liter of HC vapor** is displaced from the tank.

### 2.3.2 Stage I vs Stage II vs ORVR — Detailed Comparison

| Stage | Location | Mechanism | Capture Point | Reduces Emissions by |
|-------|----------|-----------|---------------|----------------------|
| **Stage I** | Bulk plant → Underground storage tank | Vapor return line from storage tank to tanker truck | At terminal/service station filling | ~ 95% of transfer losses |
| **Stage II** | Service station → Vehicle fuel tank | Vapor return hose on dispenser nozzle | At vehicle filler neck | ~ 95% of refueling losses |
| **ORVR** | Vehicle fuel tank → Canister | Vehicle-mounted activated carbon canister, no special nozzle required | At vehicle tank | ~ 95–98% of refueling losses |

### 2.3.3 Onboard Refueling Vapor Recovery (ORVR) — Deep Dive

In ORVR systems, the fuel tank is connected (via a vapor line) to the **engine's activated carbon canister**. As fuel is dispensed:
1. Liquid enters the tank.
2. Pressure builds in the tank vapor space.
3. Vapor flows to the carbon canister.
4. HC is adsorbed in the carbon; clean air vents to atmosphere via the canister vent.
5. Stored HC is later purged into the engine during normal driving.

> [!NOTE]
> **ORVR vs Stage II — Why ORVR is preferred in modern regulations:**
> - ORVR does not require modified fuel dispensers (lower infrastructure cost).
> - ORVR is more reliable (vehicle-integrated).
> - ORVR can capture **running losses and diurnal losses** in addition to refueling losses.
> - ORVR is the standard required by EPA Tier 3 and is integrated with BS-VI vehicles.

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | Volume of vapor displaced during refueling | $V_{\text{vap}} \approx V_{\text{fuel}}$ (liters) | Refueling emissions estimate |
| 2 | Mass of HC emitted (uncontrolled) | $m_{HC} = \rho_V \times V_{\text{vap}} \times C_{HC}$ (grams) | Quantifying refueling loss |
| 3 | Emission reduction efficiency | $\eta = \left(1 - \dfrac{m_{\text{after}}}{m_{\text{before}}}\right) \times 100\,\%$ | PCV / EVAP / ORVR effectiveness |
| 4 | Canister adsorption capacity | $m_{ads} = k \cdot A_{spec} \cdot m_{carbon}$ (grams) | EVAP canister design |
| 5 | Blowby flow rate (empirical) | $\dot{m}_{blowby} \propto \Delta P \cdot n \cdot D_b$ | Crankcase ventilation design |
| 6 | Purge flow rate | $\dot{V}_{purge} = f(\text{duty cycle}, \Delta P_{manifold})$ | ECM-controlled purge |
| 7 | Diurnal temperature swing | $\Delta T_{ambient} = T_{max} - T_{min}$ (°C) | EVAP leak test limits |
| 8 | Canister working capacity | $C_w \approx 0.1 \times m_{carbon}$ (g fuel/g carbon) | EVAP sizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where $A_{spec}$** is the specific surface area of activated carbon (typically **~1000 m²/g**), $m_{carbon}$ is the mass of carbon in the canister, $D_b$ is the cylinder bore diameter, and $n$ is the engine speed (RPM). The proportionality $\dot{m}_{blowby} \propto n$ explains why blowby increases at high engine speeds.

---

## Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Crankcase Emissions:** Understanding blowby is critical for engine durability engineering. Excessive blowby is a leading indicator of engine wear and is monitored in on-board diagnostics (OBD-II) for diesel engines.

2. **EVAP System:** EVAP system failures (e.g., loose gas cap, cracked hose) are one of the **top 5 most common OBD-II fault codes** (P0440 series). The system doubles as a leak-detection system required by regulations.

3. **Fuel Dispensing:** The shift from Stage II (station-side) to ORVR (vehicle-side) represents a fundamental regulatory and engineering shift — moving pollution control from infrastructure to the vehicle, similar to the catalytic converter transition in the 1970s.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Refueling Vapor Volume Displaced

**Problem Statement:** A vehicle fuel tank has a total capacity of 50 L. It is filled from 10% to 90% of its capacity at a service station. Calculate:
(a) The volume of fuel dispensed.
(b) The volume of fuel vapor displaced from the tank.
(c) The mass of HC released to the atmosphere if uncontrolled, given the vapor mixture has an effective density of **4.5 g/L** and an HC concentration of **50% by mass**.

### Step 1: Compute Volume of Fuel Dispensed

$$V_{\text{fuel}} = V_{final} - V_{initial} = (0.90 \times 50) - (0.10 \times 50)$$

$$V_{\text{fuel}} = 45 - 5 = 40 \text{ L}$$

**Logic:** The tank was filled from 5 L (10% of 50 L) to 45 L (90% of 50 L). The total volume of liquid gasoline pumped into the tank is **40 L**.

### Step 2: Compute Volume of Vapor Displaced

$$V_{\text{vap}} = V_{\text{fuel}} = 40 \text{ L}$$

**Logic:** In an incompressible-liquid / gas-displacement model, every liter of liquid fuel that enters the tank displaces approximately one liter of vapor from the headspace of the tank. This is a volumetric balance (not a mass balance, because liquid and vapor have different densities).

$$V_{\text{vap}} = V_{\text{fuel}} = 40 \text{ L}$$

### Step 3: Compute Mass of Vapor Mixture Displaced

$$m_{\text{vap mix}} = \rho_V \times V_{\text{vap}}$$

$$m_{\text{vap mix}} = 4.5 \text{ g/L} \times 40 \text{ L}$$

$$m_{\text{vap mix}} = 180 \text{ g}$$

**Logic:** The displaced vapor has an effective density of 4.5 g/L (a mixture of air + fuel vapor + HC). Multiplying by 40 L gives 180 grams of total vapor mixture displaced.

### Step 4: Compute Mass of Hydrocarbon Released

$$m_{HC} = m_{\text{vap mix}} \times C_{HC} = 180 \text{ g} \times 0.50$$

$$m_{HC} = 90 \text{ g}$$

**Logic:** Half of the vapor mixture by mass is hydrocarbon (gasoline vapors). So uncontrolled refueling would release 90 grams of HC into the atmosphere per refueling event.

### Step 5: Emission Reduction with Stage II / ORVR

If the station uses Stage II vapor recovery with an efficiency of **95%**:

$$m_{HC,\text{controlled}} = m_{HC} \times (1 - \eta)$$

$$m_{HC,\text{controlled}} = 90 \text{ g} \times (1 - 0.95)$$

$$m_{HC,\text{controlled}} = 90 \text{ g} \times 0.05 = 4.5 \text{ g}$$

> **Conclusion:** An uncontrolled 40 L refueling releases **90 g of HC**. With Stage II / ORVR (95% efficient), this drops to **4.5 g** — a **20× reduction**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — PCV System Flow Rate Estimate

**Problem Statement:** A 4-cylinder, 2.0 L SI engine runs at 3000 RPM. The empirical blowby coefficient is **k = 1.2 × 10⁻⁵ L per liter of displacement per RPM**. Estimate the blowby flow rate that the PCV system must evacuate.

### Step 1: Identify the Empirical Model

$$\dot{V}_{blowby} = k \cdot V_d \cdot N$$

Where:
- $k$ = empirical blowby coefficient (L/L/RPM)
- $V_d$ = engine displacement (L)
- $N$ = engine speed (RPM)

### Step 2: Substitute Numerical Values

$$\dot{V}_{blowby} = (1.2 \times 10^{-5}) \times 2.0 \times 3000$$

Step-by-step:

$$\dot{V}_{blowby} = (1.2 \times 10^{-5}) \times (2.0 \times 3000)$$

$$2.0 \times 3000 = 6000$$

$$\dot{V}_{blowby} = (1.2 \times 10^{-5}) \times 6000$$

$$= 1.2 \times 6 \times 10^{-5+3-3}$$

$$= 7.2 \times 10^{-2} \text{ L/min}$$

Wait, let me recompute carefully:

$$1.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 6000 = 1.2 \times 6000 \times 10^{-5} = 7200 \times 10^{-5} = 0.072 \text{ L/min}$$

So:

$$\dot{V}_{blowby} = 0.072 \text{ L/min} = 72 \text{ mL/min}$$

### Step 3: Convert to Common Engineering Units

$$\dot{V}_{blowby} = 0.072 \text{ L/min} \times 60 \text{ min/hr} = 4.32 \text{ L/hr}$$

### Step 4: Convert to Volume per Day (Cold-Start / Idle-Operation Estimate)

If the engine runs for ~1 hour at this speed:

$$V_{\text{blowby,total}} = 4.32 \text{ L/hr} \times 1 \text{ hr} = 4.32 \text{ L}$$

**Logic:** Over an hour of operation at 3000 RPM, the engine produces ~4.32 L of blowby gases. The PCV system must evacuate this volume through the PCV valve back into the intake manifold.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Canister Sizing for EVAP System

**Problem Statement:** A vehicle must absorb diurnal + hot-soak losses over a 24-hour period. The fuel tank has a capacity of 50 L, and the expected total vapor generation is **~50 g of HC**. Design the carbon mass required if the canister's working capacity is **0.1 g fuel / g carbon**.

### Step 1: Apply the Canister Working Capacity Formula

$$m_{carbon} = \frac{m_{HC}}{C_w}$$

Where:
- $m_{HC}$ = total HC to be adsorbed (g)
- $C_w$ = canister working capacity (g HC / g carbon)

### Step 2: Substitute Numerical Values

$$m_{carbon} = \frac{50 \text{ g}}{0.1 \text{ g/g}}$$

$$m_{carbon} = 500 \text{ g}$$

### Step 3: Add a Design Safety Factor (1.5×)

$$m_{carbon,\text{design}} = 500 \text{ g} \times 1.5 = 750 \text{ g}$$

**Logic:** A 750 g activated carbon canister provides a 50% safety margin over the 24-hour HC generation, ensuring the canister does not reach breakthrough (saturation) prematurely.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Three Emission Categories

| Parameter | Crankcase Emissions | Evaporative Emissions | Fuel Dispensing Emissions |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Source** | Piston ring blowby | Fuel tank + lines + canister | Refueling + bulk transfer |
| **Primary Pollutant** | HC (unburned fuel) | HC (fuel vapor) | HC (fuel vapor) |
| **State of HC** | Hot gas (with CO, NOₓ) | Cold vapor (mostly light HC) | Vapor + liquid aerosol |
| **Control System** | PCV | EVAP (carbon canister) | Stage I, Stage II, ORVR |
| **Operating Mode** | Continuous (engine running) | Continuous (off & running) | Intermittent (refueling) |
| **Control Efficiency** | ~ 95–99% | ~ 95–99% | ~ 95% (Stage II/ORVR) |
| **Regulatory Status** | Near-zero at tailpipe (BS-VI) | Near-zero at tailpipe (BS-VI) | Captured on-vehicle (ORVR) |
| **OBD-II Detection** | Indirect (via oil consumption) | Direct (P0440 series) | Direct (refueling vapor code) |
| **Temperature Sensitivity** | Low | High (RVP of fuel) | High (RVP of fuel) |
| **Majority Composition** | HC 70–85%, CO₂ 5–10% | C₄–C₈ light hydrocarbons | C₄–C₈ light hydrocarbons |
| **Engine Off Contribution** | Zero (no blowby) | Major (diurnal + hot-soak) | Zero (refueling) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Crankcase and evaporative emissions are the two largest **"non-tailpipe"** sources of HC from a vehicle. Together they account for nearly **30–40% of total HC emissions** from a modern SI vehicle if uncontrolled. The PCV + EVAP + ORVR systems are mandated precisely to address this large non-tailpipe contribution.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PCV System — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Combustion Chamber\nHigh Pressure 40-60 bar] --> nodeB[Piston Ring Interface\nRing flutter and wear]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Crankcase\nBlowby accumulation]
    nodeD[Fresh Air Inlet\nFiltered ambient air] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeE[Oil Separator\nRemoves oil droplets]
    nodeE --> nodeF[PCV Valve\nSpring-loaded check valve]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Intake Manifold\nVacuum source]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Combustion Chamber\nRe-burn]
    nodeC -.->|if PCV fails| nodeI[Atmosphere\nHC pollution]
```

**Description:** The diagram traces the closed-loop path of crankcase blowby gases. Fresh air enters the crankcase, mixes with blowby, passes through the oil separator and PCV valve, and is drawn into the intake manifold for re-combustion. The dashed red line shows the failure mode (PCV stuck closed) that allows HC escape to the atmosphere.

---

## 4.2 EVAP System — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Fuel Tank\nLiquid gasoline] -->|Vapor generation| nodeB[Vapor Space\nin tank]
    nodeB -->|Diurnal losses| nodeC[Vapor Line]
    nodeB -->|Hot soak| nodeC
    nodeB -->|Running losses| nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD[Activated Carbon Canister\nHC adsorption]
    nodeD -->|Fresh air in| nodeE[Canister Vent Valve]
    nodeE -->|Purge air out| nodeF[Atmosphere]
    nodeD -->|Purge flow ECM controlled| nodeG[Purge Solenoid Valve]
    nodeG -->|Intake vacuum| nodeH[Intake Manifold]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Combustion Chamber\nHC burned]
    nodeJ[Fuel Tank Pressure Sensor\nLeak detection] -.->|Monitors| nodeB
```

**Description:** Two modes are shown — adsorption (vapors stored in canister when engine off) and purge (vapors drawn into engine when running). The fuel tank pressure sensor provides closed-loop monitoring for leak detection (OBD-II P0455/P0442 codes).

---

## 4.3 Refueling Vapor Recovery (ORVR) — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Service Station Pump\nDispenses liquid fuel] -->|Liquid gasoline| nodeB[Vehicle Fuel Tank]
    nodeB -->|Pressure builds| nodeC[Tank Vapor Space]
    nodeC -->|Vapor displaced| nodeD[Vapor Return Line]
    nodeD -->|Vapor to canister| nodeE[Activated Carbon Canister\nVehicle mounted]
    nodeE -->|HC adsorbed| nodeF[Clean air vent to atmosphere]
    nodeE -->|Purge to engine| nodeG[Intake Manifold]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Combustion Chamber\nVapor burned]
    nodeI[Liquid trap\nPrevents liquid fuel reaching canister] -.->|Protection| nodeE
```

**Description:** During refueling, displaced vapors are captured by the onboard carbon canister via the vapor return line. A liquid trap protects the canister from liquid fuel ingestion. The vapor is later purged into the engine during normal operation.

---

## 4.4 Stage I vs Stage II Vapor Recovery — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph StageI[Stage I - Terminal to Service Station]
        nodeA1[Refinery Terminal] -->|Liquid fuel| nodeB1[Service Station UST]
        nodeB1 -->|Vapor displaced| nodeC1[Vapor Return Line]
        nodeC1 -->|Vapor to tanker| nodeA1
    end
    
    subgraph StageII[Stage II - Service Station to Vehicle]
        nodeA2[Service Station UST] -->|Liquid fuel| nodeB2[Vehicle Fuel Tank]
        nodeB2 -->|Vapor displaced| nodeC2[Dispenser Vapor Hose]
        nodeC2 -->|Vapor to UST| nodeA2
    end
    
    subgraph ORVR[ORVR - Vehicle Onboard]
        nodeA3[Service Station Pump] -->|Liquid fuel| nodeB3[Vehicle Fuel Tank]
        nodeB3 -->|Vapor to canister| nodeC3[Vehicle Canister]
        nodeC3 -->|Clean air vent| nodeD3[Atmosphere]
    end
```

**Description:** Three sequential control strategies — Stage I (refinery to service station), Stage II (service station to vehicle, with special nozzle), and ORVR (vehicle-integrated, no special nozzle required). Modern regulations (EPA, BS-VI) favor ORVR for new vehicles.

---

## 4.5 Crankcase Emission — Historical Evolution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Open Crankcase\nVented via road draft tube\n1900s to 1960s] -->|Pre-1960s| nodeB[Major HC source\n20-25% of total emissions]
    nodeB -->|1960s-1970s| nodeC[PCV System Introduced\nClosed crankcase ventilation]
    nodeC -->|1970s onwards| nodeD[Closed PCV with calibrated valve\nModern vehicles]
    nodeD -->|2000s onwards| nodeE[OBD-II Monitoring\nBS-VI compliance]
    nodeE -->|Future| nodeF[Electric Vehicles\nNo crankcase emissions]
```

**Description:** Historical evolution showing how crankcase emissions transitioned from a major uncontrolled source to a fully controlled, near-zero category — and ultimately eliminated by the electrification of vehicles.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model Question]**
**Define crankcase emissions. Explain the working of the Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV) system with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Crankcase emissions are the unburned hydrocarbons and combustion gases (CO, NOₓ, water vapor) that leak past the piston rings into the engine crankcase during the combustion process. This phenomenon is called **blowby**.

**PCV System Working (2 Marks):** The PCV system uses intake manifold vacuum to draw blowby gases from the crankcase back into the combustion chamber. Fresh filtered air enters the crankcase through a breather hose, mixes with the blowby gases, and is then drawn through the PCV valve into the intake manifold, where the hydrocarbons are re-burned. The PCV valve is a calibrated, spring-loaded check valve that regulates flow based on manifold vacuum:
- High vacuum (idle): partially closed — limits flow
- Medium vacuum (cruise): open — normal flow
- Low vacuum (WOT): fully open — maximum flow

**Sketch:** Standard PCV diagram with crankcase, PCV valve, intake manifold, fresh air inlet.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — December 2022 Model Question]**
**What is an activated carbon canister? Explain its role in the EVAP system.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):** An activated carbon canister is a sealed container filled with **~500 g to 2 kg of activated charcoal** (with a specific surface area of ~1000 m²/g) that adsorbs and stores fuel vapors from the vehicle's fuel system.

**Role in EVAP (2 Marks):** The canister serves two functions:
1. **Adsorption Phase (engine off):** Fuel vapors from the tank (diurnal and hot-soak losses) flow into the canister, where the HC molecules are physically adsorbed onto the carbon surface, preventing atmospheric release.
2. **Purge Phase (engine running):** The ECM opens the purge solenoid valve, allowing intake manifold vacuum to draw fresh air through the canister. The air desorbs the stored HC, which is then drawn into the engine and burned in the combustion chamber. This converts potential pollutants (HC vapors) into CO₂ and H₂O.

The canister's working capacity is approximately **0.1 g fuel per gram of carbon**.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the working of the **Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV) system**. Discuss the construction and function of the PCV valve in different engine operating conditions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What are the various **sources of evaporative emissions** from a motor vehicle? With a block diagram, explain the working of a typical **EVAP system** in a modern passenger car. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Crankcase emissions originate from the leakage of combustion gases past the piston rings into the crankcase — a phenomenon called **blowby**. These gases contain unburned HC (70–85%), CO, NOₓ, CO₂, and water vapor. Without control, they constitute 20–25% of total vehicle HC emissions.

**PCV System Components and Working (3 Marks):**

The Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV) system consists of:
1. **Fresh air breather hose** — admits filtered ambient air into the crankcase
2. **PCV valve** — calibrated, spring-loaded, plunger-type check valve
3. **Vent hose** — connects crankcase to intake manifold
4. **Oil separator** — removes oil aerosols from the blowby gases

**Working:** During engine operation, the intake manifold develops a partial vacuum. This vacuum draws fresh air into the crankcase (through the breather hose). The fresh air sweeps the blowby gases toward the PCV valve. The PCV valve meters the flow, and the blowby gases are routed into the intake manifold where they mix with the air-fuel charge and are re-burned in the combustion chamber.

**PCV Valve Function in Different Modes (2 Marks):**

| Condition | Manifold Vacuum | PCV Valve Action | Purpose |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------|---------|
| Idle | High | Partially closed | Limits flow to prevent lean operation |
| Cruise / Part Throttle | Medium | Open | Normal blowby evacuation |
| Wide Open Throttle (WOT) | Low | Fully open | Maximum flow (high blowby rate) |
| Backfire | Pressure pulse | Fully closed | Prevents flame propagation to crankcase |

**Diagram (1 Mark):** Neat sketch showing combustion chamber → piston ring gap → crankcase → PCV valve → intake manifold → combustion chamber (closed loop).

---

### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Sources of Evaporative Emissions (2 Marks):**
1. **Fuel tank** — primary source of diurnal, hot-soak, and running losses
2. **Carburetor float bowl** (in older vehicles) — high vapor generation
3. **Fuel injector fuel rail** — heat from engine bay vaporizes fuel
4. **Fuel hoses and quick-connect fittings** — small permeation losses
5. **Charcoal canister vent** — when canister saturates

**Classification of Losses (1 Mark):**
- **Diurnal Losses** — daily ambient temperature cycle (0–40 °C) causes fuel to vaporize and expand
- **Hot-Soak Losses** — residual engine heat after shutdown continues to vaporize fuel for 1–2 hours
- **Running Losses** — during operation, engine bay heat (60–100 °C) vaporizes fuel

**EVAP System Block Diagram and Working (3 Marks):**

```
Fuel Tank → Vapor Line → Carbon Canister → Purge Valve → Intake Manifold
                                       ↓
                              Canister Vent to Atmosphere
```

**Adsorption Phase (engine off):**
- Fuel tank heats (sun or residual engine heat)
- Vapors flow from tank headspace to the carbon canister
- HC molecules adsorb onto activated carbon (specific surface area ~1000 m²/g)
- Fresh air enters the canister vent to displace the stored vapor volume
- **No atmospheric pollution**

**Purge Phase (engine running):**
- ECM activates the purge solenoid valve (duty cycle controlled)
- Intake manifold vacuum draws fresh air through the canister
- Air flow desorbs the trapped HC from the carbon
- HC-laden air enters the engine and is burned → CO₂ + H₂O
- **Effective conversion efficiency: ~95–99%**

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** Modern EVAP systems reduce evaporative HC emissions by ~95–99%, making them a near-zero category in BS-VI compliant vehicles.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — December 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** What is **blowby**? Explain the composition of blowby gases and the mechanism by which they are formed. Discuss how the PCV system controls crankcase emissions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **fuel dispensing emissions**. With neat diagrams, differentiate between **Stage I, Stage II, and ORVR** vapor recovery systems. Compare their relative effectiveness and applicability. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Definition of Blowby (1 Mark):**
Blowby is the leakage of high-pressure combustion gases from the combustion chamber past the piston rings into the engine crankcase. This occurs because the piston ring seal is imperfect, and the cyclic pressure differential (peak cylinder pressure 40–60 bar vs crankcase ~1 bar) forces gases through the ring-cylinder interface.

**Mechanism of Blowby Formation (2 Marks):**
1. During the combustion stroke, peak cylinder pressure reaches 40–60 bar.
2. The piston rings (compression ring, oil control ring) are designed to seal the chamber, but:
   - Ring flutter occurs at high RPM (rings momentarily lift off the bore).
   - Wear at the ring-bore interface creates micro-gaps.
   - Cylinder bore distortion (especially in 4-cylinder engines due to inherent secondary vibration) reduces sealing.
3. The high pressure drives combustion gases through these gaps into the crankcase.
4. Blowby rate increases with engine speed, wear, and lower oil viscosity.

**Composition of Blowby Gases (1 Mark):**

| Component | Volume % |
|-----------|----------|
| Unburned Hydrocarbons (HC) | 70–85% |
| CO | 3–5% |
| CO₂ | 5–10% |
| NOₓ | 0.5–2% |
| H₂O vapor | 5–8% |
| Particulate + Oil Mist | Trace |

**PCV System Control Mechanism (2 Marks):**
- Fresh filtered air enters the crankcase (breather hose).
- The intake manifold vacuum (sub-atmospheric due to throttle restriction) draws crankcase gases through the PCV valve.
- The PCV valve is a calibrated check valve that regulates flow based on engine load.
- Blowby gases are re-introduced into the intake manifold → combustion chamber → re-burned.
- Control efficiency: **95–99%** (effectively zero at tailpipe).

**Diagram (1 Mark):** Closed loop showing crankcase → PCV valve → intake → combustion → blowby → crankcase.

---

### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Fuel Dispensing Emissions — Concept (1 Mark):**
Fuel dispensing emissions are the HC vapors released into the atmosphere during the **refueling of vehicles** and the **storage/transfer of gasoline** at terminals, bulk plants, and service stations. A typical uncontrolled refueling releases ~1–1.5 g of HC per gallon of fuel dispensed.

**Stage I Vapor Recovery (2 Marks):**

```
Refinery Tanker ──(Liquid fuel)──> Underground Storage Tank (UST)
       ↑                                     │
```
