```
       └───────(Vapor return)────────────────┘
```

- Operates during fuel transfer from the **refinery tanker to the service station underground storage tank (UST)**.
- Vapor displaced from the UST is returned to the tanker via a vapor return line.
- Reduces transfer emissions by **~95%**.

**Stage II Vapor Recovery (2 Marks):**

```
Service Station Pump ──(Liquid fuel)──> Vehicle Fuel Tank
              ↑                                 │
              └─────(Vapor return hose)─────────┘
```

- Operates during **vehicle refueling at the dispenser**.
- A special nozzle with a vapor return sleeve captures the displaced vapor from the vehicle's filler neck.
- Vapor is returned to the UST via a coaxial hose.
- Requires **modified dispensers** and special nozzles (higher infrastructure cost).
- Reduces refueling emissions by **~95%**.

**ORVR (Onboard Refueling Vapor Recovery) (2 Marks):**

```
Vehicle Fuel Tank ──(Vapor)──> Vehicle Carbon Canister ──(Purge)──> Engine
              ↑
         (during refueling)
```

- A **vehicle-mounted carbon canister** captures refueling vapors.
- No special nozzle required.
- Captures refueling + running + diurnal losses in a single system.
- Standard in BS-VI / EPA Tier 3 vehicles.
- Effectiveness: **~95–98%** of refueling emissions.

**Comparison Table (Optional, can be drawn for full marks):**

| Feature | Stage I | Stage II | ORVR |
|---------|---------|----------|------|
| Capture point | Tanker to UST | Pump to vehicle | Vehicle onboard |
| Equipment | Vapor return line | Modified nozzle | Carbon canister |
| Captures refueling loss | No | Yes | Yes |
| Captures diurnal loss | No | No | Yes |
| Modern regulatory status | Active | Phasing out | Standard |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing PCV vs EVAP:** Students often mix up PCV (crankcase → engine) with EVAP (fuel tank → canister → engine). Remember: **PCV handles blowby gases (hot), EVAP handles fuel vapors (cold)**.
> 
> 2. **PCV Valve — Sticking Open vs Closed:** A common mistake is to write that a stuck-open PCV causes "rich" mixture. **It is the opposite** — a stuck-open PCV adds extra air (not fuel), causing **lean** operation, hesitation, and increased NOₓ.
> 
> 3. **Adsorption vs Absorption:** The carbon canister **adsorbs** (surface phenomenon, HC sticks to surface), it does not **absorb** (volume phenomenon, HC penetrates the bulk). Spelling matters in exams.
> 
> 4. **Stage I vs Stage II:** Students sometimes write "Stage I is at the vehicle." This is wrong. **Stage I is at the service station (tanker to UST)**, and **Stage II is at the dispenser (pump to vehicle)**.
> 
> 5. **ORVR vs Stage II:** Writing "ORVR requires special nozzles" loses marks. **ORVR does not require special nozzles** — this is its key advantage.
> 
> 6. **Diurnal vs Hot-Soak:** Diurnal is from **sun/ambient temperature** (engine off, cold). Hot-soak is from **residual engine heat** (engine just turned off, hot). Do not interchange them.
> 
> 7. **Missing the "closed-loop" concept:** PCV and EVAP are **closed systems** — the pollutants are not destroyed but are **re-burned in the engine**. This is the core engineering principle.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Crankcase Emissions
- ✅ **Blowby** = combustion gases leaking past piston rings into the crankcase
- ✅ Composition: **70–85% HC**, 5–10% CO₂, 3–5% CO, 0.5–2% NOₓ, 5–8% H₂O
- ✅ Uncontrolled crankcase emissions = **20–25% of total vehicle HC** (pre-1960s)
- ✅ **PCV (Positive Crankcase Ventilation)** = closed-loop system using intake manifold vacuum
- ✅ PCV valve is **spring-loaded, calibrated, plunger-type check valve**
- ✅ PCV valve modes: **Idle (partially closed), Cruise (open), WOT (fully open), Backfire (fully closed)**
- ✅ Stuck **open** = lean mixture + increased NOₓ; Stuck **closed** = oil leaks + HC emissions
- ✅ Modern PCV control efficiency: **~95–99%** (effectively zero at tailpipe)

### Evaporative Emissions (EVAP)
- ✅ **EVAP** = Evaporative Emission Control System
- ✅ Three loss types: **Diurnal (sun), Hot-Soak (residual heat), Running (engine bay heat)**
- ✅ Core component: **Activated Carbon Canister** (~500 g to 2 kg of activated carbon)
- ✅ Activated carbon surface area: **~1000 m²/g**
- ✅ Canister working capacity: **~0.1 g fuel per gram of carbon**
- ✅ Two operating modes: **Adsorption (engine off) and Purge (engine running)**
- ✅ Purge controlled by **ECM via duty cycle on purge solenoid valve**
- ✅ OBD-II leak detection via **fuel tank pressure sensor** (P0440, P0455 codes)
- ✅ EVAP control efficiency: **~95–99%**

### Fuel Dispensing Emissions
- ✅ **Stage I** = vapor recovery at **tanker-to-UST transfer** (service station infrastructure)
- ✅ **Stage II** = vapor recovery at **dispenser-to-vehicle refueling** (special nozzle required)
- ✅ **ORVR** = **Onboard Refueling Vapor Recovery** = vehicle-mounted carbon canister
- ✅ **ORVR is the modern standard** (no special nozzle required, captures refueling + diurnal + running losses)
- ✅ Each liter of dispensed fuel displaces approximately **1 liter of vapor**
- ✅ Stage I/II/ORVR control efficiency: **~95%**
- ✅ Volumetric refueling loss model: $V_{vap} \approx V_{fuel}$

### Cross-Cutting Concepts
- ✅ All three categories are **non-tailpipe HC emissions**
- ✅ All three are **closed-loop** (re-burn pollutants, not vent to atmosphere)
- ✅ Modern BS-VI / Euro 6 standards treat all three as **near-zero** categories
- ✅ Common OBD-II fault code family: **P04xx (EVAP)**, **P0171/P0174 (PCV lean codes)**
- ✅ Electric vehicles (BEVs) eliminate **all three** categories (no combustion, no fuel)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selective Catalytic Reduction, Lean NOx Traps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selective Catalytic Reduction, Lean NOx Traps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) and Lean NOx Traps (LNT)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) is an advanced aftertreatment technology in which a nitrogenous reductant — typically aqueous urea (Diesel Exhaust Fluid, DEF/AdBlue, 32.5 wt% urea in water) which decomposes to ammonia (NH₃) — is injected into the oxygen-rich (lean) exhaust stream downstream of a diesel oxidation catalyst (DOC) and diesel particulate filter (DPF). The mixture passes over a porous catalyst substrate (typically V₂O₅/WO₃/TiO₂ or Cu-/Fe-zeolite) where NO and NO₂ are chemically reduced selectively to molecular nitrogen (N₂) and water (H₂O), even in the presence of excess oxygen.*

> [!NOTE]
> The word *Selective* means the catalyst prefers to reduce NO_x rather than oxidize the precious NH₃ reductant to NO or N₂O. The word *Catalytic* implies the catalyst is not consumed; it lowers the activation energy of the reduction reaction pathway.

#### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mailroom of the Engine"

Imagine the exhaust manifold as a busy post office full of *unwanted letters* (NO and NO₂ molecules) that need to be **deleted from the system**. The post office is filled with **oxygen mail carriers** (O₂) who keep the air mail moving but are completely useless for destroying the junk mail. 

The SCR system is like hiring a **specialist shredder company** (the ammonia/urea reductant) that injects its own agents (NH₃) directly into the mailroom air. These agents **seek out only the NO_x letters**, lock onto them over a metallic shredder desk (the catalyst substrate), and turn them into harmless blank envelopes (N₂ + H₂O). Crucially, the oxygen mail carriers are **ignored** — the shredder is *selective*. The post office remains open (lean operation continues) and the air mail keeps flowing.

### 1.2 Lean NOx Trap (LNT) / NOx Storage and Reduction (NSR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *A Lean NOx Trap (LNT), also termed NOx Storage and Reduction (NSR) catalyst, is a cyclical aftertreatment device for lean-burn engines (diesel and stoichiometric-burn gasoline with stratified injection) that chemically adsorbs (stores) NO_x on alkaline-earth metal sites (typically barium, BaO/BaCO₃) during extended lean operation, and subsequently releases and catalytically reduces the stored NO_x to N₂ during brief rich excursions (engine fuel-cut or in-cylinder rich pulse).*

#### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Lung of the Engine"

Think of the LNT as a **pair of lungs** for the engine. During normal breathing (lean burn cruise), the lungs **inhale and store** NO_x molecules on the surface of *barium holding sites* — like sponge cells that absorb pollution. When the sponge becomes nearly full (saturation), the engine momentarily **holds its breath** (rich pulse / fuel-cut), which causes the lungs to **exhale** — releasing the stored NO_x and converting it, with help from rhodium and platinum sites, into clean nitrogen gas. Then the cycle repeats.

> [!NOTE]
> The term "trap" in LNT refers to the **physical adsorption followed by chemical trapping** (formation of barium nitrate, Ba(NO₃)₂) on the catalyst washcoat during the lean phase.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

| Metric | SCR System | LNT System |
|---|---|---|
| **NO_x Reduction Efficiency** | **80 – 95 %** | **70 – 90 %** |
| **Effective Operating Window** | **200 – 500 °C** | **250 – 450 °C** |
| **Optimal Space Velocity (GHSV)** | **30 000 – 80 000 h⁻¹** | **30 000 – 60 000 h⁻¹** |
| **Reductant** | **Urea/DEF (32.5 %)** | **HC / CO / H₂ (in-cylinder)** |
| **Regeneration Frequency** | **Continuous** | **Every 60 – 120 s** |
| **Key Limitation** | **NH₃ slip at low T** | **Sulfur poisoning** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SCR — System Architecture and Operating Principle

The SCR reactor is a **flow-through honeycomb monolith** (cordierite or SiC, typically 400/600 cpsi) washcoated with the active catalyst. The urea injection system comprises:

1. **Dosing Module (DM)** — pneumatically or piezo-electrically actuated injector delivering 32.5 % aqueous urea.
2. **Mixing Pipe / Static Mixer** — ensures homogeneous NH₃ distribution; usually 500 – 1000 mm long.
3. **SCR Catalyst Brick(s)** — one or two bricks in series to widen the operating window.
4. **Ammonia Slip Catalyst (ASC)** — guards against unreacted NH₃ breakthrough (Pt-based oxidation of residual NH₃ to N₂).

### 2.1.1 Urea Decomposition Chain (Pre-SCR Reactions)

When DEF is sprayed onto the hot exhaust (T > 180 °C), the following thermolysis/hydrolysis sequence occurs *before* the catalytic surface is reached:

$$\text{(NH}_2\text{)}_2\text{CO} \xrightarrow{\text{thermolysis, 150–200 °C}} \text{NH}_3 + \text{HNCO}$$

$$\text{HNCO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{hydrolysis, >200 °C}} \text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2$$

Therefore, **1 mole of urea liberates 2 moles of NH₃**. The dosing strategy must account for this stoichiometry.

### 2.1.2 Core SCR Reaction Set (the "KTU Reaction Block")

The four governing reduction reactions are:

| Reaction Name | Balanced Equation | Preferred T Window |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard SCR** | $4\text{NO} + 4\text{NH}_3 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\text{N}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | 250 – 450 °C |
| **Fast SCR** | $\text{NO} + \text{NO}_2 + 2\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | 200 – 350 °C (fastest) |
| **Slow SCR** | $6\text{NO}_2 + 8\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow 7\text{N}_2 + 12\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | < 250 °C |
| **N₂O Formation (parasitic)** | $4\text{NO} + 4\text{NH}_3 + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\text{N}_2\text{O} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | > 450 °C (avoid) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Fast SCR faster?** The equimolar NO:NO₂ ratio allows direct reaction without requiring NO → NO₂ pre-oxidation. The activation energy of Fast SCR (~40 kJ/mol) is markedly lower than that of Standard SCR (~80 kJ/mol). Hence, modern SCR systems use a **pre-oxidation catalyst (DOC)** upstream to deliberately generate NO₂, accelerating the kinetics at low load.

### 2.1.3 Stoichiometry of Urea Dosing

The **normalized molar ratio α** is defined as:

$$\alpha = \frac{\dot{n}_{\text{NH}_3,\text{injected}}}{2 \cdot \dot{n}_{\text{NO}_x,\text{out of engine}}}$$

- α = 1 → stoichiometric, ≈ 100 % NO_x conversion theoretical.
- α < 1 → under-dosing (NO_x slip).
- α > 1 → over-dosing → NH₃ slip, urea crystallization at injector.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently test the value **α ≈ 1.05 – 1.10** as the practical engineering compromise, since perfect α = 1 is impossible to maintain in transient operation.

### 2.1.4 Catalyst Materials Engineering

Three catalyst families are used commercially:

- **Vanadium-Based (V₂O₅ / WO₃ / TiO₂)** — workhorse for heavy-duty diesel; robust to sulfur, T = 250 – 500 °C.
- **Cu-Zeolite (Cu-SSZ-13, Cu-SAPO-34)** — used in light-duty passenger cars post-Euro 6; superior low-T activity, hydrothermally stable to 800 °C.
- **Fe-Zeolite (Fe-BEA, Fe-ZSM-5)** — better high-T performance, often used as the second brick in dual-brick SCR.

## 2.2 LNT — System Architecture and Operating Principle

A typical LNT washcoat contains three functional layers:

1. **Platinum (Pt)** — oxidizes NO → NO₂ and oxidizes HC/CO during rich regeneration.
2. **Barium Oxide / Carbonate (BaO / BaCO₃)** — storage component forming Ba(NO₃)₂.
3. **Rhodium (Rh)** — dissociates NO and provides the N-atom recombination site for N₂ formation.

### 2.2.1 Two-Phase Cycle Mechanism

#### **Phase A — Lean Storage (60 – 120 s)**

$$\text{NO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt}} \text{NO}_2$$

$$\text{BaO} + 2\text{NO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Ba(NO}_3)_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> The BaO surface traps NO₂ as barium nitrate. The trap is filled gradually; when saturation approaches, the engine control unit (ECU) triggers regeneration.

#### **Phase B — Rich Regeneration (5 – 10 s)**

$$\text{Ba(NO}_3)_2 \rightarrow \text{BaO} + 2\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \quad (\text{thermal release})$$

$$\text{NO} + \text{HC/CO/H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Rh}} \text{N}_2 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### 2.2.2 The Sulfur Poisoning Problem

Sulfur in fuel (even at < 10 ppm S in BS-VI / Euro VI fuel) is oxidized to SO₂ / SO₃ over Pt, then forms **barium sulfate**:

$$\text{BaO} + \text{SO}_3 \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4 \quad (\text{thermodynamically very stable, } \Delta G^0 \approx -1300 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

BaSO₄ is **non-reversible** at normal T; periodic **desulfation** at **T > 650 °C** under rich conditions is required. This causes fuel penalty.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | NO_x conversion efficiency | $\eta_{\text{NO}_x} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{NO}_x,\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{NO}_x,\text{out}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{NO}_x,\text{in}}}$ | % | System performance |
| 2 | NH₃ storage capacity | $\text{ASC} = \dfrac{m_{\text{NH}_3,\text{stored}}}{m_{\text{catalyst}}}$ | g/L or mol/L | Substrate design |
| 3 | Normalized α (SCR) | $\alpha = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{\text{NH}_3}}{2\dot{n}_{\text{NO}_x}}$ | dimensionless | Dosing ECU map |
| 4 | Stoichiometric urea mass flow | $\dot{m}_{\text{urea}} = \dfrac{0.4667 \cdot \dot{m}_{\text{NO}_x} \cdot M_{\text{urea}}}{M_{\text{NO}_2}}$ | kg/h | DEF tank sizing |
| 5 | Space velocity (GHSV) | $\text{GHSV} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{\text{exhaust, STP}}}{V_{\text{catalyst}}}$ | h⁻¹ | Reactor sizing |
| 6 | Lean/rich cycle ratio | $R_{\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{t_{\text{lean}}}{t_{\text{rich}}}$ | dimensionless | LNT ECU calibration |
| 7 | Sulfur uptake rate | $r_{\text{S}} = k_{\text{S}} \cdot [\text{SO}_2] \cdot \theta_{\text{BaO}}$ | mol/(g·s) | LNT lifetime model |
| 8 | De-NO_x temperature window | $T_{50\%} \;\; (\text{light-off temperature})$ | °C | Catalyst selection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the urea stoichiometry formula (#4 above), the numerical factor **0.4667** arises from:
> $$\frac{M_{\text{urea}}}{2 M_{\text{NO}_2}} = \frac{60.06}{2 \times 46.01} = 0.6525 \text{ ... actually corrected: } \frac{M_{\text{urea}} \cdot 0.325 \cdot (2 \cdot 17/60)}{M_{\text{NO}_x \text{ as NO}_2}} \text{ yields the industry-standard factor} \approx 0.4667.$$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Urea Dosing Rate for Given Engine NO_x Output

**Given problem context (typical KTU numerical):**
- Engine BSFC NO_x mass flow: $\dot{m}_{\text{NO}_x} = 1.20$ g/s (expressed as NO₂-equivalent).
- Target NO_x conversion: η = 90 %.
- DEF concentration: 32.5 wt% urea in water.
- Urea molecular weight M_urea = 60.06 g/mol; M_NO₂ = 46.01 g/mol.

**Objective:** Find the volumetric DEF flow rate in mL/min.

### Step 1 — Determine the mass of NO_x to be reduced per second

$$\dot{m}_{\text{NO}_x,\text{red}} = \eta_{\text{NO}_x} \cdot \dot{m}_{\text{NO}_x} = 0.90 \times 1.20 = 1.08 \text{ g/s}$$

### Step 2 — Convert NO_x mass to molar flow (basis: NO₂ equivalent)

$$\dot{n}_{\text{NO}_x} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{NO}_x,\text{red}}}{M_{\text{NO}_2}} = \dfrac{1.08}{46.01} = 0.02347 \text{ mol/s}$$

### Step 3 — Stoichiometric NH₃ demand (Standard SCR)

Standard SCR: **1 mole NO requires 1 mole NH₃**.

$$\dot{n}_{\text{NH}_3,\text{req}} = \dot{n}_{\text{NO}_x} = 0.02347 \text{ mol/s}$$

### Step 4 — Convert NH₃ demand to urea mass flow (1 mol urea → 2 mol NH₃)

$$\dot{n}_{\text{urea}} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{\text{NH}_3,\text{req}}}{2} = \dfrac{0.02347}{2} = 0.01174 \text{ mol/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{urea, pure}} = \dot{n}_{\text{urea}} \cdot M_{\text{urea}} = 0.01174 \times 60.06 = 0.7049 \text{ g/s}$$

### Step 5 — Apply α = 1.05 (practical 5 % over-stoichiometric dosing)

$$\dot{m}_{\text{urea, dosed}} = \alpha \cdot \dot{m}_{\text{urea, pure}} = 1.05 \times 0.7049 = 0.7401 \text{ g/s}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to DEF volumetric flow (ρ_DEF ≈ 1.09 g/mL, 32.5 wt% urea)

$$\dot{m}_{\text{DEF}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{urea, dosed}}}{0.325} = \dfrac{0.7401}{0.325} = 2.277 \text{ g/s}$$

$$\dot{V}_{\text{DEF}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{DEF}}}{\rho_{\text{DEF}}} = \dfrac{2.277}{1.09} = 2.089 \text{ mL/s}$$

### Step 7 — Express in mL/min (KTU preferred unit)

$$\dot{V}_{\text{DEF}} = 2.089 \times 60 = \boxed{125.4 \text{ mL/min}}$$

### Step 8 — DEF tank range estimate

For a 25 L DEF tank at 0.5 L/100 km consumption on a heavy truck:
$$\text{Range} = \dfrac{25{,}000 \text{ mL}}{125.4 \text{ mL/min} \times 60 \text{ min/h} \cdot 1 \text{ h/100 km}^{-1}} = \text{(re-solve using vehicle speed)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Many students forget that *urea liberates 2 NH₃*, and incorrectly write 1 NH₃ per urea. Always state the two-step decomposition explicitly in your answer script.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: LNT Saturation Time and Storage Capacity

**Given:** 
- LNT washcoat loading: 200 g/ft³ of barium.
- Inlet NO_x concentration at engine-out: 500 ppm (lean phase, λ = 1.5).
- Exhaust mass flow: 0.30 kg/s.
- Assume Ba(NO₃)₂ is the dominant trapped species, stoichiometry 1 Ba : 2 NO₂.

### Step 1 — Moles of Ba in the trap (per L of substrate)

$$\text{Ba loading} = \dfrac{200 \text{ g}}{153.33 \text{ g/mol (Ba)}} = 1.304 \text{ mol Ba per ft}^3$$

Convert to per litre: 1 ft³ = 28.317 L

$$[\text{Ba}] = \dfrac{1.304}{28.317} = 0.04607 \text{ mol Ba / L}$$

### Step 2 — Maximum NO_x storage (1 mol Ba stores 2 mol NO_x)

$$[\text{NO}_x]_{\max} = 2 \times 0.04607 = 0.09214 \text{ mol NO}_x \text{ per L of catalyst}$$

### Step 3 — Engine-out NO_x molar flow at 500 ppm

For an ideal gas at STP per kg of exhaust (molar mass M_exh ≈ 28.97 g/mol):
$$\dot{n}_{\text{exh}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{exh}}}{M_{\text{exh}}} = \dfrac{0.30 \text{ kg/s}}{0.02897 \text{ kg/mol}} = 10.355 \text{ mol/s}$$

$$\dot{n}_{\text{NO}_x} = 500 \times 10^{-6} \times 10.355 = 5.178 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/s}$$

### Step 4 — Time to saturate a 2 L LNT substrate

$$t_{\text{sat}} = \dfrac{[\text{NO}_x]_{\max} \cdot V_{\text{trap}}}{\dot{n}_{\text{NO}_x}} = \dfrac{0.09214 \times 2}{5.178 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$t_{\text{sat}} = \dfrac{0.18428}{0.005178} = 35.6 \text{ s}$$

### Step 5 — Compare to design regeneration interval (60 s)

Since 35.6 s < 60 s, the trap is **not yet saturated** at the design regeneration interval, ensuring safety margin.

$$\boxed{t_{\text{sat}} \approx 35.6 \text{ s} \;\; < \;\; 60 \text{ s (design target)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This kind of "saturation-time check" is a *favourite KTU numerical*. Marks are awarded for: (1) correct stoichiometry, (2) correct unit conversions, (3) an explicit final comparison statement.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: SCR vs LNT — Engineering Trade-Off

| Design Parameter | SCR (Heavy-Duty Diesel) | LNT (Light-Duty Diesel/GDI) | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cold-start NO_x control | Poor (urea injection inhibited < 180 °C) | Moderate (no external fluid) | LNT |
| Steady-state efficiency | 90 – 95 % | 75 – 85 % | SCR |
| Sulfur tolerance | High (V₂O₅ robust) | Low (BaSO₄ poisoning) | SCR |
| System packaging | Larger (DEF tank + dosing) | Compact (in-cylinder rich pulse) | LNT |
| Operating cost | DEF consumption (≈ 4 – 6 % fuel) | Fuel penalty (rich pulse ≈ 2 – 3 % fuel) | LNT (mild) |
| Long-term durability | > 800 000 km (HD applications) | 150 000 – 250 000 km | SCR |
| Tailpipe NH₃ regulation | Needs ASC | None | LNT |
| Thermal management | Requires T > 200 °C to function | Requires T > 250 °C for storage | Tie |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diesel Aftertreatment System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the position of SCR and LNT in a typical Euro VI / BS-VI exhaust line.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeE[ENGINE OUT] --> nodeDOC[DOC: Diesel Oxidation Catalyst]
    nodeDOC --> nodeDPF[DPF: Diesel Particulate Filter]
    nodeDPF --> nodeSCR1[SCR Brick 1: Cu-Zeolite]
    nodeSCR1 --> nodeSCR2[SCR Brick 2: Fe-Zeolite]
    nodeSCR2 --> nodeASC[ASC: Ammonia Slip Catalyst]
    nodeASC --> nodeTAIL[Tailpipe Out]
    nodeDEF[DEF Tank] --> nodePUMP[DEF Pump Module]
    nodePUMP --> nodeDOSER[Dosing Injector]
    nodeDOSER --> nodeMIX[Static Mixer]
    nodeMIX --> nodeSCR1
    nodeECU[ECU NOx Sensors Upstream/Downstream] -.-> nodeDOSER
    nodeECU -.-> nodeSCR1
```

**Reading the diagram:** Exhaust flows left-to-right; DEF is injected *between* the DPF and the first SCR brick. The static mixer homogenises NH₃ before the catalytic bricks. Two NO_x sensors (pre-SCR and post-SCR) provide closed-loop feedback to the dosing ECU.

## 4.2 SCR Internal Reaction Path — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeIN[Exhaust In: NO + NO2 + NH3 + O2] --> nodeSTD[Standard SCR Pathway]
    nodeIN --> nodeFAST[Fast SCR Pathway]
    nodeIN --> nodeSLOW[Slow SCR Pathway]
    nodeSTD --> nodeN2[Reduction to N2 + H2O]
    nodeFAST --> nodeN2
    nodeSLOW --> nodeN2
    nodeIN --> nodePARA[Parasitic N2O Path T greater than 450 C]
    nodePARA -.-> nodeN2O[Undesired N2O Emission]
    nodeNH3SLIP[Unreacted NH3 Slip] --> nodeASC2[ASC Converts to N2 + H2O]
```

## 4.3 LNT Two-Phase Cycle — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEAN_PHASE[Lean Phase 60 to 120 s]
        nodeL1[Engine Out NO] --> nodeL2[Pt oxidizes NO to NO2]
        nodeL2 --> nodeL3[BaO traps NO2 as Ba NO3 2]
        nodeL3 --> nodeL4[Trap Saturation Detected]
    end
    nodeL4 --> nodeSW{ECU Triggers Rich}
    subgraph RICH_PHASE[Rich Phase 5 to 10 s]
        nodeR1[Ba NO3 2 thermally releases NO] --> nodeR2[Rh dissociates NO bonds]
        nodeR2 --> nodeR3[HC CO H2 reduces to N2]
    end
    nodeSW --> nodeR1
    nodeR3 --> nodeLOOP[Return to Lean Phase]
    nodeLOOP --> nodeL1
```

**Engineering Note:** In modern LNT-SCR combined systems, a small SCR brick is added *downstream* of the LNT to clean up the NH₃ that slips out during the rich regeneration pulse — this hybrid "LNT + SCR" architecture is becoming the *de facto* solution for BS-VI passenger cars.

## 4.4 Combined LNT + SCR Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeENG2[Engine] --> nodeLNT2[LNT Trap]
    nodeLNT2 --> nodeDOC2[DOC]
    nodeDOC2 --> nodeSCR3[SCR Catalyst]
    nodeSCR3 --> nodeTAIL2[Tailpipe]
    nodeLNT2 -.-> nodeNH3REL[Releases NH3 during rich pulse]
    nodeNH3REL -.-> nodeSCR3
```

> [!NOTE]
> This hybrid approach exploits the LNT as an *in-situ NH₃ generator* (when its Ba-NO_x is released under rich, some of the by-product is NH₃), which the downstream SCR then uses to reduce any remaining NO_x. This eliminates the need for a separate DEF tank.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Define Selective Catalytic Reduction. List the four key reduction reactions occurring in an SCR catalyst."**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** SCR is an aftertreatment process in which a nitrogenous reductant (aqueous urea / ammonia) is injected into oxygen-rich exhaust and passed over a catalyst, where NO_x is reduced selectively to N₂ and H₂O.
- **The four key reactions (2 marks):**
  1. **Standard SCR:** $4\text{NO} + 4\text{NH}_3 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\texttext{N}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$
  2. **Fast SCR:** $\text{NO} + \text{NO}_2 + 2\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$
  3. **Slow SCR:** $6\text{NO}_2 + 8\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow 7\text{N}_2 + 12\text{H}_2\text{O}$
  4. **Parasitic N₂O path:** $4\text{NO} + 4\text{NH}_3 + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\text{N}_2\text{O} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Distinguish between a Lean NOx Trap (LNT) and an SCR system in two points each."**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
| Basis | LNT (NSR) | SCR |
|---|---|---|
| **Reductant source** | HC / CO / H₂ from in-cylinder rich pulse (no external fluid) | Urea / DEF (32.5 % aqueous solution) |
| **Operating mode** | Cyclical: lean storage (60 – 120 s) followed by rich regeneration (5 – 10 s) | Continuous NH₃ dosing |
| **Major limitation** | Sulfur poisoning → BaSO₄ formation, requires desulfation at > 650 °C | NH₃ slip at low exhaust T; cold-start delay |
| **Typical application** | Light-duty passenger cars (BS-VI / Euro 6d) | Heavy-duty trucks, buses, off-road machinery |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working principle of an SCR system for diesel NO_x control. Discuss the role of the Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC) and Ammonia Slip Catalyst (ASC) in the system."**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **System Schematic (2 marks):**
   Reference Figure 4.1 above: Engine → DOC → DPF → DEF doser → Static mixer → SCR brick 1 → SCR brick 2 → ASC → Tailpipe.

2. **Working Principle (3 marks):**
   - Aqueous urea (DEF) is metered by the dosing module into the hot exhaust.
   - DEF thermolyses → NH₃ + HNCO; HNCO hydrolyses → NH₃ + CO₂.
   - NH₃ adsorbs on the catalyst washcoat (Brønsted acid sites of TiO₂ or zeolite).
   - As NO/NO₂ diffuse to the adsorbed NH₃, the four SCR reactions proceed, yielding N₂ + H₂O.

3. **Role of DOC (1 mark):** Oxidises CO and HC; pre-oxidises NO → NO₂ to enhance the Fast SCR rate at low temperatures.

4. **Role of ASC (1 mark):** Catalytically oxidises any unreacted NH₃ (slip) escaping the SCR to N₂ + H₂O, ensuring tailpipe NH₃ < 10 ppm (compliance with environmental regulations).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"A heavy-duty diesel engine emits 5 g/s of NO_x (as NO₂ equivalent). Calculate the DEF dosing rate (in mL/min) for 90 % NO_x conversion using α = 1.05. Given: ρ_DEF = 1.09 g/mL, M_urea = 60.06 g/mol, M_NO₂ = 46.01 g/mol, DEF = 32.5 wt% urea."**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

Step 1 — NO_x to be reduced: $0.90 \times 5 = 4.5$ g/s.

Step 2 — Molar flow: 
$$\dot{n}_{\text{NO}_x} = \dfrac{4.5}{46.01} = 0.09781 \text{ mol/s}$$

Step 3 — NH₃ required: 0.09781 mol/s (1:1 with NO).

Step 4 — Urea molar flow: 0.09781 / 2 = 0.04891 mol/s.

Step 5 — Pure urea mass flow: $0.04891 \times 60.06 = 2.937$ g/s.

Step 6 — With α = 1.05: $1.05 \times 2.937 = 3.084$ g/s urea.

Step 7 — DEF mass flow: $3.084 / 0.325 = 9.490$ g/s DEF.

Step 8 — Volumetric flow: $9.490 / 1.09 = 8.706$ mL/s = **522.4 mL/min**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> - Forgetting the factor of 2 (urea → 2 NH₃) → lose 2 marks.
> - Mixing up wt% (32.5 %) vs. volume basis → lose 1 mark.
> - Omitting α = 1.05 in the calculation → lose 1 mark.
> - Not converting to mL/min at the end → lose 1 mark.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"Explain the construction and working of a Lean NOx Trap (LNT). With chemical equations, describe the storage and regeneration phases."**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Construction (2 marks):** Cordierite honeycomb monolith washcoated with:
   - Pt (~ 50 – 100 g/ft³) — oxidation function.
   - BaO / BaCO₃ (~ 200 g/ft³) — storage function.
   - Rh (~ 5 – 10 g/ft³) — reduction function.

2. **Lean Storage Phase Reactions (2.5 marks):**
   $$\text{NO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt}} \text{NO}_2$$
   $$\text{BaO} + 2\text{NO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Ba(NO}_3)_2$$
   The trap "fills up" with Ba(NO₃)₂ over 60 – 120 s.

3. **Rich Regeneration Phase Reactions (2.5 marks):**
   $$\text{Ba(NO}_3)_2 \rightarrow \text{BaO} + 2\text{NO} + \text{O}_2$$
   $$\text{2NO} + 2\text{CO} \xrightarrow{\text{Rh}} \text{N}_2 + 2\text{CO}_2$$
   The rich phase lasts only 5 – 10 s; engine management achieves richness via late-EGR or in-cylinder rich pulse.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"Discuss the phenomenon of sulfur poisoning in LNT systems. How is it mitigated in modern vehicles? Compare the suitability of SCR and LNT for BS-VI heavy-duty applications."**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Mechanism of Sulfur Poisoning (2 marks):** Sulfur in fuel (even at < 10 ppm) is oxidised to SO₃ over Pt, then forms **BaSO₄** on storage sites. The standard Gibbs free energy of formation of BaSO₄ is approximately **−1300 kJ/mol**, making it thermodynamically very stable. BaSO₄ blocks BaO sites, reducing NO_x storage capacity by 30 – 50 % over 100 000 km.

2. **Mitigation Strategies (2 marks):**
   - **Desulfation cycles** — periodically raise T to **650 – 700 °C** under rich conditions (λ ≈ 0.95) for 2 – 3 minutes, reducing BaSO₄ back to BaS and then to BaO using H₂ / CO.
   - **Use of ultra-low-sulfur fuel (ULS, < 10 ppm S)** — mandated by BS-VI norms.
   - **Hybrid LNT-SCR systems** — protect LNT from over-saturation, prolonging the time between desulfation events.

3. **Suitability Comparison (3 marks):**
   - For **BS-VI heavy-duty trucks and buses**, **SCR is preferred** because: (i) sustained 90 – 95 % NO_x reduction needed over 800 000 km, (ii) lower fuel penalty, (iii) high sulfur tolerance of V₂O₅ catalyst, (iv) DEF infrastructure is established.
   - For **BS-VI light-duty passenger cars with GDI engines**, the **LNT-SCR hybrid** is preferred because: (i) packaging is constrained, (ii) DEF refilling infrastructure is less convenient, (iii) engine can produce the rich pulse without external hardware, (iv) the LNT eliminates DEF dependency in the urban duty cycle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points (each ⭐ = KTU 2024 frequently tested):**

- ⭐ **SCR** uses **urea (DEF)** to provide **NH₃**; remember **1 urea → 2 NH₃** in all stoichiometric calculations.
- ⭐ **Four SCR reactions** to memorize: Standard, Fast, Slow, and N₂O parasitic.
- ⭐ **Fast SCR is the fastest** because it has the lowest activation energy (~ 40 kJ/mol vs 80 kJ/mol for Standard SCR).
- ⭐ The **DOC** pre-oxidises NO → NO₂ to drive the Fast SCR pathway.
- ⭐ The **ASC** controls NH₃ slip — placed downstream of SCR.
- ⭐ **Optimal urea dosing α ≈ 1.05** in real ECU maps; α < 1 → slip; α > 1.1 → NH₃ breakthrough.
- ⭐ **V₂O₅/WO₃/TiO₂** = heavy-duty SCR; **Cu-zeolite** = light-duty SCR; **Fe-zeolite** = high-T SCR second brick.
- ⭐ **LNT operates in two phases**: **lean storage (Ba(NO₃)₂ formation)** and **rich regeneration (NO₃ release → N₂)**.
- ⭐ **LNT key components**: Pt (oxidation), BaO (storage), Rh (reduction).
- ⭐ **Sulfur poisoning** of LNT forms **BaSO₄** (ΔG° ≈ −1300 kJ/mol) — mitigated by **desulfation at T > 650 °C** under rich conditions.
- ⭐ **Modern BS-VI / Euro 6d architecture** often uses a **hybrid LNT + SCR** for light-duty passenger cars and a **DOC + DPF + SCR + ASC** chain for heavy-duty trucks.
- ⭐ **Cold-start weakness** of SCR: DEF injection inhibited below **180 °C** exhaust temperature → use of **HC dosing** or **close-coupled SCR** as engineering fix.
- ⭐ **GHSV range**: SCR 30 000 – 80 000 h⁻¹; LNT 30 000 – 60 000 h⁻¹.
- ⭐ **DEF consumption** is typically **4 – 6 % of fuel consumption**; DEF tank sized for ≥ 10 000 km between refills.
- ⭐ For LNT numericals: storage stoichiometry is **1 mol Ba : 2 mol NO_x** (as Ba(NO₃)₂).

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Reference Hint (Module 3 Context):** This topic links directly to *BS-VI / Euro 6 emission norms*, *NO_x chemistry*, *EGR limits*, and *NO_x sensor closed-loop dosing* — all part of the broader KTU Module 3 syllabus on advanced diesel aftertreatment.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engine Modification for Effective Emission Control - Advanced Combustion Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Engine Modification for Effective Emission Control - Advanced Combustion Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 : ENGINE MODIFICATION FOR EFFECTIVE EMISSION CONTROL
## Advanced Combustion Technologies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PEMUT522):** This module is a high-weightage, application-oriented segment of the KTU Automotive Pollution and Control course. It directly addresses **Course Outcome CO4** — *“Apply engine modification techniques and advanced combustion strategies to control automotive emissions complying with prevailing emission norms.”*

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** *Advanced Combustion Technologies (ACTs)* refer to a class of in-cylinder combustion strategies that decouple the conventional, simultaneous high-temperature and high-pressure conditions of premixed and diffusion flames inside a Spark Ignition (SI) or Compression Ignition (CI) engine. These technologies—including **HCCI, PCCI, RCCI, LTC (Low-Temperature Combustion)**, and **stratified-charge GDI**—achieve controlled, dilute, low-temperature reactions to simultaneously suppress **NOx and Particulate Matter (PM)** formation, which are the two mutually exclusive pollutants produced in the conventional Otto and Diesel cycles.

In simple board-exam language:
> *“Advanced combustion technologies are smart ways of burning fuel inside the engine cylinder so that the peak temperature is kept low and the air–fuel mixing is controlled, which means harmful oxides of nitrogen (NOx) and soot particles are formed in much smaller amounts.”*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Slow-Cook Pan vs. The Blowtorch"

Imagine you are cooking food in a kitchen:

| Cooking Method | Engine Equivalent | Result |
|---|---|---|
| **Blowtorch** (very hot, fast flame) | Conventional Diesel (Diffusion flame, ~2500 K) | High NOx + high PM (soot) |
| **Stove on low flame** (controlled, slow, well-mixed) | HCCI / PCCI (Premixed, ~1800 K) | Very low NOx + very low PM, but high HC/CO |
| **Smart induction stove** (precise temperature control) | RCCI / LTC | Best of both worlds — low NOx & low PM |

A chef does **not** always need maximum flame. The same logic applies to an engine — *“cooler and more uniform”* combustion drastically cuts pollutants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Pollutant Metrics used in KTU evaluations:**
> - **NOx** — Oxides of Nitrogen (NO + NO₂), formed above ~1800 K
> - **PM** — Particulate Matter (soot), formed in fuel-rich diffusion flames
> - **HC** — Unburnt Hydrocarbons, dominant in low-temperature combustion
> - **CO** — Carbon Monoxide, formed in fuel-rich zones
> - **BSFC** — Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (g/kWh)
> - **η_c (combustion efficiency)** — ratio of heat released to fuel energy

### 1.3 The Classical Trade-off Triangle

A foundational concept in KTU Module 4 is the **NOx–PM–Fuel Consumption Trade-off**:

$$
\text{Lower NOx} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Higher PM or Higher Fuel Consumption}
$$

Conventional CI engines sit at one corner (low PM, high NOx). Conventional lean-burn SI engines sit at another (low NOx, higher HC). **Advanced combustion technologies aim to occupy the *interior* of this triangle** — a zone previously thought unattainable.

![Traditional Trade-off Triangle (Described in Mermaid Section 4)]
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Advanced Combustion Technologies

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes advanced combustion technologies along **three primary axes**:

1. **Ignition Mode** — Spark-initiated (SI) vs. Compression-initiated (CI)
2. **Fuel–Air Mixing** — Premixed vs. Diffusion vs. Partially Premixed
3. **Combustion Temperature** — High-Temperature Combustion (HTC) vs. Low-Temperature Combustion (LTC)

### 2.2 Major ACT Families — Structured Logic Breakdown

#### A. Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition (HCCI)

- The fuel and air are **thoroughly premixed** (homogeneous mixture) *before* the start of compression.
- There is **no spark plug**; ignition occurs when the homogeneous mixture auto-ignites due to compression heating reaching the fuel's auto-ignition temperature.
- Combustion is **quasi-premixed** and occurs at **low flame temperatures** (~1500–1800 K).
- **Why it works:** Low peak temperatures suppress *thermal NOx* (Zeldovich mechanism). No rich diffusion zones suppress *soot (PM)*.
- **Key Challenge:** Difficult to control ignition timing — the mixture auto-ignites whenever conditions are right, not when desired.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HCCI — KTU Board Point:** HCCI is the *ideal* premixed low-temperature combustion mode. It is **NOT** a production technology in pure form due to control difficulties, but forms the *theoretical benchmark* against which all practical ACTs are compared.

#### B. Premixed Charge Compression Ignition (PCCI)

- A *practical* approximation of HCCI, achieved in **modern common-rail diesel engines** by injecting fuel **very early** during the compression stroke.
- Because injection happens early, there is sufficient time for fuel–air mixing *before* ignition begins.
- Result: a partially premixed, low-temperature combustion that significantly reduces both NOx and PM compared to conventional Diesel.
- PCCI is **easier to control** than HCCI because injection timing acts as the primary control parameter.

#### C. Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition (RCCI)

- Uses **two fuels of different reactivity** (e.g., low-reactivity gasoline + high-reactivity diesel).
- The low-reactivity fuel is port-injected (premixed), while the high-reactivity fuel is directly injected to create a **reactivity gradient** inside the cylinder.
- This gradient *spatially and temporally* controls the combustion rate — slow at first, faster later.
- Considered the **most advanced** ACT currently in research; can achieve simultaneous low NOx and low PM at high efficiency.

#### D. Low Temperature Combustion (LTC) — The Umbrella Term

- LTC is a *family* of strategies (including HCCI, PCCI, RCCI) that maintain in-cylinder peak temperatures below the **NOx formation threshold (~1800 K)**.
- Achieved through **high EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation)**, **late injection**, and **highly dilute mixtures**.

#### E. Stratified-Charge Gasoline Direct Injection (SGDI)

- A gasoline (SI) engine technology where fuel is **directly injected into the cylinder** at high pressure.
- The mixture is **stratified** — rich near the spark plug, lean elsewhere.
- Allows operation at very lean *global* equivalence ratios.
- Reduces pumping losses (throttling loss), improves fuel economy, and lowers NOx.
- Used in production vehicles (e.g., Toyota D-4S, Mitsubishi GDI).

#### F. Lean-Burn Spark Ignition (Lean SI)

- Conventional SI engine operated with a **lean air–fuel mixture** (excess air).
- Lower peak temperature → lower NOx.
- Challenge: poor three-way catalyst efficiency in lean conditions, requiring **NOx trap catalysts** or **lean NOx catalysts**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Range / Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoichiometric}}$ | $\phi < 1$ (lean), $\phi = 1$ (stoich), $\phi > 1$ (rich) | Defines mixture strength |
| 2 | Wiebe Mass Fraction Burned | $x_b = 1 - \exp\!\left[-a\left(\dfrac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1}\right]$ | $a \approx 5$, $m = 2\text{–}4$ | Heat release modelling |
| 3 | Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_c = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \cdot Q_{LHV} - \dot{Q}_{loss}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot Q_{LHV}}$ | 0.95–0.99 (CI), 0.90–0.97 (SI) | Energy balance |
| 4 | Brake Specific NOx | $\text{BSNOx} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{NOx}}{P_{brake}}$ | g/kWh | Emission index |
| 5 | NOx Equilibrium (Zeldovich) | $\dfrac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} = k_f[\text{O}][\text{N}_2] - k_r[\text{NO}][\text{N}]$ | mol/m³·s | Kinetic rate |
| 6 | Auto-ignition Delay (Arrhenius) | $\tau_{ig} = A \cdot p^{-n} \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{R T}\right)$ | ms | HCCI / PCCI control |
| 7 | Specific Heat Release | $\dfrac{dQ}{d\theta} = \dot{m}_f \cdot Q_{LHV} \cdot \dfrac{dx_b}{d\theta}$ | J/deg | Combustion phasing |
| 8 | EGR Tolerance Limit | $\text{EGR}_\text{max} \approx 50\text{–}60\%$ for LTC | % (by mass) | LTC enabler |
| 9 | Soot Formation Threshold | Local $\phi > 2$ AND $T > 1400$ K | Local | PM prediction |
| 10 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | — | Otto / Diesel cycle |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU paper setters frequently test the equivalence ratio classification and the Wiebe function. Memorize the meaning of $a$, $m$, $\theta_0$, and $\Delta\theta$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These technologies are the *enabling foundation* for meeting **BS-VI (India)**, **Euro-VI (EU)**, and **EPA Tier 3 (USA)** norms. Production examples include:
- **Toyota Dynamic Force Engine** (high-combustion-efficiency + HCCI-like operation)
- **Mazda Skyactiv-X** (Spark Controlled Compression Ignition — SPCCI)
- **GM HCCI Research Engine** (research demonstrator)
- **Honda i-VTEC + Lean Burn** (Japan domestic market)
- **Bosch Common-Rail + Multi-Pulse Injection** (enables PCCI in modern diesels)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Industrial Context:** The Government of India has mandated **BS-VI Phase 2** norms. ACTs such as PCCI and stratified GDI are the *only* in-cylinder pathways that simultaneously deliver fuel economy, low NOx, and low PM. Every modern Indian SUV (e.g., Mahindra Scorpio-N 2.2L, Tata Nexon 1.5L) uses an advanced combustion technology as a baseline.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wiebe Mass-Fraction-Burned Function

The Wiebe function is the **standard KTU board-exam model** for the rate of combustion inside the cylinder. It gives the cumulative fraction of fuel mass that has been burned as a function of crank angle.

**Starting Assumption:** Combustion rate is governed by an exponential law in the normalized combustion duration.

Let:
- $\theta$ = instantaneous crank angle (degrees)
- $\theta_0$ = crank angle at start of combustion (SOC)
- $\Delta\theta$ = total combustion duration (crank angle)
- $a$ = Wiebe efficiency parameter (typical 4.6 – 6.9)
- $m$ = Wiebe shape parameter (typical 2 for SI, 3 for CI)

The instantaneous mass fraction burned $x_b(\theta)$ is defined by the differential equation:

$$
\frac{dx_b}{d\theta} = \frac{a(m+1)}{\Delta\theta}\,(1 - x_b)\,\left[-\ln(1 - x_b)\right]^{\frac{m}{m+1}}
$$

**Step 1 — Variable substitution**

Let $u = -\ln(1 - x_b)$, so that $(1 - x_b) = e^{-u}$ and $du = \dfrac{dx_b}{1 - x_b}$.

$$
\frac{dx_b}{d\theta} = (1 - x_b)\frac{du}{d\theta}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the governing equation**

$$
(1 - x_b)\,\frac{du}{d\theta} = \frac{a(m+1)}{\Delta\theta}\,(1 - x_b)\,u^{\frac{m}{m+1}}
$$

Cancel $(1 - x_b)$ from both sides:

$$
\frac{du}{d\theta} = \frac{a(m+1)}{\Delta\theta}\,u^{\frac{m}{m+1}}
$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate**

$$
u^{-\frac{m}{m+1}}\,du = \frac{a(m+1)}{\Delta\theta}\,d\theta
$$

Integrating from $\theta_0$ to $\theta$:

$$
\int_{0}^{u} v^{-\frac{m}{m+1}}\,dv = \frac{a(m+1)}{\Delta\theta}\int_{\theta_0}^{\theta}d\theta
$$

The left side gives:

$$
(m+1)\,u^{\frac{1}{m+1}} = \frac{a(m+1)}{\Delta\theta}\,(\theta - \theta_0)
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $(m+1)$ and raise to the $(m+1)$-th power**

$$
u = a\,\left(\frac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1}
$$

**Step 5 — Back-substitute $u = -\ln(1 - x_b)$**

$$
-\ln(1 - x_b) = a\left(\frac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Wiebe Mass Fraction Burned Function**

$$
\boxed{\;x_b(\theta) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-a\left(\frac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1}\right]\;}
$$

**Step 7 — Heat Release Rate (Useful in KTU Numericals)**

The instantaneous rate of heat release is:

$$
\frac{dQ}{d\theta} = \dot{m}_f \cdot Q_{LHV} \cdot \frac{dx_b}{d\theta}
$$

Substituting the derivative of the Wiebe function:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{dQ}{d\theta} = \dot{m}_f \cdot Q_{LHV} \cdot \frac{a(m+1)}{\Delta\theta}\,(1 - x_b)\,\left[-\ln(1 - x_b)\right]^{\frac{m}{m+1}}\;}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Auto-Ignition Delay (Arrhenius Form)

The ignition delay $\tau_{ig}$ is **the central parameter controlling HCCI and PCCI**. KTU expects the student to derive the Arrhenius-type ignition delay expression.

**Starting Point:** Auto-ignition kinetics follow a first-order Arrhenius-type rate law:

$$
\frac{dC_f}{dt} = -A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)\,C_f^n
$$

where:
- $C_f$ = fuel concentration (kmol/m³)
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor (s⁻¹)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (J/kmol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant (8314 J/kmol·K)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $n$ = reaction order

**Step 1 — Order of reaction $n = 1$ (typical simplification for HCCI kinetics)**

$$
\frac{dC_f}{C_f} = -A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) dt
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from initial concentration $C_{f,0}$ to the value at ignition $C_{f,ig}$:**

$$
\int_{C_{f,0}}^{C_{f,ig}} \frac{dC_f}{C_f} = -A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)\int_0^{\tau_{ig}} dt
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{C_{f,ig}}{C_{f,0}}\right) = -A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)\tau_{ig}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\tau_{ig}$:**

$$
\tau_{ig} = -\frac{1}{A}\ln\!\left(\frac{C_{f,ig}}{C_{f,0}}\right)\exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)
$$

Defining $B = -\dfrac{1}{A}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_{f,ig}}{C_{f,0}}\right)$ (a constant for a given fuel):

$$
\boxed{\;\tau_{ig} = B\,\exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Add Pressure Dependence (Empirical Extension)**

Experimental HCCI data shows $\tau_{ig} \propto p^{-n}$. The full KTU-ready correlation is:

$$
\boxed{\;\tau_{ig} = A'\,p^{-n}\,\exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)\;}
$$

where $p$ is the cylinder pressure (bar) and $n \approx 1$ for many hydrocarbons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** The Arrhenius form explains *why* HCCI is hard to control. A small change in cylinder temperature (e.g., 10 K) causes a multiplicative change in $\tau_{ig}$ — this sensitivity is the **knock / ringing** problem of HCCI.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Combustion Efficiency $\eta_c$

The combustion efficiency measures how completely the chemical energy of the fuel is converted to heat energy in the cylinder.

**Energy balance on the cylinder** during the combustion interval:

$$
\dot{m}_f \cdot Q_{LHV} = \dot{Q}_{released} + \dot{Q}_{loss,HC} + \dot{Q}_{loss,CO} + \dot{Q}_{wall}
$$

where $\dot{Q}_{loss,HC}$ and $\dot{Q}_{loss,CO}$ are the energy carried away by unburnt hydrocarbons and incomplete combustion CO.

**Step 1 — Define the energy loss fractions:**

$$
f_{HC} = \frac{\dot{m}_{HC} \cdot Q_{HC}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot Q_{LHV}}, \quad f_{CO} = \frac{\dot{m}_{CO} \cdot Q_{CO}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot Q_{LHV}}, \quad f_w = \frac{\dot{Q}_{wall}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot Q_{LHV}}
$$

**Step 2 — Combine losses:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{released} = \dot{m}_f \cdot Q_{LHV}\,(1 - f_{HC} - f_{CO} - f_w)
$$

**Step 3 — Define combustion efficiency:**

$$
\boxed{\;\eta_c = \frac{\dot{Q}_{released}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot Q_{LHV}} = 1 - f_{HC} - f_{CO} - f_w\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Typical numerical values (memorize for KTU):**

| Engine | $\eta_c$ | $f_{HC}$ | $f_{CO}$ | $f_w$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Conventional SI | 0.95 | 0.025 | 0.020 | 0.005 |
| HCCI | 0.92 | 0.060 | 0.015 | 0.005 |
| PCCI | 0.96 | 0.020 | 0.015 | 0.005 |
| Conventional CI | 0.98 | 0.005 | 0.005 | 0.010 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** HCCI shows a *lower* $\eta_c$ than CI because of incomplete oxidation at low temperature — this is the **HC–CO penalty** of LTC strategies.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Indicated Mean Effective Pressure (IMEP) for ACT

IMEP is the standard metric for comparing ACT performance. The derivation uses the First Law of Thermodynamics for an open system.

**Step 1 — Work per cycle from pressure–volume data:**

$$
W_{cycle} = \oint p\,dV
$$

**Step 2 — Mean effective pressure:**

$$
\text{IMEP} = \frac{W_{cycle}}{V_d} = \frac{1}{V_d}\oint p\,dV
$$

**Step 3 — Net work per cycle in terms of heat released:**

$$
W_{net} = \eta_{th} \cdot \dot{m}_f \cdot Q_{LHV}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine (for an engine running at $N$ cycles/s):**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{IMEP} = \frac{\eta_{th}\,\dot{m}_f\,Q_{LHV}}{N\,V_d}\;}
$$

This expression is used to **quantify how much useful work** each advanced combustion technology extracts per unit displacement volume — the lower IMEP of HCCI (due to its low-temperature, dilute mixture) is a *direct indicator* of its power-density limitation.

---

| **HC** | Medium | Low | **High** | Medium | Medium | Low | Medium |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper PEMUT522]`
**Define Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition (HCCI) engine and list its two main advantages and two main disadvantages.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> *HCCI is a form of internal combustion in which a homogeneously mixed fuel–air charge is compressed to the point of auto-ignition, without a spark or direct injection event triggering combustion. (1 mark)*

**Two advantages:**
- Very low NOx (because peak temperature < 1800 K) — **(0.5 mark)**
- Very low PM / soot (because there are no rich diffusion zones) — **(0.5 mark)**

**Two disadvantages:**
- Difficult to control ignition timing; susceptible to **knock** and **ringing** at high loads — **(0.5 mark)**
- High HC and CO emissions due to incomplete oxidation at low temperature; poor power density — **(0.5 mark)**

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary PEMUT522]`
**What is stratified charge combustion? How does it help in reducing automotive emissions?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> *Stratified charge combustion is a process in which the air–fuel ratio inside the cylinder is intentionally non-uniform — rich in a small zone near the spark plug and lean elsewhere. (1 mark)*

- The **rich zone near the spark** enables stable flame initiation, while the **overall lean mixture** reduces pumping losses and lowers peak temperature. **(1 mark)**
- The reduced peak temperature suppresses **NOx formation**, and the lean overall mixture reduces **CO** and **pumping loss-related HC**. **(1 mark)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)
### *(Module Internal Choice Pattern — KTU ESE 2024 Scheme)*

---

### OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper PEMUT522, ESE Pattern]`**

> **Q.A.** (a) Explain the working principle of **HCCI engines** with a neat schematic of the in-cylinder process. Discuss in detail the **advantages, limitations, and control strategies** used to extend the HCCI operating range. (7 marks)
>
> (b) With the help of a **P–θ (pressure–crank angle) diagram**, explain how **PCCI** differs from a **conventional Diesel** cycle. Show that PCCI bridges the gap between HCCI and conventional CI combustion. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Working Principle (2 marks):**
- HCCI uses a *premixed, homogeneous* fuel–air charge (prepared during the intake stroke).
- During compression, the charge is uniformly heated until the auto-ignition temperature is reached everywhere in the cylinder.
- Auto-ignition occurs *simultaneously at multiple sites* — a distributed, low-temperature, low-pressure-rise-rate combustion follows.
- No spark plug is required; no diffusion flame is formed.

**Step 2 — Schematic (2 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.3 (HCCI PROCESS SEQUENCE). Describe verbally the in-cylinder flow:
- Intake stroke: air + fuel enter as a homogeneous mixture
- Compression: charge heated uniformly
- Auto-ignition: distributed
- Combustion: low temperature, low rate of pressure rise

**Step 3 — Advantages (1 mark):**
- Very low NOx and PM emissions
- High theoretical thermal efficiency (premixed, similar to Otto)
- Fuel-flexible

**Step 4 — Limitations (1 mark):**
- Difficult ignition timing control
- Limited operating range (low-to-mid load only)
- HC and CO penalties at low temperature

**Step 5 — Control Strategies (1 mark):**
- **Variable Valve Timing (VVT)** — to trap hot residuals for auto-ignition
- **EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation)** — to control combustion rate
- **Dual-fueling (RCCI)** — to manage reactivity
- **Pre-heated intake air** — to extend auto-ignition limit

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Conventional Diesel P–θ (2 marks):**
- Late injection near TDC → diffusion-controlled combustion
- Premixed burn phase (very short) followed by mixing-controlled phase
- Very high peak pressure rise rate (5–10 bar/deg) → high peak temperature (~2500 K)
- Result: **High NOx, high PM**

**Step 2 — PCCI P–θ (2 marks):**
- Early injection (60–80° BTDC) — fuel has time to premix
- Combustion occurs as a controlled, premixed low-temperature burn
- Smooth pressure rise (1–2 bar/deg) → low peak temperature (~1900 K)
- Result: **Low NOx, low PM**

**Step 3 — Bridge between HCCI and CI (1.5 marks):**
- HCCI: fully premixed, no injection, ideal control
- PCCI: partially premixed, early injection, practical control
- Conventional CI: late injection, diffusion-controlled, high emissions
- PCCI is the *engineering realization* of HCCI in a production-feasible common-rail diesel

**Step 4 — Sketch the P–θ comparison (1.5 marks):**
Draw three curves: HCCI (gentle slope, low peak), PCCI (moderate slope, moderate peak), CI (steep slope, very high peak). Label:
- $p_{max,HCCI} < p_{max,PCCI} < p_{max,CI}$
- $\left(\dfrac{dp}{d\theta}\right)_{max}$ follows the same order

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall:**
> - **Do not confuse** "premixed HCCI" with "premixed SI". In SI, the spark ignites a flame; in HCCI, the whole mixture auto-ignites.
> - **Do not write** "HCCI has no PM at all". HCCI *significantly reduces* PM but small amounts still form in wall-quench regions and crevice volumes.
> - **For the P–θ sketch**, examiners check the **rate of pressure rise** — a common error is drawing HCCI with a steeper slope than CI, which is the exact opposite of reality.

---

### OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper PEMUT522, ESE Pattern, Alternative Choice]`**

> **Q.B.** (a) Derive the **Wiebe mass-fraction-burned function** $x_b(\theta)$ used in combustion modelling, and explain the physical meaning of the parameters $a$, $m$, $\theta_0$, and $\Delta\theta$. (7 marks)
>
> (b) With reference to **Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition (RCCI)**, explain the concept of *reactivity gradient*, and discuss why RCCI achieves simultaneous low NOx and low PM. List the practical challenges of RCCI implementation. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Derivation** (5 marks — full credit if the chain from governing ODE to final closed form is shown):

Step 1 — Write the governing differential equation for the Wiebe function:

$$
\frac{dx_b}{d\theta} = \frac{a(m+1)}{\Delta\theta}\,(1 - x_b)\,\left[-\ln(1 - x_b)\right]^{\frac{m}{m+1}}
$$

Step 2 — Substitute $u = -\ln(1 - x_b)$, $du = \dfrac{dx_b}{1 - x_b}$:

$$
\frac{du}{d\theta} = \frac{a(m+1)}{\Delta\theta}\,u^{\frac{m}{m+1}}
$$

Step 3 — Separate variables:

$$
u^{-\frac{m}{m+1}}\,du = \frac{a(m+1)}{\Delta\theta}\,d\theta
$$

Step 4 — Integrate both sides from $\theta_0$ to $\theta$:

$$
(m+1)\,u^{\frac{1}{m+1}} = \frac{a(m+1)}{\Delta\theta}\,(\theta - \theta_0)
$$

Step 5 — Solve for $u$:

$$
u = a\left(\frac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1}
$$

Step 6 — Back-substitute $u = -\ln(1 - x_b)$:

$$
\boxed{\;x_b(\theta) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-a\left(\frac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1}\right]\;}
$$

**Physical meaning of parameters** (2 marks):

| Parameter | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $a$ | Wiebe efficiency parameter — fraction of fuel mass that is completely burned at end of combustion | 4.6 – 6.9 |
| $m$ | Wiebe shape parameter — controls how early or late the peak heat release occurs | 2 for SI, 3 for CI |
| $\theta_0$ | Crank angle at **start of combustion (SOC)** | BTDC or ATDC |
| $\Delta\theta$ | Total **combustion duration** in degrees of crank angle | 30° – 90° |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Reactivity Gradient Concept (2 marks):**
- RCCI uses two fuels of **different auto-ignition reactivities**.
- Typically: a **low-reactivity fuel** (e.g., gasoline, ethanol) is port-injected to form a homogeneous premixture; a **high-reactivity fuel** (e.g., diesel) is directly injected to create localized high-reactivity zones.
- This produces a **spatial gradient of reactivity** inside the cylinder — from low reactivity in the bulk premixture to high reactivity near the diesel spray.

**Step 2 — Why It Reduces NOx and PM Simultaneously (2.5 marks):**
- Because of the reactivity gradient, combustion begins *first* in the high-reactivity (diesel-rich) zones — but only mildly, since they are spatially limited.
- Combustion then propagates *progressively* to the low-reactivity (gasoline-rich) zones, which burn slowly and at low temperature.
- Result: peak temperature is **kept below the NOx formation threshold** (1800 K) — low NOx.
- At the same time, the **premixed low-reactivity fuel never forms rich diffusion pockets** — low PM.
- This is why RCCI breaks the classical **NOx–PM trade-off**.

**Step 3 — Practical Challenges (2.5 marks):**
- **Fuel infrastructure** — requires two separate fuel tanks and supply systems
- **Combustion control** — the reactivity gradient is sensitive to in-cylinder temperature, EGR, and injection timing
- **High-pressure injection requirements** — multiple direct injections needed
- **Cost and complexity** — not yet viable for mass-market passenger vehicles
- **Cold-start difficulty** — auto-ignition is unreliable at low temperatures

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall:**
> - **Do not write the Wiebe function without showing the integral step.** Examiners specifically allocate **1.5 marks** to the integration step — skipping it costs significant marks.
> - **Do not confuse** the parameter $a$ with the Wiebe function itself. The constant $a$ is the *efficiency* parameter, not the function argument.
> - **For RCCI**, do not simply say *"use two fuels"*. You must explicitly describe the *reactivity gradient mechanism* and link it to the suppression of *both* NOx and PM.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU PEMUT522 — Module 4: Advanced Combustion Technologies — Rapid Revision**

### A. Definitional Anchors (Memorize Verbatim)
- **HCCI:** Homogeneous charge, compression-ignited, no spark, auto-ignition at multiple sites.
- **PCCI:** Premixed CI, achieved by early fuel injection in common-rail diesel.
- **RCCI:** Reactivity Controlled CI, dual-fuel, port-injected low-reactivity + direct-injected high-reactivity.
- **LTC:** Low-Temperature Combustion — umbrella term for HCCI/PCCI/RCCI.
- **Stratified GDI:** Gasoline Direct Injection with non-uniform air–fuel ratio.

### B. Pollutant–Technology Map
- **Low NOx + Low PM:** HCCI, PCCI, RCCI
- **Low NOx only (with higher HC/CO):** Lean SI
- **Low PM only (with higher NOx):** Stratified GDI
- **Conventional (high NOx, low PM):** SI
- **Conventional (low PM, high NOx):** CI

### C. Key Equations (Must Be On Your Fingertips)
- Wiebe: $x_b = 1 - \exp\!\left[-a\left(\dfrac{\theta-\theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1}\right]$
- Heat Release: $\dfrac{dQ}{d\theta} = \dot{m}_f\,Q_{LHV}\,\dfrac{dx_b}{d\theta}$
- Ignition Delay: $\tau_{ig} = A'p^{-n}\exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$
- Combustion Efficiency: $\eta_c = 1 - f_{HC} - f_{CO} - f_w$

### D. Numerical Mnemonics
- **HCCI peak temperature:** 1700 K
- **PCCI peak temperature:** 1900 K
- **Conventional CI peak temperature:** 2500 K
- **NOx formation threshold:** ~1800 K
- **EGR limit for stable LTC:** ~50–60% (mass basis)
- **Wiebe $a$:** 5 (typical) ; **Wiebe $m$:** 2 (SI), 3 (CI)

### E. The Three Trade-off Decoupling Strategies
1. **High EGR** — dilutes mixture, raises specific heat, lowers flame temperature
2. **Early injection** — promotes premixing, reduces diffusion-rich zones
3. **Dual fuel** — creates reactivity gradient, smooths combustion rate

### F. KTU Board-Exam Favourite Question Types
1. *"Compare HCCI and conventional CI"* — always use the table format
2. *"Derive Wiebe function"* — show every integration step
3. *"Why does RCCI achieve low NOx and low PM simultaneously?"* — answer using reactivity gradient
4. *"Sketch the P–θ diagram for stratified GDI"* — emphasize two-stage heat release
5. *"Discuss the role of EGR in LTC"* — always mention heat capacity and dilution effect

### G. Top Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing HCCI with SI (HCCI has *no spark*)
- ❌ Saying HCCI eliminates PM entirely (small PM from wall-quench still forms)
- ❌ Skipping integral steps in Wiebe derivation
- ❌ Mixing up "premixed" and "pre-combustion" mixing
- ❌ Writing $r^{\gamma-1}$ as $r^{\gamma} - 1$ in thermal efficiency

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Advanced Combustion Technologies**
