## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level OBD-II System Architecture (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[O2 Upstream Sensor] --> ECU[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    B[MAF Mass Air Flow Sensor] --> ECU
    C[MAP Manifold Pressure Sensor] --> ECU
    D[CKP Crankshaft Position Sensor] --> ECU
    E[Coolant Temperature Sensor] --> ECU
    F[Fuel Rail Pressure Sensor] --> ECU
    G[NOx Sensor BS-VI] --> ECU
    H[DPF Differential Pressure Sensor] --> ECU

    ECU --> CAN[CAN Bus SAE J2284]
    CAN --> TCM[Transmission Control Module]
    CAN --> ABS[ABS Module]
    CAN --> BCM[Body Control Module]

    ECU --> M[MIL Malfunction Indicator Light ON Dashboard]
    ECU --> MEM[Non-Volatile DTC Memory + Freeze Frame]

    MEM --> DLC[16 pin SAE J1962 Data Link Connector]
    DLC --> SCN[Generic OBD II Scan Tool]

    ECU --> INJ[Fuel Injectors Pulse Width Modulated]
    ECU --> IGN[Ignition Coils Dwell Control]
    ECU --> EGR[EGR Valve Position Control]
    ECU --> SCR[SCR Dosing Injector for BS-VI]

    SCN --> M01[Mode 01 Live Data]
    SCN --> M03[Mode 03 Read DTCs]
    SCN --> M04[Mode 04 Clear Codes]
    SCN --> M06[Mode 06 Catalyst Test]
    SCN --> M09[Mode 09 VIN and CVN]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram traces the **full signal chain** from sensor input through the ECU's diagnostic decision logic, out to the MIL and DTC memory, and finally to the technician's scan tool via the standardized DLC.

### 4.2 DTC Code Structure Decoder (SAE J2012)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DTC["P0301 Full DTC"] --> SYS["P Powertrain"]
    DTC --> TYPE["0 Generic SAE"]
    DTC --> SUB["3 Ignition Misfire"]
    DTC --> IDX["01 Cylinder Number"]

    SYS --> S1["B Body"]
    SYS --> S2["C Chassis"]
    SYS --> S3["U Network"]

    TYPE --> T1["1 Manufacturer Specific"]
    TYPE --> T2["2 Generic SAE"]
    TYPE --> T3["3 Reserved"]

    SUB --> U1["1 Fuel Air Metering"]
    SUB --> U2["2 Fuel Air Metering"]
    SUB --> U3["3 Ignition Misfire"]
    SUB --> U4["4 Auxiliary Emission"]
    SUB --> U5["5 Vehicle Speed Idle"]
    SUB --> U6["6 ECU Output Circuit"]
    SUB --> U7["7 8 9 Transmission"]

    IDX --> X1["00 to 99 Specific Fault"]
```

### 4.3 OBD-II Diagnostic Workflow (Mechanic's Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Vehicle Arrives for Service] --> S1[Connect Scan Tool to 16 pin DLC]
    S1 --> S2[Read Mode 03 Stored DTCs]
    S2 --> S3{Any DTCs Present?}

    S3 -- No --> S3A[Check Readiness Monitors]
    S3A --> S3B{All Monitors Complete?}
    S3B -- Yes --> S3C[Vehicle Passes OBD Health Check]
    S3B -- No --> S3D[Perform Drive Cycle to Complete Monitors]

    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Read Mode 02 Freeze Frame]
    S4 --> S5[Read Mode 01 Live Data PIDs]
    S5 --> S6[Analyze Fuel Trims O2 Sensor Voltages]
    S6 --> S7[Diagnose Root Cause per DTC Manual]
    S7 --> S8[Perform Repair Replace Component]
    S8 --> S9[Clear Codes Using Mode 04]
    S9 --> S10[Perform Post Repair Drive Cycle]
    S10 --> S11{Reproduction DTC?}

    S11 -- No --> S12[Recheck Readiness Monitors All Complete]
    S12 --> S13[Repair Verified - Release Vehicle]
    S11 -- Yes --> S7
```

### 4.4 OBD-II DLC Pinout (16-Pin SAE J1962 Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PINROW[SAE J1962 16 Pin Trapezoidal Connector]
        direction LR
        P1[Pin 1 Manufacturer Reserved] --- P2[Pin 2 J1850 Bus Positive] --- P3[Pin 3 Manufacturer Reserved] --- P4[Pin 4 Chassis Ground] --- P5[Pin 5 Signal Ground] --- P6[Pin 6 CAN High ISO 15765] --- P7[Pin 7 K Line ISO 9141] --- P8[Pin 8 Manufacturer Reserved]
    end

    subgraph PINROW2[Bottom Row]
        direction LR
        P9[Pin 9 Manufacturer Reserved] --- P10[Pin 10 J1850 Bus Negative] --- P11[Pin 11 Manufacturer Reserved] --- P12[Pin 12 Reserved] --- P13[Pin 13 Reserved] --- P14[Pin 14 CAN Low ISO 15765] --- P15[Pin 15 L Line ISO 9141] --- P16[Pin 16 Battery Positive 12V]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin 6 (CAN-H)** and **Pin 14 (CAN-L)** are the two most commonly used pins in **all BS-VI vehicles** because the ISO 15765 CAN protocol is mandatory for emission-related diagnostics. **Pin 16** provides unswitched 12 V battery power to the scan tool, and **Pin 4/5** provide chassis and signal grounds.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Style]**
**Define OBD-II. List any four standardized diagnostic modes defined under SAE J1979.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
**On-Board Diagnostics, Second Generation (OBD-II)** is a legislatively mandated, standardized self-diagnostic system in motor vehicles that continuously monitors emissions-related components and powertrain systems, stores fault information as Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs), and alerts the driver via the Malfunction Indicator Light (MIL) when monitored parameters exceed calibrated thresholds.

**Any four SAE J1979 Modes (4 × 0.375 = 1.5 marks):**

1. **Mode $01** — Read live Powertrain data (PIDs such as RPM, coolant temperature, MAF, O₂ voltage, fuel trim).
2. **Mode $02** — Read freeze-frame data captured at the moment a DTC was stored.
3. **Mode $03** — Read stored (confirmed) emission-related DTCs.
4. **Mode $04** — Clear DTCs, freeze-frame data, and reset readiness monitors and MIL.

> *(Optional extra: Mode $06 — On-Board test results for non-continuous monitors; Mode $09 — Vehicle information including VIN and CVN.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**Explain the meaning of the Diagnostic Trouble Code "P0420" using the SAE J2012 decoding structure. What is its emission-control significance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Decoding (2 marks):**
| Position | Character | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1st | **P** | Powertrain system (engine, transmission, emissions) |
| 2nd | **0** | Generic SAE-defined code (applies to all manufacturers) |
| 3rd | **4** | Auxiliary Emission Control subsystem |
| 4th–5th | **20** | Specific fault index — "Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold, Bank 1" |

**Emission Significance (1 mark):**
**P0420 indicates that the three-way catalytic converter (TWC) on Bank 1 has lost its oxygen storage capacity and is no longer converting hydrocarbons (HC), carbon monoxide (CO), and oxides of nitrogen (NOx) efficiently. The ECU detects this by comparing the amplitude of the downstream O₂ sensor waveform with the upstream sensor — a degraded catalyst allows the post-catalyst O₂ voltage to closely track the pre-catalyst signal. This results in a **substantial increase in tailpipe HC, CO, and NOx emissions** and the vehicle will fail the PUC/BS-VI in-use compliance test.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of an OBD-II system. List the various emission-related components monitored continuously and non-continuously by the ECU.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the OBD-II catalyst efficiency monitoring principle. How does the ECU distinguish between a healthy and a degraded three-way catalyst using the upstream and downstream oxygen sensors? Derive the catalyst efficiency expression and compute the efficiency for the following data: $V_{\text{up}} = 0.90$ V, $V_{\text{down}} = 0.55$ V. State whether a DTC will be stored.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) OBD-II Architecture (7 marks):**

*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.1 for the block diagram — 2 marks)*

The OBD-II system consists of **five functional layers**:

1. **Sensor / Input Layer (1.5 marks):** A network of sensors provides real-time data to the ECU:
   - **Wide-band / narrow-band O₂ sensors** (upstream & downstream of catalyst) — measure exhaust oxygen concentration.
   - **Mass Air Flow (MAF) sensor** — measures intake air mass.
   - **Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) sensor** — measures manifold vacuum.
   - **Crankshaft Position (CKP) & Camshaft Position (CMP) sensors** — measure engine speed and timing.
   - **Engine Coolant Temperature (ECT) sensor** — for closed-loop enable.
   - **Fuel Rail Pressure sensor**, **Vehicle Speed Sensor**, **Knock Sensor**, **NOx sensor** (BS-VI), **DPF differential pressure sensor** (diesel).

2. **Processing Layer (1 mark):** The **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** runs the diagnostic software algorithms, executes the Built-In Tests (BITs), compares measured values with pre-calibrated thresholds stored in non-volatile memory, and makes Pass/Fail decisions.

3. **Communication Layer (0.5 marks):** The **CAN bus (ISO 15765)** enables the ECU to communicate with other modules — TCM, ABS, BCM — and with the external scan tool.

4. **Output / HMI Layer (0.5 marks):** The **Malfunction Indicator Light (MIL)** alerts the driver, while the **DTC memory** stores the fault code along with **freeze-frame data** (snapshot of engine parameters at the moment of fault).

5. **External Diagnostic Interface (0.5 marks):** The **16-pin SAE J1962 Data Link Connector (DLC)** allows the technician to connect a scan tool and access standardized SAE J1979 modes.

**Continuous monitors (1 mark):** Misfire, Fuel System, Comprehensive Components.
**Non-continuous monitors (1 mark):** Catalyst Efficiency, EVAP System, O₂ Sensor, EGR, Secondary Air, DPF, SCR (BS-VI).

---

**(b) Catalyst Efficiency Monitoring (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** A healthy three-way catalyst (TWC) has a high **oxygen storage capacity (OSC)** due to cerium oxide (CeO₂) in its washcoat. As the ECU switches between rich and lean closed-loop commands, the catalyst **absorbs and releases oxygen**, smoothing the exhaust oxygen profile. Consequently, the **downstream (post-cat) O₂ sensor** sees a **slow, low-amplitude voltage swing** (~0.1–0.3 V), while the **upstream (pre-cat) O₂ sensor** swings rapidly between ~0.1 V (lean) and ~0.9 V (rich).

When the catalyst degrades, the OSC drops, and the downstream waveform begins to **track the upstream** — both signals show high-amplitude, in-phase switching.

**Efficiency Expression (2 marks):**

$$
\eta_{\text{cat}} = \left(1 - \frac{V_{\text{down}}}{V_{\text{up}}}\right) \times 100\%
$$

**Numerical Computation (2 marks):**

Given: $V_{\text{up}} = 0.90$ V, $V_{\text{down}} = 0.55$ V.

$$
\eta_{\text{cat}} = \left(1 - \frac{0.55}{0.90}\right) \times 100\% = (1 - 0.6111) \times 100\% = 38.89\%
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Since $\eta_{\text{cat}} = 38.89\%$ is **well below the OBD-II calibrated threshold of ~50–60%**, a **DTC P0420 (Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold, Bank 1)** **WILL be stored** and the MIL will be illuminated. The vehicle will require catalyst replacement to clear the code and restore emission compliance.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With reference to BS-VI emission norms in India, explain the role of OBD in emission control. Discuss how OBD-II helps in **in-use compliance testing** versus the older OBD-I system.

**(b) [7 marks]** A vehicle reports DTC **P0171 (System Too Lean, Bank 1)** with Short-Term Fuel Trim of **+19%** and Long-Term Fuel Trim of **+14%**. Calculate the **Combined Fuel Trim (CFT)** and explain its diagnostic significance. List any **three probable root causes** and outline the systematic OBD-driven repair procedure using Modes $01, $03, $04, and $06.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Role of OBD in BS-VI Emission Control (7 marks):**

**Legislative Context (1.5 marks):** India adopted **BS-VI emission norms** (equivalent to Euro 6) from **1 April 2020**, mandating drastic reductions in tailpipe NOx (~70% reduction from BS-IV) and particulates. To ensure that vehicles remain compliant **throughout their operational life** (up to 10 years / 160,000 km), the Indian government, under the **Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR)** and aligned with **AIS-137 (Automotive Industry Standard)**, made **OBD-II with Stage 2 (OBD-II) requirements mandatory** for all BS-VI vehicles from inception.

**Role in Emission Control — Five Key Functions (4 marks):**

1. **Continuous Emission Surveillance:** Misfire detection prevents raw HC breakthrough; fuel system monitoring prevents mixture excursions.
2. **Catalyst & Aftertreatment Protection:** Monitors TWC, DPF (Particulate Filter), SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction) and GPF (Gasoline Particulate Filter) in real time.
3. **Driver Awareness:** The MIL warns the driver; the Indian PUC system cross-references OBD readiness.
4. **Workshop Repair Standardization:** Standardized codes enable any service center to diagnose accurately.
5. **Regulatory In-Use Compliance:** The Calibrated Verification Number (CVN — Mode $09) and Permanent DTCs (Mode $0A) prevent tampering and code-clearing fraud.

**OBD-II vs OBD-I — In-Use Compliance (1.5 marks):**

| Aspect | OBD-I (Pre-1996) | OBD-II (Mandatory) |
|---|---|---|
| Connector | Manufacturer-specific | **Standardized 16-pin SAE J1962** |
| Codes | Proprietary | **Standardized SAE J2012** |
| Live Data | Limited | **Mode $01, $02, $06** |
| Readiness Monitors | Not standardized | **Mandatory** (fails inspection if incomplete) |
| Protocol | Vendor-specific | **CAN, ISO 9141, KWP2000** |
| Tamper Resistance | None | **Mode $0A permanent DTCs, CVN** |
| Coverage | Engine only | Engine + Transmission + EVAP + Aftertreatment |

**Conclusion:** OBD-II transforms emission compliance from a one-time *homologation event* into a **lifetime, enforceable contract** between the manufacturer, the owner, and the regulatory authority.

---

**(b) Combined Fuel Trim Diagnosis (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Recall Combined Fuel Trim (CFT) (1 mark):**

$$
\text{CFT} = \text{STFT} + \text{LTFT}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute (1 mark):**

$$
\text{CFT} = (+19\%) + (+14\%) = +33\%
$$

**Step 3 — Threshold Classification (1 mark):**

- $\vert \text{CFT} \vert \leq 10\%$ → Normal operation.
- $10\% < \vert \text{CFT} \vert \leq 25\%$ → Advisory, watch for vacuum leak.
- $\vert \text{CFT} \vert > 25\%$ → **Confirmed DTC; immediate diagnosis required.**

Since $\text{CFT} = +33\% > 25\%$, the lean condition is **severe and confirmed**.

**Step 4 — Diagnostic Significance (1 mark):** A +33% combined positive trim means the ECU is **adding 33% more fuel** than the base calibration to keep the air-fuel ratio at stoichiometry. This is a substantial compensation indicating a **real lean fault**, not a transient one. Without this OBD-driven correction, the actual mixture would be extremely lean, leading to **misfire, NOx spikes, and possible engine damage**.

**Step 5 — Three Probable Root Causes (1.5 marks):**

1. **Unmetered air ingress / vacuum leak** in the intake manifold gasket, PCV hose, brake booster line, or throttle body seal.
2. **Contaminated / dirty MAF sensor** producing a falsely low air-mass reading.
3. **Low fuel rail pressure** due to a failing fuel pump, clogged fuel filter, or leaky pressure regulator.

**Step 6 — Systematic OBD Repair Procedure (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Mode \$03:} & \quad \text{Confirm P0171 and check for related codes (P0101 MAF, P0300 misfire).} \\
\text{(ii) Mode \$01 PIDs:} & \quad \text{Read STFT (\$0106), LTFT (\$0107), MAF g/s (\$0110), short-term O₂ voltage (\$0114).} \\
\text{(iii) Physical inspection:} & \quad \text{Smoke-test intake for vacuum leaks, clean MAF, verify fuel pressure spec.} \\
\text{(iv) Mode \$06:} & \quad \text{Review catalyst and O₂ sensor test results to rule out secondary damage.} \\
\text{(v) Mode \$04:} & \quad \text{After repair, clear codes and reset fuel trim KAM.} \\
\text{(vi) Drive cycle:} & \quad \text{Verify STFT and LTFT return to within } \pm 5\% \text{ and confirm readiness.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in OBD Questions — Strictly Avoid:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Positive vs Negative Fuel Trim:** Positive trim = **adding fuel = lean condition** (NOT rich). This is the **#1 valuation trap** for fuel trim questions.
> 
> 2. **Confusing DTC Letter Codes:** Writing "B" for powertrain (it should be "P"), or "P" for body (it should be "B"). The first character is **case-sensitive in the standard**.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up Mode numbers:** Mode $01 = live data, Mode $03 = stored codes, Mode $04 = clear codes. Many students incorrectly state "Mode $04 reads codes."
> 
> 4. **OBD vs EOBD:** India follows OBD-II under AIS-137 (aligned with Euro 6), not the US-only OBD-II. Mention of "CARB" or "California" in the Indian context loses marks.
> 
> 5. **Skipping the derivation step:** KTU examiners **allocate 2–3 marks explicitly for the formula derivation** in calculation questions. Writing only the final answer without showing the substitution and computation steps invites **step-marking penalties**.
> 
> 6. **Missing freeze-frame and readiness monitor discussion:** In a 7-mark architecture question, omitting freeze-frame data and readiness monitors costs at least **1.5 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — OBD and Its Interpretations**
> 
> - **OBD-II** is the standardized, legislatively mandated self-diagnostic system defined by **SAE J1962, SAE J1979, SAE J2012, and ISO 15765** protocols.
> - **DTC Format:** 5-character code `P/B/C/U` (system) + `0/1/2/3` (type) + `1–9` (subsystem) + `00–99` (specific fault).
> - **P0xxx** = Generic powertrain; **P1xxx** = Manufacturer-specific; **B** = Body; **C** = Chassis; **U** = Network.
> - **MIL illumination** occurs on the **second consecutive drive cycle** with a confirmed fault (to avoid false positives — this is the "two-trip" logic).
> - **SAE J1979 Modes:** **$01** Live, **$02** Freeze Frame, **$03** Stored DTCs, **$04** Clear, **$05** O₂ Test, **$06** On-Board Test, **$07** Pending, **$08** Bidirectional Control, **$09** Vehicle Info, **$0A** Permanent.
> - **Readiness Monitors** must be **complete** for the vehicle to pass in-use compliance; incomplete monitors = automatic failure.
> - **Lambda (λ) = 1** at stoichiometry; **λ > 1 = lean; λ < 1 = rich.** Stoichiometric AFR = 14.7 (gasoline), 14.5 (diesel), 17.2 (CNG).
> - **Catalyst efficiency** is monitored via **upstream vs downstream O₂ sensor amplitude comparison**; formula: $\eta_{\text{cat}} = (1 - V_{\text{down}}/V_{\text{up}}) \times 100\%$.
> - **P0420** = Catalyst Bank 1 degraded; **P0171** = System lean Bank 1; **P0300–P0308** = Misfire (HC critical); **P2463** = DPF restriction; **P20EE** = NOx/SCR fault.
> - **Fuel trim convention:** Positive = lean correction (adding fuel); Negative = rich correction (reducing fuel). CFT > ±25% is a confirmed fault.
> - **BS-VI India:** OBD-II Stage 2 mandated from 1 April 2020; AIS-137 governs; in-use compliance period up to **10 years / 160,000 km**.
> - **DLC Pinout:** Pin 6 = CAN-H, Pin 14 = CAN-L, Pin 16 = +12 V, Pin 4 = Chassis GND, Pin 5 = Signal GND.
> - **CVN (Calibration Verification Number)** in Mode $09 detects ECU software tampering and is a **legal safeguard** for in-use compliance.
> - **OBD does NOT repair the vehicle** — it **informs, records, and standardizes** the diagnosis for service technicians and regulators.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Replacement and Retrofit Programs - Scrappage and Relocation, Replacement, Retrofit Program
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Replacement and Retrofit Programs - Scrappage and Relocation, Replacement, Retrofit Program

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: VEHICLE REPLACEMENT AND RETROFIT PROGRAMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Vehicle Replacement Programs (Formal Definition)

A **Vehicle Replacement Program (VRP)** is a structured, government-backed or corporate-led initiative that systematically retires, recycles, or relocates older, high-emission vehicles from active service and substitutes them with newer, cleaner, and more fuel-efficient units compliant with the latest emission standards (such as **BS-VI / Euro 6**).

In the Indian regulatory context, this is governed by the **Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH) Notification G.S.R. 653(E)** and the **Vehicle Scrappage Policy 2021**, which establishes a formalized framework for identifying End-of-Life Vehicles (ELVs) and incentivizing their removal from the road network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight – Definition Standard**
> Any definition in your KTU exam answer must use the phrase *"End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV)"* and reference the *"Fitness Certification"* regime. Examiners specifically look for these KTU 2024 Scheme-aligned terms.

### 1.2 Retrofit Program (Formal Definition)

A **Retrofit Program** is an engineering intervention strategy wherein an existing in-use vehicle is upgraded—without full replacement—by installing additional emission-control hardware or fuel-system components to bring its tailpipe emissions closer to current regulatory norms. Common retrofits include:
- **Diesel Particulate Filters (DPF)**
- **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) systems**
- **CNG / LPG conversion kits**
- **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) retrofits**

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy – The "Old Bicycle" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Two-Wheeler Garage**
> Imagine your college friend owns a 15-year-old two-wheeler that belches black smoke. He has two options to fix it:
> 1. **Replacement (Buy New)**: He sells the old vehicle to a scrap dealer for a small incentive, then buys a brand-new BS-VI compliant vehicle, possibly with a government subsidy. This is the **Vehicle Replacement Program**.
> 2. **Retrofit (Add-On Fix)**: He keeps the same vehicle but installs a catalytic converter upgrade kit or a CNG conversion kit at an authorized workshop. This makes the old engine "cleaner" without buying a new one. This is the **Retrofit Program**.

In essence, **Replacement removes** the polluting source, while **Retrofit cleans** the existing source.

### 1.4 Standardized Regulatory Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **ELV – End-of Life Vehicle** (typically > 15 years for petrol, > 10 years for diesel in India under the 2021 policy).
- **Fitness Test Validity**: **15 years** for petrol / CNG vehicles, **10 years** for diesel vehicles.
- **Scrappage Incentive**: Up to **₹1,5,00,000 (₹1.5 Lakh)** for personal vehicles; up to **₹25 Lakh** for commercial heavy vehicles.
- **GST Rebate on New Purchase**: **25%** for personal vehicles and **15%** for commercial vehicles.
- **Registration Fee Rebate**: Up to **100%** waiver on road tax (state-specific).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students**
> The replacement policy is essentially a **Polluter-Pays (PPP)** instrument combined with a **Carrots-and-Sticks** mechanism: carrots (incentives) for scrapping, sticks (high re-registration fees / NGT bans) for retaining old vehicles.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of the Indian Vehicle Scrappage Policy 2021

### Pillar 1: Voluntary Scrappage of Personal & Commercial Vehicles
- Age-based eligibility criteria trigger a **mandatory fitness test**.
- Failure to pass the test results in **de-registration**.
- The vehicle owner is paid a **scrap value** based on the weight of the vehicle and prevailing metal rates.

### Pillar 2: Mandatory Fitness Testing Regime
- Conducted at **Automated Testing Stations (ATS)**.
- Tests cover **braking, emissions, suspension, lights, and structural integrity**.
- Digital Fitness Certificates issued via **VAHAN 4.0 portal**.

### Pillar 3: Establishment of Scrapping Infrastructure
- **Registered Vehicle Scrapping Facilities (RVSF)** – for passenger vehicles.
- **Authorized Scrapyards** – for bulk dismantling.
- Target: At least **75 RVSFs by 2025** across India.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of a Replacement Program

The operational logic can be broken into six structured steps:

1. **Identification of ELVs** through VAAHAN database queries (e.g., age > 15 years, no fitness clearance).
2. **Notification & De-registration** issued by the Regional Transport Authority (RTA).
3. **Incentive Calculation** based on vehicle category, weight, and age.
4. **Transfer to RVSF** for scientific dismantling, depollution (oil, battery, tyres, fluids removal), and shredding.
5. **Issuance of Scrap Receipt (Certificate of Deposit / CoD)** recognized by the bank for discount on new vehicle loan.
6. **Disbursement of Scrappage Value** and **issuance of road tax/GST rebate** on the replacement vehicle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works in Engineering terms**: Replacement is technically superior to retrofit when the *marginal cost of emission reduction* per old vehicle is *higher* than the *marginal cost of producing one new compliant vehicle*. This is called the **Marginal Abatement Cost (MAC)** principle.

## 2.3 Retrofit Program – Operational Logic

| Stage | Activity | Engineering Purpose |
|-------|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Vehicle survey & emission baseline | Establish pre-retrofit CO, HC, NOx, PM levels |
| 2 | Retrofit kit selection (DPF / SCR / CNG) | Match technology to engine & fuel type |
| 3 | Installation at authorized center | Ensure OEM-grade integration |
| 4 | Post-retrofit emission testing | Verify compliance with BS-VI norms |
| 5 | Issuance of Retrofit Compliance Certificate | Legal recognition by RTO |
| 6 | Annual maintenance schedule | Sustain long-term emission performance |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Exam Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE**: All absolute value / norm symbols in this table use `\vert` and `\mid` instead of `|` to preserve the Markdown table integrity. Do not use raw pipes in math!

| # | Formula / Expression | LaTeX Form | Engineering Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|----------------------|------------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Per-vehicle annual emission | $E_{annual} = VKT \times EF$ | VKT = Vehicle Kilometers Travelled, EF = Emission Factor | $\text{g} \cdot \text{km}^{-1}$ |
| 2 | Fleet-level reduction | $\Delta E = N \times (E_{old} - E_{new})$ | N = Number of vehicles replaced | $\text{tonnes} \cdot \text{year}^{-1}$ |
| 3 | Emission reduction percentage | $\eta_{red} = \dfrac{\Delta E}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$ | Efficiency of replacement program | dimensionless ( $\vert \eta_{red} \vert \le 100$ ) |
| 4 | Net Present Value (NPV) | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{B_t - C_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | B = Benefit, C = Cost, r = discount rate, t = year | ₹ (Indian Rupees) |
| 5 | Benefit-to-Cost Ratio (BCR) | $BCR = \dfrac{\sum \dfrac{B_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}$ | Threshold: BCR $\geq 1$ for viable project | dimensionless |
| 6 | Scrappage incentive value | $S_v = W_{scrap} \times P_{steel} \times k$ | $W_{scrap}$ = kerb weight, $P_{steel}$ = steel price, k = realization coefficient | ₹ |
| 7 | Retrofit cost per gram reduced | $MAC = \dfrac{C_{retrofit}}{E_{before} - E_{after}}$ | Marginal Abatement Cost | ₹ $\cdot$ $\text{g}^{-1}$ |
| 8 | Fleet renewal rate | $R_{fleet} = \dfrac{N_{scrapped}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | Measures policy penetration | dimensionless |
| 9 | Discount factor | $d_t = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^t}$ | Used in time-value analysis | dimensionless |
| 10 | Net emission reduction (lifecycle) | $\Delta E_{lifecycle} = \int_{0}^{L} \Delta E(t) \, dt$ | Integrated over vehicle life L | $\text{tonnes}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> These formulas are deployed by organizations like the **International Council on Clean Transportation (ICTT)**, the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)**, and consultancy firms handling **Environmental Impact Assessments (EIA)** for vehicular fleet studies in Tier-1 Indian cities (Delhi NCR, Mumbai, Bengaluru, Chennai, Kolkata).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Fleet-Wide Annual Emission Reduction Due to Replacement

**Problem Setup**:
A city has $N$ old diesel buses (BS-IV era) that emit at a rate of $EF_{old} = 5.0 \text{ g} \cdot \text{km}^{-1}$ of PM. Each bus travels $VKT = 50,000 \text{ km} \cdot \text{year}^{-1}$. They are replaced by BS-VI CNG buses emitting $EF_{new} = 0.05 \text{ g} \cdot \text{km}^{-1}$ of PM. Calculate the total annual PM reduction in tonnes for a fleet of $N = 1000$ buses.

**Step 1: Write the per-vehicle annual emission for the old bus.**

$$E_{old} = VKT \times EF_{old}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$E_{old} = 50{,}000 \text{ km} \times 5.0 \text{ g} \cdot \text{km}^{-1} = 250{,}000{,}000 \text{ g} \cdot \text{year}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Convert to tonnes (1 tonne = $10^6$ g).**

$$E_{old} = \dfrac{250{,}000{,}000}{10^6} = 250 \text{ tonnes} \cdot \text{year}^{-1}$$

**Step 4: Write the per-vehicle annual emission for the new bus.**

$$E_{new} = VKT \times EF_{new} = 50{,}000 \times 0.05 = 2{,}500{,}000 \text{ g} \cdot \text{year}^{-1} = 2.5 \text{ tonnes} \cdot \text{year}^{-1}$$

**Step 5: Calculate per-vehicle annual reduction.**

$$\Delta E_{per\,vehicle} = E_{old} - E_{new} = 250 - 2.5 = 247.5 \text{ tonnes} \cdot \text{year}^{-1}$$

**Step 6: Multiply by fleet size to obtain total annual reduction.**

$$\Delta E_{fleet} = N \times \Delta E_{per\,vehicle} = 1000 \times 247.5 = 247{,}500 \text{ tonnes} \cdot \text{year}^{-1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single calculation **will likely appear in your KTU Part B answer** for a 14-mark problem. Practice this exact algebraic chain with at least three different emission species (CO, HC, NOx, PM).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Net Present Value (NPV) of a Replacement Program

**Problem Setup**:
A state government implements a replacement program for 5000 old commercial vehicles over 5 years. Each replacement costs ₹2,00,000 (total cost = $C_t$) but yields an annual health + fuel benefit of ₹50,000 per vehicle. Discount rate $r = 8\%$. Compute NPV per vehicle.

**Step 1: Write the NPV formula.**

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{B_t - C_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

**Step 2: Identify the cash flows. Initial cost is at $t = 0$.**

$$C_0 = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 3: Annual benefits for $t = 1$ to $t = 5$ are $B_t = 50{,}000$ ₹.**

**Step 4: Compute the discount factor for each year.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= \dfrac{1}{(1+0.08)^1} = 0.9259 \\
d_2 &= \dfrac{1}{(1+0.08)^2} = 0.8573 \\
d_3 &= \dfrac{1}{(1+0.08)^3} = 0.7938 \\
d_4 &= \dfrac{1}{(1+0.08)^4} = 0.7350 \\
d_5 &= \dfrac{1}{(1+0.08)^5} = 0.6806 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Present Value of Benefits (PVB).**

$$PVB = 50{,}000 \times (0.9259 + 0.8573 + 0.7938 + 0.7350 + 0.6806)$$

$$PVB = 50{,}000 \times 3.9926 = 1{,}99{,}631 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 6: Compute NPV per vehicle.**

$$NPV = PVB - C_0 = 1{,}99{,}631 - 2{,}00{,}000 = -369 \text{ ₹}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: A slightly negative NPV indicates that the program *breaks even* with an 8% discount rate. A reduction in discount rate, or a 6-year horizon, would make the program NPV-positive. In a 14-mark KTU answer, **always include the interpretation step**—it carries 2 marks.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Marginal Abatement Cost (MAC) – Retrofit vs. Replacement Decision

**Problem Setup**:
An old taxi emits $E_{before} = 4.5 \text{ g} \cdot \text{km}^{-1}$ of CO. A retrofit kit (₹40,000) reduces it to $E_{after} = 1.2 \text{ g} \cdot \text{km}^{-1}$. A brand-new replacement costs ₹6,00,000 and reduces it to $E_{new} = 0.5 \text{ g} \cdot \text{km}^{-1}$. If annual VKT = 30,000 km, compute MAC of each option.

**Step 1: Write the MAC formula.**

$$MAC = \dfrac{C_{tech}}{VKT \times (E_{before} - E_{after})}$$

**Step 2: Compute annual reduction from retrofit.**

$$\Delta E_{retro} = 30{,}000 \times (4.5 - 1.2) = 30{,}000 \times 3.3 = 99{,}000 \text{ g} \cdot \text{year}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Compute MAC of retrofit.**

$$MAC_{retro} = \dfrac{40{,}000}{99{,}000} = 0.404 \text{ ₹} \cdot \text{g}^{-1}$$

**Step 4: Compute annual reduction from replacement.**

$$\Delta E_{replace} = 30{,}000 \times (4.5 - 0.5) = 30{,}000 \times 4.0 = 120{,}000 \text{ g} \cdot \text{year}^{-1}$$

**Step 5: Compute MAC of replacement.**

$$MAC_{replace} = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{120{,}000} = 5.00 \text{ ₹} \cdot \text{g}^{-1}$$

**Step 6: Decision rule.**

$$MAC_{retro} (0.404) \ll MAC_{replace} (5.00)$$

Therefore, **retrofitting is the more cost-effective engineering choice** in this scenario. This is the *core* decision matrix in fleet management.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Replacement vs. Retrofit

| Parameter | Replacement | Retrofit |
|-----------|-------------|----------|
| Capital Cost | High | Low to Moderate |
| Emission Reduction | 80–95% | 40–70% |
| Implementation Time | Months (procurement) | Days–Weeks |
| Residual Vehicle Life | 10–15 years (new) | Limited by chassis age |
| Regulatory Compliance | Full (BS-VI) | Partial / BS-IV equivalent |
| Disruption to Operations | High (fleet downtime) | Low |
| Best Use Case | Heavily polluting, old fleet | Moderately aged, fuel-flexible fleet |

> [!NOTE]
> **For 14-mark KTU questions** asking *"Compare replacement and retrofit programs"*, reproduce this exact decision matrix and justify with at least two MAC calculations from §3.3.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: End-to-End Vehicle Replacement Program

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Owner Initiates Process] --> B{Is Vehicle Age > 15 yrs?}
    B -- No --> C[Continue Normal Use]
    B -- Yes --> D[Fitness Test at Automated Testing Station]
    D --> E{Test Result}
    E -- Pass --> F[Fitness Certificate Issued for 5 yrs]
    E -- Fail --> G[Vehicle Declared End-of-Life ELV]
    G --> H[Owner Receives Scrappage Incentive]
    H --> I[Certificate of Deposit CoD Issued]
    I --> J[Transfer to Registered Vehicle Scrapping Facility RVSF]
    J --> K[Scientific Dismantling and Depollution]
    K --> L[Shredding and Material Recovery]
    L --> M[Owner Uses CoD + GST Rebate to Buy New BS-VI Vehicle]
    M --> N[Vehicle Re-registered with RTO]
    N --> O[Closed Loop: Cleaner Fleet Operational]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Retrofit Program Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_STAGE
        A1[Old In-Use Vehicle] --> A2[Pre-Retrofit Emission Baseline]
    end
    A2 --> B{Technology Selection Matrix}
    B -- Diesel Heavy --> C1[Install DPF and SCR]
    B -- Diesel Light --> C2[Install Oxidation Catalyst]
    B -- Petrol --> C3[Install TWC Upgrade]
    B -- Fuel Switch --> C4[CNG / LPG Conversion Kit]
    C1 --> D1[Post-Retrofit Emission Test]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> E{Compliance Achieved?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Issue Retrofit Compliance Certificate]
    E -- No --> G[Reject and Recommend Replacement]
    F --> H[Annual Maintenance and Re-Certification]
    H --> I[Sustained Emission Compliance]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree: Replacement vs. Retrofit Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Analyze Old Vehicle] --> T1{Vehicle Age}
    T1 -- Above 20 yrs --> P1[Strongly Recommend Replacement]
    T1 -- 10 to 20 yrs --> T2{Engine Structural Integrity}
    T2 -- Poor --> P1
    T2 -- Good --> T3{Annual VKT}
    T3 -- High > 50,000 km --> P1
    T3 -- Low --> T4{Compliance Gap to BS-VI}
    T4 -- Large gap PM > 70% --> P1
    T4 -- Small gap --> P2[Recommend Retrofit with DPF or CNG]
    P1 --> OUT1[Replace with New BS-VI Vehicle]
    P2 --> OUT2[Retrofit Existing Vehicle]
    OUT1 --> END[End: Net Emission Reduced]
    OUT2 --> END
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Stakeholder Ecosystem of the Scrappage Policy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph POLICY_FRAMEWORK
        GOV[Central and State Government]
        RTO[Regional Transport Office]
        CPCB[CPCB and Pollution Control Agencies]
    end
    subgraph OPERATIONAL_LAYER
        OWN[Vehicle Owner]
        ATS[Automated Testing Station]
        RVSF[Registered Vehicle Scrapping Facility]
        BANK[Bank for Certificate of Deposit]
        OEM[Vehicle Manufacturer for Rebate]
    end
    GOV -->|Notifies Policy| RTO
    GOV -->|Sets Emission Norms| CPCB
    RTO -->|Mandates Fitness| OWN
    OWN -->|Submits Vehicle| ATS
    ATS -->|Fitness Certificate| RTO
    ATS -->|Failure Notice| OWN
    OWN -->|Surrenders ELV| RVSF
    RVSF -->|CoD Issued| BANK
    BANK -->|Discounted Loan| OWN
    OEM -->|GST and Road Tax Rebate| OWN
    RVSF -->|Recycled Steel| GOV
    CPCB -->|Audits Compliance| RVSF
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: Memorize at least **one Mermaid-style process flow** and reproduce it as a labelled ASCII block diagram in your answer sheet. KTU examiners give **2 bonus marks** for any well-drawn schematic, even if it is not asked explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]
**"Define the term 'End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV)' as per the Indian Vehicle Scrappage Policy 2021. List any two key eligibility criteria for a vehicle to be declared as ELV."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks)**: An End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV) is defined under the Vehicle Scrappage Policy 2021 as a vehicle that has either completed its useful service life (typically **15 years for petrol / CNG vehicles** and **10 years for diesel vehicles**), or has failed the mandatory fitness and emission test conducted at an Automated Testing Station (ATS), and is therefore deemed unfit for further safe operation on public roads.
- **Eligibility Criteria (1 mark)**:
  1. Age-based: Vehicle age exceeds the prescribed threshold.
  2. Fitness-based: Failure to pass the automated fitness test (brakes, lights, suspension, structural integrity).
  3. Emission-based: Non-compliance with prescribed emission norms (BS-IV and below as of 2024).

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper 1]
**"Differentiate between a Vehicle Replacement Program and a Retrofit Program with one engineering example of each."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Vehicle Replacement Program (1.5 marks)**: It is a strategy that retires the entire old vehicle and substitutes it with a new compliant unit. **Example**: A municipal corporation scrapping its 15-year-old BS-II diesel buses and inducting brand-new BS-VI electric buses under the FAME-II scheme.
- **Retrofit Program (1.5 marks)**: It is a strategy that retains the existing vehicle but upgrades its emission-control hardware. **Example**: Installing a Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF) on an old truck fleet to reduce PM emissions by 60–80% without buying new vehicles.

---

## PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) – Internal Choice Format

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Pitfall 1**: Students often confuse the *engineering* concept of retrofit with the *mechanical* act of just changing a part. Always explain the **emission-reduction logic** in your answer.
> - **Pitfall 2**: In 14-mark questions, students forget to **draw a diagram**. KTU 2024 Scheme mandates 2–3 marks for diagrams/schematics. Always include one Mermaid-style flowchart or block diagram.
> - **Pitfall 3**: Avoid writing only "definitions" in 14-mark answers. Examiners specifically look for **numerical analysis, formula usage, and decision matrices**.
> - **Pitfall 4**: Do not exceed the 4-page answer booklet norm. Use **bullet points and tables** to be concise.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) – OPTION 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper Set A]

**(a)** Explain the salient features of the Indian Vehicle Scrappage Policy 2021. Discuss its objectives and the incentives offered to vehicle owners. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A municipal fleet of 2,000 diesel buses (BS-IV) travels 40,000 km per bus per year. The emission factor is $EF_{old} = 4.0 \text{ g} \cdot \text{km}^{-1}$ for PM. After replacement with BS-VI CNG buses, the emission factor drops to $EF_{new} = 0.04 \text{ g} \cdot \text{km}^{-1}$. Calculate: (i) the annual PM emission per old bus, (ii) the annual PM emission per new bus, and (iii) the total annual PM reduction in tonnes for the fleet. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution – Part (a) (7 Marks):**

The **Vehicle Scrappage Policy 2021**, announced by the Hon'ble Minister of Road Transport and Highways, has the following salient features:

1. **Voluntary and Phased Implementation (1 mark)**: The policy is voluntary initially, with mandatory fitness tests introduced for vehicles older than the prescribed age. Commercial heavy vehicles (15 years) and personal vehicles (20 years) fall in the first phase.

2. **Incentives Structure (2 marks)**:
   - **Scrappage Value**: Calculated based on kerb weight and prevailing steel prices. Personal vehicles: up to ₹1.5 Lakh; commercial heavy vehicles: up to ₹25 Lakh.
   - **Road Tax Waiver**: Up to 100% waiver (state-specific).
   - **GST Concession**: 25% for personal vehicles, 15% for commercial vehicles.
   - **Certificate of Deposit (CoD)**: A scrap receipt valid as a financial instrument, recognized by banks for discounting on new vehicle loans.

3. **Fitness Test Regime (1 mark)**: Conducted at Automated Testing Stations (ATS) using digital tools. Valid fitness certificate is mandatory for re-registration of vehicles older than 15 years (petrol) or 10 years (diesel).

4. **Scrapping Infrastructure (1 mark)**: Establishment of Registered Vehicle Scrapping Facilities (RVSFs) and Automated Testing Stations. Target: 75 RVSFs by 2025.

5. **Objectives (2 marks)**:
   - **Environmental**: Reduce vehicular air pollution, especially PM and NOx in urban areas.
   - **Economic**: Stimulate the automotive sector by boosting demand for new vehicles.
   - **Resource Recovery**: Promote circular economy through scientific recycling of steel, aluminium, plastics, and rubber.
   - **Road Safety**: Remove unsafe, accident-prone vehicles from the road.

---

**Model Solution – Part (b) (7 Marks):**

**Given Data**:
$N = 2{,}000$ buses; $VKT = 40{,}000 \text{ km} \cdot \text{year}^{-1}$; $EF_{old} = 4.0 \text{ g} \cdot \text{km}^{-1}$; $EF_{new} = 0.04 \text{ g} \cdot \text{km}^{-1}$.

**Sub-question (i): Annual PM emission per old bus (2 marks).**

$$E_{old} = VKT \times EF_{old} = 40{,}000 \times 4.0 = 1{,}60{,}000 \text{ g} \cdot \text{year}^{-1}$$

Converting to kg (1 kg = 1,000 g):

$$E_{old} = 160 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{year}^{-1}$$

**Sub-question (ii): Annual PM emission per new bus (2 marks).**

$$E_{new} = VKT \times EF_{new} = 40{,}000 \times 0.04 = 1{,}600 \text{ g} \cdot \text{year}^{-1}$$

$$E_{new} = 1.6 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{year}^{-1}$$

**Sub-question (iii): Total annual PM reduction for the fleet (3 marks).**

$$\Delta E_{per\,vehicle} = E_{old} - E_{new} = 160 - 1.6 = 158.4 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{year}^{-1}$$

$$\Delta E_{fleet} = N \times \Delta E_{per\,vehicle} = 2{,}000 \times 158.4 = 3{,}16{,}800 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{year}^{-1}$$

Converting to tonnes:

$$\Delta E_{fleet} = \dfrac{3{,}16{,}800}{1{,}000} = 316.8 \text{ tonnes} \cdot \text{year}^{-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: The replacement program results in an annual PM reduction of approximately **317 tonnes** for a fleet of 2,000 buses—a 99% reduction. This is the kind of analysis that fetches full marks in KTU valuation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – OPTION 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper Set C]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Retrofit Program. Discuss DPF, SCR, and CNG conversion kits in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A retrofit DPF kit costs ₹50,000 and reduces PM emission from $2.5 \text{ g} \cdot \text{km}^{-1}$ to $0.4 \text{ g} \cdot \text{km}^{-1}$ in a commercial truck. If the truck runs 80,000 km per year, compute the Marginal Abatement Cost (MAC) per gram of PM reduced. Compare this with buying a new truck (cost ₹15,00,000, new emission factor $0.05 \text{ g} \cdot \text{km}^{-1}$) in terms of MAC. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution – Part (a) (7 Marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks) – Redrawn from §4.2 above:**
The retrofit program follows the sequential architecture:
**Old Vehicle → Baseline Emission Test → Technology Selection → Installation (DPF/SCR/CNG) → Post-retrofit Test → Compliance Certificate → Annual Maintenance**.

**Technology Discussion (4 marks):**

1. **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF) – 1.5 marks**:
   - A wall-flow ceramic filter that traps soot particles from exhaust gases.
   - **PM reduction efficiency**: 70–95%.
   - Requires periodic **regeneration** (burning off trapped soot) using post-injection of fuel or a burner system.
   - Best suited for **diesel commercial vehicles** in urban delivery applications.

2. **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) – 1.5 marks**:
   - Injects **AdBlue (urea solution 32.5%)** into the exhaust stream upstream of a catalyst.
   - Converts NOx into **N₂ and H₂O** through the reaction: $4 \text{NO} + 4 \text{NH}_3 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4 \text{N}_2 + 6 \text{H}_2\text{O}$.
   - **NOx reduction efficiency**: 80–95%.
   - Used for **heavy-duty diesel trucks and buses**.

3. **CNG Conversion Kit – 1 mark**:
   - Replaces diesel/petrol with Compressed Natural Gas as fuel.
   - Reduces **PM by ~90%**, **CO by ~70%**, **NOx by ~40%**.
   - Requires installation of a high-pressure CNG cylinder, pressure regulator, and modified ECU.
   - Approved kits are issued with **PESO (Petroleum and Explosives Safety Organization)** certification in India.

---

**Model Solution – Part (b) (7 Marks):**

**Given Data**:
$C_{retrofit} = 50{,}000$ ₹; $E_{before} = 2.5 \text{ g} \cdot \text{km}^{-1}$; $E_{after} = 0.4 \text{ g} \cdot \text{km}^{-1}$; $VKT = 80{,}000 \text{ km} \cdot \text{year}^{-1}$; $C_{new} = 15{,}00{,}000$ ₹; $E_{new} = 0.05 \text{ g} \cdot \text{km}^{-1}$.

**Step 1: Annual PM reduction by retrofit (2 marks).**

$$\Delta E_{retro} = VKT \times (E_{before} - E_{after}) = 80{,}000 \times (2.5 - 0.4)$$

$$\Delta E_{retro} = 80{,}000 \times 2.1 = 1{,}68{,}000 \text{ g} \cdot \text{year}^{-1}$$

**Step 2: MAC of retrofit (2 marks).**

$$MAC_{retro} = \dfrac{C_{retrofit}}{\Delta E_{retro}} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{1{,}68{,}000} = 0.2976 \text{ ₹} \cdot \text{g}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Annual PM reduction by replacement (1.5 marks).**

$$\Delta E_{replace} = 80{,}000 \times (2.5 - 0.05) = 80{,}000 \times 2.45 = 1{,}96{,}000 \text{ g} \cdot \text{year}^{-1}$$

**Step 4: MAC of replacement (1.5 marks).**

$$MAC_{replace} = \dfrac{15{,}00{,}000}{1{,}96{,}000} = 7.653 \text{ ₹} \cdot \text{g}^{-1}$$

**Comparison and Conclusion**:
$$MAC_{retro} \approx 0.30 \text{ ₹} \cdot \text{g}^{-1} \ll MAC_{replace} \approx 7.65 \text{ ₹} \cdot \text{g}^{-1}$$

The **retrofit option is approximately 25 times more cost-effective** than complete replacement. Therefore, from a marginal abatement cost perspective, **DPF retrofit is the engineering recommendation**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: Students compute MAC using **per km emission** instead of **annual emission**. Always use the **annual** emission reduction in the denominator, not the per-km value. This is a **frequently tested trap**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Definition Anchors**: Memorize *ELV (End-of-Life Vehicle)*, *ATS (Automated Testing Station)*, *RVSF (Registered Vehicle Scrapping Facility)*, *CoD (Certificate of Deposit)*, and *MAC (Marginal Abatement Cost)* — these are KTU keyword markers.
- **Age Cutoffs (India 2024 Scheme)**: **15 years** for petrol / CNG; **10 years** for diesel. Personal vehicle mandatory fitness test: **20 years**.
- **Incentive Structure**: Personal vehicles up to **₹1.5 Lakh**, commercial heavy vehicles up to **₹25 Lakh**, **25% GST rebate** (personal), **15% GST rebate** (commercial).
- **Retrofit vs. Replacement Decision**: Use **MAC** as the primary decision metric. Lower MAC = more cost-effective engineering option.
- **Emission Reduction Math**: $E_{annual} = VKT \times EF$. Fleet reduction = $N \times (E_{old} - E_{new})$. Always convert grams to tonnes at the end ($1 \text{ tonne} = 10^6 \text{ g}$).
- **DPF efficiency**: 70–95% PM reduction. **SCR efficiency**: 80–95% NOx reduction. **CNG conversion**: ~90% PM, ~70% CO, ~40% NOx reduction.
- **NPV Rule**: Program is viable if **NPV $\geq$ 0** and **BCR $\geq$ 1** at the social discount rate (typically 8% in Indian government projects).
- **Circular Economy Angle**: Scrappage is not waste disposal — it is a **resource recovery operation** yielding steel, aluminium, copper, plastics, rubber, and rare earths.
- **Diagram Expectation**: Always include a **flowchart** of the process and a **block diagram** of the system. KTU examiners award 2–3 marks explicitly for neat, labelled diagrams.
- **Pitfall to Avoid**: Never write a 14-mark answer that is *only* textual. Use **tables, formulas, and diagrams** to compress information and score higher.
- **Retrofit Compliance Authority**: The Retrofit Compliance Certificate is issued by the **RTO** (Regional Transport Office) after verification by an **emission testing laboratory** accredited by **NABL (National Accreditation Board for Testing and Calibration Laboratories)**.
- **Polluter-Pays Principle**: The Vehicle Scrappage Policy is a textbook application of the **PPP principle** — the polluter (old vehicle owner) is given an opportunity to switch to cleaner technology with a financial carrot, and penalized with a re-registration fee stick if they continue using the old vehicle.
- **Future Direction (KTU NEP 2020 Vision)**: The next phase of policy will integrate **electric vehicle (EV) subsidies**, **battery scrappage guidelines**, and **mandatory ELV buyback** by OEMs under **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)** norms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy**: For this topic, expect at least one Part A question (3 marks) and one Part B question (14 marks) with internal choice. Memorize the **four formulas** ($E_{annual}$, $\Delta E$, $NPV$, $MAC$) and the **three retrofit technologies** (DPF, SCR, CNG). This alone covers approximately **70% of the marks** in this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intelligent Vehicle-Highway Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Intelligent Vehicle-Highway Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Intelligent Vehicle-Highway System (IVHS)**, also globally standardized as **Intelligent Transportation System (ITS)**, is an integrated, cyber-physical framework that synergizes advanced information processing, communication technologies, sensor fusion, automation, and real-time control algorithms to deliver safer, more coordinated, and environmentally sustainable movement of people and goods on road networks. From an **automotive pollution and control** perspective, IVHS is the technological enabler that minimizes fuel wastage and tailpipe emissions arising from congestion, stop-and-go driving, suboptimal gear selection, and cold-start operation.

The architecture of IVHS rests on three interacting layers:
- **Layer 1 — Vehicle Subsystem:** On-board diagnostics, ECU, GPS, radar/LiDAR, and emission sensors.
- **Layer 2 — Roadway / Infrastructure Subsystem:** Inductive loop detectors, roadside units (RSU), traffic signal controllers, variable message signs (VMS), and weigh-in-motion stations.
- **Layer 3 — Control & Communication Subsystem:** DSRC (Dedicated Short-Range Communication), C-V2X, fiber-optic backbones, and centralized traffic management centers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Within Module 4 of PEMUT522, the Intelligent Vehicle-Highway System is studied specifically as a *pollution-mitigation* technology. The examiner expects you to link ITS features (e.g., adaptive cruise control, eco-routing, platooning) directly to reductions in **CO, HC, NOx, CO₂, and PM emissions**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy Indian city like Kochi or Bengaluru during peak hour. Hundreds of drivers, each guessing the next red light, accelerating hard, braking late, and idling at junctions — every one of these behaviors is a "mini pollution event." Now picture a single, invisible conductor standing on a high tower, whispering precise instructions into every driver's ear: *"Ease off the accelerator in 200 m — the light is turning green soon,"* or *"Switch to 5th gear, the slope ahead is gentle."* That invisible conductor is the **Intelligent Vehicle-Highway System**. It does not eliminate the car; it eliminates the *stupidity* in how the car is driven, which is the root cause of most urban automotive pollution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Grounding:** Toyota's **Eco-Vehicle Energy Control (Eco-VEC)**, Bosch's **Predictive Cruise Control (PCC)**, and India's **BIRAC-funded intelligent traffic corridors** all operationalize this conductor analogy at a fleet scale.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in IVHS

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vehicle Headway | $h$ | s | Time gap between successive vehicles |
| Traffic Density | $k$ | veh/km | Vehicles per unit road length |
| Traffic Flow | $q$ | veh/h | Vehicles passing a point per hour |
| Space Mean Speed | $\bar{v}_s$ | km/h | Average speed over a road segment |
| Fuel Consumption Rate | $\dot{m}_f$ | g/s or L/100 km | Direct pollution proxy |
| Specific Emissions | $e_{CO}, e_{NOx}$ | g/km | Per-km pollutant output |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fundamental relation **$q = k \cdot v$** is the cornerstone of traffic flow theory, and any IVHS ultimately manipulates one or more of $q$, $k$, or $v$ to reduce aggregate emissions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Decomposition of an IVHS

An IVHS achieves pollution reduction through six operational sub-functions, each tied to an emissions outcome:

1. **Sensing & Perception Layer**
   - Inductive loops, video image detectors, radar, LiDAR, floating car data (FCD) from probe vehicles.
   - *Why it matters:* Without accurate traffic state estimation, control becomes blind and emissions rise from sub-optimal green-time allocation.

2. **Communication Backbone (V2X)**
   - **V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure):** Cars receive signal phase and timing (SPaT) data.
   - **V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle):** Cars share speed, position, and acceleration profiles.
   - *Why it matters:* SPaT data lets approaching vehicles **avoid unnecessary stops**, eliminating the cold-idle emissions concentrated at signalized junctions.

3. **Traffic Signal Optimization**
   - SCOOT (Split Cycle Offset Optimization Technique), SCATS, and model-based predictive control.
   - Minimizes **delay** ($d$) and **number of stops** ($N_{stop}$), both of which correlate strongly with CO/HC spikes.

4. **Eco-Driving Advisory (EDA)**
   - Real-time HMI prompts: optimal gear, coasting zones, and target speed.
   - Field studies show **5–15 % fuel and CO₂ reduction** for compliant drivers.

5. **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) & Cooperative ACC (C-ACC)**
   - Maintains a constant time headway $h$, smoothing the *stop-go wave* that propagates upstream.
   - **Platooning** shrinks effective headway to ~0.3 s, raising road capacity and reducing aerodynamic drag for trailing vehicles by up to **10–15 %**.

6. **Route Guidance & Eco-Routing**
   - Centralized engines (Google Maps, HERE) compute minimum-emission or minimum-fuel paths using real-time speed profiles and elevation data.
   - Reduces VMT (Vehicle Miles Travelled) on congested, high-emission corridors.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Control Loop

- **Why does congestion increase emissions per km?**
  Because vehicles in stop-go cycles operate in the low-speed, high-acceleration region of the engine fuel map, where the **air-fuel ratio** is rich, and the catalyst is below its **light-off temperature** (~250–300 °C for TWC). Cold catalysts emit unburned HC and CO almost directly from the tailpipe.

- **How does IVHS counter this?**
  By **reducing the variance of speed** ($\sigma_v$), keeping the engine in its high-efficiency operating region, and ensuring the catalyst stays above light-off through longer, smoother engine-on durations.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q = k \cdot \bar{v}_s$ | Fundamental traffic flow relation |
| 2 | $h = \dfrac{1}{q}$ (in time) | Average time headway |
| 3 | $s = \dfrac{1}{k}$ (in space) | Average space headway (jam spacing) |
| 4 | $E_{agg} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} e_i \cdot L_i$ | Aggregate fleet emissions (g) |
| 5 | $e_{CO_2} = \dfrac{m_f \cdot \eta_{C:O}}{12/44}$ | CO₂ from fuel mass (carbon balance) |
| 6 | $F_{drag} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_d \rho A v^2$ | Aerodynamic drag (basis for platooning benefit) |
| 7 | $a_{opt} = \dfrac{v_{target}^2 - v_{current}^2}{2 d_{rem}}$ | Eco-advisory acceleration |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{f,inst} = f(v, a, \text{gear}, \text{grade})$ | Instantaneous fuel rate (VT-Micro model) |
| 9 | $\eta_{conv} = 1 - e^{-k(T_{cat} - T_{lo})}$ | Catalyst conversion efficiency (Arrhenius-like) |
| 10 | $T_{delay} = \dfrac{L_{queue}}{v_{jam}} - \dfrac{L_{queue}}{v_{arr}}$ | Signal-induced delay |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** The constants $C_d$ (typical **0.25–0.40** for modern cars), $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ at STP, and $T_{lo}$ (light-off temperature, usually **~250 °C** for CO/HC and **~300 °C** for NOx) are favorite exam values.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban Air Quality Management:** IVHS underpins EU-mandated **Low Emission Zones (LEZ)** and India's **odd-even schemes** by providing real-time vehicle identification and corridor pricing.
- **Logistics & Freight:** Truck platooning projects (e.g., EU ENSEMBLE) directly target **GHG reduction** in the heavy-duty segment, which contributes ~25 % of road transport CO₂.
- **Smart City Integration:** ITS feeds data into digital twins of cities, enabling predictive emissions forecasting for compliance with **WHO PM2.5 guidelines**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Speed Variance and Emissions

Empirical research (Ahn et al., 2002; Rakha et al., 2004) shows that instantaneous vehicle emissions depend strongly on the **second-by-second variance** of speed, not just the average. The instantaneous fuel rate can be modeled as:

$$
\dot{m}_f(t) = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 \, v(t) + \alpha_2 \, v(t)^2 + \alpha_3 \, v(t)^3 + \beta_1 \, a(t) + \beta_2 \, a(t)^2
$$

Taking the expected value (time-averaged over a trip) and decomposing $v(t) = \bar{v} + \delta v(t)$:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\dot{m}_f] = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 \bar{v} + \alpha_2 (\bar{v}^2 + \sigma_v^2) + \alpha_3 (\bar{v}^3 + 3\bar{v}\sigma_v^2) + \beta_1 \bar{a} + \beta_2 (\bar{a}^2 + \sigma_a^2)
$$

where $\sigma_v^2 = \mathbb{E}[(\delta v)^2]$ is the speed variance. For a cruise-dominant trip ($\bar{a} \approx 0$, $\sigma_a \approx 0$), the marginal increase in fuel consumption per unit increase in speed variance is:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbb{E}[\dot{m}_f]}{\partial \sigma_v^2} = \alpha_2 + 3\alpha_3 \bar{v}
$$

**Interpretation:** Because $\alpha_3 > 0$ for typical VT-Micro coefficients, an IVHS that reduces $\sigma_v$ directly reduces mean fuel consumption and hence the **CO, HC, and CO₂ emissions** integrated over the trip.

**Conversion to mass emissions using a typical carbon-balance relation:**

$$
\dot{m}_{CO_2}(t) = 44 \cdot \frac{\dot{m}_f(t)}{12} \cdot \chi_C
$$

where $\chi_C$ is the carbon mass fraction of the fuel ($\chi_C \approx 0.86$ for gasoline, $\approx 0.87$ for diesel).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Platooning Drag Reduction and CO₂ Saved

For two identical trucks in tandem, the leading vehicle experiences full aerodynamic drag, but the trailing vehicle in a **drafting** configuration sees a reduction factor $\eta_d$ (typically **0.4–0.65**). The drag force on a single vehicle is:

$$
F_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} C_d \rho A v^2
$$

For a 3-truck platoon, the per-vehicle drag is:

$$
F_{drag,avg} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^2 \cdot \frac{C_{d,lead} + C_{d,2} + C_{d,3}}{3}
$$

With inter-vehicle spacing of ~10 m, $C_{d,2}/C_{d,lead} \approx 0.55$ and $C_{d,3}/C_{d,lead} \approx 0.5$. Therefore:

$$
C_{d,avg} = C_d \cdot \frac{1 + 0.55 + 0.5}{3} = 0.683 \, C_d
$$

The power required to overcome drag is $P_{drag} = F_{drag} \cdot v$, and fuel rate scales roughly linearly with $P_{drag}$ at cruise:

$$
\dot{m}_{f,platoon} = \dot{m}_{f,solo} \cdot \frac{F_{drag,avg}}{F_{drag,solo}} = 0.683 \, \dot{m}_{f,solo}
$$

The **fuel savings per trailing vehicle** is therefore:

$$
\Delta\dot{m}_f = \dot{m}_{f,solo} (1 - 0.683) = 0.317 \, \dot{m}_{f,solo} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 31.7\% \text{ saving}
$$

For a long-haul truck cruising 8 hours at $\dot{m}_{f,solo} = 30$ L/h, the trailing truck saves:

$$
\Delta V_{fuel} = 0.317 \times 30 \times 8 = 76.08 \text{ L per trip}
$$

Converting to CO₂ avoided using gasoline-equivalent factor **2.31 kg CO₂/L**:

$$
\Delta m_{CO_2} = 76.08 \times 2.31 = 175.7 \text{ kg CO}_2 \text{ per trip}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Adaptive Signal Control and Idle Reduction

Consider a signalized intersection with cycle time $C$, red time $r$, and queue discharge headway $h_d$ (≈ 2 s). The average number of stops per cycle is:

$$
N_{stop} = \frac{(r)^2}{2 C \cdot h_d}
$$

Average delay per vehicle (uniform arrival model):

$$
\bar{d} = \frac{C(1-\lambda)^2}{2(1-\lambda g)} \quad ; \quad \lambda = \frac{q}{q_{sat}}, \; g = \frac{C-r}{C}
$$

A signal optimizer (e.g., SCOOT) reduces $r$ by $\Delta r$ where feasible, decreasing $N_{stop}$ by:

$$
\Delta N_{stop} = \frac{(r-\Delta r)^2 - r^2}{2 C h_d} = \frac{-2r\Delta r + \Delta r^2}{2 C h_d}
$$

With $r=40$ s, $C=90$ s, $h_d=2$ s, $\Delta r = 10$ s:

$$
\Delta N_{stop} = \frac{-2(40)(10) + 100}{2 \cdot 90 \cdot 2} = \frac{-700}{360} = -1.94 \text{ stops/cycle/lane}
$$

Each avoided stop saves ~5 g of CO and ~3 g of HC (cold/catalyst-deactivated conditions). Over 100 cycles per day on a 4-lane arterial:

$$
\Delta m_{CO,day} = 1.94 \times 5 \times 100 \times 4 = 3880 \text{ g CO/day} = 3.88 \text{ kg CO/day/km}
$$

This is the **direct, tangible pollution benefit** that a KTU examiner expects you to articulate.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Layered Architecture of an IVHS for Pollution Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Layer 1: VEHICLE SUBSYSTEM] --> A1[ECU and OBD-II]
    A --> A2[GPS and IMU Sensors]
    A --> A3[LiDAR and Radar]
    A --> A4[Catalyst Temp Sensor]
    B[Layer 2: ROADWAY SUBSYSTEM] --> B1[Inductive Loop Detectors]
    B --> B2[Variable Message Signs]
    B --> B3[Traffic Signal Controller]
    B --> B4[Weigh-in-Motion Stations]
    C[Layer 3: COMMUNICATION SUBSYSTEM] --> C1[DSRC or C-V2X]
    C --> C2[5G Backbone]
    C --> C3[Fiber-Optic Network]
    D[Layer 4: CENTRAL CONTROL] --> D1[Traffic Management Center]
    D --> D2[Emissions Inventory Engine]
    D --> D3[Route Optimization Server]
    E[EMISSIONS OUTCOME] --> E1[Reduced CO and HC]
    E --> E2[Lower NOx via smoother flow]
    E --> E3[Lower CO2 from eco-routing]
    A1 --> C1
    A2 --> C1
    A3 --> C1
    A4 --> C1
    B1 --> D1
    B2 --> D1
    B3 --> D1
    B4 --> D1
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    C1 --> A
    C1 --> B
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Eco-Loop of an Intelligent Vehicle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sensor Cluster: Speed, Accel, Grade] --> P1[Signal Processing Unit]
    P1 --> P2[State Estimator: Traffic Ahead Distance]
    P2 --> C1[Controller: Target Speed and Gear]
    C1 --> A1[Driver Advisory HMI or Actuator]
    A1 --> V1[Vehicle Powertrain]
    V1 --> E1[Emissions at Tailpipe]
    E1 -. feedback via V2I .-> P2
    C1 -. V2V share .-> V2[Other Vehicles in Platoon]
    V2 -. cooperative ACC .-> C1
```

### 4.3 Mapping IVHS Features to Specific Pollutants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FEAT1[Adaptive Cruise Control] --> POLL1[CO, HC, PM reduced via smooth torque]
    FEAT2[Eco-Routing] --> POLL2[CO2 reduced by 5 to 15 percent]
    FEAT3[Signal Phase Advisory] --> POLL3[CO and HC reduced by 30 to 50 percent at junctions]
    FEAT4[Truck Platooning] --> POLL4[CO2 reduced by 10 to 15 percent trailing trucks]
    FEAT5[Cold-Start Mitigation via Pre-conditioning] --> POLL5[HC reduced by 60 to 80 percent]
    FEAT6[Variable Speed Limits] --> POLL6[NOx reduced via flow homogenization]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Block Diagram instead of a physical sketch?** A literal illustration of a highway with cars and antennas would carry zero technical information for the examiner. The block diagram above conveys the **flow of data, control, and emissions outcome**, which is precisely what a KTU 14-mark question on IVHS demands.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (Model Q1 — Definition Type)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Similar)]**
*Define the term Intelligent Vehicle-Highway System (IVHS). List any two of its sub-systems.*

**Model Answer:**
Intelligent Vehicle-Highway System (IVHS) is a cyber-physical integration of vehicles, roadway infrastructure, and communication networks that uses sensors, processors, and control algorithms to optimize the safety, efficiency, and environmental performance of road transport. Two sub-systems are: (i) **On-Vehicle Subsystem** — comprising ECU, GPS, radar, and driver HMI; (ii) **Roadside Infrastructure Subsystem** — comprising traffic signal controllers, inductive loops, and variable message signs.

> [!TIP]
> Always end a definition answer with an *application line* — for PEMUT522, mention "reduction of vehicular emissions" or "smooth traffic flow" to align with the module objective.

#### Question 2 (Model Q2 — Conceptual Type)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Similar)]**
*How does an adaptive cruise control (ACC) system contribute to the reduction of vehicular emissions?*

**Model Answer:**
ACC maintains a constant time headway with the preceding vehicle, eliminating the human-driven *stop-go wave* that causes repeated acceleration and braking. This keeps the engine in a narrow, high-efficiency band of the fuel map, avoids catalytic-converter light-off excursions, and reduces the speed variance $\sigma_v^2$. Lower $\sigma_v^2$ correlates directly with reduced CO, HC, NOx, and CO₂ per kilometre.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (Choice 1)

**(a) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Similar)]**
*With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of an Intelligent Vehicle-Highway System. Explain how the three layers interact to reduce automotive emissions.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Architecture (3 marks):** Draw/describe the three-layer block model:
   - **Layer 1 — Vehicle Layer:** On-board sensors, GPS, OBD, ECU, HMI.
   - **Layer 2 — Infrastructure Layer:** Inductive loops, signal controllers, VMS, RSUs.
   - **Layer 3 — Communication & Control Layer:** DSRC/C-V2X, traffic management centre, route-optimization engine.

2. **Interaction (2 marks):** Vehicles broadcast speed/position via V2V (Layer 3 → Layer 1). Infrastructure collects flow data via loops and cameras (Layer 2 → Layer 3). The TMC computes optimal signal timing, eco-routes, and VMS messages, then broadcasts back (Layer 3 → Layer 1, Layer 2).

3. **Emissions linkage (2 marks):** Optimal signal timing reduces stops; platooning cuts drag; eco-routing lowers VMT on congested corridors; the cumulative effect is reduced CO, HC, NOx, and CO₂. The *emissions inventory engine* within Layer 3 quantifies this in g/km and feeds compliance reports to regulators.

**(b) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Similar)]**
*Derive an expression showing how speed variance affects instantaneous fuel consumption. A vehicle travels a 10 km urban corridor in two scenarios: (i) cruise at constant 40 km/h (σ_v = 0); (ii) stop-go with average speed 40 km/h but σ_v = 15 km/h. Using VT-Micro coefficients α₂ = 7.5 × 10⁻⁴ L/s and α₃ = 2.5 × 10⁻⁵ L/s, estimate the percentage increase in fuel consumption for the stop-go case.*

**Model Solution:**

From the VT-Micro model:

$$
\dot{m}_f(t) = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 v + \alpha_2 v^2 + \alpha_3 v^3 + \beta_1 a + \beta_2 a^2
$$

Averaging over time with $\bar{a} = 0$:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\dot{m}_f] = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 \bar{v} + \alpha_2(\bar{v}^2 + \sigma_v^2) + \alpha_3(\bar{v}^3 + 3\bar{v}\sigma_v^2)
$$

For cruise, $\sigma_v = 0$:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\dot{m}_f]_{c} = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 \bar{v} + \alpha_2 \bar{v}^2 + \alpha_3 \bar{v}^3
$$

For stop-go, $\sigma_v = 15$ km/h, $\bar{v} = 40$ km/h:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\dot{m}_f]_{sg} = \mathbb{E}[\dot{m}_f]_{c} + \alpha_2 \sigma_v^2 + 3 \alpha_3 \bar{v} \sigma_v^2
$$

Excess fuel rate:

$$
\Delta \dot{m}_f = (7.5 \times 10^{-4})(15)^2 + 3(2.5 \times 10^{-5})(40)(15)^2
$$

$$
\Delta \dot{m}_f = (7.5 \times 10^{-4})(225) + (7.5 \times 10^{-5})(9000)
$$

$$
\Delta \dot{m}_f = 0.16875 + 0.6750 = 0.84375 \text{ L/h}
$$

The cruise base rate (using all terms) is approximately:

$$
\dot{m}_{f,c} \approx \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 (40) + \alpha_2 (1600) + \alpha_3 (64000)
$$

For typical VT-Micro values ($\alpha_0 \approx 0.5, \alpha_1 \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-2}$), the baseline is ~**3.0 L/h**. The percentage increase:

$$
\%\text{ increase} = \frac{0.84375}{3.0} \times 100 \approx 28.1\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The stop-go scenario consumes ~28 % more fuel than cruise at the same average speed — this is the **central, quantifiable justification** for IVHS-based smoothing of traffic flow.

#### Question B (Choice 2)

**(a) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Similar)]**
*Explain the concept of vehicle platooning. Derive the drag-reduction benefit for a 4-truck platoon cruising at 80 km/h, and estimate the daily CO₂ saved for a long-haul fleet of 100 such platoons operating 10 hours per day.*

**Model Solution:**

**Platooning concept (2 marks):** Platooning is a cooperative-driving scheme in which a convoy of vehicles travels at a small, electronically controlled inter-vehicle gap (~0.3 s headway) using V2V communication and ACC. The small gap exploits **aerodynamic drafting**, reducing the drag on trailing vehicles.

**Drag model (2 marks):** For a single truck:

$$
F_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_d \rho A v^2
$$

With $C_d = 0.6, \rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3, A = 10 \text{ m}^2, v = 80 \text{ km/h} = 22.22 \text{ m/s}$:

$$
F_{drag} = 0.5 \times 0.6 \times 1.225 \times 10 \times (22.22)^2 = 1813.4 \text{ N}
$$

For a 4-truck platoon, drag coefficient reductions (literature values for 10 m spacing): $C_{d,1}/C_d = 1.0$, $C_{d,2}/C_d = 0.55$, $C_{d,3}/C_d = 0.5$, $C_{d,4}/C_d = 0.5$. Average:

$$
C_{d,avg} = C_d \times \frac{1 + 0.55 + 0.5 + 0.5}{4} = 0.6375 \, C_d
$$

Savings per trailing vehicle (3 vehicles):

$$
\Delta F = (1 - 0.6375) \times 1813.4 = 657.4 \text{ N}
$$

Power saved: $\Delta P = \Delta F \cdot v = 657.4 \times 22.22 = 14{,}610$ W per trailing vehicle.

**CO₂ computation (3 marks):** Diesel fuel rate reduction scales with $\Delta P / \eta_{engine}$ where $\eta_{engine} \approx 0.40$:

$$
\Delta \dot{m}_f = \frac{14{,}610}{43 \times 10^6 \times 0.40} \times 1000 \text{ (g/s to kg/h)} \approx 1.22 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Wait — let me redo this in L/h. Using diesel LHV = 43 MJ/kg and density 0.832 kg/L:

$$
\Delta \dot{m}_f = \frac{14{,}610}{0.40 \times 43 \times 10^6} \times 3600 \text{ s/h} = 3.06 \text{ L/h per trailing truck}
$$

For 3 trailing trucks in each platoon, 10 hours of operation:

$$
\Delta V_{fuel,platoon} = 3 \times 3.06 \times 10 = 91.8 \text{ L/day}
$$

For 100 platoons:

$$
\Delta V_{fleet} = 9180 \text{ L/day}
$$

CO₂ avoided (diesel factor = **2.68 kg CO₂/L**):

$$
\Delta m_{CO_2} = 9180 \times 2.68 = 24{,}602 \text{ kg/day} \approx 24.6 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

**Annual impact (300 operating days):** ~**7,380 tonnes CO₂ avoided per year.**

**(b) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Similar)]**
*Discuss, with examples, the role of V2I and V2V communication in reducing tailpipe emissions at signalized intersections.*

**Model Solution:**

**V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure) [3.5 marks]:**
- Cars receive **Signal Phase and Timing (SPaT)** data and **MAP** (intersection geometry) messages from roadside units.
- The on-board HMI advises the driver to *coast*, *ease off*, or *prepare to stop* — minimizing harsh braking and idling.
- Example: Audi's **Green Light Optimized Speed Advisory (GLOSA)** field tests in Europe show **15 % fuel savings** for approach speeds of 50 km/h.
- Reduced cold-idle duration also keeps the **Three-Way Catalyst** above its light-off temperature, dropping CO and HC emissions by up to **30–50 %** at the intersection.

**V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle) [3.5 marks]:**
- Vehicles share position, speed, and acceleration profiles within a 300 m radius using DSRC (5.9 GHz) or C-V2X (PC5 sidelink).
- This allows **Cooperative Adaptive Cruise Control (C-ACC)** to maintain a *shorter and smoother* time headway than human-driven ACC.
- Smoothed car-following reduces the magnitude and propagation of **shockwaves** (the upstream-traveling stop-go waves that double emissions at junctions).
- Example: USDOT's Safety Pilot Model Deployment in Ann Arbor showed that shockwave damping via V2V reduced average emissions per vehicle by **10–20 %** in peak traffic.

**Synergy [included in marks]:** When V2I provides SPaT and V2V synchronizes headways, the intersection functions as a *coordinated node* in a city-wide emissions control network, producing multiplicative (not just additive) emission reductions.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones in IVHS Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing ITS and IVHS:** IVHS is the *vehicle-highway* subset; broader terms like "Smart City" or "IoT" are not the same. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for vague definitions.
> 2. **Forgetting to link to emissions:** A purely "smart traffic" answer that omits the pollution/CO₂ angle is incomplete for PEMUT522. Always quantify the *emission impact*.
> 3. **Unit errors in derivations:** Mixing km/h with m/s, or L/100 km with g/km, is a ½-mark penalty. Convert **before** substituting.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram:** In a 7-mark sub-question on architecture, a missing block diagram costs 1–2 marks. Always draw one — a rough Mermaid-style sketch is acceptable.
> 5. **Omitting catalyst light-off:** Whenever you discuss cold-start or idle emissions, mention the catalyst light-off temperature (~250 °C for TWC) — this is a high-frequency examiner cue.
> 6. **Wrong constant values:** Using $C_d = 0.3$ (car) where the question implies a *truck* ($C_d = 0.6$–$0.8$) loses 1 mark. Read the question stem carefully.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IVHS = ITS** for road transport; it is the *enabling technology* for **pollution-aware traffic management**.
- **Three-layer model:** Vehicle, Infrastructure, Communication/Control — memorize and reproduce in any 7-mark sub-question.
- **Key pollution-control levers of IVHS:** smooth speed profiles (lower $\sigma_v^2$), reduced idling, eco-routing, platooning, and pre-conditioned cold starts.
- **Traffic flow identity:** $q = k \cdot \bar{v}_s$ — the foundation of all traffic-engineering derivations.
- **Catalyst light-off** is the physical link between *engine-on duration* and *tailpipe emissions*; mention it whenever idling/cold-start is discussed.
- **Emission factors** to memorize for the exam:
  - Gasoline: **2.31 kg CO₂ / L**
  - Diesel: **2.68 kg CO₂ / L**
  - Air density: **1.225 kg/m³** at STP
  - Typical car $C_d$: **0.25 – 0.40**
  - Typical truck $C_d$: **0.60 – 0.80**
- **Platooning benefit:** ~10–15 % drag reduction for trailing vehicles at 10 m spacing; up to 30 % at 5 m spacing — *always state the spacing assumed*.
- **Communication standards:** DSRC (IEEE 802.11p, 5.9 GHz) and C-V2X (3GPP Release 14+) — name-drop them to demonstrate current technical literacy.
- **Numerical questions dominate Module 4:** practice at least three derivation problems (variance effect, platooning drag, signal-induced delay) before the exam.
- **Real-world exam keywords to embed:** *cooperative ACC, eco-routing, GLOSA, SCOOT, SCATS, V2X, light-off temperature, aerodynamic drafting, emission inventory* — these are the terms KTU examiners scan for.
- **Last-mile reminder:** When asked "How does IVHS reduce pollution?", your answer must hit *all three* of: (a) reduces idling/stops, (b) reduces speed variance, (c) reduces VMT on congested corridors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pedagogy Note:** Course Outcome CO4 of PEMUT522 directly maps to "Evaluate the role of emerging technologies such as IVHS in vehicular emission control." Every question on this topic is graded against this CO. Frame your answers around **quantified emission reduction**, not just feature-listing.
